THE
PARLIAMENTARY DEBATES
(OFFICIAL REPORT)
IN THE
FIRST SESSION OF THE FIFTH PARLIAMENT OF NORTHERN IRELAND
APPOINTED TO MEET THE FIRST DAY OF MARCH, IN THE
SECOND YEAR OF THE REIGN OF
HIS MAJESTY KING GEORGE VI.
THE SENATE.

---

Tuesday, 1st March, 1938.
VOLUME XXI.
The First Session of the Fifth Parliament of Northern Ireland, appointed to
meet on 1st March, 1938, in the second
year of the reign of King George VI, was
opened by the Governor (His Grace the
Duke of Abercorn, K.G., K.P.) in
person.
The House met at half-past Eleven of
the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER (Right Hon.
the Viscount Bangor, O.B.E., D.L.) in
the Chair.
His Grace, the Governor of Northern
Ireland, being seated on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black
Rod to let the Commons know "It is His
Grace's pleasure they attend him immediately in this House," who being come,
The Clerk Assistant read the Proclamation dissolving Parliament as followeth :
WHEREAS it is provided by section.
eleven of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, as modified by and in pursuance of
certain enactments amending the said Act
that the Governor of Northern Ireland
shall, in his Majesty's name, summon,
prorogue and dissolve the Parliament of
Northern Ireland:
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that
the present Parliament of Northern Ireland should be dissolved and that a new
Parliament should be called:
I, JAMES
NOW, THEREFORE,
ALBERT EDWARD, DUKE OF ABER-
CORN, Knight of the Most Noble Order
of the Garter, Knight of the Most Illustrious Order of Saint Patrick, Governor
of Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation,
do publish and declare his Majesty's
pleasure that the said Parliament be as
from the date of this Proclamation
dissolved, and that the Senators and
Members of the House of Commons be
discharged from further attendance thereat:
AND I do hereby make known to all
his Majesty's loving subjects his Royal
will and pleasure to call a new Parliament
of Northern Ireland, and do further
declare that I have taken order that writs
shall be issued forthwith in his Majesty's
name under the Great Seal of Northern
Ireland, in due form and according to
law, for calling a Parliament of Northern
Ireland to meet at Stormont, Northern
Ireland, on Tuesday, the first day of
March next, and for causing the Senators
and Commons who are to serve in the said
Parliament to be duly returned to and
give their attendance in his Majesty's
said Parliament on Tuesday, the first day
of March next, which writs are to be
returnable in due course of law.
Given this twentieth day of January in
the year of our Lord One Thousand Nine
Hundred and Thirty-eight.
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
A
Election of Speaker
Members Sworn
THE SENATE
His GRACE was pleased to speak as
follows:
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons of Northern Ireland,
in the name and on behalf of his Majesty
I have to let you know that, as soon as
the Members of both Houses shall have
been sworn, the causes of His Majesty's
calling this Parliament of Northern Ireland will be declared to you; and, it
being necessary that a Speaker of the
House of Commons of Northern Ireland
should be first chosen, I declare his
Majesty's pleasure that you, Members
of the House of Commons, do proceed
to the choice of a Speaker, and that you
present such person whom you shall so
choose here for his Majesty's Royal
approbation.
Then the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
Speaker of the House of Commons.
His Grace the Governor, being seated
on the Throne, again commanded the
Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to
let the Commons know "It is His Grace's
pleasure they attend him immediately in
this House," who being come,
The SPEAKER ELECT OF THE
HOUSE OF COMMONS (The Rt. Hon.
Henry George Hill Mulholland) said:
Your Grace, I have to acquaint you that
in obedience to His Majesty's commands
His Majesty's most faithful Commons of
Northern Ireland have, in the exercise
of their undoubted rights and privileges,
proceeded to the election of a Speaker,
and that their choice has fallen upon
myself. I now present myself to your
Grace, and submit myself with all
humility for His Majesty's gracious
approbation.
His GRACE: Mr. Mulholland, being
sensible of your zeal in the public service,
and of your ample sufficiency to execute
all the important duties of the position
which his faithful Commons of Northern
Ireland have selected you to discharge,
I do, on His Majesty's behalf, most
readily approve and confirm you as their
Speaker.
The SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF
COMMONS: Your Grace, I submit myself with all humility and gratitude to
His Majesty's gracious commands. It
is now my duty in the name and on
behalf of the Commons of Northern
Ireland to lay claim by humble petition
to the rights, privileges, and immunities
assured to the Commons of Northern
Ireland by the Government of Ireland
Act, 1920, and that the most favourable
construction shall be put on all their
proceedings.
In regard to myself, I pray that, if in
the discharge of my duties I should inadvertently fall into any error, it may
be imputed to myself alone, and not to
His Majesty's most faithful Commons of
Northern Ireland.
His GRACE: Mr. Speaker of the
Commons of Northern Ireland, in the
name of His Majesty I do most readily
confirm the rights, privileges, and
immunities to which you have laid claim.
With respect to yourself, Sir, although
His Majesty is sensible that you stand
in need of no such assurance, His
Majesty will ever put the most favourable construction on your words and
actions.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons, it will now be the
duty of Members of both Houses of
Parliament of Northern Ireland to take
the required oath.
To-day, at half-past Three o'clock, the
causes of His Majesty's calling this
Parliament of Northern Ireland will be
declared to you.
Then the Commons withdrew.
Members Sworn.
The Senators following took and Subscribed the Oath, or made and subscribed
the Affirmation required by law and
signed the Roll.
Mr. Speaker (the Right Hon. the
Viscount Bangor, O.B.E., D.L.).
John Hanna Robb, Esquire, K.C.
John Andrew Long, Esquire, D.L.
Sir Emerson Crawford Herdman, K.B.E.
James Hill Dickson, Esquire.
Colonel Thomas Sinclair, C.B.
Major John Charters Boyle, D.L.
Sir Joseph Davison, D.L.
King's Speech
King's Speech
1 MARCH 1938
Captain John Robert Perceval-Maxwell,
D.L.
Hugh Duff, Esquire.
John Clements Glendinning, Esquire,
D.L.
Dr. William Gibson.
The Right Hon. Samuel Cunningham.
The Mayor of Londonderry (Sir James
McIlmunn Wilton, M.C.).
Thomas McLaughlin, Esquire.
Joseph Maguire, Esquire.
John McHugh, Esquire.
James Graham Leslie, D.L.
Major Arthur Frederick Dobbs, D.L.
William Barclay, Esquire.
Joseph Cunningham, Esquire.
House adjourned until 3-30 p.m.
House resumed.
KING'S SPEECH
His Grace the Governor, being seated
on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman
Usher of the Black Rod to let the
Commons know "It is his Grace's
pleasure that they attend him immediately in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with
their Speaker,
His Grace delivered the following
Speech from the Throne:-
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
On the occasion of the opening of
this, the first session of the fifth Parliament of Northern Ireland, I feel confident that wisdom and zeal will
characterise your deliberations on the
important matters entrusted to your
control.
It is with sincere regret that my
Ministers have to report that the
volume of employment in 1937, which
showed a steady increase until the
month of September, began to diminish
substantially until January of this
year. This was undoubtedly a reflection of the set-back in trade activity
throughout the United Kingdom which
marked those months. The linen
industry and its ancillary trades, in
particular, felt the full effect of unsettled conditions in the markets of
the Far East, America and the
Continent.
Notwithstanding
this
recent decline in business and commerce, it is noteworthy that in 1937
comparable employment in insured
industries reached the highest level for
seven years, and that there were
the average over thirty thousand more
persons in such employment in that
year than in 1931.
on
My Ministers will continue the policy
of fostering trade in the Home and
Over-seas markets, thereby assisting in
the expansion of the volume of employment in Northern Ireland.
My Government is taking all the
steps within its power to stimulate the
establishment of new industries and
the extension of existing undertakings
likely to provide additional employment of a permanent nature, and I am
gratified to learn that the measures
taken to make more widely known the
extended facilities now available to
industrial undertakings
new
have
aroused considerable interest in the
United Kingdom. It is my earnest
hope that a substantial contribution
towards the solution of the unemployment problem will eventually be
achieved along these lines.
While strife and uncertainty abroad
have unfortunately checked the forward movement of the linen trade, I
trust, as a result of the negotiations
now about to begin between His
Majesty's Government in the United
Kingdom and the Government of the
United States of America, that the
industry may once again be started
upon an upward curve of production
by widening the scope of what has
always been its principal Overseas
market.
I am gratified that local authorities
and public bodies are co-operating with
my Government in promoting works of
public utility which provide useful employment, and that during last year
forty-four schemes were approved,
involving a total capital expenditure
of over nine hundred thousand pounds.
I am glad to know that substantial
assistance is being rendered to building and allied industries by reason of
the large programme of works which
the Government has in hand, both
directly for its own departmental
activities and for departments, such as
King's Speech
King's Speech
THE SENATE
the Post Office Department, which are
not under its control but are of
intimate concern to the community.
I learn with pleasure that the programme of providing suitable accommodation for our Police Force is
steadily proceeding, although a few
years must elapse before the programme
is completed. Again in our provincial
towns
I observe the provision of
Employment Exchanges in conformity
with modern standards of building and
comfort. The success of the Bann
Drainage Works, also, I am informed,
is assured, even though their final completion is still some time off. Large
areas have been saved from the flooding to which they would have been
subjected but for the amelioration provided by these works.
I also see everywhere new elementary
schools being constructed on up-to-date
lines, and for this activity our local
authorities are to be congratulated,
the Government having also given substantial assistance in financing and in
some cases designing these works.
You will be invited to consider a
Measure to amend and consolidate the
law regulating conditions in factories
and workshops on lines similar to the
Bill on this subject which was introduced in the last Parliament.
Proposals will also be laid before
you to amend the law relating to Unemployment Insurance and National
Health Insurance, and to extend the
statutory provisions for the welfare of
the blind, including the lowering by
ten years of the age of qualification for
pension.
A Bill will be introduced authorising my Government to give financial
assistance
to
urban authorities to
enable them to provide houses for our
industrial workers at reasonable rents
and also to facilitate the issue of loans
to the owners of working-class houses,
both in towns and in rural areas, for
the repair of such houses as can be
made fit for human habitation.
A Measure will be laid before you to
enable local authorities to prepare and
carry out schemes for the protection of
life and property in the event of attack
from the air in time of war.
A Bill will be submitted for your
consideration to amend the Exported

i
Animals (Compensation) Acts (Northern Ireland), 1928 and 1932, in order
to give shippers of cattle from
Northern Ireland ports greater protection in the case of foot and mouth
disease discovered or suspected at a
British landing centre.
You will be invited to consider proposals for the amendment of the
Marketing of Potatoes Acts (Northern
Ireland), 1928 and 1934, providing
further powers for the purpose of
maintaining a reasonable price level
in respect of potatoes for shipment.
It was impracticable in the last Parliament to proceed with the
the Bill
designed to encourage and assist the
provision of facilities for physical
training for young persons still at
school and for adult members of the
population who wish to take advantage
of them. Consequently this matter
will be brought forward for your consideration during the forthcoming
Session.
My Government will also lay before
you a Measure to ensure that every
boy and girl shall, subject to necessary
exemptions on account of beneficial
employment, such as farm work, industrial occupation, or other reasonable cause, continue his or her education in an appropriate manner until at
least fifteen years of age.
were
con-
The proposals which
templated in the last Parliament to
enable teachers on retirement to
surrender a portion of their pension in
favour of the grant of a pension payable to a named beneficiary such as a
wife or a daughter will be laid before
you.
A Bill will be introduced extending
the operation of the Loans Guarantee
Act by a further period of two years
until the thirty-first day of March,
1940.
Members of the House of Commons.
The Estimates for
the Public
Services will be laid before you in due
course.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I am glad to learn that preparations
are well advanced for the representa-
1 MARCH 1938
Papers Presented


King's Speech
Regulations, dated 30th November,
1937, made by the Ministry of Agriculture under the Milk and Milk Products
Act (Northern Ireland), 1934.
at the
tion of Northern Ireland
Empire Exhibition to be held this year
in Glasgow, and I am confident that
this Exhibit will enhance the industrial
prestige of the Province and make its
attractions to the tourist even
widely known.
more
It is a great tribute to all concerned
with the conduct of industry in Northern Ireland that industrial relations
between employers and employed have
been so harmonious, and the steady
development of voluntary machinery
for adjusting by amicable negotiations
such points of difference as may arise
from time to time gives me great
satisfaction.
I am gratified to know that facilities
for the instruction and training of unemployed persons are being extended
on practical lines and that increased
opportunities for physical training are
being provided for those who are out
of work.
The report of the Departmental
Committee which has been investigating the question of the protection of
the young and the welfare of the
young offenders has now been received
and will be given full and sympathetic
consideration by my Government.
I have observed with interest the
results of the Census of Northern Ireland which was taken exactly a year
ago to-day and I am sure that the
more detailed reports which are due to
be published during the coming twelve
months will contain much material of
general interest.
I pray that the blessing of Almighty
God may rest upon your deliberations.
Then his Grace was pleased to retire,
and the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.

and proceedings under the Railways Construction Facilities Act, 1864.

The following Senator took and subscribed the Oath:

Sir Crawford McCullagh, Bart.).
KING'S SPEECH.
MOTION FOR ADDRESS.
Mr. SPEAKER having read the
King's Speech,
Major BOYLE (in the uniform of a
Deputy Lieutenant): I beg to move:
"That the following Humble Address
be presented to his Grace the Governor
of Northern Ireland, to offer the
humble thanks of this House for the
Speech which his Grace has addressed
to both Houses of Parliament:
"Your Grace,
"We, his Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave
to offer our humble thanks to your
Grace for the Speech which your
Grace has addressed to both Houses
of Parliament."
I am deeply sensible of the honour
which has been conferred on the county
from which I come by my selection to
move the Address of thanks for the
Gracious Speech at the opening Session
of the Fifth Parliament. I am also
conscious of my inability to perform
adequately this honourable and important duty and must ask for the
indulgence of the House and that forbearance which they always give to
members addressing the House for the
first time.
It is with great satisfaction that we
note the results of the election which
has just taken place. It shows that
there is great confidence in the Prime
Minister and his Government. It shows
that the people are determined to remain
in the security of Ulster and remain a
portion of the British Empire. Throughout the county to which I belongthough it is the smallest in the Province,
it borders Southern Ireland by some 60-
70 miles-good feeling prevails among
all parties.
The great increase in unemployment
figures is most regrettable, but the causes
of it are world wide, as also is our trade.
I am glad to see among the many
measures set out in the Gracious Speech
some provisions are to be made for the
welfare of the blind. Also provisions
for working-class houses that can be let
at reasonable rents-and for facilities
for the issue of loans to owners of working-class houses for their repair and
betterment. It is to be hoped that these
latter facilities will not be so hedged-in
with regulations as will nullify the good
intentions and so prevent the owners
availing themselves of the facilities.
I would like to have seen a drainage
measure included. To the agricultural
community drainage is of the utmost
importance. Under the present many
Acts the necessary preliminary expenses
of major and minor schemes are so great
that the remedial work is not carried
out. The Bann Scheme has undoubtedly been a greater success than
ever contemplated, even if more costly.
A short time ago a progressive mill-owner
living some 15 miles South of Lough
Neagh informed me that now during the
greatest floods he only suffered from back
water for a few hours where previously
he was closed down for days.
I welcome such a progressive programme of useful and practical measures,
as set out in the Gracious Speech, all
designed to meet the needs of the community as a whole.
I am
I am
very conscious of my shortcomings, more especially as the successor
to one who sat in this House from its
inauguration but who through failing
eyesight felt it his duty to retire.
glad to say I still have the advantage of
his wise counsel. I have to thank the
House for so patiently listening to me.
I beg to move.
Mr. DUFF (in morning dress): I
consider it a great privilege to have this
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Duff]
opportunity of seconding the address of
thanks for the Gracious Speech from the
Throne, which has been so ably moved by
As a
the hon. and gallant Gentleman.
representative from one of the border
counties I much appreciate the compliment paid to me and to my native county
of Tyrone, which is honoured by having
of our number as our esteemed
one
Governor.
As is customary, the words contained
in the Gracious Speech again convey to
our people the deep interest his Grace
entertains for their welfare, and knowing
this one considers it natural that the first
reference in it would be to the important
question of unemployment. It is obvious.
that it is always his first concern, and I
am sure all the Members of this House
will join in the fervent hope that, with
the assistance of our Government and a
better feeling in international affairs, an
improvement in our home and overseas
markets will show its effect in reducing
the numbers who are still unemployed.
Having taken a keen interest in public
affairs for many years, I am glad to see
how advantage has been taken of the
Government schemes to improve the housing of our working classes, without which
these would have been impossible. In the
Gracious Speech I see that further financial assistance is to be given to urban
authorities to improve the housing conditions in their areas, and I would like to
suggest to the Government that the proposed new legislation might also be extended to include areas outside urban
districts, where better housing conditions
are also required. I would also hope that
the suggested facilities for the repairs of
working-class houses in the towns and in
the rural districts will be fully taken
advantage of so as to bring them up to the
standard of present-day requirements.
The improvements to the post offices,
labour exchanges, police barracks and
schools, which were long overdue, add
greatly to the comfort, health and happiness of the inhabitants and reflect great
credit on the Government for their financial assistance. The agricultural community will be pleased if the contemplated
legislation regarding shipping of cattle,
and a more reasonable price level for
potatoes for shipment can
plished.
be accom-
In this connection I am glad to see that
reference is made in the Gracious Speech
to the effort the Government is making to
establish new industries. In my own
district I am glad to report that a new
bacon factory is at present being erected,
which will be for the most part under the
control of the Pigs Marketing Board, and
which will help all those who are engaged
in that branch of the agricultural industry. I hope that the Government will
not only give whatever financial assistance
they can to encourage new industries that
are being brought into existence, but
where it is possible they will also give
some financial assistance which might be
necessary to carry on existing industries
over a period of trade depression.
Reference has been made in the Gracious
Speech to the fact that several Measures,
which were to be introduced in the last
Parliament, were unavoidably deferred.
Now that the Prime Minister and his
Government have been returned to power
by an increased majority may I express
the hope that no time will be lost in bringing the legislation referred to into being.
I beg to second the Motion.
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House):
It is with some little diffidence that I
come to address this Senate for the first
time. I follow in the footsteps of two
Leaders of this House who were for a
time my own departmental chiefs. Lord
Londonderry, under whom I served for
only a comparatively short time, I found
always most willing to assist me in anything on which I asked his advice, and
I am sure he brought to the conduct of
the business of this House that ability
which he showed in the administration
of his own department. With Lord
Charlemont I was associated for a still
longer period, and I grew to know, as
this House must also have known, not
only his ability but his enduring and
enlivening charm. I am sure I am only
expressing the anxiety of this House
when I say we all hope he will be
speedily restored to the full enjoyment
of health.
I am diffident in following those two
honoured men, but being, like the last
speaker, a County Tyrone man, associated with him and with others in that
county, I approach my task with the
natural modesty inherent in one who
comes from that well-known county.
May I be allowed to offer my congratula-
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
1 MARCH 1938
tions to the mover and seconder of the
Motion now before the House. They
have discharged what is, after all, a
pleasant, if difficult, task with ability and
with what to me, coming from another
place, was welcome brevity. I am sure
we are all deeply grateful to them for
what they have said to us to-day.
Coming to the Gracious Speech itself,
I am reminded by turning up the records
of what has happened before in this
House, and that many of the matters referred to in the Speech have already been
commented upon in this House. They are
matters upon which legislation was
previously projected and which it was.
not possible to carry out. We hope in
this Session, or in this Parliament at any
rate, to carry out the intentions of the
Government in regard to these matters,
but as they have been referred to in this
House before, perhaps it is not necessary
that I should deal with all of them.
But I would like to say a word or two
with regard to some of them.
The Government, like everybody else,
deeply regrets the large measure of unemployment in our area. It is all the
more disappointing that the end of 1937
and the beginning of 1938 should have
seen a reduction in employment after the
very welcome increase that the early
months of 1937 showed. But when we
talk about unemployment we ought
always to remember that the conditions
that make for the present volume of unemployment are not conditions peculiar
to ourselves, and are not conditions over
which we can have any control. Much
of the present unemployment in our area
is due to the restriction of markets owing
to disturbed conditions in different parts
of the world with which we were, in
trade and commerce, so intimately connected. But I think I can claim that
so far as Government action can go our
Government has not been unmindful of
its duty in regard to this very pressing
problem of unemployment. The legislation which has been passed by our Parliament in an endeavour to attract new
industries to our area is a form of legislation with which I think our own people
are scarcely familiar enough, and in
regard to which I do not think our own
people, apart from the Government, put
forth sufficient effort to make known in
other parts of the world.
May I remind the House that under
the New Industries Act, where a new
industry is likely to provide permanent
employment to a substantial extent, or
where the development of an existing industry is likely to provide permanent
employment to a substantial extent, provision has been made for exemption from
the payment of rates, rent, and possibly
of income tax for the first five years of
the existence of the new undertaking.
Where new buildings are erected or existing buildings purchased and adapted to
the requirements of the undertaking,
loans free of interest for the first five
years are available to cover either the cost
of erection or the purchase price and cost
of adaptation. Further, where the buildings are rented, the Government is prepared to offer an out-and-out grant
equivalent to a reasonable rent for the
first five years.
Those are considerable inducements.
In addition to these facilities the Government is empowered to make loans at
favourable rates towards capital requirements other than expenditure incurred
on the purchase of premises, and make a
contribution towards income tax during
the first five years of the new undertaking's operation. Site grants are also
available, and local authorities are empowered to exempt new undertakings
from rates for varying periods. In addition, loans are also available, although
without the interest-free period, for substantial extensions to existing undertakings.
Having made these provisions by
legislation, the Government sought to
make them as widely known as possible,
and the Ulster Development Council,
under the presidency of Sir Roland
Nugent, was established. May I say that
already there have been inquiries with
regard to the possibility of establishing
in our area one hundred new undertakings. There have been definite
applications in regard to thirty. How
many of those applications will come to
fruition of course no one can yet tell,
because sometimes the applications are not
such as any man of common sense would
support. At any rate, in the short time
that has elapsed since this legislation was
passed and since these facilities became
available, some progress has been made
towards making them widely known, and
some result has been achieved.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Robb]
May I say one other word in regard to
that matter. The Government does a
great deal in the way of publicity, but
surely it is the duty of every man and
woman to do the very best he or she
can to develop new industries, and in
addition to try to make known the possibilities of our country as an attractive
area for the planting of a new
or the
development of an existing industry.
Practically every retailer in our area
must come in contact with wholesalers
from the United Kingdom or from
abroad, and surely it ought to be possible
for some of those retailers, if not all of
them, to suggest to those with whom they
are in personal contact that here is a field
where the business, or any contemplated
extension of the business, of those wholesalers might be planted.
the
I think it would be a good thing if the
public conscience in regard to this matter
were aroused, and men and women made
to recognise that they have a duty
towards the area in which they live; that
they should not leave everything to the
Government, but that they should themselves do something to help on the work
which Parliament has authorised
Government to undertake. I was glad to
hear the last speaker saying that in the
district from which he comes a bacon
factory was being established. And we
have all seen in the newspapers in recent
days the prospect of Nestlé's establishing
a factory in connection with their milk
products near Ballymoney. It is hoped
that both of these undertakings will be
successes in themselves and a splendid
advertisement of what can be done in our
area, and that they will have many
followers.
In addition to that branch of the
Government's activities might I point out
that at the Glasgow Exhibition which
will be opened in May an Ulster pavilion
will be established and erected where our
products, agricultural and manufacturing, and the beauties of our scenery will
be brought to the notice of all the many
thousands perhaps millions-who will
attend at that exhibition. There were
two choices with regard to the exhibit at
the Glasgow Exhibition put before those
in charge of it. One was to have a
pavilion to ourselves; the other was that
we should form part of the exhibit of the
United Kingdom. For reasons partly
political, but mainly practical, it was
decided that our exhibit should form part
of the United Kingdom exhibit. In the
first place the Government conceived it
to be its duty that the fact should never
be lost sight of, so far as we could enforce
it, that Northern Ireland is part of the
United Kingdom, and upon the commercial and industrial side it was felt
that a small pavilion by itself might only
attract a much smaller number of sightseers than an exhibit in the Palace of
Industry to which practically every sightseer at the exhibition was bound to go.
For that reason, although an item by
itself, our exhibit does form part of the
exhibit of the United Kingdom.
Although there is and continues to be
such a large volume of unemployment in
our area a great deal has been done of
course in the social services to help those
who are the victims of unemployment.
And directly the Government has done a
great deal by the development through
the local authorities of schemes of public
utility and also by the development of
new buildings of its own for the purpose
of the police and labour exchanges and
other elements of that sort. In connection with the Post Office I am glad to be
able to inform the House that the extension of the large telephone exchange has
already begun. The plans for that building were plans to cover a much larger
site than the existing building covers.
The work has already been begun to complete the building, which will be one of
the finest of its sort anywhere.
Reference was made in the Speech to
the Bann drainage. The Bann drainage
was not primarily undertaken as a relief
work, but it did its part as a relief
scheme. I am sure it will interest the
House to have some details with regard to
the efficacy of that scheme although its
completion is not yet. Hon. Senators
will be interested to know that 5 million
cubic yards of material have been removed from the bed of the river, including approximately one-quarter million.
cubic yards of solid rock. Most hon.
Senators will also be aware that three
batteries of sluices have been installed
for the purpose of flood control, and in
this connection the House will be interested to know that during the
abnormally heavy rainfall culminating in
a snow blizzard which was experienced
during the five months from November.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
1 MARCH 1938
1936, to March, 1937-not this winter
but last winter-the level of Lough
Neagh was below flood level except for
three days, and even on those days the
level was only half an inch above flood
level. The level of Lough Neagh could
have been still further reduced during
that period but for the fact that current
velocities had to be kept under control so
as not to hinder or endanger the contractors' plant. But even so, only for
three days during those five months was
the level above flood level, and then only
half an inch.
Calculations show that had conditions
remained as before the scheme, flood
level would have been exceeded for more
than four months and the excess would
have been more than 18 inches above
flood level. Surely work of that sort
reflects very great credit on those who
designed and carried out the improvement works. As I said, it was not
originally or at all intended as a scheme
to relieve unemployment.
It was a
scheme undertaken on its merits, and
with that report given to the House, I
hope the House will agree with me that it
reflects the greatest possible credit on the
prescience and skill of those by whom it
was undertaken. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
The Gracious Speech refers to a scheme
for training those who are unemployed
and a scheme for keeping them fit. The
training scheme, which is in its experimental stage only, is interesting, and I
fancy the House will be glad to know
what is being done. There are in
England numbers of training centres for
the uenmployed and an arrangement has
been made whereby 100 at a time of the
unemployed men can be sent there from
our area. There they learn during an
intensive course, which runs up to six
months, under factory conditions, how
to use the tools of a skilled trade.
method of recruitment is this. They must
be between 18 and 35 years of age. They
apply at their own employment exchanges
and then they are interviewed and the
most likely candidates are selected and
sent.
The
The scheme was first operated in July,
1937. Up to the present 187 men have
been sent. Not all of them completed the
course and only four of those that did
complete the course have not yet been
placed in employment. Fifty-two have
been found suitable employment. There
were 38 who for a variety of reasons, sickness or ill health, failed to complete the
course, and there are 93 still under instruction. For an experimental scheme
I think that is a good record. While
they were there the men received unemployment benefit or unemployment
assistance during their training. There
are also schemes in existence for training
boys. The first scheme was for men. The
Gravesend Sea School, run by the
Shipping Federation, trains boys between.
15 and 19 for deck hands. The first boys
left Northern Ireland in June, 1937, and
out of 91 who have so far been sent to
the school 54 are in employment, 18 are
still under training, and of the rest, some
were sent for by their parents and
brought back, others have found other
work, and one or two were expelled.
These efforts that are being made to
brighten the lot of those who are afflicted
with the awful scourge of prolonged unemployment will, I am sure, find welcome
praise in the hearts of hon. Senators.
There is one other matter I might
mention, that is, air-raid precautions. A
Bill will be laid before you with regard
to that. It was at one time suggested
that the Bill passed in England should
include Northern Ireland or be made
applicable to it either by an Act of this
Parliament or by Order in Council. But
there were many disadvantages in regard
to that course. Air-raid precautions are
bound up with the co-operation of fire
brigades, lighting authorities, and local
authorities, in so many ways that it was
felt better that our scheme, although
following the same lines as the English
scheme, should be Our own scheme
adapted to our own conditions, and
accordingly a separate Bill will be laid
before you in this Session to carry out
these aims.
There are two other matters of which
I should like to make some mention connected particularly with my own Department. Mention is made of the intention
of the Government to introduce a Bill,
more or less on the English lines, for the
encouragement of physical training of our
people generally. Anyone who listened
to the broadcast of His Majesty's speech
at the Guildhall the week before last will
be already familiar with the aims with
which this campaign concerns itself. We
here propose to follow the general lines
Debate on the Address
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Mr. Robb]
of the scheme in England, with one
marked exception, and that is, our scheme
it is intended shall help the schools in a
way that is not done under the English
scheme, because under the English
Education Acts the schools are provided
for in other ways. The aim of the whole
scheme is that greater interest will be
taken and greater enthusiasm aroused in
physical exercises of all sorts, not only
amongst children in school, but amongst
the adult population. The need of such
a scheme has been impressed upon the
nation time and time again by those who
have been responsible for the health of
the people and for recruiting the Army.
In no sense will any scheme introduced
here be compulsory, and it will be dependant for its prosperity and development generally on the hearty co-operation
of outside authorities of all sorts-
Government authorities, and not only
local authorities but such bodies as Girl
Guides, Boy Scouts, Y.M.C.A.'s, or any
sort of gymnastic club or athletic society
or agencies which at present cater for
outdoor and indoor development of boys
and girls.
The other matter I want to mention is
the project which the Government has of
laying before Parliament a Bill with
regard to what is called the raising of
the school-leaving age, although it is not
exactly that in practice. What is
intended is that all those for whom there
is provision in schools and who are not
required by the necessities of their
parents or who have no opportunities of
beneficial employment, shall continue to
receive education suited to their needs up
to the age of fifteen. Owing to the number of small schools we have throughout
the country the provision of higher grade
classes is a difficult problem in itself, but
that is the object the Government has set
before it in this Measure, which has been
recommended by a Committee, and which
the Government hopes shortly to lay
before Parliament.
I do not think there is anything else
I desire to refer to. One cannot very well
refer to everything mentioned in the
Speech from the Throne, but these are
matters which seem to me to be outstanding and in regard to which I hope the
Government will receive the hearty cooperation not only of this House but of
another place. I commend very heartily
the Motion before the House.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, "That
the debate be now adjourned."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Debate to be resumed on Thursday
next.
Private Business.
The Speaker acquainted the House,
That the Clerk of the Senate had laid
upon the Table the certificates from the
Examiner that the Standing Orders
applicable to the following Bills had been
complied with:-
Belfast Water,
The Belfast
Children,
Belfast Harbour,
Hospital for
Sick
River Bann Navigation.
Adjournment.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn until Thursday next”.—(Mr.
Robb.)
Adjourned accordingly at ten
minutes before Five o'clock
until Thursday next.
Papers Presented
Committees Appointed
3 MARCH 1938
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 3rd March, 1938.
The Senate met at a quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented.
Ministry of Finance:
Schedule of documents of the Unemployment Assistance Board, the destruction of which is approved by the Inspecting Officers appointed under Rule 2 of the
Schedule appended to Council Order,
Northern Ireland, 20th January, 1925.
Laid before the House (pusuant to Act)
and ordered to lie on the Table.
Joint Unopposed Committee on
Local Bills.
Resolved: "That the Senators following be named of the Joint Unopposed
Committee on Local Bills:-Mr. J.
Cunningham, Mr. McLaughlin."-(Mr.
Robb.)
Joint Kitchen and Refreshment
Committee.
Resolved: "That the Senators following be named of the Joint Kitchen and
Refreshment Room Committee:-Mr. J.
Cunningham, Sir J. Davison, Mr. Glendinning, Mr. Hill Dickson, Mr.
McHugh."(Mr. Robb.)
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
McAllen's Divorce Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons
and read a First time.
Deputy Speakers.
Resolved: "That the Senators following be named Deputy-Speakers of the
Senate during the present Session of
Parliament-Sir J. Davison, Captain
Perceval-Maxwell."-(Mr. Robb.)
Joint Standing Committee on Divorce
Bills.
Resolved: "That the Senators following be named of the Joint Standing
Committee on Divorce Bills: -Major
Dobbs, Dr. Gibson, Captain Perceval-
Maxwell." (Mr. Robb.)
Joint Standing Orders Committee.
Resolved: "That the Senators following be named of the Joint Standing
Orders Committee:-Mr. Barclay, Mr.
Leslie, Sir R. Nugent."-(Mr. Robb.)
BILL PRESENTED.
Hydrogen Cyanide (Fumigation)
Bill [S.],
"to regulate the fumigation of premises
and articles with hydrogen cyanide and
for purposes connected with the matters
aforesaid," presented by Mr. Robb, read
a First time, and ordered to be printed,
and to be read a Second time on the next
sitting day. (Bill 15.)
