Debate on the Address.
Order read for resuming Adjourned
Debate on Question (1st March)—
"That the following Humble Address
be presented to His Grace the Governor,
to offer the humble thanks of this
House for the speech which His Grace
has addressed to both Houses of
Parliament:-
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave
to offer our humble thanks to your
Grace for the speech which your Grace
has addressed to both Houses of
Parliament."-(Major Boyle.)
Question again proposed.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I trust that I may be
permitted to open the few remarks I am
about to make by tendering my respectful
congratulations to the Mover and
Seconder of the Address in respect of
the carefully prepared speeches to which
we listened on Tuesday. Both Members
are of comparatively recent standing in
this House, and what we heard from
them on Tuesday encourages us to hope
we shall very often in future have the
advantage of their participation in our
debates.
The Speech itself does not perhaps give
ground for much in the way of comment.
It opens with an expression of His
Grace's confidence in the wisdom and zeal
of Parliament, and as the Speech was
delivered to both Houses of Parliament
one may perhaps be excused for imagining that the wisdom referred to is expected of this House while the question
of zeal is left for those in another place.
Indeed, it is perhaps becoming that
special stress should be laid on zeal at
the moment as regards an assembly
of which almost a quarter consists
of untried men. We do hope that
Bills will come to us in future bearing
signs if I may humbly say so of
rather more careful examination than
has been the case in the past. And if
we are to exercise our wisdom to its
fullest extent it is above all things necessary that we should be allowed ample
time between the several stages of the
Bills in order to form our opinions about
them.
From that the Speech passes naturally
enough to what is the great question at
the present moment, and that is, the
unfortunate prevalence of unemployment
in Northern Ireland. Explanations
have already been given of the causes
of increasing unemployment amongst us,
and I have no doubt that those explanations are in the main correct. But I am
afraid that there is one cause of unemployment which has not received the
attention which it deserves, and that is
the steps that have been taken to alleviate
the effects of unemployment. Speaking
as one from a rural district, I am afraid
that I must say I have come to the
conclusion that the extension to agricultural labourers of insurance has not
tended to promote employment. There
are still too many cases-in fact I am
afraid the number is increasingin which it pays a man
to
better
draw benefit for himself and
his dependents rather that accept the
rate of wages that local conditions enable
an employer to offer. That is perhaps
one of the difficulties to which the then
Minister of Labour referred when introducing the Bill in this House. We can
only hope that some solution of the difficulty may eventually be found.
Perhaps a more hopeful note is struck
when reference is made to the endeavours
of the Government to foster trade, both
internal trade and trade overseas. I
venture to think that the expansion of
our trade Overseas is of greater importance than the home trade, because,
after all, if you are doing nothing but
supplying your own local necessities you
are very much in the legendary position.
of the race that tried to eke out an
existence by taking in each other's
washing. What we do want is an
expansion of overseas trade. But as
regards the other question of fostering
home industries it is certain that some
success is going to attend the second New
Industries Act passed last session.
The right hon. Gentleman referred.
specially on Tuesday to the establishment
of Nestlé's factory at Ballymoney, the
town near where I live. I may, perhaps,
be pardoned for telling the House that
those who are in any way concerned in
those negotiations as a member of the
local chamber, I myself took a
humble
endeavouring
to
in
part
give as much encouragement as
possible-attach very great importance
to this new industry. We understand
that it is hoped by the proprietors ultimately to arrive at a consumption of
30,000 gallons of milk per day. They do
not expect to do that in five minutes;
they will, perhaps, be fortunate if they
get it in five years. It is obvious that
anything approaching to that must mean
an enormous amount of additional cattle
in the district with the accompanying
extras in the way of employment and
also in trade, because it is obvious that
an increase in the number of cows in
the district means increased feeding
stuffs that have to be supplied and
handled. I believe the number of cows
in the adjacent rural districts is approximately 18,000. Anyone who knows the
habits of cows will know it takes more
than 18,000 COWS to supply 30,000
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
3 MARCH 1938
gallons per day even if the large amount
still required for liquid consumption is
left out of consideration altogether. We
do look forward to the establishment of
that industry and its probable growth as
a very helpful sign.
on
Again, His Grace dwells with approval
on the endeavours which are being made
by the public authorities with Government assistance to expend large sums of
money
works of public utility.
Again, thinking, perhaps, rather of the
rural districts than of the city and
suburbs, one is faced with the obvious
necessity of improving not only the water.
supply but also sanitation of many of
our small towns and villages such as they
are. All these things cost money and
money can only be provided by the
municipal authorities at the cost of the
ratepayers.
Sooner or later the ratepayers must bear the burden in respect
of any additional amenities provided,
and it cannot, I am afraid, be denied
that the limit of rate-bearing toleration
has been very nearly reached.
There are some places, the City of
Derry, for instance, which, as we know,
suffers very severely from its ratings. The
City of Belfast has managed to escape
fourpence, for the moment, at all events.
I do not know whether the ninepence will
come into the picture at a later stage, or
whether there is in this case any necessary association between the ninepence
and the fourpence, but the fact does
remain that the ratepayer is beginning to
feel his burden, and to express the opinion
that however necessary this or that may
be he cannot foot the bill much longer.
All the same, it is impossible for anybody to do anything to restrain the
normal expansion of expenditure when it
is a question of providing such essentials
as water, drainage, and, perhaps, even
lighting, and the provision of those
amenities does necessarily involve the
employment of labour.
We are promised again a Bill to deal
with factories and workshops. I think
this is the third Gracious Speech in which
reference to that proposed Bill has been
made, and, therefore, perhaps it is unnecessary to say anything about it this
afternoon. Again, there has been some
further alteration in the law relating to
insurance, with special reference to those
who have the misfortune to be blind. The
alleviation of the sufferings of the blind
is a matter in which the hon. and gallant
Member who sits in front of me (Sir
James Wilton) takes a great interest, and
I think I spoke about that also two years
ago, and perhaps on the intermediate
occasion also.
same.
We are promised a Government Bill to
assist in the provision of houses in the
urban districts. That is a matter which
has been before the Parliament of the
United Kingdom certainly since 1890,
when the Act which many of us will
remember was passed. The problem is
still before us, and the problem is still the
It is to provide a suitable house
at a rent which the person for whom the
house is intended can reasonably be ex-
Whether that will be
pected to pay.
found possible without encroaching upon
the pockets of the ratepayers is a question
for those responsible for the Bill.
can only give them one's best wishes and
hope that some really satisfactory palliative, if not complete solution, may be
found at an early date.
One
We are
Agriculture is not neglected.
promised an extension of the principle of
compensation in respect of cattle, which I
believe is much needed, and something
more has to be done about the selling of
potatoes. Unless something can be done
to get a better price for the potato, in my
district at any rate it will become an
exotic plant, because nobody will bother
to grow it. Potatoes cannot be produced
at the price recently prevailing.
Perhaps the most important item in the
Gracious Speech is this proposal for the
extension of the school-leaving age. That
will, I think, give rise to something in
the nature of controversy, and I think it
may be that the opinions will be different
in the country and in the towns, and
different again in some towns to other
towns. The answer to the problem would
seem to depend to a great extent on local
conditions. There are so very many
aspects of that particular question; there
are many points of view from which its
consideration can be approached, and I
think the Members of this House would
be well advised to wait to cross the bridge
until they come to it, or, in other words,
to wait until they see the Bill.
Again, still, of course with a view to
helping employment, it is proposed to
deal once more with the Loans Guarantee
Acts. I gather from the wording of the
reference in the Gracious Speech that it is
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
proposed only to extend the period during which those loans will be made, and
it is not intended to increase the total
amount of money which may be guaranteed. The House may remember that
under the Bill we passed two years ago
the limit of guarantee is now fourteen
and a half million pounds, and it was
provided a year or two before that the
whole of that might be outstanding at the
same time. I pointed out two years ago
that fourteen and a half million pounds
was a fairly heavy burden on a population.
of 1,300,000, and I think I expressed the
hope that the limit of guarantee had been
reached. I meant the limit of the amount
of money, not the time during which the
money might be lent.
I pointed out that we had added to that
fourteen and a half millions, two and a
half millions guaranteed in respect of
electricity undertakings, for a more
limited period. Taking the whole of that
possible debt together, the possible debt
on the country is certainly rather heavy.
Again, as regards the guarantee, we have
to remember that practically none of the
money guaranteed has, in fact, been lost,
and we know that, particularly in shipbuilding, the use of the guarantee has
been of the greatest value. But I should
like to be assured that the intention is,
as I say, only to extend the time and not
increase the amount.
There are other matters in the Speech
to which I do not propose to allude this
afternoon, because I do not desire to
detain the House unduly. I will content
myself by supporting the humble Address
in reply to the Gracious Speech.
Sir JAMES WILTON: I would like
to add my congratulations to the mover
and seconder of the Address for their
admirable speeches. I would also like to
say how pleased I was to learn from the
Gracious Speech that we are promised
legislation which is long overdue. Speaking for an urban community, I am particularly pleased that the Government intend to introduce a housing Bill. It is
recognised by practically everybody who
is conversant with housing conditions in
urban areas that conditions are in some
respects and I use the word advisedlyappalling. The Government propose to
give grants for the purpose of providing
suitable houses at a suitable rent for the
working classes, and I am pleased to
observe that they also propose to add to
that I was going to say a grant, but I
regret it is only a loan, to put in repair
such houses as are capable of being economically repaired, but the owners of which
are not in a financial position to repair
them.
I would prefer to see the Government
giving a grant instead of a loan because
I can see great difficulty in getting back
a loan. Any of you who have had any
experience of municipal administration
will know that it is very hard to recover
loans of that description, and quite a
number of them are never recovered. I
hope that when the Bill is introduced we
shall discover that the Government are
generous because I can substantiate what
has been said by Senator Leslie that the
liabilities of the ratepayers, at least in
some places, have reached what I might
describe as breaking point. I sincerely
trust that, recognising as I know the Government have recognised the great necessity of legislation regarding this matter,
they will be generous in their grants.
I also wish to say how pleased I am
that legislation is about to be introduced
on the subject of pensions for the blind.
Speaking as I can from personal experience as a member of a court of referees,
where cases of blind persons seeking pensions are investigated, I would like to say
this: that while the Government are to
be thanked for reducing the qualifying
age from fifty to forty years, there is one
other thing I hope they will take into
consideration when drafting the Bill. That
is the very harsh conditions that are at
present attached to the grant of a pension
to a blind person.
If I remember rightly the words used
in the present Act, a person must be
so blind as to be unable to do any work
for which eyesight is necessary. In
practice that means that a person must
be practically stone blind before he or
she can qualify for a pension. I have
known cases such as that of a housewife
who, through having been for forty or
fifty years in the same room, was able
to sweep the floor, and it was held that
she was able to do housework, and consequently she was not eligible for a pension.
I would like the Government to consider
making an alteration in the existing definition of blindness.
I cannot say the same with regard to
the promised legislation on the subject
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address

3 MARCH 1938
of the school leaving age. That will be a
controversial matter, because quite a
number of people have very divergent
views on it. Of course, a great deal will
depend upon the nature of the Bill. If
it is to be a success the exemptions will
require to be very small in number. If
the number of exemptions is large the
object of the Bill will be entirely defeated, because one must take into consideration the fact that parents have
hitherto been able to take their children
from school at fourteen years of age and
send them to work, and there will be
a great temptation to parents to get their
children home to seek work. Therefore,
I hope the Government will not leave too
many loopholes, because if they do there
is not the slightest doubt the intentions
of the Act will be defeated.
Before I sit down I would like to
request that more time than usual might
be given for the consideration of Bills,
and particularly the three Measures I
have mentioned. Much will depend upon
the Bills themselves, and I would ask
the Government to extend the time so
that we may have ample opportunity of
studying and discussing them, and, if
necessary, amending them. I support the
Motion for the Address.
Mr. GLENDINNING: Knowing from
my own personal experience that the
honour of being asked to propose or second
the Motion of thanks for his Majesty's
Gracious Speech is one that carries with
it, especially in the case of a comparatively inexperienced Member, something
of a responsibility, I am glad to be able
to congratulate the mover and seconder
on the adequate manner in which they
performed this duty on the present
occasion. I must also say that I
have seldom heard from a Leader
of the House a more interesting digest of
the Gracious Speech than that given us by
the right hon. Gentleman who now occupies that important position.
In my opinion it is not out of place
here to congratulate the Government on
the distinct tribute of confidence awarded
them by the General Election, not only as
a body responsible for the legislation controlling the public affairs of this Province,
but as a convincing proof that the large
majority of the people are behind
the Government in their determined
resistance to
to any proposals to sever
this
Province from its connection
and allegiance to the United
Kingdom and the Throne.
with
The only distressing fact which emerges
from the speech is that the number of
unemployed in our area has increased. A
speaker in another place stated that there
was no immediate cure for this state of
affairs. It is a reflex of conditions in the
world at large, which occur at certain
times-conditions that even the United
States of America, the greatest of all
industrial countries, appears unable to
cope with.
But I have faith that the
Government will neglect no possible step
to mitigate this evil and will encourage
every course that can be taken to provide
work for the unemployed, whether it be
in attracting new industries here, assisting our manufacturers to obtain a share
of overseas trade, or in the extension of
existing manufactories.
The employment provided by the erection of new elementary schools, labour
exchanges, police barracks and post office
premises, coupled with the Bann drainage
scheme, is evidence that the Government
has not been unaware of its duty regarding this serious problem.
In this respect I should like to inquire
if the Government is aware of the serious
coast erosion which is going on at Portrush, particularly at the West Strand.
This winter's storms have played havoc
with the sand hills and have washed them
away at one point to within a few yards
of the roadway. To provide a satisfactory.
defence against further encroachment is a
task far beyond the capacity of the urban
council to tackle, and I think the Government should get a report from a competent engineer. Here is a real occasion
for the employment of labour, and the
position is critical.
It was to be expected that Measures
mentioned in the last Speech from the
Throne, such as those relating to factories
and workshops, unemployment insurance
and national health insurance, provision
for the welfare of blind persons, provision
of houses for industrial workers in towns
and rural areas, would be again introduced. They will give both Houses useful
and worthy employment during the coming months. It was interesting to hear
from the Leader of the House that Ulster
will implement its own schemes for the
protection of life and property in time of
If each of the larger local authoriwar.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Glendinning]
ties were authorised, under instruction,
to carry out the work of its own defence
that again would necessitate a certain
amount of employment of labour and help
to relieve distress.
The right hon. Gentleman's remark
regarding physical fitness were good hearing. If we are not to be left behind by
Great Britain in the provision of facilities
for physical training the sooner the Bill
is brought forward, passed, and put into
operation the better. Irish manhood and
womanhood, from a physical point of
view, is naturally at least equal to that
of our friends and rivals across the
Channel, and it is, in my opinion, a duty
the Government cannot safely or wisely
neglect, to keep Ulster in line with Great
Britain, and even more especially with
Continental countries in this most necessary and long-needed undertaking. Great
Britain at present is far behind such
countries as, say, Sweden and Germany,
in the development of the physique of the
people in general, but it is to be hoped
that that state of affairs will soon be a
thing of the past.
On reading over the Gracious Speech
it struck me that it was a wise plan to
keep young boys and girls at school
another year or so if they are unable to
get employment. But after listening to
the two previous speakers I wish to keep
an open mind on the subject. I trust
that something will be done in the matter
and whatever is done I am sure it will be
well-spent money.
With the other Measures mentioned in
the Gracious Speech I am in full agreement, and they shall have my support
when they are before the House. I have
pleasure in supporting the Motion.
Sir JOSEPH DAVISON: I am going
to be very brief, but I wish in supporting
the Motion to congratulate the mover and
seconder on the way they performed their
task. It has been described as a difficult
position, and it is a rather difficult position for the younger members of this
assembly, and I heartily congratulate
them. At the same time I wish to thank,
and I believe that this assembly, as a
whole, will thank the right hon. Gentleman the Leader of the House, for his
explanation of the legislation that is
pending and the conditions of existing
legislation. It was very helpful, I am
sure, to everyone of us and we appreciate
the outline he gave.
With regard to unemployment we have
heard a tremendous lot about that during
the past few weeks. It is not alone in
Northern Ireland that unemployment has
increased. In Great Britain and a great
many other places you have increased
unemployment. Then our local conditions have been interfered with by the
Sino-Japanese difficulty-they do not call
it a war; but it is a state of war. It
presented difficulties to the linen merchants in the disposal of their manufactures.
In the United States we see a
terrible increase in unemployment with a
consequential lessening of the purchasing
power, so that we have not the market in
the United States that we had. Take the
two greatest industries in that great
country and you find that they are working from 20 to 30 per cent. below capacity
-the General Motors and the United
States Steel with thousands of thousands
of employes, and with other industries
hanging on to them.
I agree with the learned Senator Leslie
in what he has stated with regard to
agriculture. It is presenting difficulties
which I am afraid will have to be faced
and faced very soon by the Department.
It has added its quota to unemployment
because it was only recent legislation
brought the farm labourers into insurance
and these add to the number. I feel
sure that the Government, as a whole,
will do everything they can to meet this
unemployment question and lessen it as
much as they possibly can. I have pleasure
in supporting the Motion.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
would like to join with the other speakers
in congratulating the mover and seconder
on the very able way they performed their
duty. It was very pleasant to have two
rather new Members discharging that
duty so satisfactorily. What I notice in
the Speech again on this occasion, as has
been the case for a good number of years
past, is that it principally referred to unemployment. Year after year we have
listened and each year we have hoped that
next year would bring some improvement
a change, and that there would be
more employment for the unemployed who
are so anxiously seeking work.
or
There is one matter I would like to
refer to, and that is a rather peculiar
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
3 MARCH 1938
position so far as we are concerned. In
this part of the country we seem to be
overstocked. There seems to be a surplus
population for which there is no work,
and yet in the Empire, of which we are
all very proud, there are other places
vacant where they are extremely in need
of men and women to populate those
districts. Indeed if something is not
done in the course of a number of years it
is quite possible there will be a difficulty
on the part of the British Government in
holding some of the Colonies they have.
Surely it is not beyond the power of
statesmen to do something to persuade the
younger people in our midst to go out,
because I feel we still have adventurous
young men and women as we had years
ago. It is not so long ago that looking
over the Queen's Bridge we saw boats
filled to overflowing with young men and
women going to the Colonies or to the
United States. Now we have a considerable number returning and we have to
provide for them.
It stands to sense that providing for the
unemployed is a tremendously heavy tax
on the producer because someone has got
to keep these unemployed. It falls
entirely on the employed and the business
people. I do feel our Government should
raise the matter with the Imperial Government to see if they could not offer
some greater encouragement to young men
and women to go out and try their luck
where they are so much in need of the
men and women we have to spare in
Northern Ireland. This is an agricultural country and we have a fine class of
men and women, and it is heartbreaking
to see a considerable number of these fine
young people unable to find work to provide for themselves, and being entirely
dependent on their neighbours to provide
for them. Surely it would be possible to
give some inducement or some encouragement.
I think if it were possible to extend unemployment benefit or the insurance
scheme and let them draw for a short time
in the Colonies the money would be well
spent. I think the young men and
women are afraid to venture out lest they
should not succeed. They are afraid to
leave behind them what they have to keep
them from starving. But if there was
some provision made on which they could
rely for a short time when they land in
the new country, it would be the means
of getting a considerable number out to
try their luck, and if they made good
they would benefit the British Empire.
Then the extension of the school-leaving
age is with the intention of preventing a
further number being added to the unemployed heap. The whole thing is intended for that purpose, so that they
will not be put on the market looking for
work. If it were possible to remove a
large number of boys and girls to the
Colonies I do not think there would be
any desire to extend the school age, and
places would be found for them unless
the parents wished to keep and allow them
to continue at school for the better education of the boys and girls.
There is another matter referred to in
the Speech, and that is housing accommodation in the urban districts. That is
a matter I have been interested in for
many years, and I have no doubt the
Government will be spending money
wisely by removing the hovels and shacks
one sees in the country. That would not
only provide employment for a good
number of workmen, but improved housing accommodation would also guarantee
better health for the people. You would
thus have a more fit population, and you
would not have to resort to artificial
means of keeping them fit because they
would grow up healthy and fit. The
Government should lose no time, but
should do everything possible to provide
houses in the urban districts.
I do not know that there is a city in
well
the United Kingdom that is so
situated as regards houses as is Belfast at
the present time. There is an abundance
of houses in Belfast, while in other cities
and towns across the water there is a
shortage. The subsidies provided by the
Government and the Belfast Corporation
have been the means of encouraging the
building of houses to such an extent that
now we have splendid housing accommodation for our people, and there is no
need for anyone to live in slums or in
slum districts. I have much pleasure in
supporting the Motion for the Address.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I would like
to join the previous speakers in offering
congratulations to the mover and
seconder of the Motion for the Address.
They are both comparatively new Members of the Senate, but they acquitted
themselves remarkably well. The chief
421787
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Hill Dickson]
point in the Gracious Speech deals with
unemployment. All the previous speakers
seem to have lost sight of the fact that
unemployment is largely due to the fact
that machinery has taken the place of
man power. Where at the Queen's Island
we used to have a machine that required
six or seven men to work it, a machine
to do the same work can now be operated
by one man. That is the chief cause of
so much unemployment-that and the
stoppage of emigration.
With regard to the Bann drainage.
scheme, it has cost £200,000 beyond the
original estimate. It has undoubtedly
removed flooding in certain areas, but
some of that work was very, very costly.
We had a little scheme in County Down,
and if it had been let out to a contractor
it would have been done at about half
what it has cost the Government. They
sent down engineers to take levels, which
I suppose was necessary, and they started
to make syphons and things like that, but
the men were not attending to their work
at all. All through the winter time they
were kept working up to their necks in
cold water, and the same thing applied
in the case of the Quoile drainage scheme.
I saw men standing up to their waist in
water, whereas a contractor would have
stopped them working in November and
started them again in May. The Government kept them working until 4 p.m. and
4-30 p.m. when it was ridiculous to go
on.
There is a reference in the Speceh to
the cost of farm produce. It would pay
no man to grow potatoes to be sold at two
shillings per hundredweight. We could
not do it. Men can get as much money
now for doing nothing as they can by
working on a farm. I really think the
Government ought to look into matters
of this kind, and they should, at the same
time, not lose sight of the fact that
machinery taking the place of man power
is chiefly responsible for unemployment.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
also would like to congratulate the mover
and seconder of the Motion for the
Address on the manner in which they
discharged their duty. I should not have
intervened in this debate had not two
hon. Members said something which
tempted me to rise. Senator Joseph
Cunningham referred to unemployment,
and rather suggested the Government
should do something to assist in the
emigration of our surplus population. A
few months ago I attended a conference
in London that was representative of
every phase of industry and philanthropic
work. That conference was summoned for
the one purpose of calling upon the Government to place the surplus population
in those of our Colonies where men,
women and children are required.
The conference was held in the Mansion
House, London, and I had the honour of
speaking. I pointed out that our Northern Government had a training centre in
County Armagh. Every speaker had to
admit that the cause of unemployment
in the United Kingdom, as well as in
every other country, was overproduction.
and machinery, and that you could not
place people in industry in any part of
the Empire, because there was over-production in nearly every industry in nearly
every country. The only hope was to
place the surplus population on the land.
It was demonstrated that the British
Government and our own Government
here had done valuable work in the past
in placing persons on the land in Canada
and other places..
At this conference committees were
appointed to go thoroughly into the problem, and these committees are still in
existence. The object is to take as many
persons who cannot be absorbed into industry and place them on the land in the
Colonies. It is receiving the full support
of the Imperial Government, and I am
sure it will have the full support of the
Government here. I would not like it to
go forth that nothing is being done in
regard to this important matter.
I think the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Leslie), wanted to know what is the
difference between fourpence and ninepence.
It is true that we were succesful
in inducing the Belfast Corporation to
reduce the rate from 10s 4d to 10s. I
take the view that the lower taxation can
be kept the better it is for the country,
and the lower the taxation the more
hope we have of attracting new industries. The Government are doing all
that is possible to induce people to establish new industries in Northern Ireland,
but I want to make it clear that persons
who are thinking of starting new industries are not going to flock over here on
the day the announcement of special
facilities is made. That is impossible.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
3 MARCH 1938
ARLE
RE K
生
New industries grow up gradually.
Take the case of the Dunlop Rubber Co.
-the greatest industry of its kind in the
world. Belfast refused to take up that
industry when it had the opportunity to
do so at the time Dunlop patented his
tyre. Dublin took it up, and then it
went to Birmingham and Liverpool. Had
that industry been established here we
should have possessed one of the greatest
industries in the world, but we missed
the opportunity. When I visited the
works a few weeks ago I made it my
business to ask the company if it would
be possible for them to establish a branch
in Northern Ireland. The best way to
get a new industry is to induce an established business across the water to open a
branch here. I do not think you will get
anyone to establish an entirely new industry here because it takes brains,
money, and time, and in my opinion you
are not going to get them just as quickly
as you think.
We will have to turn our
attention to something else if we are to
help the unemployed.
Unfortunately the number of unemployed in Belfast is greater than ever.
That has been brought about by circumstances over which neither the Government nor the municipality have any control. You cannot absorb the people into
employment unless you create a tremendous debt, and if you do that you rush
up the rates. It is a serious problem,
and in my opinion it will take time to
solve. We need not get panicky about
it. This war in the Far East will pass,
and it will bring additional work for our
linen manufacturers. Let us also hope
that the shortage of money in America.
will soon disappear, and when it does
buyers will be flocking across to Northern
Ireland and will buy goods that will start
the wheels of industry going again.
To sum up, the cause of the trouble
to-day all over the world is machinery
and over-production of goods. Countries
cannot absorb all the goods they manufacture, or dispose of the surplus to
other countries.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
should like in the first place to add my
congratulations to those already expressed in this House to the mover and
seconder of the Address in reply to the
Gracious Speech. I am not sure that
the remarks of the Lord Mayor of Belfast and Senator Hill Dickson are a true
explanation of the unemployment
problem. Some time ago most careful
calculations were made in America, and
those calculations did not lead to the
conclusion that machinery alone was the
cause of unemployment. I understand
that the Ford works now employ far
more men than they could have if they
were not using machinery. What really
is the secret of this unemployment
problem is currency not machinery.
You cannot go on expanding production
on an unlimited scale unless other
countries who want your produce can
buy it, and anything in our foreign
policy-which is outside the scope of this
House but is as vitally important to us
as to England-anything which tends to
release exchange restrictions which apply
to nearly every country in Europe, and
anything which tends towards peace, is
going to improve employment more than
any development which we ourselves are
capable of producing.
I was talking not long ago to the acting
Prime Minister of Australia and he said,
"You cannot expect us to buy your goods
unless you take some of our goods because
we cannot buy goods unless we have sterling with which to pay for them." There,
again, you have another restriction in
currency and trade restrictions, and until
these restrictions are removed and the
laws of economics allowed to come into
play harsh as they sometimes may
be-
I do not believe anyone will be able to
solve this terrible problem of unemployment. Not long ago when speaking on
another King's Speech, in reply to some
comments made on the trade of this
country, I made an appeal to encourage
trade to supply the amenities of life, the
small things that we all enjoy, but that
we can do without, and not rely entirely
on the great industries which we have
built up in this country-linen and shipbuilding, which are susceptible to world
trade influences. The noble Viscount,
who then led the House, in a voice that
sounded almost pained asked me if I had
no recollection of a certain Act of Parliament passed to facilitate the development
of trade in this country and mentioned
that, unfortunately, nobody had taken
very much notice of it. I think the fact
that no one can get up and make similar
remarks to-day indicates that that complacent attitude in which my question
was answered has gone in a few short
years.
I think it was a year or so ago.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Captain Perceval-Maxwell]
The Government have taken steps to
develop industry by going to England and
finding out what industry requires, and
they have done that partly by appointing
a Committee, over which a distinguished
Member of this House presides, a position
for which his duties as director and
founder of the British Industries Federation admirably qualify him. The Committee got information about what
British industry wants, and on that Committee there are men who are able to tell
the English manufacturer what we can
give him. If you want to sell anything
the only way to do it is to find out what
the purchaser requires, and then take
stock of your own resources and ascertain
how much of the requirements you can
supply-not only ascertain but go to him
direct and tell him you have railways
here and harbours there, and electric light
here and water there. That is the way
the English country is developed. They
have organisations and local authorities
who go out to the manufacturer and tell
him what they can produce, and how they
can assist him. Until we do this on a
large scale in this country we will never
encourage the Englishman to come over
here. He does not want to go and search
out these things for himself. He wants
the information laid on his table, and
when he has laid on his table the inducements we can offer it will, I believe,
encourage the manufacturers to come to
this country.
There is one great danger in a Committee such as has been formed. It is
naturally composed of men who have
made reputations for themselves in the
existing trade of the country-trade
which has developed and is now suffering
a relapse, and there is a danger that these
men with great knowledge of industry
which exists here to-day will be inclined
to develop and encourage trade on similar
lines to those on which trade in the past
has flourished. And if they do this alone
it will have a very dangerous effect on
the greatest trade of all, which is agriculture. I believe the Government are to
be congratulated in that they have, on
parallel lines to those on which they have
tried to encourage the English to come
over here, set about seeing how they can
develop the basic trade which, after all,
is agriculture. And it is to their credit
that in doing so they have taken into
account the ancillary trades connected
with agriculture, trades which can be
developed by agriculture. And if they
continue on those lines I have no doubt
they will do a great deal to reduce the
unemployment problem as time goes on.
You
But in a small community such as ours
there is great danger that as trade is
developing the social services will develop
at an increased pace, faster than the trade
is developing itself. This, I think, has
been pointed out by other Members of
this House before to-day, and agriculture
is one of those that have been very
seriously affected by the development of
the social services. I can see a very
serious problem arising in future.
cannot develop the creamery business
without having a large stock of cattle,
and you cannot have a large stock of cattle
depending entirely on summer grass. You
cannot make the creamery business pay
if you spend your money buying imported
foodstuffs. The plough must follow the
creamery business, and if the farmer because of the development of the social
services is unable to break up his land,
and is unable to grow profitably the crops
it is necessary for him to grow in order to
provide winter keep, the creamery business cannot have a permanent footing in
this country.
It is a serious problem, and it is one
which sooner or later the Government will
have to address itself to. There is no
doubt in my mind that this problem which
seriously affected agriculture in past years
is a problem which will affect it very much
more in the future unless something is
done. A great deal of the country that
ought to be tilled and resown will not
be resown, and we are laying up for ourselves a problem for the future which
need never be presented if steps are taken
in time to remedy the defects.
Mr. ROBB: By leave of the House I
would just like to say one thing. With
regard to many matters touched upon I
am not as familiar as perhaps I ought to
be, and, consequently, I ask the indulgence of the House not to deal with them
to-day, but I would like to give the House
the assurance asked for that as far as in
me lies-I am inexperienced in this matter
-but as far as in me lies I will endeavour
to see that the House will have reasonable
opportunity for the consideration of
measures sent to us from another place.
I do not know exactly what steps can be
taken, but I would like to give the House
Debate on the Address
Adjournment
3 MARCH 1938
chat undertaking that as far as I am able
to control such matters ample time will
be given for the consideration of any Bills
that are brought up.

Governor to offer the humble thanks of

Grace has addressed to both Houses-
Your Grace,
We His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave to
offer our humble thanks to your Grace
for the Speech which your Grace has
addressed to both Houses of Parliament."
Ordered, That the said Address be

of this House as are Members of His

Ireland.

House do now adjourn."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not know whether
we can get any idea as to the date of

Mr. ROBB: One cannot, of course, be
certain, but as far as I can see at the

month.

at

Address to His Grace the Governor.
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House)
reported that a Privy Councillor of
Northern Ireland had waited on his Grace
with the Address from the Senate (pursuant to the Order of 3rd March, 1938),
to which his Grace was pleased to return
the following Answer:-
"I have received with great satisfaction the expression of your thanks for
the Speech with which I opened the
present Session of Parliament."
PRESERVATION OF PEACE.
Assurance to the Government of the
United Kingdom.
Mr. ROBB: I beg to move the Motion
which stands in my name on the Order
Paper. It reads as follows:
That this House, fully realising the
anxieties besetting his Majesty's Government of the United Kingdom at the
present time, and praying that its
efforts to attain and preserve peace
throughout the world will be completely
successful, assures the Prime Minister
of that Government that, should any
crisis arise, he can confidently rely
upon the people of loyal Ulster to share
the responsibilities and burdens with
their kith and kin in other parts of the
United Kingdom and the Empire, to
the utmost of her resources.
That the Speaker do transmit this
resolution to his Majesty's Government
of the United Kingdom forthwith.
I have only one object in moving this
resolution. It seems to me that we in
this House should lose no time in placing
it on record that both we and the loyal
people whom we represent share the
anxieties of the present time and the
aspirations for world-wide peace which
fill the minds of our fellow subjects
throughout the British Empire.
I do
not wish to be taken as initiating any
discussion on foreign affairs; indeed I
know that you would at once point out
from the Chair that such a discussion is
outside the competence of this House. But
I feel that it cannot be amiss that His
Majesty's Government in the United
Business of the House
Consolidated Fund Bill
22 MARCH 1938
us
that
Kingdom should learn from
Northern Ireland will be found to be at
one with Great Britain in any efforts
which may have to be made in the interests of our common citizenship.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Business of the House.
Mr. ROBB: I beg to move, "That
Standing Order No. XXV be suspended
for this day's sitting for the purpose of
taking all stages of the Consolidated.
Fund Bill and the Second Reading of the
Unemployment Insurance Bill.”
Mr. LESLIE: None of us, I am sure,
have the least objection to suspending
Standing Orders in order to accelerate
the progress of the Consolidated Fund
Bill. That has become almost a matter
of course, but as regards the other Bill,
I do not think it is a course which this
House ought to take or ought to be asked
to take. I have more than once pointed
out that many Members of this House
live in the country parts of Northern Ireland some distance from this House, and
if they receive Bills by post, unaccompanied by a little code of the existing
Statutes referred to in the new Bill, they
are at some disadvantage as compared
with those living near at hand who can
resort to the library of the Houses of
Parliament and read up anything they
wish to study. I do not think it is right
that such an important Bill as the Unemployment Extension Bill, which is
what it really is, should be rushed through
this House, or rather should have its progress through this House in any way
accelerated.
Your distinguished predecessor once
remarked that Standing Orders were
meant to be observed. I venture to
think that that is a sound rubric, and I
move an Amendment to the Motion to
leave out all the words after the first
"Bill." That would leave the Motion
limited to the Consolidated Fund Bill.
Mr. ROBB: I quite recognise the point
the hon. and learned Member has made
with regard to the difficulty of those not
living adjacent to this House in examining
the Statutes referred to in a Bill coming
before the House. The reason for asking
the House to facilitate the progress
soon
of the Unemployment Insurance Bill is
that the scheme which it embodies has
to come into force on 4th April, and it
is necessary that the Bill should be passed
and receive the Royal Assent as
as possible, because a large number of
forms and books will have to be distributed between the giving of the Royal
Assent and the coming into force of the
Bill. On this ground the Ministry of
Labour has asked that the progress of
the Bill should be accelerated.
Might I point out that it is not proposed to take the Committee stage of
the Bill to-day, but only the Second
Reading. I believe hon. Senators had
the Bill or ought to have had it last
Wednesday, so that there has been a
certain not unreasonable interval before
asking the House to give the Measure
a Second Reading. For these reasons I
ask the House to agree to the Motion.
Mr. LESLIE: I will not pursue the
matter any further.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
