51 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE

Mr. ROBB: I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now read a Second time."
Although this Bill is a relatively small
Measure, and not a major Measure in any
sense, it is important to the sections of
the community to which it applies, and
it will, I think, be welcomed as another
instalment in the improvement of our
social services. The Bill follows the lines
of a similar Measure recently passed by
the Imperial Parliament, and will keep
the unemployment insurance scheme of
Northern Ireland on the same basis as the
scheme in Great Britain. There are
three main objects of the Bill. The first
is the provision of medical service and
meals for juveniles attending authorised
courses of instruction. The second is the
inclusion of certain classes of outdoor
private servants within the scheme of
agricultural insurance, and the third is
the maintenance of the finances of the
Northern Ireland fund in parity with the
finances of the fund in Great Britain.
The duty of providing authorised
courses of instruction for unemployed
juveniles between the school-leaving age
and 18 years was
local
placed on
education authorities by the Unemployment Insurance Act (Northern Ireland),
1936. The authorities have powers to
provide for elementary school children
medical and dental inspection and treatment, and in certain circumstances, free
meals, and Clause 1 of the Bill extends
these powers to cover juveniles attending
authorised courses. In addition the
Clause empowers the authorities to provide free milk and biscuits for the
juveniles attending the courses. Hon.
Members may not be aware that a great
proportion of the training that is done in
these juvenile instruction centres is
physical training, and it has been found
that owing to the low state of health of
many of the trainees they were not able
to carry on this physical instruction.
without some refreshment during the
course of the classes. Accordingly, the
Bill enables education authorities, where
they find this to be the case, to supply
not merely meals but other sustenance in
the form of free milk and biscuits. The
expenses incurred by education authorities
under this Clause will rank for
Exchequer grant under Section 83 of the
Unemployment Insurance Act (Northern
Ireland), 1936.
The next Clause-Clause 2-brings
into insurance under the agricultural
scheme the classes of outdoor private
servants set out in the Schedule to the
Bill. These classes have been included
in the agricultural scheme because their
duties are more akin to agriculture than
to the industries covered by the general
scheme. The Clause also gives the Ministry of Labour power to bring into the
agricultural scheme by regulation any
other classes of outdoor servants who can
be more appropriately provided for in
that scheme than in the general scheme.
It is proposed that the new classes
should come into insurance on 4th April,
1938. On the same date a number of
other persons who come within the
definition of domestic servants will, by
regulation under
under the 1936 Act, be
brought into insurance under the general
scheme. These will include private
chauffeurs and a number of servants in
Government departments, clubs, schools,
and other institutions.
Clause 3 of the Bill deals with a matter
of finance. The Unemployment Fund in
Great Britain has recently been given
additional borrowing powers, and should
these powers be exercised it will be
necessary under the Reinsurance Agreement to keep the advances to their Unemployment Fund in parity with the
increased advances made to the Great
Britain Fund. Clause 3 gives power
enabling that to be done.
The remaining Clauses of the Bill
require but a brief reference. Clause 4
is a drafting amendment, Clause 5 provides the necessary authority for the expenditure entailed by the provisions of
the Bill, and Clause 6 is merely a
definition Clause. These are the objects
of the Bill, which is not in
controversial.
any sense
Mr. LESLIE: I do not rise to oppose
the Second Reading of this Bill, but that
is not to be taken as any derogation on
the part either of others or myself to deal
separately with the second Clause when
we reach the Committee stage. I will
come back to that second Clause in a
moment. As regards the first Clause, I
entirely agree with paragraph (a),
medical and dental treatment. I think
that is merely following up what we did
as regards the elementary school children
last Session. I rather think the right
hon. Gentleman said so. As regards
paragraph (b), from the taxpayers point.
53 Unemployment Insurance Bill- 22 MARCH 1938
Second Reading

Insurance Bill, but I think the time has
come when the matter should be reexamined. At present the position is
that a man on benefit gets more money
than if he were working, and that is a
wholly indefensible position.
of view I suppose we must be thankful
that it is proposed to stop at milk and
biscuits, although I cannot but think
that any man approaching eighteen years
of age
will prefer beef and beer. But
I hope it is not intended to go further
in that direction, although milk and
biscuits for boys between 17 and 18 does
strike one as, perhaps, a little out of
the ordinary. However, it is not going.
to cost the ratepayers anything, and
that, after all, is the main point.
As regards Clause 3 there is nothing,
I think, that need be said. Clause 4 has
a few words in it which I will ask the
House in Committee to remove in order
to get a further explanation. Those are
words which seem to give a certain retrospective effect to the Clause, and I do
not believe that is the intention. But
owing to the fact that Section 52 of the
principal Act is in two parts I am a little
uneasy about the effect of the new proposal as regards the second sub-clause of
Section 52. I have very little doubt
that the matter will be satisfactorily
explained, although for convenience a
formal motion will appear on the Paper.
Clauses 5 and 6 give no difficulty, and
we come back to Clause 2.
The effect of Clause 2 will be to extend
the scope
of so much of the insurance
scheme as relates to persons employed in
agriculture. I should rather like to ask
the Members of this House whether they
are thoroughly satisfied with the results
of the extension of unemployment insurance to persons engaged in agriculture.
Agricultural insurance has not been of
very long duration, but I have heard
many complaints that the effect of that
Act is that it pays a man better to
remain idle than to go to work. Under
the benefits of the Act a man will receive,
I think, for himself 14s a week, 7s for
his wife, if he has got one, and 3s for
each child. If he has got three children,
which in the country is not an unusual
number, he has 30s a week when he is
unemployed. How many agricultural
labourers in full work are able to earn
30s a week?
The position has really become
ridiculous. We have ridden the horse of
step-by-step practically to death. It is
all very well to follow on the same lines
as England, and that is why the House,
although not without hesitation, gave a
Second Reading to the Agricultural
ex-
Then again, certain classes are
pressed in the Schedule. Private
chauffeurs are to be brought in on April
4th. I do not know on what authority,
but there was something in the nature
of authority under the main Act of
1936, which was used to bring in private
gardeners, and these other people, gamekeepers, and so on, will be brought in,
because, apparently, they are employed
in domestic service. That is what it
says. Domestic service means service by
people resident in their employers'
household. A gamekeeper as a rule does
not live in his employer's house. Of
course, there may be cases where he does,
but speaking generally, he does not, and
domestic service has not been specially
defined for the purposes of the Act.
What they mean is, I think, to use the
words which I think the right hon.
Gentleman himself used twice in his
speech-private service. He referred to
private chauffeurs and private gardeners,
and private gardeners is the expression
used in the second part of the Second
Schedule to the 1936 Act.
I do not myself like this interference
by compulsory insurance with private
servants. It seems to me a further step
on the downward path, and the next
proposal will be that indoor family
domestic servants will be brought into
insurance. I am very much afraid that
that is the direction in which the minds
of insurance advocates are tending, and
I hope that is not going to be the case.
Again, I do not understand why we
should draw any distinction, how we can
defend drawing any distinction between
one class of indoor servant and another.
I suppose the answer will be that these
people are essentially outdoor servants.
Speaking generally, I think that is so,
but if you go on extending the scope in
the way proposed in this Bill we shall
lose ourselves in a wilderness of single
instances. You have got your footman,
who serves various purposes. Sometimes
he merely waits at table; sometimes he
goes out with mistress's carriage, or probably motor-car in these days, and perhaps
he helps part of the day in the stables.
55 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
You get lost in that wilderness to which
I have referred.
Again, I think we ought to be told.
something of the numbers affected by the
new proposals. I gather that the Ministry has some figures in its possession,
because there is a note in the White
Paper circulated with the Bill which says:
The increased charge to the
Exchequer will be substantially off-set
by a saving in the cost of unemployment assistance.
If a statement like that is made in the
White Paper one must assume it is based
on some knowledge of figures, and I suggest that those figures ought to be given
to the House. Then again, I do not like
the selections made and, to some extent,
I have got to say that I do not understand the terms that are used in the
Schedule. I know we still have that
which is now in Section 4 of the 1936
Act, allowing certain questions to be
referred to a court of summary jurisdiction rather than to the Ministry, and
that, of course, will still remain.
But
we start with gamekeeper, and the
question is how much game-keeping
makes a gamekeeper, because in many
cases a gamekeeper is clearly defined
individually, but in many other cases of
small estates he is not. However, that
will not give us much trouble. It is
rather when we get to the people who
assist the gamekeeper that there is a risk
of confusion.
The only
Then we go on to warrener.
warrener I have heard of is a person
clothed with the franchise of a warren.
I imagine that what the Bill means is a
rabbit catcher, but why does not the Bill
say so. The third term is that of ghillie.
In Northern Ireland I do not know what
a ghillie is. I do understand what he is
in Scotland, but it is a term one is very
much afraid of. I looked at a fairly
large English dictionary. I also searched
two other dictionaries and I drew them
blank; the word was not there.
definition in the ordinary dictionary was
"a Scottish man servant," and it stopped
at that. I do not think the word ghillie
has any meaning in Northern Ireland,
and I am afraid of it if only because
under the second sub-clause of Clause 2
the suggestion is that the Ministry is to
have power to decide whether this or that
man is to come into the scheme, and I
The
do not think that is a power that ought
to be given to the Ministry of Labour.
I do not think they are the proper people
to decide whether a man is a warrener or
a ghillie or even a gamekeeper.
The terms river keeper and water bailiff
are fairly simple, and the only danger
there is the difficulty about people who
are over 65 years of age, to which I drew
attention on a former Measure. A large
proportion of these people will be men
of 65 years of age, and I suppose it is
again gravely proposed that the employer
is to be fined 44d a week in respect of
each of them. I pointed out before that
if a man is over 65 and loses his employment he will usually lose the house he
is in as well. I do not think it is in the
interests of a man over 65 that he should
remain, to use a common expression, in a
stampable condition. I think we were
wrong in letting it go through on a
former occasion, although we did it only
by a very narrow majority, and I am
sorry it is proposed to do it again.
I do trust we shall have the views of
other hon. Members on that Clause and
then one can form an opinion as to
whether one ought to ask the House in
Committee to take out the whole Clause
or whether the Committee will be satisfied
to take out one or two words in the
Schedule, with the possible substitution.
of rabbit catcher for warrener, and take
away that part of the Clause which gives
the Ministry certain powers, in spite of
the possibility of appealing to a court of
summary jurisdiction; and in spite of the
fact that any regulations made have to be
laid before Parliament for the statutory
period and are liable to cancellation by
the House. I am not opposing the Second
Reading, but I have very strong mental
reservations as regards Clause 2.
Captain PERCEVAL MAXWELL:
While I support the Second Reading of
this Bill I think a further explanation is
required regarding the Schedule. There
may be some reason why gamekeepers and
grooms and stablemen engaged in trade
or business for gain shall be excluded
from the Schedule-
Mr. ROBB: They are already insurable.
-
Captain PERCEVAL MAXWELL:
I understand that is the case, but I think
57 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
22 MARCH 1938

Second Reading
on
there are some cases where it is rather
difficult to say whether a man is a
domestic servant and is or is not engaged
in any business or trade carried on for the
purpose of gain. I take it that the object
of this Schedule is to put both domestic
and publicly employed servants
exactly the same footing, so that although
there was some confusion in the case of
estate companies and other undertakings
of that sort under the old regulations,
the present regulations make it clear that
whether they are employed by estate companies or not they will all be on exactly
the same footing. In future there will
be no difference between a gamekeeper
who is employed under one private
employer and one who is employed under
an estate company simply because in one
case his employment led to gain. I think
that clears up that matter.
Those
Sir JAMES WILTON: I welcome this
Bill, and particularly the Clause which
refers to unemployed juveniles.
who have been dealing with the unemployed think that that is a question
which should have been tackled a considerable time ago. A committee dealing
with this question has been sitting and
they have come to certain conclusions
which will be of great assistance to local
education authorities. I particularly
welcome the fact that no further burden
will be placed on local authorities as a
result of this concession. I have painful
recollections of a previous occasion when
a kind of continuation classes
established by the local authorities, and
at the end of the course it was found that
the Ministry were not disposed to bear
the entire cost. I am glad that an
assurance has now been given that the
Ministry will bear the entire cost of the
scheme, and although it will involve a
great deal of hard work and throw a good
deal of further responsibility on local
education authorities I think they will
welcome the Bill and assist the Ministry
in every way they possibly can. I intend
to vote for the Second Reading.
were
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I also
support the Second Reading, and I am
especially pleased with the Clause which
enables refreshment to be provided free
for juvenile trainees. An effort is being
made to improve the physical fitness of
our people, and in the case of young
people coming from homes where it is
impossible to obtain sufficient nourishing
food it is very necessary that they should
be given some suitable refreshment while
attending these instruction centres. I
am sure no Member of this House would
object to that.
With regard to the additional classes
that are to be brought into the insurance
scheme, it is well known that grooms
employed in certain places were already
insured, while others were not, and great
confusion was caused by the uncertainty
as to whether particular grooms were
insurable or not. This Bill will bring
all grooms under the insurance scheme.
We have heard a good deal of talk and
many complaints about the result of
bringing agricultural labourers into insurance. I can remember very well that
the same statements and the same complaints were made when industrial
workers were first insured. It was said
that men preferred unemployment benefit
to work. I am of opinion that in the
course of time that will right itself.
There is no doubt that there are many
agricultural workers with big families
who will find it more beneficial to draw
unemployment benefit than to work for at
farmer, but, after all, does not the solution of this problem lie in something
being done for the man with a big family.
As I have stated before in this House,
the State should make some allowance to
the man who has a family larger than
the average. Quite a number of firms
across the water are already dealing with
this matter, and I believe the time will
come when the State will help the
married couple with more than three or
four of a family. If the agricultural
worker was getting a family allowance
from the State he would find it more
beneficial to work than to draw benefit.
I hope that before long the farmers
will be in a position to pay better wages
than they are doing at the present time.
The Government have done and are doing
their best to assist the farming community by giving a guaranteed price for
pigs and raising the price of milk, and
the farmer should do something for the
farm labourer. If that was done there
would be no complaints about the insurance of agricultural workers. As I said,
I am of opinion that in course of time the
problem will right itself, and I believe.
that the step the Government are taking
in this Bill is in the right direction. I
have pleasure in supporting the Second
Reading.
59 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
Fumigation Bill
THE SENATE

Mr. HILL DICKSON: It is all very
well talking about the agricultural
labourer with a big family getting in
unemployment benefit more than he can
earn, but the mill worker with a similar
family does not receive in benefit more
than he gets in wages. When it is wet
the agricultural labourer may lose two or
three days work in the week, so he is not
on the same plane as the industrial
worker. The agricultural worker can
live very much more cheaply than the
man in the town, and I think this insurance scheme is a mistake.
Mr. ROBB: I am glad the House is
prepared at once to give this Bill a
Second Reading. Might I make one or
two remarks in answer to some of the
points that have been raised. Senator
Leslie criticised the Bill from the point of
view that in dealing with this matter at
all we were on the downward path. I do
not admit that; I rather suggest we are
on the upward path. The various other
points he raised I will deal with on the
Committee stage. The Mayor of Derry
inferred from what I said that the whole
of the cost of providing free biscuits and
milk would fall upon the Exchequer.
What I did say was that the expenses incurred by local education authorities in
exercising the powers given to them would
rank for grant under Section 83 of the
Act; not that the Exchequer would bear
the whole cost.
With regard to the point raised by
Captain Perceval-Maxwell, there is a
difference between a gamekeeper employed upon an estate in pursuit of
business and a gamekeeper on an estate
who is solely serving the sporting
instincts of his employer. The gamekeeper employed in pursuit of game for
business comes under the ordinary
scheme. He pays larger contributions
and gets larger benefits. The private
gamekeeper will come under the agricultural insurance scheme, and he will pay
lower contributions and receive lower
benefits. The other points that have been
raised I will deal with on the Committee
stage.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.
Exported Animals (Compensation) Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."—(Mr. Long.)
Hydrogen Cyanide (Fumigation) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: This is a small and noncontroversial Bill. Its object is to safeguard human life by regulating the use
of hydrogen cyanide in enclosed places.
Fumigation by hydrogen cyanide is one
of the best methods known for the
destruction of vermin, and is verv
largely used for clearing agricultural land
of rabbits, and ships of rats and cockroaches. It is a very dangerous subject.
In the open air the gas is so volatile that
there is really no risk, but where it is
used in buildings such as clearing-houses
on rats and mice there is serious danger
to human life, as the gas is hardly perceptible, and it lingers. We are happy
to say that in this part of the world
there have been no deaths from cyanide
poisoning, but in other parts of the world
there have been cases of fatal infection,
and most countries have passed legislation regulating the use of hydrogen
cyanide for fumigation purposes.
This Bill proposes to make regulations
with regard to the use of hydrogen
cyanide in enclosed places in this area.
The first Clause gives the Ministry of
Home Affairs power to make regulations
with regard to the use of hydrogen
cyanide. Those are not to apply to the
fumigation of rabbit. warrens or to
fumigation carried out in the open air,
but all regulations made shall be laid
before Parliament as soon as may be
after they are made. In addition, the
Bill requires that should any accident
occur from the use of hydrogen cyanide
notice of that accident shall be sent to
the Ministry of Home Affairs, as well as
notice of any loss of human life or injury
to persons, and it goes on to apply to
such accidents provision with regard to
inquiries that may be held upon the
notification of such an accident.
The last Clause of the Bill enables His
Grace the Governor to apply the provisions of this Bill, or some of them, to
other dangerous substances. That has
been done because the progress of science
is such that fresh agents for the destruction of vermin are constantly being discovered, and it is intended to apply the
Fumigation Pill-
Second Reading
22 MARCH 1938
provisions of this Bill to the use of any
other substances which may be used in
the same manner, and carry with it the
same danger. I beg to move, "That the
Bill be now read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: I rise for the purpose
of supporting the Bill, and incidentally
of thanking the right hon. Gentleman for
clearing up two of the difficulties which
were in my unchemical mind. I had a
note to ask whether the principal object
was the destruction of rodents, which we
have now been told is the case, or
whether it was disinfection, and also
whether the danger was from infection,
or from inhalation of the noxious gas,
and I gather that the latter is the
answer. That disposes of two little difficulties, but there remains one or two
other small ones.
The Bill is a reproduction of the
United Kingdom Measure which received.
the Royal Assent on 6th July last, and
it is following the English Bill exactly.
I am sorry to say that as this Bill comes
to us it does not seem to me to be
effectively workable. What it seems to
me to require is the omission of that sub-
Clause which relates to the non-applicability of the regulations to warrens and
the open air. We do not define the open
air any more than we define a ship. I
have often heard of ship's articles, but I
never heard of a ship being called an
article before. I presume it is a definite
article like the Queen Mary, and not an
indefinite article like the Flying Dutchman.
But if you are going to fumigate a
ship is not part of it done in the open air?
That seems to me to be a difficulty. Aircraft are in the same position. They may
be under a hangar, and with that sub-
Clause you can exclude anything you
want to exclude. But you must maintain
the power because of the difficulty of
seeing in some cases whether fumigation
does take place in the open air. The
cabin might not be in the open air, but
the deck would be. However, those are
matters we can discuss at a later stage.
There is another point, to which the
right hon. Gentleman referred, and that
is the inquiries in the unfortunate event
of an accident, whether fatal or otherwise. Arrangements for that are made
in the Schedule, which is practically
adopted from the Petroleum Act. There
does not seem to be any difficulty there,
but there remains this point. If a man
has to give notice of the accident, is the
man required to give other notice under
the Factories and Workshops Act, or the
Notification of Accidents Act. That is
not referred to in the Bill, and there is
no special provision in the Petroleum Act
either, but it is a point on which the
Government should direct us later. The
only serious matter I want to call attention to is whether we are wise in cutting
out rabbit warrens and open air from the
scope of the regulations.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
think this is a Bill which should receive
the hearty support of this House. We
all know the enormous damage that is
done by vermin, and I think this Bill
will lead to the more effective destruction of vermin in warehouses
and stores. Some years ago there
was a serious outbreak of insects in
a large London tobacco warehouse, and
had it not been for the fact that they
were able to fumigate the warehouse by
the means described in this Bill very
valuable merchandise would have been
destroyed. I believe a similar thing has
happened in other warehouses. A person
who is accustomed to work with this
hydrogen cyanide told me that the
danger was in this poison being used by
persons who were not experienced in its
use. A person who fumigated a warehouse and entered it too soon, and without carrying out certain preliminary experiments, was liable to be overcome by
the gas.
I think the regulation of the use of
this particular means of fumigation will
prove to be very beneficial in the case of
warehouses where valuable merchandise
is stored. As regards the use of this gas
for fumigating rabbit warrens and other
haunts of vermin in the open air, my
informant assured me that there was
practically no risk. I am very glad the
Government have introduced this Bill,
and I am sure the House will give it a
Second Reading.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn until Thursday next".-(Mr.
Long.)
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
minutes after Four o'clock
until Thursday next.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 24th March, 1938.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Labour:
The Unemployment Insurance (Insurable Employments) Regulations (Northern Ireland), 1938.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act), and ordered to lie on the Table.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Quin's Divorce Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons,
and read a First time.
Questions to Minister.
COST OF COAL TO CONSUMERS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie-
Whether any information can be
given to the House respecting an inquiry to be held under Government
auspices in England with regard to
the cost of coal to consumers requiring
it for commercial or domestic purposes,
and particularly whether any steps are
being or will be taken to protect the
interests of Northern Ireland at the
inquiry.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the question
on the Paper explains itself. It is
common knowledge that the price of coal
in Northern Ireland has trebled in the
last thirty years, and there is naturally
some anxiety for information as to how
consumers in Northern Ireland are likely
to be affected by the proceedings now in
contemplation across the water.
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House): On
the 16th March the Prime Minister in
the Imperial Government announced in
the House of Commons that it had been
decided to set up a Departmental Committee, composed of persons independent
of the interests affected, with the following terms of reference:
"To inquire into the organisation
and methods of distribution of coal,
coke and manufactured fuel; to investigate the various items which make
up the differences between the prices
received by the producers and those
paid by the consumers, and to make
recommendations."
As the inquiry is being conducted by
a Departmental Committee set up by the
Imperial Government, its terms of reference do not extend to Northern Ireland.
The subject matter of the inquiry is,
however, of direct interest to Northern
Ireland, and, as the investigations of
the Committee will, no doubt, bring to
light interesting information in regard
to distribution costs, which will have a
bearing on conditions in Northern Ireland as well as in Great Britain, my
right hon. Friend the Minister of Commerce informs me that he has already
approached the Minister of Mines with
the object of being kept in touch with
the Committee's investigations.
AGRICULTURAL ORGANISATIONS
AND LEGISLATION.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Maguire:
To ask the Leader of the House if
when considering the establishment of
Nestlé's factory at Ballymoney, Co.
Antrim, the Ulster Agricultural
Organisation Society was consulted, in
view of the fact that the society represents the bulk of Ulster milk producers.
and whether the Ulster Farmers' Union
is consulted by the Ministry of Agriculture when legislation affecting agriculture is being drafted and prior to the
introduction of such legislation in
Parliament.
Mr. MAGUIRE: My reason for putting down this question is that I fear
there will be a great shortage of milk not
only for the supply of Messrs. Nestlé's
milk factory at Ballymoney, but also for
the supply of creameries. During the
past two years there has been a great
reduction in the milk production in
County Antrim and County Derry, from
which two counties Nestlé's will require
to draw their supplies. The total quantity of milk produced in County Antrim
in 1936 was 3,817,682 gallons and in 1937
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
24 MARCH 1938
the quantity fell to 3,550,420 gallons.
That is a very big reduction, and I fear
that producers will not be able to produce
sufficient milk to keep both Nestlé's
factory and the creameries supplied.
I want to know whether the Ulster
Agricultural Organisation Society was
consulted before a licence was granted
to Messrs. Nestlé's. That society represents the producers and the creameries in
the Six Counties, and I feel that the
Ministry should have consulted the society
in order to find out what supplies would
be available. While the Ministry need
not necessarily adopt all the recommendations and suggestions made by agricultural
societies, they could gain a good deal of
information from them.
Mr. ROBB: The Ministry of Agriculture has from time to time been consulted
by representatives of firms interested in
the establishment of new industries in
Northern Ireland, which are dependent
upon agriculture for their raw material.
The functions of the Ministry in this
matter are purely advisory, and it is
entirely a matter for the firm in question
to decide whether or not to commence
operations in Northern Ireland. It may
help to clear the position if I state that
the Ministry has no more power in such
affairs than it has over the opening of a
grocery business in any of our towns.
Obviously, therefore, the question of consultation with the Ulster Agricultural
Organisation Society or any other body
did not arise. Only about one-fifth of
the milk produced in Ulster goes to the
creameries, and the Ulster Agricultural
Society does not represent the bulk of the
milk producers.
With regard to the latter part of the
hon. Member's question, it has been the
practice of the Ministry to consult various
agricultural bodies, and amongst them
the Ulster Farmers' Union, before agricultural legislation is introduced, and it
is the hope of the Ministry that consultation of this nature will continue in force.
With reference to the figures Senator
Maguire has given regarding the production of milk in certain counties, Messrs.
Nestlé's know that the output of milk at
the moment would not be sufficient for
their ultimate requirements and the requirements of the community, but they
hope, as I am sure everybody hopes, that
the cow population will be increased to
such an extent that their ultimate
requirements will in the end be satisfied.
Mr. LESLIE: Am I not correct in
assuming that it is the declared policy
of the Minister of Agriculture to do
everything he can to increase the cow
population of Northern Ireland, and if
that be so, whether any better method
of increasing the cow population can be
suggested than
than that of
that of providing a
channel for the disposal of the product
of the cow?
Mr. ROBB: I quite agree.
RAVAGES OF CATTLE ABORTION.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Maguire:
To ask the Leader of the House
whether any steps are being taken by
the Ministry of Agriculture to control
the
ravages of cattle abortion in
Northern Ireland; whether it is a fact
that before any noticeable increase in
milk production can be brought about
this disease must be stamped out; and
whether the Ministry of Agriculture
can give any explanation as to the
reason why so many small milk producers are going out of business, with
the result that in many districts residents are unable to obtain supplies of
milk, and, as a consequence, shopkeepers report that sales of condensed
milk have increased ten-fold.
Mr. MAGUIRE: This question of
cattle abortion in Northern Ireland is a
very serious one. According to my information it is increasing all over the
Six Counties. The Ministry is certainly
doing something in the way of suggesting treatment by various methods, but it
has not met with the success we all hoped
for. The Ministry should intensify its
research work in this direction in order
to try to combat this deadly disease in
milk cattle, which is one cause of the
reduction in the milk production of this
country.
With regard to the question of small
milk producers going out of business, I
know for a fact that in several villages
and small towns in Northern Ireland
there has been a great shortage of milk
due to the fact that many small milk
B
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Mr. Maguire]
producers who were supplying households.
have ceased producing milk. They give
as their reason the fact that they are
being supervised to too great an extent.
One man who has ceased production told
me that on one day alone he had four
inspectors call upon him at his place of
business, and another man had three
inspectors visit his premises on one day.
They seem to be greatly harassed by
this inspection system, and the result is
a shortage of milk in certain towns and
villages.
Shopkeepers have told me that their
business in condensed milk has very
largely increased. One shopkeeper told
me that within the last few years his
sales of condensed milk had increased
tenfold, while others have informed me
that they are selling five and six times
as much condensed milk as formerly.
That cannot be good for the country.
One shopkeeper told me that it was impossible in his business to get liquid milk
for food for babies, and on one occasion
a mother had to purchase a tin of condensed milk which was labelled as not
to be used as food for babies. That is
a condition of affairs that should not
exist, and the Ministry should take steps
to help the people out of this predicament.
Mr. ROBB: Under an Order made by
the Ministry of Agriculture, of which I
have furnished a copy to the hon.
Senator, owners of cows which have prematurely calved are required to take
certain precautions to prevent the spread
of contagious abortion. A similar Order
is in force in Great Britain. The difficulty, however, of making such measures
really effective is great, since research.
work in the disease is still very far from
complete. The Ministry's Veterinary
Research Laboratory is, at present, conducting research into this matter, and
similar work is being done on an extensive scale by all the leading livestock
producing countries in the world. So far
as Northern Ireland is concerned, if any
breeder wishes to clear his herd of this
disease the Ministry's Laboratory is prepared to assist him by taking and testing
blood samples and by giving any available advice.
I am afraid I cannot accept the pessimistic view that the stamping out of this
difficult disease must precede any noticeable increase in milk production. The
disease has been prevalent in all stock
raising countries for generations. In
point of fact, our aggregate production
of milk is increasing. The suggestion
that many small milk producers are
going out of business is not borne out
by the facts.
For example, the number of licensed.
milk producers on the 31st December,
1936, was 7,610, whereas on the 31st
December, 1937, the number was 7,656.
I am not aware that in many districts
consumers are unable to obtain supplies
of milk, and if there are such districts,
I would be very glad to have particulars;
nor can I accept the suggestion that
sales of condensed milk have increased
in the proportion suggested by the hon.
Senator. For example, there has only
been a two per cent. increase in imports
of this commodity in 1937 as compared
with 1936, and it can hardly be seriously.
contended that this slight increase is due
to a scarcity of fresh milk.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would like to
know if it is a fact that one of the
causes for this decrease in milk production is the subsidy that is given on fat
cattle leaving this country. I understand that this subsidy is given in
respect of young heifers as well as
bullocks, and I think that if the Ministry stopped giving this subsidy in
respect of fat heifers and those heifers
were kept in the country it would
increase the milk production. From
what I have been able to discover in going
through the country the farmers are
losing heart as far as milk is concerned.
They are not getting the price they think
they should get. I asked a farmer to
whom I was talking some time ago how
he benefited by the subsidy given on
manufactured milk. He told me that he
took no milk to the creamery; that it did
not pay him; that it paid him better to
keep fewer milk cows-just enough to
supply himself and his family with milk
and butter, and to give the surplus to the
pigs. The Government should take steps
to put a stop to this subsidy being given
to young heifers leaving the country.
Mr. ROBB: I do not profess to be an
expert on agriculture, but I will convey
to the Minister of Agriculture the remarks of hon. Members on this subject.
69 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

24 MARCH 1938
Committee
Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Mr. ROBB: I beg to move, "That this
House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: I rather hope to be able
to induce the right hon. Gentleman not
to proceed with that Motion this afternoon. Indeed I am going to suggest that
he should let it stand over until Tuesday,
and the reason is not entirely the shortness of time. If we approach the Committee Stage this afternoon we shall be
doing so without the print of the Debates
of Tuesday in our hands, and with the
Amendment Paper before us for only a
very few hours. But I am not laying
great stress on that aspect of the question.
The point is that another factor has come
into the situation. An Order made by the
Ministry of Labour under the powers of
Section 3 of the 1936 Act has been laid on
the Table of this House to-day, and I
think it is only reasonable that hon.
Members should have an opportunity of
construing that Order in conjunction
with the Bill in order to see how far they
react upon each other.
Moreover, I have reason to believe the
right hon. Gentleman is in a position to
give us certain information on the Bill
which will be of great assistance to us
before we deal with it in detail in Committee, and if that information is given
to us now, on this Motion, and the Committee Stage postponed until Tuesday, I
think it will be found that most, if not
all, of the Amendmnets on the Paper will
disappear. As a matter of fact, I may
say that in the interval between Tuesday
and this afternoon the word "warrener"
has been detected in Shakespeare, though
without throwing any great light on its
meaning, and, more important,
"ghillie" has been discovered in the
County of Tyrone. Therefore, the Committee will not be troubled as regards
those matters.
a
If the Committee Stage is postponed
until Tuesday, and if the right hon.
Gentleman should accede to my suggestion, I can promise on my own behalf
that I certainly will not oppose taking
all the remaining stages of the Bill on
Tuesday, and so prevent any discord as
regards the commencement of the operation of what will then become an Act.
Meanwhile, I am sure the House will
listen to the observations the right hon.
Gentleman is now prepared to make.
Mr. ROBB: I thank the hon. and
learned Senator for the way he has put his
proposal before the House. I had intended at some stage of to-day's proceedings to make a statement on the general
position which this Bill seeks to modify,
and it is this.
The Unemployment Insurance Act,
1920, excepted from compulsory insurance
"Employment in domestic service
except where the employed person is
employed in any trade or business
carried on for the purposes of gain."
The final interpretation of the scope
of the Act is a matter for the High
Court, and on 10th October, 1920, Mr.
Justice Roche, in dealing with an appeal
by the Junior Carlton Club, referred in
the following terms to the provision in
respect of employment in domestic service:
"I shall not endeavour to formulate a
definition of what is a domestic servant,
even for the purposes of this Act, but
if I had in my own words to describe
what is of the characteristics of such a
servant, I should express it and do
express it for present purposes very
much in the way that I sought to express it on the occasion on which the
case was argued before me, as follows:
'Domestic servants are servants
whose main or general function it
is to be about their employer's persons or establishments, residential or
quasi-residential, for the purpose of
ministering to their employer's needs
or wants, or to the needs or wants
of those who are members of such
establishments, or of those resorting
to such establishments, including
guests.'
He held that the Club servants were
domestic servants, and outside the scope
of the Act, and in later decisions held
that hunt servants, gamekeepers, river
keepers, chauffeurs, and a number of
other outdoor servants were also domestic
servants within the meaning of the Act.
These decisions have led to certain
anomalous results, due to the fact that
71 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
THE SENATE

Committee
or
[Mr. Robb]
before a domestic servant can be insured
he must be employed in a trade
business carried on for the purpose of
gain. Staff employed in the Dining Club
in Stormont and in the Members' Dining
Room are, therefore, not insurable,
although staff employed on similar duties
in a proprietary restaurant are insurable. Similarly, a doctor's chauffeur is
insurable because he is employed in the
doctor's business, while a private
chauffeur is not insurable.
Anomalies such as these led to the
whole question of the insurability of
domestic servants being referred to the
Unemployment Insurance Statutory
Committee appointed under Section 56
of the Unemployment Insurance Act,
1935, the Great Britain Act. The Section provides that one of the Members
shall be appointed after consultation
with the Minister of Labour for Northern
Ireland, and Captain C. C. Craig was
appointed under this provision. The
Committee reported that chauffeurs and
the staff employed on domestic duties in
clubs, educational institutions, and
Government Departments should, with
certain minor exceptions, be brought into
insurance under the General Scheme.
Private gardeners had been made insurable under the Agricultural Scheme
by the Order for which provision was
made in the Unemployment Insurance
(Agriculture) Act. The Statutory Committee recommended that every outdoor
domestic servant-apart from chauffeurs,
who were to be included in the General
Scheme-should be included in the
Agricultural Scheme. The High Court
decisions had resulted in gamekeepers,
warreners, ghillies, river keepers, water
bailiffs, grooms, stablemen, boatmen,
coachmen, gatekeepers, lodge keepers,
kennelmen, and handymen being included in the category of domestic servant, and the Statutory Committee
reported unanimously that the classes
mentioned should be brought into the
Agricultural Scheme.
It was clear that the group dealt with
in the Schedule to the Bill should be
brought into the Agricultural Scheme at
once, but further enquiry was necessary
in regard to the position of coachmen,
boatmen, gatekeepers, and kennelmen
before a decision could be taken as to
whether their employment was more
appropriate to the Agricultural Scheme
or the General Scheme. It has now been
decided that they also should be included
in the Agricultural Scheme, and their
inclusion will be effected by regulation
under sub-clause (2) of Clause 2.
the decision, which was taken
jointly with the Ministry of Labour in
Great Britain, had been reached in time,
the additional classes would have been
included in the Schedule. To do so at
this stage would make it impossible for
the Measure to come into force on 4th
April, and it is, therefore, necessary to
deal with the classes by regulation, as
is being done in Great Britain.
There are, of course, objections to dealing by regulation with matters that
might be put into a Bill, but this is a
peculiar case, and when Members consider the facts as I have stated them I
am sure they will agree that it is not
unreasonable to deal with these matters
As
in the way proposed. I think my
explanation covers one of the hon. and
learned Senator's Amendments.
regards the others, when the hon. and
learned Senator considers the interpretation the High Court has placed on a
domestic servant he will probably agree
that it is wise to leave the words
"domestic servant" in the Bill rather
than substitute the term he suggests.
As the hon. and learned Member
stated, a "ghillie" has actually been discovered in the County of Tyrone, and I
think the use of that word is advisable,
because a person who was employed as a
ghillie in Scotland or Yorkshire and was
insured there might come over here, and
it would be desirable that his qualifications for insurance should be continuous,
and that he should get credit here for
the contributions he had paid across the
water. I hope hon. Members will be
satisfied with this explanation. On the
understanding that no objection will be
raised to the suspension of Standing
Orders to enable all the remaining stages
of the Bill to be taken on Tuesday, I
have no objection to the postponement
of the Committee stage until that day.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL:
It has become a common practice to make
an explanation such as that which has
just been given by the right hon.
Gentleman when a difficult point arises.
In his statement the Leader of the House
73 Unemployment Insurance Bill
24 MARCH 1938

Exported Animals Bill
mentioned several points that might,
with equal propriety, have been given
in his speech on the Second Reading.
Unless questions which come before this
House are fully explained on the Second
Reading they are liable to lead to misunderstandings which might never arise
if a more detailed explanation had been
given.
There is one point that seems to me to
require further explanation, and it arises
out of the right hon. Gentleman's statement. He has cited a decision of the High
Court of England, and in doing so her
mentioned that that decision is held to be
binding on us and on the British Government in regard to the reinsurance agreement. I think I am right in saying that
decisions of the High Court of Justice in
England are not binding on our High
Court of Justice. When I used to
practice in England I found that many
excellent Irish decisions were not regarded
in England as being more than decisions
of great authority. Here you have a
possible source of conflict between our
own courts over here and the courts in
England.
We are bound, for the purposes of
reinsurance, to assume that the courts in
England are a binding authority, and
now that some definition of domestic
servants has been given by the High
Court in England a definition should be
inserted in our own Acts to ensure there
is no possible chance of our own courts
making a different decision as to the
meaning of domestic servant. I think
that is a matter that ought to be considered before the Committee Stage.
have not had very much time to consider
the point, because I was not aware what
the right hon. Gentleman was going to
say.
I
Moreover, I should like an opportunity
to read what the right hon. Gentleman
has said, because although his voice is
very clear, there were parts of his statement which did not quite reach me, sitting behind him, and which I have not
been able to grasp as thoroughly as I
would like to do before considering such
an important matter. Therefore I hope
we will have an opportunity of reading
what the right hon. Gentleman has said
before we are obliged to proceed with this
Bill.
The right hon. Gentleman has suggested taking all the further stages of the
I
Bill on Tuesday. That would seem to
make it impossible or very difficult to put

seems desirable, having regard to what
the right hon. Gentleman has said.
agree that we ought to pass this Bill as

Bill will be passed on Tuesday until we

On Question, Motion negatived.
House to be in Committee on Tuesday

Exported Animals (Compensation) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: The main purpose of this
Measure is to enable compensation to be
given to owners of livestock exported
from Northern Ireland in respect of
deterioration in their condition due to
longer detention at United Kingdom
ports than is usual, because of an outbreak or suspected outbreak of foot-andmouth disease.
May I remind hon. Members that by
the Slaughtered Animals (Compensation) Act (Northern Ireland), 1928, it is
provided that where livestock shipped
from Northern Ireland to Great Britain
are, while undergoing detention at a
British port, slaughtered by reason of the
requirements of the British authorities in
connection with an outbreak or suspected
outbreak, of foot-and-mouth disease, the
owner of the animals concerned shall be
entitled to be paid compensation to cover
the loss sustained by such compulsory
slaughter. The Act of 1932 extended the
legislation so as to provide compensation
to cover extra lairage and feeding charges
due to abnormal detention in British
ports on account of foot-and-mouth
disease.
This compensation is defrayed from an
insurance fund made up of small levies
paid by the owners on animals exported.
This levy is suspended whenever the fund
reaches an amount thought to be sufficient
to meet possible liabilities over a reasonable time. The scheme does not, therefore, involve any charge on the Exchequer
Fumigation Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Robb]
and there is now about £10,000 in the
insurance fund, which should be ample
to meet any probable emergency, either
under the existing legislation or under the
extension now proposed. The fund is
controlled and the scheme administered
by trustees appointed by the Northern
Ireland Livestock Trade.
The trade now desires that the scheme
shall be extended so that in addition to
the compensation already referred to it
will cover losses in respect of the deterioration of animals due to detention in
British lairages on account of foot-andmouth disease beyond the normal period.
This is a natural and praiseworthy development of the existing scheme, and, as
indicated, the necessary funds subscribed
by the shippers are available.
abnormal detention as that referred to is,
fortunately, not frequent, but is the cause
of loss when it does arise which should
clearly be covered under the scheme.
Such
It is intended to have a definite scale
of compensation to cover deterioration,
but in order to avoid rigidity an arrangement has been arrived at with the livestock trade whereby such scale is to be
prescribed by the Ministry of Agriculture
after consultation with the recognised
trade association. This arrangement will
enable the rate of compensation to be
amended from time to time if circumstances so require.
The Bill also makes it possible to pay
compensation to owners of cattle ultimately slaughtered for lairage and feeding
charges in cases where the animals have
been detained for a longer period than
usual. I beg to move, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committeed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday.
Hydrogen Cyanide (Fumigation)
Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Robb.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair].
CLAUSE 1 (Power to make regulations).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
1, to leave out Sub-clause (2).
I am
suggesting that the Committee should expunge Sub-clause (2) from this Bill.
Although I do not think rabbit warrens
affect the question materially, the question of fumigation in the open air does
present, to my mind, various difficulties,
and I think it is only wise to restrict the
Such
powers to be taken by the Bill.
powers, if granted by the House, need
not be exercised. The whole thing is
permissive. The Ministry may make
regulations, or in drafting the regulations
this or that proceeding can be exempted
if it is thought safe to do so.
It appears from what the right hon.
Gentleman told us the other day that
hydrogen cyanide gas is very volatile,
somewhat inflammable, and extremely
dangerous, and, moreover, that its presence is not very easy to detect. We are
not told in the Bill, and I do not know
myself, where it is proposed that this gas
should be generated, but the generation
of the composition is one of the things in
respect of which regulations may be
made. One imagines that generation
would normally take place in some
laboratory and the gas be confined in a
cylinder and taken to the place where it
is intended to bring it into operation. If
that is so there is some risk of the gas
being diverted in transit, and no control
over it remaining.
Again, there would seem to be the
possibility of someone using the gas for
the extermination of rats in a hen-house
in a back garden. If it is in a comparatively built-up area, and there is any
escape of gas and any windows are open,
there would again seem to be a possible
risk of danger. Therefore I think it would
be better for us to leave that question
entirely to the Ministry, which is to have
power to make regulations, and all that is
necessary to do that is to leave this Subclause out of the Clause.
Mr. ROBB: I cannot accept this
Amendment. If it is not intended to
regulate fumigation in the open air or
rabbit warrens, it can do no harm to leave
this Sub-clause in the Bill. As to the
fumigation of a hen-house, if the henhouse is a house, then the fumigation by
Fumigation Bill-
Committee
24 MARCH 1938
+ hydrogen cyanide ought very well to be
regulated. I cannot see that the words
in the Sub-clause can do any harm in
the present intention of the Government,
and merely to leave them out because
originally they might have been superfluous does not seem to me to be reasonable. Accordingly, the Government
cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I suppose the difficulty
of carrying out fumigation in ships and
air craft has been considered. If a ship
is to be effectively fumigated part of it
must be done in the open air. I do not
know how it is proposed to operate the
arrangement; whether it is to be done
by means of a pump operated from outside, or whether they put in a little
cylinder, turn on the tap, and run. But
it seems to me that to fumigate a ship
or airship you must do your operation in
the open air, or partly in the open air.
However, if the Government have considered the point I do not desire to press
the Amendment unduly.
Mr. ROBB: I cannot see any difficulty
about it.
Mr. LESLIE: We are left in a
difficulty about the transporation of the
poison, but it is for the Ministry to take
the risk of accident. I ask permission to
withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Notice to be given of
accidents resulting from fumigation).
(1) Whenever any accident which
occasions loss of human life or personal
injury occurs as the result of the fumigation of any premises or article, the
person by whom, or by whose agent,
the fumigation was carried out shall
forthwith send or cause to be sent to
the Minister of Home Affairs notice of
the accident and of the loss of human
life or personal injury.
(2) Every such person as aforesaid
who fails to comply with the provisions
of this section shall be liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding
five pounds.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in subclause (1) of Clause 2, after the word
"article" (any premises or article), in
line 24, to insert, "with hydrogen
cyanide."
This is a very small matter.
It will be seen that Clause 1 of the Bill
is particularly limited to fumigation by
hydrogen cyanide. This Clause 1S
general, and I do not think that is the
intention. There is always a risk of the
Clause being disconnected from its text,
and then things may happen.
Mr. ROBB: I accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 3 (Application of certain
provisions relating to inquiries and
coroners' inquests), 4 (Power to make
Orders in Council applying the Act to
fumigations with other gases), and 5
(Short title), ordered to stand part of
the Bill.

(i) they may enter and inspect any

for the said purpose;
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in subparagraph (i) of paragraph (c) of the
Schedule, after the word "building," to
insert, "and may inspect any article."
Clause 1 of the Bill takes power to fumigate any article and to do various things
in the way of restricting its use and so

Petroleum Act, but I think these words
ought to be in the Schedule.
Mr. ROBB: I accept the Amendment

Butter and Cream Marketing Scheme.
Mr. ROBB: I beg to move,
Cream
"That the Butter and
Marketing Scheme (Northern Ireland),
1936, which came into force on the
fifth day of May, 1936, by virtue of
an order made by the Ministry of
Agriculture under section one of the
Agricultural Marketing Act (Northern
Ireland), 1933, entitled 'the Butter
and Cream Marketing Scheme (Northern Ireland) (Approval) Order, 1936'
and has been amended by subsequent
orders so made be hereby approved."
The purpose of the Resolution which
stands upon the Order Paper is to enable
the Butter and Cream Marketing Scheme
to continue in operation beyond the 4th
May next. The scheme was originally
framed by the Ministry of Agriculture
under the provisions of the Agricultural
Marketing Act, 1933, and was approved
on the 5th May, 1936, coming into operation on that day. It has subsequently
been amended in the light of experience
and a copy of the scheme in the form in
which it exists after these amendments
is in the hands of hon. Senators.
In order to enable the scheme to continue in existence beyond the 4th May
next it is necessary for this House 10rmally to approve the scheme because it
is provided by sub-section five of section
one of the Agricultural Marketing Act
that:
"A scheme approved by the Ministry
shall in any event cease to have effect
upon the expiration of a period of two
years from the date upon which it
came into force, unless at some time
before the expiration of that period it
has been approved by a Resolution
passed by each House of Parliament."
The purpose of the Butter and Cream
Marketing Scheme and the success which
has been achieved by the Butter and
Cream Marketing Board in regulating
the sales of butter and cream from
Northern Ireland creameries are all well
known to hon. Senators. I am sure the
House will wish to give its approval to
the continuance of the scheme which has
now passed its experimental stage and
has proved its worth.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

referred a few minutes ago. It must be
done within five days, and I think
Thursday would be a convenient day.

Sir James WILTON: The Leader of

the Government did not propose to bear
all the cost of these courses which are to
be established under this Bill, and I, as

hardly necessary to remind the House
that the rates in our municipal communities at the present time are
sufficiently high, and
very
anxious to know what is the percentage

voted for the Second Reading, and I

Mr. LESLIE: I do not rise to oppose

83 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE

aversion to the Clause, that the House

were
It appears from the debates in the
House of Commons at Westminster on
Friday last, published on Saturday in
the London newspapers, which
obtainable in Northern Ireland on Monday, that it is now proposed that the
agriculturally insured person will after
the 4th July, have to bear an 8d. stamp;
that is, 4d. from the employer and 4d.
from the employee, instead of, as at
present, a 9d. stamp, or 4. each from
employer and employee. I therefore
wish the House to understand that,
assuming that we follow over here the
same practice as is adopted over there,
we shall be putting people into insurance
as from 4th April on a 9d. stamp in
order that they may come into insurance
on 4th July on an 8d. stamp. That
appears to be the effect of this Clause,
plus an Order in Council or Regulations
in the not too distant future.
It seems to me that the right thing
to do is to mention the matter on this
Clause, and after the Third Reading it
can conveniently be discussed whether
to leave out "April" and insert "July"
in the three places where the word
occurs. The other question of people to
be included under this Clause would
seem to arise on the Scheldule. For the
moment I do not oppose the Clause
before the Committee.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

in
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move,
Clause 2, sub-clause (1), to leave out the
word "April" in line 21 and insert
instead the word "July." The point I
want to get discussed is whether the
House really desires that people should.
be brought into insurance on one rate
in the month of April, to remain in
insurance at another rate in the month
of July. I understand from the report
of the debates at Westminster that there
are other trivial alterations that will be
made. If I remember rightly, the waiting period is slightly to be reduced from
six days to three days, and additional
benefit is to be provided for young
people, I think between 18 and 21.
But the point I want to direct attention to is whether it is really advisable
that we should bring people into insurance at one rate in April, in order that
they may be qualified at another rate
in July. If the House decided to make
the alteration I cannot see that anything would happen. The rest of the
Bill, of course, would come into operation on the day it received the Royal
Assent. The only effect would be that
the Bill would have to go back to the
House of Commons for them to agree,
if they decided to agree, with the
Amendment, and then there would be
no great hurry about it, because the only
thing of immediate importance is the
introduction into insurance of these
people on 4th April at the existing rate.
As regards the other question as to
what the classes brought in are to do,
how they are to get their benefit, it is
not, perhaps, a question appropriate to
the moment, but the idea of a gamekeeper waiting in a queue at the local
exchange, either for benefit or to see if
he can get a job, in company with a
dozen or more agricultural labourers
against whom he may have launched
prosecutions for poaching rabbits is a
little curious, but I do not think we
need go into that now.
$5 Unemployment Insurance Bill

29 MARCH 1938
Fumigation Bill
Mr. ROBB: There is really no difficulty at all about the alteration in the
rate of contributions. It is an ordinary
factor in the administration of unemployment insurance that the rate may be
varied either up or down according to
the state of the insurance fund. The
alteration that may be made in July
next will be an alteration that will affect
not only the people brought in under
this Bill, but all those who are in the
agricultural insurance scheme, if the
alteration be in fact made. The only
effect of postponing the operation of the
date will be that people who ought to
be brought into this scheme will be delayed three months.
A mere variation in the rate is a common element in all these insurance
schemes, and it creates no difficulty at
all. Rates have been altered both up
and down in the past, and to decline to
apply this provision as from 4th April
next would only mean that this class of
person whom the Bill affects would be
delayed from getting any benefit under
it for three months. It would have the
additional disadvantage from the administrative point of view that the system
of insurance in this country would be
different from the system in Great
Britain, and that is a thing we are very
diffident to agree to. It is part of our
reinsurance agreement that the services
should be kept the same, and the only
effect of passing this Amendment would
be to delay the entry into insurance of
these people for three months. As I
have said, the alteration in the rate of
contribution will not create the slightest
difficulty, and for these
reasons the
Government cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: Surely the point is that
the element introduced will be comic
rather than common. If the rate is to
come down in July it must be agreed
that the premium of insurance has
hitherto been too high. The proposal in
the Bill is to put people into a scheme
for the first time at admittedly too high
a premium in order that we may bring
the scheme into operation on the same
date as in England. In default of support
I do not propose to press the Amendment,
but I must say that the position is, on
the face of it, absolutely ridiculous. It is
agreed that the rate of premium is too
high, and, therefore, you bring some
other people in for a period of three
months.
Mr. ROBB: It is not agreed that as
from to-day the rate of premium is too
high. What is agreed is that upon the
recommendation of the Unemployment
Insurance Statutory Committee the rate
may well be reduced as from 4th July.
That is not to say the rate is too high
at the moment.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think we need
go on. It is really a very small matter,
and no one else cares about it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Resolved, "That this Bill do now
pass'.-(Mr. Robb.)
And a message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any Amendment.

that there was
a question asked as to

given under the Factories and Workshops
Act and the Notification of Accidents

Bill. I understood we would have information on that subject, but perhaps
the Leader of the House proposes to give

Mr. ROBB: I have inquired into this
matter. Technically two notices will be

Mr. LESLIE: Thank you.

Exported Animals (Compensation) Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill". (Mr. Robb.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSES 1 (Compensation in respect
of depreciation in value of animals), 2
(Compensation in special cases), 3
(Provisions as to compensation under the
Exported Animals (Compensation) Acts
and this Act), 4 (Extension of purposes
of Exported Animals (Compensation)
Acts), 5 (Interpretation) and 6 (Short
title, construction and citation) ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered, That the Bill be reported
without Amendment to the House.
Ordered, That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without Amendment.
Resolved, "That this Report be now
received."-(Mr. Robb.)
Ordered, That the Bill be read the
Third time on Thursday.
Adjournment.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
House do now adjourn".
Mr. LESLIE : Until?
Mr. LONG: Thursday.
Sir JAMES WILTON: Could any
information be given as to the days on
which the House will sit next week.
Mr. LONG: I am not yet in a position
to say.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
minutes before Four o'clock
until Thursday next.

THE SENATE.

Thursday, 31st March, 1938.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
The Inspectors' Reports for the year
1937 respecting each Private and Public
Mental Hospital in Northern Ireland and
the Criminal Lunatic Asylum.

Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.

The Belfast Hospital for Sick Children
Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a Second time."
River Bann Navigation Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a Second time."
The record of the people of that
part of the United Kingdom in the
annals of the British Commonwealth of
Nations puts beyond doubt the earnestness of their desire that peace should
be ensued throughout the world and the
fixity of their determination to defend,
should the need arise, our common
inheritance at all costs.
Yours sincerely,
N. CHAMBERLAIN.
Royal Assent.
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House): I
have to report to the House that his
Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland,
in the name and on behalf of his Majesty
the King, has been pleased to give his
Assent to the following Bill agreed upon
by both Houses:
Unemployment Insurance.

The Commons have passed the Blind
Persons Bill and the Loans Guarantee
Bill, to which they desire the concurrence
of this House.
McAllen's Divorce Bill.
The following report was made from
the Joint Committee on Divorce Bills:-
That they had considered McAllen's
Divorce Bill, and that the averments in
the Preamble had been proved to their
satisfaction, and that they had gone
through the Bill, and directed the same
to be reported to the House without
amendment.
Assurance.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have received the
following reply from the Prime Minister
of the Government of the United
Kingdom to the Resolution of the House
dated the 22nd of March, 1938:-
My dear Speaker,
It was with great pleasure that I
received the Resolution of the Senate
of Northern Ireland which you were
good enough to transmit to me.
