Unemployment Insurance Regulations.
MOTION TO ANNUL.
The following Motion stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
That the Unemployment Insurance.
(Insurable Employments) Regulations
(Northern Ireland), 1938, as laid on
the Table of the House on Thursday,
24th March, be annulled.
Mr. LESLIE: The House, I am sure,
understands why the Motion standing in
my name on the Paper is worded in the
form which I have adopted, but lest there
should be any misunderstanding or lest
perchance some hon. Member may not
have received his Order Paper or his copy
of the Regulation, I would venture to
explain that the only power that Parliament has retained to itself in matters of
this kind is that to which Section 112 of
the Unemployment Insurance Act of
1936 relates. I am afraid I must trouble
the House by reading the Section in
question. I am reading from the Unemployment Insurance Act, which was
Consolidation Measure, and which received the Royal Assent on 12th
November, 1936. The Section runs as
follows:
a
(1) Regulations made under this Act
shall, as soon as may be after they
are made, be laid before each
House of Parliament. If either
House of Parliament, within the
statutory period next after any
Regulation made as aforesaid has
been laid before such House, resolves that the Regulations shall
be annulled, the Regulations shall,
after the date of the resolution, be
void, but without prejudice to the
validity of anything previously
done thereunder or to the making
of a new Regulation.
(2) Regulations made under this Act
shall have effect as if enacted in
this Act.
(3) This Section applies to all Regulations made under this Act,
whether by the Ministry or otherwise.
The statutory period is defined in the
Interpretation Clause as "a period comprising five days at least on which the
Senate or House of Commons has sat,
but not being in any case shorter in
duration than ten days." The regulation
before us is dated 15th March, and purports to come into operation on 4th April,
so it will be seen that the statutory period
of ten days is being run remarkably close.
The ten days is there, but it is not much
more than there. Such a regulation as
the one before us, I may perhaps further
explain, is made under Section 3 of the
Act of 1936. I think Section 3 of the
Act is well known, and therefore I do
not propose to trouble the House by
reading it.
Therefore, it differs, being a regulation,
from that Special Order to which Section
113 of the Act relates. I confess I do
not know for what purpose Special Orders
are issued, but I do think that if Special
Orders are to be issued at all this would
have been a good opportunity for doing
it. The reason I should have preferred
a Special Order is to be found in Section
113, which says that before a Special
Order is made the Ministry shall publish
in such manner
as it
may think best
notice of the proposal to make an Order,
and where copies can be obtained, and
of the time during which objection shall
be made. That is a procedure which
would seem to me to have some advantage
which the mere issue of a regulation, with
ten days' interval, does not possess.
It will then be appreciated, I hope,
that although either House can annul a
regulation, neither House can amend it.
They can discuss the regulation, and
they can criticise it, but the only method
of making their criticism effective is to
annul the regulation, leaving it open,
of course, to the Ministry to make a new
regulation as provided by the Act. It
may be presumed that in making any new
regulation the Ministry would have due
regard to the criticisms passed in the
House by whose direction the regulation
was annulled.
93 Unemployment Regulations-

31 MARCH 1938
Motion to Annul
I am asking the House to annul the
regulation now before us, but before
starting on a campaign of criticism, with
its attendant suggestions, I should like to
remind the House that the circumstances
as regards the discussion of this regulation.
are not the same circumstances as those
in which we found ourselves last Tuesday
when discussing sub-clause (2) of Clause
2 of what I was going to call Tuesday's
Bill, but what I must now refer to as
Tuesday's Act, as it has this afternoon
received the Royal Assent. I refer to
the Unemployment Insurance Act of this
year. In speaking on that Clause I
said that much as I disliked the Clause
I did not feel that I could properly move
to have it left out of the Bill, because
it had passed in another place. But that
is not the case to-day. In considering
this regulation we are sitting entirely as
a Chamber of first instance.
I begin my objection to the regulation.
by saying I think it would have been
better to proceed by Special Order or by
Act. That is perhaps a pious expression
of opinion which does not very greatly
affect the argument, but I do regret that
before this regulation was made more
notice was not given. I do not mean so
much notice to Members of the House,
but to the country, the people affected,
and those who employ those people. I
shall be referring to that matter again.
Passing to criticism, I notice and I am
giving this for the moment as an instance
—that in paragraph (2) of the regulation
the expression "business" is defined.
It will be noticed that the more or less
correlative expression "trade" is not
defined. I think it ought to have been
possible to express the whole of this regulation in far plainer language than has
been
used-language which, in fact,
would not only have been appreciated by
Members of this House readily without
undue mental strain, but appreciated by
all concerned. I think, in fact, that
words should have been used which would
have made the intention and the effect
of the regulation far plainer than is the
case in the regulation before us.
The regulation, I understand, is practically taken from the one prepared in
England. We did not construct it for
ourselves in this country. In paragraph
3 of these regulations we are told-it is
on the second page we are told rather
indirectly that the Schedule to the regulations shall not affect the operation of
Part II. of the First Schedule to the 1936
Act and Part II. of the First Schedule of
the Act of 1938, I may venture to remind
the House, gives a general list of accepted
employments. According to the regulation the regulations are not to affect the
operation of part 2 of the First Schedule
other than paragraph 3 thereof. Paragraph 3 thereof runs-"Employment in
domestic service except where the
employed person is employed in horticulture or in any trade or business carried
on for the purpose of gain."
That was paragraph 3 of Part II. of the
First Schedule. That was the law on 15th
March. We are now at the 31st
March, and what I called Tuesday's Bill,
which received the Royal Assent this
afternoon, is now law. In Tuesday's
Act occurs the following new provision:
For paragraph 3 of Part II. of the First
Schedule, which contains a general list
of excepted employments, shall on and
after 4th April be substituted a new paragraph, which runs "employment in
domestic service. .. That is in the
regulation and I need not read it. The
point I am making is that on 15th March
that paragraph was expressed in one form
and on 31st March in this Bill, which has
become an Act in the meantime, it is
something else. Now persumably from
paragraph 2 of the regulations, that is
the second paragraph of the second paragraph of the regulations: "Unless the
context otherwise requires any reference
in these regulations to the Unemployment
Insurance Act (Northern Ireland), 1936,
shall be construed as a reference to that
Act as amended by
any order
subsequent enactment." The Act said one
thing and the Act now says something
different. I want to know, and I think
I am entitled to ask what is the present
position. What does that paragraph
mean? Could we not have been told
plainly at the cost of a few days' delay?
or
I pass to the Schedule of the regulations, and I begin with paragraph one.
I am sorry to have to refer so extensively
to notes, but it is obvious in a matter of
this kind one must do so. I am passing
to the Schedule to paragraph one and the
expression that is there used "domestic
service." Now I entirely agree with the
difficulty which my hon. learned and
gallant Friend opposite (Captain Perceval
Maxwell) found when speaking on
Unemployment Regulations--
Motion to Annul
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
Tuesday as regards that expression,
"domestic service." The point was that
there had been various decisions by the
English courts as to this or that being
domestic service which were rather contrary to the old definition of domestic
service, which meant a man who rendered
domestic service and lived under the roof
of his employer. I do say arising particularly out of these regulations that if
the term "domestic service" is to be
used it ought to be defined. If the old
definition is gone we ought to be given
a new one and we ought to do it for
ourselves.
Again, as to trade or business, I mentioned that business was defined in the
regulations but trade was not.
I understand the definition in the regulationsit is on the first page-to relate
to the employer. It is the business
or trade of the employer, I understand, that is in question, not the
business or trade of the employee. But
that, I venture to suggest, is somewhat
doubtful. If we take it that it is the
employer, what is to be the position of
the private cook whether male or female?
I am going back to the definition of
business. I am rather inclined to wonder
whether the word employer where it
secondly appears is not a misstype for
employee. I think employee would
make sense of it, and I do not think
employer does. No doubt we shall be
told at a later stage.
Now I want to go to paragraph 2 of
paragraph 1 of this unfortunate schedule.
A gamekeeper is introduced here and
the whole paragraph relates, it will be
remembered, to exceptions. "Persons
employed in domestic service in any
trade or business not being a trade or
business carried on for the purpose of
gain except persons so employed-
Only this morning when Tuesday's Act
came in the private gamekeeper was in
excepted employment. Now he is no
longer in excepted employment. But
the question is how this new regulation is going to get on with the Act of
Parliament. They do not seem to me
to agree.
It is very difficult. They
have a curious habit in the Ministry. I
presume they have a special laboratory
for the preparation of these regulations.
They do seem to have the habit of taking
a thing out of here and putting it in there.
That is one of the reasons why I think
plainer language might advantageously
have been used. Gamekeeper is merely
taken as an example.
The House will remember that to some
extent regulations made by the Ministry can override something said in an
Act of Parliament. To my mind that is
all the greater shame for the Parliament
that made it possible, but still it remains.
That is the position. I must ask
whether the ordinary man can be expected to understand this schedule in
conjunction with Tuesday's Act. I do
not think I should have a very ready
response even from this House. Now
the gamekeeper and stableman are
included in Tuesday's Act, but other
people are in a different category. It
may be intended to bring them into
insurance by another order to be issued
at some later date. If so they will of
course come into insurance on a different
date from the gamekeeper. If a proposal of the kind I have adumbrated is
made I myself shall be disposed to object
to "beater" and to "rabbit trapper.'
Why rabbit trapper and leave out rat
trapper and fox trapper I do not know.
But these points I do not think really
arise to-day.
I have done for the moment with the
first paragraph of this incomprehensible
schedule. I pass to paragraph 2, which
relates entirely to chauffeurs in domestic
service. I understand from the report
of the Statutory Committee on Insurance
dated 23rd October of last year, of
which by the courtesy of an officer of the
Department I was furnished with a copy,
there are supposed to be 50,000 chauffeurs
in private service in Great Britain. It
is one of the points I will put to the right
hon. Gentleman, because I cannot
believe that before this regulation was
foisted upon an unexpectant world some
inquiry was not made as to the number
of chauffeurs in Northern Ireland. I
should like to believe that some steps
were taken in Northern Ireland to consult the chauffeurs as to whether they
would like to be insured. In England
the Ministry sent out a circular. They
went beyond that. They had a broadcast talk to let the employers and
employees know the proposals with
regard to insurance. Out of 50,000
chauffeurs something less than 120
answers were received expressed in
Unemployment Regulations-
Motion to Annul
31 MARCH 1938
language which was found impressive,
although I find nothing impressive in it
myself, but it impressed these gentlemen
with the desire of the 120 chauffeurs to
come into insurance. That was the case
in England.
Again it seems to be assumed in this
report of 23rd October of last year that
the private chauffeur who drives a motor
car is practically interchangeable with
a lorry driver. I say he is not.
I say
the private chauffeur who also drives a
lorry is an extremely rare individual.
There may be one caught and put in a
cage opposite the ghillie who was mentioned the other afternoon. However,
the points that were made by some chauffeurs who replied to the Ministry were
to some extent very good. It was pointed
out that the private chauffeur was dependant for continuous service not only
on ability and integrity and so on, but
on the chance of the death of his master
or some change in his circumstances. He
might be thrown out of employment after
many years' service. Stress was also laid
on the fact that many chauffeurs in the
country occupied a house. It might be a
gate lodge. I think a number of wealthy
employers have gate lodges. But the
point was made that he stood the chance
of losing his job, but not only his job
but his house, and that would put him
to considerable expense. On the other
hand, one or two employers who replied
to the circular stated that so far as they
knew a good chauffeur was never out of
work for more than a few days at a time.
That, I believe, is also a point.
Therefore two questions arise as regards
the chauffeur. The first is-and I am not
going to presume to suggest what the
answer is whether the chauffeur should
be insured in the same way as the groom,
gardener, and so on. On that I think
the House will form its own opinion and
would rather resent anything in the way
of suggestion. The second question is,
if the chauffeur is to be insured, is he
to be insured agriculturally or industrially. That is, is he to come in with
his colleagues, the
groom and the
gardener and so on, on a 4d stamp,
or is he to come in with the lorry driver
of the local coal merchant or the motorcar driver of the local doctor, who are
and always have been in the industrial
class. That is a very different point.
The Regulation, made apparently without
consultation with the Northern Ireland
chauffeur, brings him into the industrial
class, and I cannot myself but think that
he ought to have come in in the 4d
class with the other domestic servants,
using the expression "domestic servant"
in the wider sense, which alone makes
sense not only of the Regulation, but of
Tuesday's Act of Parliament.
If the man is insured at all he ought
to come in with the other servants;
otherwise anomalies will be endless. It
is rather ridiculous, having regard to the
comparatively small number of private
chauffeurs in Northern Ireland, to
gravely suggest sending down an inspector from the Ministry to inquire how
many hours a day a chauffeur employs
himself driving a car, and how many
hours he employs himself in the garden
or the stable, and in the thousand and
one jobs that nine chauffeurs out of every
ten do in effect perform. If the man is
insured in the industrial class, of course
he and his employer each pay 9d a week
in stamps. The chauffeur gets 17s a week
for himself if he is over 21, and 15s for
his wife. If, on the other hand, he pays
a 41d stamp he gets 14s for himself and
12s 6d for his wife. I am going to
suggest that having regard to the comparative security of the chauffeur's employment the difference in possible
benefit between 17s and 14s is not worth
the extra 4d stamp he has to pay.
As regards the insurance of the
chauffeur, I should like to take this
opportunity of explaining that when I
spoke the other day of the inclusion of
additional classes in insurance being a
step on the downward path, with which
the right hon. Gentleman, the Leader of
the House, did not agree-he said it was
a step on the upward path-I do not
think he quite appreciated what I meant.
What I did mean was that the step on the
downward path was bringing new classes
into collective insurance, which, I believe,
is entirely wrong. I believe the whole
system is wrong. I should prefer something more in the nature of a private
scheme rather than a collective one,
something that would encourage the
insured person to keep his hand out of
the
common kitty and SO avoid the
demoralisation we have spoken of.
But I do not think the chauffeur is
in any more danger in that respect than
other men; that is the honest chauffeur.
Unemployment Regulations-
Motion to Annu
THE SENATE
Mr. Leslie]
but if you go into the country villages.
you get to know of a relatively large
number of people who once had a licence
to drive a car and hold themselves out
as chauffeurs. Some, perhaps, for a
short time have an old motor-car and
take passengers, but there are a great
number of these people who ought not
to be encouraged, and whom I do not
want to be insured. If you take the
thing up generally you cannot help it,
but I do think we ought to keep to the
agriculutral scale, which includes the
groom and the stable man, whose work
the chauffeur often shares.
Again, there is that question of the
lodge-keeper. The chauffeur frequently
occupies a lodge, and if he does he sleeps
there at night, or should do, and if
anybody comes in the middle of the night
that man opens the gate. He is really
a lodge-keeper for more hours of the day
than he is driving a car. That is one
of the small absurdities I detect in the
Regulation before us. I do suggest that
if we put the chauffeur in the industrial
class we shall bring in most hopeless
anomalies, and I further suggest there
are practically no chauffeurs in Northern
Ireland who are interchangeable with
lorry drivers, or who do not perform
some other household duty in the
domestic establishment in addition to
their duty as chauffeur.
I am
If we reject this Regulation and leave
a new one to be brought in we shall be
told, I suppose, that we are falling
out of step, because we are not following
wholesale, with ten days' consideration,
what has been done in England.
not in the least affected by that. I agree
with the main principle of the step-bystep policy in social services, although I
would prefer to see a system adopted in
Great Britain such as I indicated, something more individual than is at present
the case. I quite agree, apart from
that, with the step-by-step policy, but I
cannot agree that step by step means that
all the marchers are to wear toe-caps on
their boots of exactly the same pattern,
or that it is essential that they should
start at exactly the same moment.
If any delay__occurs as a result of
rejecting these Regulations we may remember that that can be adjusted
between April and July, say in thirteen
weeks, and the people who will come
into insurance have been waiting, not
thirteen weeks, but twice that number of
years to get into insurance at all, and I
decline to believe that the delay is of
any great consequence. I do think,
and I repeat again, that we ought not
to have been hurried over this matter,
and not so much that Parliament should
have been hurried as the people themselves. Steps should have been taken to
obtain information over a wide area, and
Northern Ireland is not so very large.
too
It will be observed from the reports of
the House of Commons at Westminster
that even that House of Commons is
getting a little restive on this matter of
regulations for unemployment insurance.
Remarks were made last week which would
not have been made a few years ago, and
generally one was given the impression.
that the House of Commons in England
thought there had been just a little bit.
much regulation by Ministries.
Accordingly I invite the House to annul
this Regulation, and I do so on those four
main grounds-firstly, insufficiency of
notice; secondly, the lack of definition.
of those words "domestic servant," and
so on, to which I have alluded; thirdly,
the lack of clearness in the form of words
used, particularly as regards the matter of
paragraph (iii) with which I dealt at some
little length; and, finally, as regards the
inclusion of the chauffeur, not in insurance altogether, but in the industrial
class instead of in the agricultural class
with his fellow servants. I think I have
to formally move, "That the Regulation
be anulled," and I do so.
Captain PERCEVAL- MAXWELL:
Since the last day we discussed the
Unemployment Insurance Act I have
had an opportunity of reading the
remarks which the Leader of the House
made in the course of the debate. I
must say that I was very much disturbed
by the attitude which seemed to underlie
those remarks. If I understood the
right hon. Gentleman correctly—and he
will no doubt correct me if I am wrong
-he assumed that in matters relating
to the construction of Acts of Parliament
the decision of the High Court was final.
If the right hon. Gentleman had been
speaking to a judge or to a member of
the profession of which he is a distinguished ornament, I should have entirely agreed with him, but under the
circumstances in which he made that
101 Unemployment Regulations-

31 MARCH 1938
Motion to Annul
a
statement I am afraid I cannot do so.
You always have the uncomfortable feeling, when advising client, that
although you think you ore interpreting
the law correctly, some judge may conveniently upset your opinion.
But to come before a House of Parliament and say to that House that as
regards a particular Act which it is considering, and which it has the great and
responsible duty of criticising, that the
responsible authority to decide what a
particular word in that Act means is the
High Court of Justice, is to my mind
the very antithesis of the idea of Parliamentary Government. We are not a
judicial body; we are a legislative body,
and it is right and important that we
should put our legislation into words that
do not require these complicated and
rather obstruse constructions that have to
be put on badly drafted legislation by
courts of law. I feel that this is a
matter to which this House should give
very serious consideration.
We are most grateful to the British
Government for providing us with a
place to work in, so large and so expensive and so very well designed, and we
appreciate that this House has been built
with a magnificent view of the surrounding country, but we do not come here
for the good of our health, or to admire
the scenic beauties that are to be seen
from this building. We have a serious
duty to perform, under our Constitution,
to the people of this country. And to
be told that we, a sovereign authority;
we, the Parliament of Northern Ireland,
are to take as a definition of the word
that is used in our legislation what some
puisne judge may have stated that
seems to me to be a most serious reflection
on this House. And I would say without any hesitation that no Member of
this House and no Member in another
place who takes his duty of legislation
with the seriousness which he should
take it would regard that as any excuse.
The fact that a judge made a certain
dictum on the meaning of a word is no
excuse for not having that word properly
defined. I think the dictum of a learned
judge, possibly based on the thought of
the moment, who had to try to make
sense of an Act which is very difficult for
anyone with an ordinary knowledge of
English to make sense of, should not
necessarily be regarded as binding
this House. I think his deupon
finition of domestic service, although
to
in regard
right in
the particular
circumstances in which he considered
it, is not a
definition which any
other human being who was not trying
to apply this word to a very difficult
Act of Parliament would have adopted.
I do not think that definition of the
word domestic is the definition that any
ordinary employer of labour, or that any
ordinary person who understands the use
of the English language in a nontechnical sense, would have applied. I
do not think that is the meaning the
ordinary person would put on the word
domestic.
I shall continue to press upon this
House the necessity, where it is necessary
to use a word which is in common use
in the English language in a purely
technical sense, of giving a definition so
that it would be clear, without expensive
reference to a court, that that word is
not being used in the ordinary meaning,
but is used in a special or technical
sense. I hope when regulations are
being drafted these expressions will be
put in proper language that can be understood by the ordinary person who has
to read the regulations. There is some
excuse for using technical expressions in
an Act of Parliament largely used by
lawyers and in technical form. But these
regulations are on an entirely different.
footing. These regulations are regulations that the ordinary man in the street
reads, or is supposed to read. If he
does not understand them it puts the
subject to very great expense, and puts
everyone to great confusion. I have had
in the past long correspondence with
the Ministry as to whether a particular
type of servant doing particular
work was in one category or another. The
whole thing could be avoided if care
was taken in the expression used when
drafting.
There is another expression which I
have looked at backwards and forwards
and sideways, and every other way you.
can look at an expression, and I am completely unable to understand it. It is
Class 2. Perhaps the right hon. Gentleman, the Leader of the House, can explain it, but I cannot understand what
is meant by the words, "persons employed in domestic service in driving or
attending to a mechanically propelled
vehicle where the employer is neither in
103 Unemployment Regulations-

THE SENATE
Motion to Annul
[Captain Perceval-Maxwell]
a trade or business carried on for the
purpose of gain nor in horticulture.
There may be some reason for it, but I
cannot understand why the words "nor
in horticulture" have been used. Is it
suggested that the word horticulture
must necessarily mean a market gardener.
Perhaps the right hon. Gentleman will
explain that. It seems to me to except
one occupation. Why should it except
horticulture alone? Why pick on the
apparently innocent occupation of a
gardener. I cannot understand it at all.
Perhaps the right hon. Gentleman will
explain? I do impress on those responsible that we must do something to make
these regulations clear to the man in
the street.
Sir JOSEPH DAVISON: I would
like some information with regard to the
Schedule. I confess I cannot understand
it. It starts with the class of persons
employed in excepted employment to be
included amongst the classes of persons
employed in insurable employment, and
then goes to persons employed in
domestic service in any trade or business
not a trade of business carried on for
the purpose of gain, except persons so
employed in horticulture. It goes on to
enumerate a long list of occupations and
finishes up with that Clause 2: "Persons
employed in domestic service in driving
or attending to a mechanically propelled
vehicle where the employment is neither
in a trade or business carried on for the
purpose of gain nor in horticulture." I
would like to know are all these persons
exempted from being insured. If they
are exempt why cannot they be plainly
told that they are exempt or excluded?
I confess I cannot understand it, and I
hope some information will be given.
Otherwise I may have to support the
Motion before the House.
Mr. ROBB: May I say at once that I
agree very heartily with the opinion expressed that it takes a long time to
understand what these regulations are.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) Frankly,
I may say that I spent the whole weekend trying to understand what they
meant, and even at the end of that time,
not being familiar with unemployment
insurance to start with, I had to go and
get help. But I may say that I am satisfied now that the result of the Order
that this House is asked to annul to-day
--and I hope they will not assent-is
this, that there will be brought into
insurance all indoor servants except those
in private houses, and those attending
residents in educational establishments,
and of outdoor servants there will be
brought into the general scheme of insurance private chauffeurs. That is the
result of all this wording. I do not
know whether the House is prepared to
accept that. I hope they are. But I
may say that this is the result of the
passing of this Regulation or the giving
of sanction. I think it is a result to
which the House
House will probably be
inclined to agree. That is the general
purpose of what we are doing to-daysanctioning the bringing into insurance
of indoor servants except those in private
houses and except those attending
residents in educational establishments
and private
private chauffeurs on
outdoor
service.
To take first what was said by Senator
Leslie, he brought his remarks under
four general heads. First of all, lack of
notice. Well, I really think, considering
the amount of matter he has been able
to produce in the short time he has had
to consider these regulations, that-he
may have been hurried, but at any rate
he has done his job. And I am thankful to him for the energy and industry
he has displayed. I do not think on the
question of lack of notice the House
would be justified in saying we will not go
on with this to-day. His next objection
was that there was no definition, and
Senator Perceval-Maxwell made virtually
the same point, that there was lack
of clearness and definition. Captain
Perceval-Maxwell objected entirely to
any theory that the legislation of this
House, a sovereign assembly, is to be
governed by what is decided in the
courts, and he wished the House to go
on to give definitions which no judge
could read in any but one way. The
first point he made is this. That this is a
sovereign assembly and is not governed by
the decisions of judges. Of course it is
a sovereign assembly, but when this
sovereign assembly says certain thingswhen it says that A.B. commits an
offence if he does this or that, and A.B.
is brought up before a magistrate, then
the person who has to decide what the
Act of Parliament means is the judge.
105 Unemployment Regulations-

31 MARCH 1938
Motion to Annul
This
House may say that Captain
Perceval-Maxwell is guilty of the offence
of driving a motor-car to the danger of
the public. But when he is brought
before the magistrates or on appeal
before the King's Bench Division, the
who
whether what he has
person
says
done is to the danger of the pubic or
not is the judge. The mere reference in
this House to what the judges have or
have not decided upon an Act of Parliament passed by this House, with the
concurrence of another place, in no way
impinges upon the sovereign power of
this House. It only means that this
House has not said it in language that
every person must interpret in the same
way.
Captain
PERCEVAL-MAXWELL:
May I interrupt? I agree with what he
says. They are the words I have used.
a
in
Mr. ROBB: This is sovereign
assembly and Captain Perceval-Maxwell
wants us to say what we mean
language which nobody could interpret
otherwise. That is an impossibility.
Sir JAMES WILTON: Where would
the courts be?
Mr. ROBB: Where would I be?
Where would we all be? The classic
example of endeavouring to do that was
the Workmen's Compensation Act of
1897. Mr. Chamberlain, introducing it
into the House of Commons, boasted that
this Bill was drafted not by the Parliamentary draughtsman, but was put into
the hand of business men who would say
what they meant. That would show the
Parliamentary draughtsman what they
were. Accordingly the Bill was passed,
and there has never been an Act so
fruitful in litigation as that. The
difficulties of Parliamentary draughtsmanship are not appreciated by the
general public. Might I assure this
House that it is no easier to draft an
Act of Parliament than it is to draft a
deed, and my learned friends will know
that it takes years of experience to be
able to put what seems to A.B. the most
simple matter in the world into a form
that will be accepted by all persons as
meaning what it is intended to say. The
difficulties that have arisen and have
necessitated the Act we passed on Tuesday and this Regulation have arisen, to
a large extent, owing to the fact that
in the Act of 1936 the words "domestic
service" were used. That seemed a perfectly simple thing, but cases came before
the courts and the courts decided that
different people came within the category
of domestic servant and different people
did not. Take this building itself. If
you come in at the side door you meet a
messenger. That messenger is not,
according to the decision of the court,
in domestic service, but if you go upstairs and get into the lift, the liftman
the courts have held, is in domestic service, and so with various other classes.
One may disagree with the decisions that
have been given, but we must accept
them and legislate accordingly.
Captain
I disagree.
PERCEVAL-MAXWELL:
Mr. ROBB: Then may I ask the hon.
and learned Member what form of words
he would use to get over the difficulty?
Captain PERCEVAL
-
MAXWELL:
I suggest that if the Government want
to adopt the learned judge's interpretation they should put it into the Act as
a definition.
Mr. ROBB: But the hon. and learned
Member said he disagreed with that
judgment, and another judge might give
a different definition. It is one of the
difficulties of drafting documents that
words are not precise in their meaning,
and it was only after an accumulation
of decisions of the courts that the matter
was referred to the Statutory Insurance
Committee. That committee made these
recommendations which were adopted in
England, and our Ministry has also
adopted them. I do not think that this
Regulation, or the Act that was passed
on Tuesday, will abolish anomalies.
They will not, but they will remove some
anomalies that have already been discovered.
a
Another objection that Senator Leslie
made was that there had not been
canvas of employers and employees in Northern Ireland as regards
their inclusion or non-inclusion, but
he also stated that there are 50,000
chauffeurs in England, and that in
response to inquiries and a broadcast appeal they got 120 replies. If you
are going to get only 120 replies out of
50,000 queries I do not know that your
canvas will be worth very much. Of the
Unemployinent Regulations-
Motion to Annul
THE SENATE
[Mr. Robb]
replies that were received the great
majority approved of the inclusion of
chauffeurs in insurance. That opinion
was the recommendation of the Statutory
Committee and was adopted in England,
and we propose to follow it here.
The hon. and learned Member asked,
why include chauffeurs in the ordinary
scheme of insurance and not in the agricultural scheme? A test that was made
in England suggested that the number of
motor drivers in England who were
private chauffeurs was a very small percentage of the total number, and the
Committee recommended that on the
whole it was advisable that the private
chauffeur should be included, not in the
agricultural scheme but in the ordinary
scheme, on the ground that his employment was more akin to the employment
of a motor-lorry driver than it was to
an agricultural labourer.
With regard to the point Senator
Leslie made as to a mixed employment,
if a man is partly a chauffeur and partly
employed as a gardener, he and his employer can agree that he should be
insured under the agricultural scheme
and not under the ordinary scheme. If
they can both agree he can be put into
either class. There is one limitation,
and that is, that if for a continuous
period of thirteen weeks he is employed
only in one or other of the different
classes of employment then he falls
automatically into the scheme applicable
to that class of employment. I think it
is not an unfair or unjust recommendation that the private chauffeur should be
in the ordinary scheme of insurance.
as a
If we take Northern Ireland
whole the private chauffeur of the
doctor is automatically in the ordinary
insurance scheme, and if we take all
the others I venture to think that the
number who are employed by dwellers in
towns and cities far exceed the number
employed by those who dwell outside the
towns and cities. Surely it is not unreasonable to ask that that class should
not be split, and that when the majority
of them are, in fact, not living in the
country they should be brought into the
ordinary scheme of insurance.
Captain Perceval-Maxwell asked for an
explanation of the words, "nor in horticulture," at the end of paragraph (2)
of the Schedule. What was in mind was
this. You might have a man whose
main employment was driving a motor
lawn-mower, and it is solely to cover that
man that these words are put in.
Mr. LESLIE: Is that man, the Atco
man, to come in or stay out?
Mr. ROBB: He will be excepted
from-
Mr. LESLIE: From insurance?
Mr. ROBB: Not from insurance altogether, because he will be in the agricultural scheme. He will be in the ordinary
scheme if he drives a motor vehicle, but
if he drives a motor lawn-mower he will
come into the agricultural scheme.
Captain
PERCEVAL-MAXWELL :
Is the right hon. Gentleman sure that
cutting a lawn is horticulture?
Mr. ROBB: If that is not horticulture
he is not affected, but that is the man
these words are intended to cover. Some
judge or other will perhaps decide that
point. I regret as much as anybody can
regret the form that legislation on this
matter has taken. I regret it from the
general public's point of view, and I
regret it very much from my own personal point of view, because I had to
spend such a long time trying to understand what it meant. But the difficulty
arises from the fact that it seems impossible to arrive at definitions that are all
comprehensive, and, secondly, it is only
by a wilderness of special single instances
that one can ultimaely arrive at what is
intended. I have explained to the
House as clearly as I can the effect of
this Regulation, and I ask the House to
sanction it.
Sir JOSEPH DAVISON: Will the
right hon. Gentleman tell the House
whether the classes of persons referred
to in paragraphs (2) and (3) of the
Schedule are or are not excepted from
insurance?
Mr. ROBB: The effect of the Regulation is this. A person employed in
horticulture will come in under the agricultural scheme, and also those mentioned
in paragraph (2). Those in paragraph
(3), if they are in private houses or
in residence in educational establishments, will not be insurable; otherwise
they will be insurable.
be insurable. That is the
Unemployment Regulations-
Motion to Annul
31 MARCH 1938
result of it all. That it is clear I do not
pretend to say, but that is what it
amounts to in the end.
Mr. LESLIE: I want to reply only
for a few moments. The right hon.
Gentleman was kind enough to deal
rather briefly, and I am afraid I
must say not very convincingly, with the
four points on which I based my Motion:
As regards the question of notice, he
rather suggested that my point was that
we had not got sufficient notice of the
Regulation. But I laid far greater stress
on the point that the people immediately
concerned in the proposed Regulation
had had no notice at all.
He went on to say that the canvass in
England cannot seriously be considered
to have been very successful, since out of
50,000 only 120 responded. Therefore,
apparently, it was thought unnecessary
to canvass in this country. That may have
been a very sound decision, but it does
not seem to me to be any ground for
deciding how that canvass would have
gone if in fact it had been held, which
was what was not done.
As regards the question of definition,
what the hon. and gallant Member
opposite (Captain Perceval-Maxwell) and
I complained of was the absence of
definition. The reply of the right hon.
Gentleman made one think of the oldfashioned conveyancer who, when presented with an Act of Parliament said,
"Take it away.
I do not think much
of that Act of Parliament. Bad law."
I think he forgot that in the past
such expediencies had been adopted as
using such an expression as "beneficial
owner" in the Conveyancing Act of
1881, and carefully telling you what
exactly that meant; how it should be
Barclay, Mr. William
Boyle, Major J. C
Davison, Sir Joseph
McCullagh, Sir Crawford
Wilton, Sir James
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
held to imply things it does imply, with
which I will not weary the House.
Then again, as regards the chauffeur,
the right hon. Gentleman dealt particularly, I think, with the question as
to whether he should be in the 9d or
4d class. I am afraid he did not give
us any figures. He did not suggest, as
is suggested in England, that the
majority of private chauffeurs are also
drivers of lorries, but he did suggest that
there were more private chauffeurs in
the towns than in the country. And he
therefore, I very much regret, gave justification for the complaint which is so
often heard in Northern Ireland that
the country districts are dragged at the
heels of Belfast. Then he came to the
lawn-mower, which seems to occupy a
half-way house between the lorry and
the private car, because if the lawnmower is big enough it is quite as hard
to drive as an ordinary lorry. I am
afraid we leave, if we do not put the
chauffeur with the stableman, a greater
anomaly than we avoid. With regard to
interchange of occupation you cannot
really have an inspector of the Ministry
in every house.
CONTENT
Mr. ROBB: May I say one word?
May I remind the hon. Senator that it
took about 400 years to reach the meaning of beneficial owner.
Mr. LESLIE: But it was reached 50
years ago or more.

Question to Minister.
LAW RELATING TO CHILDREN
AND YOUNG PERSONS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Captain
Perceval-Maxwell:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether the Government propose to
introduce legislation to amend the law
relating to children and young persons
and to assimilate this law to the law
in force in England; and, if so, when
this legislation will be introduced.
Captain PERCEVAL- MAXWELL:
The object of my question is to raise this
matter in connection with a Committee
which has been sitting for the last two
years. When the Summary Jurisdiction
Bill was passed it was, I think, under-
Oral Answers
Anglo-Eire Negotiations
26 APRIL 1938
stood both in this House and in another
place that if there was a flaw in that
Bill it was that in matters of summary
jurisdiction it eliminated the lay element.
Some Members of this House, and some
Members of another place, regarded that
as a very serious defect, and others regarded it as particularly serious in
regard to the administration of the law
relating to children. The Government
appointed this Committee to inquire as
to how far the law relating to children
should be assimilated to the law in England, and the result of the work of that
Committtee has been before this House
in the form of an official paper.
I think it is obvious from the report
of that Committee that the law in
relation to young persons and children
in this country is very much behind the
law relating to those persons in England. It is a fact which can be discovered from reading the report that
the Probation of Offenders Act of 1907
has never been properly administered in
this country, and one of the serious
charges which has been made against the
Probation of Offenders Act is that it is
a let-off. I agree that that Act is a
let-off if you regard binding over as the
only part of probation. We took the
view, and I think rightly, that probation should be binding over with
supervision. If that is done, as it is
done in England, I believe that the
law in this country cannot be charged
with having done nothing to prevent
the growth of juvenile crime which is
is so prevalent in this country and
in England.
In England it may be said that the
growth of juvenile crime is only
apparent, because under the new
Children Act there has been more of a
tendency to bring young offenders
before the courts than there was before,
and there is more confidence now that
those offenders will be dealt with. That
excuse for the growth of juvenile crime.
in this country cannot be urged, for
there is no adequate method in this
country of dealing with juvenile crime.
The methods which there are are out of
date, and ought to be superseded by
methods which have proved successful in
England and in other parts of the
world.
Not only is the question of juvenile
crime of paramount importance, but there
are other matters relating to the protection of child life, and even if Members of this House regard the report as
being something of an innovation in
regard to the judicial matters referred
to, I think nobody who has studied the
report carefully will be content with the
methods now in existence for protecting
children from contamination and other
sources of hardship. I refer specifically
to the provisions relating to the adoption
of children and the nursing out of
children, which in this country are very
far from satisfactory. I will not weary
the House by further discussing the
report, but I feel that it is only due to
the House that I should preface my
question with an explanation of why I
put it down.
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House):
The Government view with great sympathy the efforts made by various classes
of persons to prevent the growth of
juvenile crime, and for the protection.
of child life. The report of the Committee appointed by the Minister of
Home Affairs to inquire into the existing
provisions of the law in Northern Ireland for the protection and welfare of
the young and the treatment of young
offenders generally was recently issued
and the various recommendations contained therein are now receiving careful
consideration. The hon. and gallant
Senator will realise that legislation in
an important matter of this sort needs
very careful preparation and a great
deal of preliminary work. While I am
unable to give any definite statement as
to the date at which a Bill may be laid
before Parliament, I can assure him
that it is the intention of the Government to deal with this subject at as early
a date as may be found possible, and
as fully as the circumstances require.
ANGLO-EIRE NEGOTIATIONS.
Position of Northern Ireland.
Mr. LESLIE: May 1, by leave, ask
the right hon. Gentleman, the Leader
of the House, whether he has any statement that he can make to the House
respecting the arrangements recently
made by the Imperial Government, after
consultation with our Ministers, and regarding matters which affect Northern
Ireland.
Anglo-Eire Negotiations 116
Anglo-Eire Negotiations
THE SENATE
Mr. ROBB: In another place the
Prime Minister is making a statement
of which I have obtained a copy, and
which, by leave of the House, I will now
read. It is as follows:-
His Majesty's Government, with a
view to promoting harmonious relations
and facilitating trade between the
United Kingdom and Eire, have entered
into an Agreement, the terms of which
are embodied in a White Paper which
is being presented at Westminster. The
Agreement deals with matters which are
not within the competence of the Government of Northern Ireland and it will
be understood that we are therefore not
a party to it.
The negotiations, however, which led
up to the Agreement afforded the United
Kingdom Government and our Government opportunities of conferring upon
certain questions which had been causing us much concern in recent years.
That Government, recognising the
special difficulties of Northern Ireland
in respect of trade with Eire resulting
from the latter's tariff policy, has agreed
to make certain adjustments in its financial relations with Northern Ireland
which will, in other directions, provide
benefits for the Province. The Agreement provides for immediate modifications of duty on certain goods of special
interest to Northern Ireland, and the
United Kingdom Government have
undertaken that, when decisions
being taken as to the order in which
various goods are to be considered by
the Prices Commission, goods in which
we are interested will be among the first
to be considered.
are
I trust, too, that Northern Ireland
may reap advantages which are not
bounded by the four walls of the Agreement itself. Nevertheless, should advantages fail to accrue from the Trade
Agreement with Eire, the United
Kingdom Government will be prepared
to consider sympathetically any request
made by the Government of Northern
Ireland for dealing with the matter
under the provision of the Agreement
as to consultation between the Governments of the United Kingdom and Eire,
the parties to the Agreement.
I now turn to the financial matters
which have arisen out of discussions
which have taken place between our
Government and the Government of the
United Kingdom. They fall under four
heads, namely:
Firstly, Agricultural Subsidies;
Secondly, Expenditure on Unemployment Insurance and Assistance;
Thirdly, The General Financial
Relations between the two Exchequers;
Fourthly, Northern Ireland's
of rearmament work.
-
share
agricultural
On the first point
subsidies Northern Ireland farmers
have in the past shared in various agricultural subsidies such as the beef and
milk subsidies, the land fertility scheme
embracing lime and basic slag, and so on.
But no general agreement has hitherto
been reached as to whether, in principle,
such agricultural subsidies should be
borne on United Kingdom Votes or on
our Votes. I am glad, therefore, to be
able to state that an amicable agreement
has been arrived at which ensures that
where agricultural subsidies are granted
in Great Britain, and circumstances are
such as to justify corresponding subsidies
in Northern Ireland, no objection will
be raised to the cost being borne on
United Kingdom Votes.
Turning now to the Unemployment
Reinsurance Agreement, it will be recalled that the United Kingdom Government recoup to us 75 per cent. of the expenditure incurred by our Exchequer in
maintaining our Unemployment Insurance and
Assistance
Unemployment
Fund in parity with the funds in Great
Britain. It was an understanding, however, confirmed in a letter to the then
Chancellor of the Exchequer that if the
United Kingdom contribution in any year
exceeded one million pounds the United
Kingdom Government would be entitled
to reopen on its merits the question of
the amount of the contribution. In the
year just closed, owing in the main to the
considerable balance on the right side in
the British Insurance Fund, their contribution to us in the way of parity payment reached the sum of nearly
£1,700,000, and the understanding to
which I have referred caused apprehension to our Minister of Finance.
The full payment was generously conceded and the United Kingdom Govern
Anglo-Eire Negotiations
Anglo-Eire Negotiations
26 APRIL 1938
ment have now definitely agreed that so
long as the Trade Agreement with Eire
is in force the United Kingdom Government will not exercise their right to reopen the question of the amount of the
contribution of the United Kingdom
Exchequer under the Northern Ireland
Unemployment Insurance Agreement of
the 29th April, 1935, if such a contribution exceeds one million pounds.
I come now to our general Exchequer
relations. A difficult situation would
arise if at some future time our revenue
were to prove insufficient to maintain our
social services at the United Kingdom
level. Such a situation might eventuate
as a result of reductions of taxation in the
United Kingdom or the expansion of
social services.
The United Kingdom
Government has recognised this possible
difficulty and has agreed that it would be
equitable that means should be found to
make good this deficit in such a way as
to ensure that Northern Ireland should
be in a financial position to continue to
enjoy the same social services and
have the same standards
as Great
Britain.
The fourth matter to which I have
to refer is the question of Northern
Ireland's share in the British rearmament programme. We have, to some
extent, benefited from such work in
recent years, mainly in the construction
of warships. Since April 1st, 1936,
orders have been placed by the Admiralty
for work exceeding £3,600,000 in value.
In the same period orders to the value
of £2,067,000 have been received from
the Air Ministry as well as considerable
orders from the War Office. We have
not ceased to press on the United
Kingdom Government our view that even
these substantial orders do not represent an adequate share of armament
work, regard especially being had to our
serious unemployment problem. I am
glad to say that the United Kingdom
Government recognises our position and
further rearmament orders, subject to
satisfactory tenders being received, are in
prospect, and I have reason to hope that,
as a result of our representations, substantial orders will be placed in Northern
Ireland by the various Defence Departments.
We have further pressed for the
establishment of a Government factory in

Northern Ireland and, in so doing, we
have not overlooked
Londonderry.
the claims
of
Apart from the matters to which I have
referred, I have further satisfactory
information to impart to the House.
Under the Coal-Cattle Pact which has
been in operation between the United
Kingdom and the Irish Free State during
the past year or so, there has been a
detailed schedule of the f.o.b. prices of
United Kingdom coal to be charged to
Free State buyers. These prices were
agreed between the United Kingdom
coal owners and the Free State importers, and one of the conditions of
application of the original schedule was
that Free State buyers of United Kingdom coal should receive not less favourable treatment than that accorded to
buyers in Northern Ireland.
No such assurance was enjoyed by
Northern Ireland buyers, and difficulty
was experienced in the past in securing
for Northern Ireland the acceptance of
the principle that the price charged in
respect of shipments of coal to Northern
Ireland should not exceed the price of
comparable coal shipped to the Irish
Free State. Owing to the persistent
efforts of our Minister of Commerce, this
difficulty has now been removed, and I
am glad to say that an arrangement has
been reached whereunder the schedule
prices will apply uniformly to Ireland,
irrespective of whether the destination of
the coal is Northern Ireland or Eire.
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: May I ask
the Leader of the House whether an
opportunity will be given for the House
to discuss this statement. I am sure he
will appreciate that it is scarcely possible
to discuss a statement dealing with such
complicated matters at a moment's
notice, and I think the House would
desire a further opportunity to consider
and discuss that statement.
Mr. ROBB: Should the House wish
to discuss this matter, it being clearly
understood that the discussion relates only
to any agreement entered into between
our Government and the United Kingdom Government, and not between the
Government of Eire and the Government
of the United Kingdom-
119 Anglo-Eire Negotiations
Anglo-Eire Negotiations 120
THE SENATE
Captain PERCEVAL - MAXWELL:
On a point of order, is it in order for
the Leader of the House to indicate to
Mr. Speaker what rules shall apply to
debate?
Mr. ROBB: I do not exactly know the
point the hon. and gallant Member is
making whether I am being reproved
for suggesting that it would be out of
order to discuss certain aspects of this
matter in this House. But I respectfully submit that it would not be in order,
and that you, Mr. Speaker, would be
bound so to rule, to discuss any agreement come to between the United Kingdom Government and the Government of
Eire to which we were not a party. As
regards the Agreement come to between
our Government and the Government of
the United Kingdom, should the House
wish for an opportunity to discuss that
it will be afforded.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I might
venture to remind the House that the
situation to-day is somewhat different to
what it was in the early days when this
Parliament was established. It will be
within the recollection of some of us that
at the period before the Free State came
into existence debates did take place in
this House, and I think in another place
also, dealing with the general relations
betwen Great Britain and Ireland, but
that was before the establishment of the
Free State, and, of course, equally before
the establishment of Eire. Therefore, it
would follow from the change in circumstances that a new situation arises; that
is to say, we have to have more regard
now than we had then to the question
whether matters under consideration are
matters for discussion at Westminster,
where we are represented, or in this
House.
Captain PERCEVAL - MAXWELL:
While I do not wish to increase any
tension that may have arisen on this
question of the Agreement between Eire
and Great Britain, it does appear that in
the course of discussion certain consideration, whether directly or indirectly, has
been given to our Government collaterally
to the Agreement which has been made
between Eire and Great Britain. It also
appears from the statement that appeared
in the daily papers this morning that
certain trade interests may be affected by
this Agreement. I am not going to
adopt the principle of the right hon.
Gentleman, the Leader of the House, of
informing the Speaker of this House
whether that is or is not in order, because
I am confident that you, Mr. Speaker,
are quite capable of informing the House,
and it is not the duty of private Members to inform you what you are to do
and what you are not to do.
If the question does arise as to whether
the consideration which may off-set any
disadvantage that
this
arises under
Agreement is adequate, and whether steps.
should or could be taken by the Government of this country, independently of
the Government of Great Britain, to offset the disadvantages which may have
arisen, I do not wish to commit myself
as to whether there are such possibilities,
and whether it would be possible for our
Government to take action within its
jurisdiction and within its powers to offset this disadvantage. But I submit with
all humility that it is within the powers
of this House to discuss this matter, and
if it appears to any Member of this House
that the Government can off-set this disadvantage, it is his duty to point the
matter out, and it is the duty of the
Government to say whether they are able
and willing to do that.
Sir JAMES WILTON: Do I understand that the hon. and gallant Member
visualises that it would be advisable that
this Government should take separate and
individual action, apart altogether from
the Imperial Government? Perhaps I
misunderstood him.
Captain PERCEVAL - MAXWELL:
I do not wish to commit myself, but an
hon. Member of this House asked for time
to discuss this statement. I cannot
possibly tell what is in his mind, but if
he has any suggestion to make which, in
the view of Mr. Speaker, is proper to be
brought before this House, I think he
ought to be allowed to make it.
Sir JAMES WILTON: It rather
occurred to me that in the past we have
been very jealous of our position as an
integral part of the United Kingdom, and
I would view with a great deal of apprehension any suggestion that our Government should take any action as a separate
unit in a matter of this kind.
Blind Persons Bill-
Second Reading
26 APRIL 1938
Blind Persons Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: This is a small Bill which
has two main objects. The first is to reduce the age at which a blind person shall
become entitled to an old age pension
from fifty years to forty years, and the
second is to require that any assistance
other than institutional relief needed by
a blind person shall be given by the local
authorities and not by boards of
guardians.
With regard to the first main point,
it is proposed to reduce the age at which
a blind person may become entitled to
an old age pension from fifty years to
forty years. I may be very well asked,
why forty, why not thirty? At any rate,
it is a step in the right direction, and one
of the considerations that have guided the
framers of this legislation in fixing on
forty years is this; that blind persons of
forty years do seem, from experience, to
have an opportunity of earning, if not
the whole of their own living, at any
rate some part of it. But there seems
to arrive a point where, after attaining
forty, the chance of that person either
getting new employment or continuing
his existing employment materially
lessens, if it does not entirely disappear.
Therefore forty years was decided upon
as a suitable point at which to make this
experiment in the extension of benefits
to blind persons.
With regard to the second Clause, relief
can be given to needy blind persons under
our present law by two classes of authorities, boards of guardians and local
authorities, and it has been pressed on
the Government both here and in the
United Kingdom that it would be a good
thing if the taint of the poor law were
entirely removed from the assistance
given to blind persons. Accordingly in
this Bill it is proposed that in the future
assistance that is to be given to blind
persons shall be given by local authorities
and no longer by poor law guardians.
Those are the two main purposes embodied in this Bill. The first Clause
deals with the lowering of the age, and
the second Clause makes a small but important improvement upon the legislation
at present in existence.
As the Blind Persons Act of 1920
stands it is the duty of every council and
county borough council to make arrangements to the satisfaction of the Ministry
of Home Affairs for promoting the welfare of blind persons ordinarily resident
within the area of the council and under
this Bill the local authority is to take
into consideration not only the needs of
the blind person himself, but also the
needs of members of the household who
are dependent upon the blind person.
That is an extension which should commend itself to the House. The new
Clause goes on to provide that in addition to providing for blind persons while
they are alive, a council may contribute
to the payment of their funeral expenses.
Clause 3 provides that Section 2 of the
principal Act should be extended in this
way. As the law stands at present a
local authority may provide for a blind
person who is an inmate of an institution,
but cases arose where a person was not
an inmate in the ordinary sense, but was
employed in an institution. It was
found that that case was not covered by
the principal Act, and accordingly Clause
3 provides that a blind person who enters
into employment in any institution, and
immediately before doing SO was
ordinarily resident in that area, shall be
deemed to be resident in that area.
Those are the provisions of this Bill, and
I commend it to the House.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise to welcome this
Bill on three points, and if I may I will
deal separately with each of the three
points. First of all, I welcome the first
Clause in so far as it brings into force a
reduction in the age at which a blind person can be qualified to receive his pension.
That Clause, so far as it goes, is all to
the good, but I am not satisfied with the
definition of "blind person" for that
purpose which, although it does not
appear in the Clause before us, does
appear in the Clause of the Old Age Pensions Act which this Clause amends.
The provision is that a blind person
must be a person so blind as to be unable
to perform any work for which eye-sight
is esential. Those words were used first
of all in the first Section of the Blind
Persons Act, 1920. They were used in
the Section, but they were not in the
Interpretation Clause of the Act, and I
cannot myself accept that definition as
applicable to any part of the 1920 Act
other than the Section in which it appears
which deals with pensions. I will refer to
Blind Persons Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Lesliej
that matter again, but it does seem to me
at the moment that the definition is too
harsh for general application. As
regards altering that definition in so far
as it is a qualification for pension, I
recognise we are helpless. It would be
entirely out of order in this House to propose an alteration there as regards pensions, because it would be indirectly increasing a charge on the public, and that
we are forbidden to do.
As the House knows, the rules on that
subject are very strict, although as regards another suggestion which would
merely make
difference
a
to the
rates and practice of municipalities
and inter-cameral practice the spirit of
the Government of Ireland Act is conless
spicuously
meticulous. At the
moment therefore I cannot suggest any
change in the definition, although it is
on those two points that discussion has
ranged not only in another place but also
in the Imperial House of Commons at
Westminster when the Act was before
them.
The second reason for which I welcome
the Bill is to be found in sub-clause (1)
(a), but I cannot help wondering whether
that sub-clause will work as it is, although
it follows the wording of the Clause in
the Bill which I understand is still before
the Parliament at Westminster. It
seems to me open to question whether we
ought not to extend the power of the
council and council of a county borough
so as to include also the council of a
borough which is not a county borough,
and even an urban district council.
There is no doubt there is something to
be said on both sides, but it is a matter
we should consider at a later stage. I
do not think any financial difficulty would
arise, because I think the powers contained in the 1920 Act are ample. There
is no limited definition of "council"
given.
The third point on which I welcome the
Bill is the opportunity that it affords us
for the repeal and re-enactment of the
1920 Act, or so much of it as will be left
when Parliament has done with this Bill.
I am afraid I must run very briefly through
the 1920 Act. The right hon. Gentleman told the House, I think, that it was
a very short Act. It contained only five
Sections The first section dealt with the
lowering of the pension age to a point not
so low as that in this Bill, and introduced
that definition of which I have spoken.
That Section has been entirely repealed by
the Old Age Pensions Act of 1936, but lest
I mislead the House I ought to say that I
do not suggest that any influence which
the wording of that Section may have
had on other Sections of the 1920 Act
disappears as a consequence of the repeal.
I am afraid that the position is the contrary. It is a case in which the evil that
men do lives after them, but I do not
know of any Statutory provision or any
judicial interpretation which leads me to
conclude that the limited definition was
ever intended to apply to the whole of
the Act.
Looking through the rest of the 1920
Act I came to the conclusion that there
is distinct evidence that it is not intended
to apply generally. On the other hand,
I am afraid it is possible to read those
other Sections differently. At all events,
Section 1 has gone, and if we re-enact the
1920 Act at a later date that Section will
not appear again at all. Section 2 of the
Act of 1920 dealt, as has already been
stated, with matters to which Clause 2 of
our Bill relates, and there will be very
little left of the 1920 Act, except Section
2, when we have done with the Bill before
us.
But I do not think our new Clause 2
It leaves the same
goes far enough.
yawning chasm that was left in the 1920
Act, and to that I will refer again in a
few moments. Section 3 of the 1920 Act
dealt with the application of the War
Charities Act, 1916, which does not apply
to Northern Ireland, and Section 4 dealt
with the application to Scotland and Ireland, as it was then, particularly to Scotland, and Section 5 was merely citation
and commencement. So the House will
see that practically all we are concerned
with at the moment is Section 2 of the
Act of 1920, which one of the main purposes of this Bill is to further amend.
I am going to suggest that it is almost
imperative that the two Acts should be
blended into one as soon as possible,
because if not we shall have nothing on
our Statute Book to indicate what our
law is in regard to blind persons.
Moreover, since the 1920 Act was
passed this Parliament has passed the
Education Act of 1923, a Statute of
which the right hon. Gentleman the
Leader of the House has, I think, some-
Blind Persons Bill-
Second Reading
26 APRIL 1938
what special knowledge. That Act does
contain a rather broader definition of
technical education than is the definition
which is contained in one of the subsections of Section 2 of the Act of 1920.
That is, a matter of some little importance, but I need not deal with it to-day.
I welcome the Bill because it affords an
opportunity of consolidating our law.
Now I think I have done with my
salvo of greetings, and let me come more
particularly to criticism. That is what I
have described as the yawning chasm
which will be left by Clause 2 in the
original Section 2. The whole Bill might
really be described as a Bill for amending
the 1920 Act, for that is what it comes
to. In this case they have not brought
in an amendment which I think is
necessary; that is, there is no provision
under this new Clause for providing
technical instruction for the man who is
not stone blind and therefore not qualified, independently of his age, for a pension, but who is on the way to becoming
stone blind.
That is the man who is suffering from
a disability owing to defective or failing
eye-sight which prevents his performing
the ordinary work he has been accustomed
to, but not any work. Such a man is in
need of technical training in the widest
sense, such as is provided for in the Education Act of 1923. I say you will do
more good for that man when he is in
that stage than you will when he is completely blind. Take the terrible case of
the man who has been to his oculist and
is told to go home and take care of himself and keep out of strong light. He is
told by the oculist, "I should only deceive you if I did not tell you that in
twelve months you will lose what sight
you have." Surely we should make it
possible for that person to use that twelve
months to enable him to learn something
like using a typewriter and so on.
is no special provision made for that in
this Bill.
There
It happens that hitherto our local
authorities, I think with the exception of
Belfast, have not been prone to apply
very vigorously such powers as they
and that being so the time is
possess,
opportune for us to take stock of the
situation and see if there is anything we
can do which is within the powers of this
House, and which does not involve any
real alteration in the law. Accordingly
I
propose to put on the Paper certain
suggested interpolations in two of the
Clauses. I hope the effect will be, not
to introduce in practice any change in
what I believe to be the law, but I think
it will emphasise and clarify what I
believe to be the intention of the law. It
will not involve any alteration in the title
of the Bill, nor the creation of any new
law whatever, but it will, I hope, turn
those possibilities which exist under the
Education Act and I am thinking particularly of sub-section (2) of Section 57
in Part 9, dealing with the future treatment of the afflicted child into rights.
In that part of that Act a definition is
given of a child afflicted by blindness as
a child who is too blind to be able to
read the ordinary books supplied to school
children, and that comes very near what
I am driving at the man who is not
stone blind but has a serious affliction of
the eyes.
I want to turn the possibility
which exists under Part 4 of that Education Act into a right, and give him more
opportunity than he has at present, because there is nothing in that part of the
Act which prohibits your partially blind
man from attendance at a technical
school. I hope that in that I shall have
the sympathy and support of the House.
I shall put amendments on the Paper
this evening, and I ask that the Committee stage of this Bill should not be
taken until Thursday week in order to
allow ample time for the consideration of
to-day's debate and of the amendments,
and perhaps of any modifications in the
wording of the amendments which might
be thought advisable and would tend to
produce agreement. I would also ask
that before the next stage those hon.
Members who have not already done so
will refresh their memories, not only of
the 1920 Act, but of those provisions of the
Education Act which deal with technical
instruction and the afflicted child. For
the moment I support the Second
Reading.
Major BOYLE: Might I ask the
Leader of the House for a clear definition of the word "dependents"? It
says in Clause 2, "also the needs of any
member of the household of which
blind person
member who
dependent on him."
