a
a
are
Sir JAMES WILTON: With the
greatest respect I should like to say that
Blind Persons Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Sir James Wilton]
I agree entirely with almost everything
Senator Leslie has said. I am quite
sure that everyone will welcome the
reduction in the age, and I am almost
equally sure that everyone will not welcome the continuance of the definition
of a blind person. I am speaking from
experience when I say the present
definition is harsh and that in practice
it militates very severely against blind.
persons. I do not wish to say a single
word with regard to members of the
medical profession who deal with blind
persons' pensions, but it entirely depends.
on the opinion of different medical men
as to whether a person who is seeking a
pension comes within the definition or
not. Speaking again from experience,
I think a person requires to be stone
blind before he can qualify for a pension
under the present conditions. That
definition might be modified SO that
those gentlemen who have the last word
in deciding whether a person is or is
not blind within the meaning of the
Act might have a little easier conscience
in deciding the question. That is one
blot not only on the 1920 Act, but on
the Bill now before the House. I shall
be
much interested in the Amendvery
ments which are to be moved at a later
stage by Senator Leslie, and I hope to
be present when they are discussed..
Mr. ROBB: With regard to the
points Senator Leslie has raised, I recognise that a consolidation of blind persons legislation would be a good thing,
but, as he very well knows, the ordinary
course is to make your Amendments first
and consolidate afterwards. Without
making any promise to the hon. and
learned Member I may express the hope
that that will be done. With regard to
what he described as the yawning chasm
in the Bill in consequence of provision
not having up to the moment been made
for the instruction of men and women
who are going blind so that they may
be able to continue to earn their livelihood, I can assure him that that is a
problem that is before the Government
at the present time. It is a matter to
which they have been giving very
serious and very sympathetic consideration, but they have found very many
difficulties in working out any scheme
whereby this could be done. If you take
the case of a man at 35 who is a farmer
and he finds he is going blind within a
certain time, you can realise there are
great difficulties in trying to find some
method of teaching him a trade he will
be able to follow when he is blind. It
is a case for
for training at a special
institution, and we find that at the
moment all the special institutions
which exist in Northern Ireland are not
only full, but have a waiting list. That
is one difficulty. There is another difficulty. That same man finds that he can
still work at his farm and keep it going
and there would be great difficulty in
inducing him to leave his farm to go into
an institution without at the same
time making some financial provision for
his dependents. These are only some of
the difficulties that have been encountered,
but I can
assure the hon. and learned
Senator and the House that this is a
problem that has engaged, and still is
engaging, the serious attention of the
Government, although they were not in
a position to incorporate any proposal in
the Bill now before the House. It is a
matter with which the Government have
every sympathy, and it is a problem they
hope to solve, although they are not in
a position to solve it at the moment.
With regard to the hon. and learned
Member's suggestion that the Committee
stage of the Bill should be deferred until
Thursday week, I can give him the assurance that that will be done. Senator
Major Boyle asked for a definition of
"dependents." The general definition of
a dependent in Acts of Parliament is a
person who is wholly or partly maintained
by a particular person. If a child or
wife, brother or sister, or parent is
partly not necessarily wholly maintained out of the resources of any particular person, then each child or wife,
brother or sister, or parent is a dependent. It is entirely a question of fact
as to whether another person is or is not
partly dependent on the resources of a
particular individual.
With regard to the definition of a
blind person-the point raised by the
Mayor of Derry-it is, of course, an old
question as to what ought to be the
definition of a blind person in Acts dealing with this subject. The existing
definition was arrived at and is one that
has been found to work. There have
been many suggestions made and investigated by committees as to whether a
different definition might not have been
Blind Persons Bill
Adjournment-
26 APRIL 1938
adopted, but hitherto no Government has
felt itself able to adopt a definition other
than the present one. Many of these
cases are on the border line, and it is
a matter of opinion as to whether a
person comes within the definition or not.
We can only hope that those who are
called upon to interpret the definition
will do so sympathetically. I am glad to
find the House so willing to give a Second
Reading to this Bill.
Dr. GIBSON: I have listened to this
debate with great interest. I have been
in the medical profession for upwards of
fifty years, and persons have come before
me in various stages of blindness. One
of the most difficult things in the world
is to define a blind person.
It very
largely depends on their occupation. For
instance, a chauffeur who becomes afflicted
with partial blindness is no longer able
to drive a motor-car, and such a man
ought to be trained in some other occupation to enable him to earn a living. I
do not think Senator Leslie referred
specifically to institutional training when
he mentioned training. I think he had
a wider field in his mind for training
people threatened with blindness.
It is remarkable the number of cases
that come before a doctor in the course
of a long professional life such as I have
had. A man has come to my surgery
saying, "I do not think I see as well as
I did," and on examination it was found
that he did not see at all with one eye,
and very imperfectly with the other. In
fact, his sight was so bad that I insisted
on driving him home in his car. If such
a man was trained in some other occupation by the time he became totally blind.
he might be able to continue to earn his
living. The whole problem is full of
difficulties and requires great consideration.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: It is
easy to imagine the case of a chauffeur
who is colour blind and unable to distinguish between the red, green, and
amber lights. Such a man would be
quite unfit to drive a car, but he would
not be qualified to receive a pension because he was not totally blind. Therefore on can realise the difficulties in defining blindness, and I do not envy the task
of those responsible for giving a watertight definition.
Mr. LESLIE : May I explain that
Senator Dr. Gibson is quite right. I did
not intend to refer specially to institutional treatment, nor was institutional
treatment in my mind at all.
On question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time'), agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday, 5th May.
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: This Bill is designed to
extend the operation of the Loans Guarantee Act for a further two years, until
1940.
It follows the customary procedure of extending these powers for two
years. There cannot be much doubt as to
the desirability of keeping this Act on
the Statute Book. No extension is required in the maximum amount within
which guarantees may be given. At the
present moment the maximum amount is
£14,500,000, and the amount guaranteed
is £4,000,000 short of that. This Bill
does not affect the total amount of loans
guaranteed, but merely extends for two
years the time within which loans may
be made.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Hydrogen Cyanide (Fumigation)
Bill [S.].
Returned from the House of Commons,
agreed to without Amendment.
Exceptional Distress (Transitional
Provisions) Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons
and read a First time.-(Mr. Long).
Adjournment.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
House do now adjourn until Thursday."
May I also say that it is intended to meet
next week on Tuesday and Thursday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
twenty-five minutes after
Four o'clock until Thursday next.

Papers Presented
Air Raid Precautions
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 28th April, 1938.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Labour:
Unemployment Insurance (Additional
Benefits) Order (Northern Ireland), 1938.
Unemployment Insurance (Additional
Benefits and Reduction in Contributions)
(Agriculture) Order (Northern Ireland),
1938.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
McClure's Divorce Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons,
and read a First time.
Member Sworn.
Thomas Stanislaus McAllister, Esq.,
took and subscribed the Oath.
Question to Minister.
AIR RAID PRECAUTIONS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Major Dobbs :
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he can state what steps are
being taken to minimise the effect of air
raids in Northern Ireland.
Major DOBBS: I am asking this
question because I do not think sufficient
attention is being paid to this subject in
Northern Ireland. Air raids here are
possible. They are not very probable,
but so long as they are possible it would.
be criminal not to do everything within
our power to minimise their effectiveness.
I had the good fortune to discuss this
matter with a technical expert-if I may
use that term in regard to this matterin London, one who has something to do
with helping one of the local authorities
on the outskirts of London. I gather
that the accepted view of the experts is
that the damage from an air raid, where
high explosives, incendiary bombs, or
gas, or a combination of the three, are
used, would not be serious unless there
was panic, but if there is panic the result
will be devastating. That is why, I
understand, besides organising firefighting squads, first-aid units, and so on,
they are at the same time doing all they
possibly can to educate and teach the
populace what they may expect in the
case of an air raid, because panic is
essentially the terror of the unknown.
We can do nothing with regard to protecting ourselves against air raids. That
is entirely a matter for the fighting forces,
but I do think we can do a very great
deal in teaching ourselves what to expect,
so that we may definitely reduce the
effectiveness of an air raid and, at any
rate, not lose our morale. I beg to ask
the question standing in my name.
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House):
The hon. and gallant Senator is aware
that the question of air raid precautions
in Northern Ireland has been the subject
of a committee, and as copies of the report
of that committee have been circulated he
is, no doubt, acquainted with the recommendations which have been made.
It will be seen from the Estimates,
Class III, No. 1, that provision has been
made for certain expenditure in the current financial year, and the Government
hope shortly to introduce a Bill to give
the necessary statutory powers and to provide for financial assistance from Government funds on approximately the same
lines as in Great Britain.
In the meantime, local authorities are
being advised to press forward with the
preparation of schemes suitable for their
requirements, and every advice and
assistance in the preparation of these
schemes is being given by the Ministry's
air raid precautions officials.
A medical instructor has just been
appointed, and he will shortly take up
duty, and it is hoped that a mobile gas
van, which will be used for the purpose
of demonstrations throughout the
country, will shortly be available.
Perhaps I might supplement that
answer by a short statement on what has
already been done in regard to air raid
133 Air Raid Precautions

28 APRIL 1938
Air Raid Precautions
precautions. A certain amount of money
for this purpose is provided in the
Estimates for the current year. This
includes a grant to the Ulster Joint Committee of the Order of St. John of
Jerusalem and the British Red Cross
Society. The grant is really only a refund of out-of-pocket expenses. It is in
no sense a payment for the voluntary
assistance given by the members of this
organisation in informing the general
public as to the first-aid steps to be taken
in the event of air raids and in providing,
through the machinery of their organisation, a most important nucleus for any
scheme of air raid precautions.
The Minister of Home Affairs has
appointed an air raid precautions officer,
who has already visited practically every
local authority in Northern Ireland, and
is always ready and available to give
advice and assistance in the preparation
of schemes. While some of the local
authorities have taken considerable
interest in the matter and have done a
good deal in the direction of initiating
schemes, there seems generally a good deal
of apathy in the matter, due, perhaps, to
a disposition to regard Northern Ireland,
from its geographical position, as immune from attack. The Government
have been advised that under present-day
conditions an air raid in Northern
Ireland is by no means outside the bounds
of possibility, and this possibility cannot
be ignored.
ex-
While, therefore, it is, perhaps, unnecessary generally throughout Northern
Ireland to prepare elaborate and
pensive schemes, the preparation of
schemes suitable for the requirements of
the various local authorities is one which
should be taken in hand by all local
authorities without delay.
As already stated, a Bill to give the
necessary statutory powers and to provide
for assistance from Government funds on
approximately the same lines as in Great
Britain will shortly be introduced. Meanwhile, there is no reason whatever why the
local authorities should not press forward
with the preparation of their schemes.
As a further help in educating the
public and in assisting local authorities.
in making arrangements for medical and
hospital requirements in case of air raids,
an item is included in this Estimate for
the appointment of a medical instructor
whose duties, corresponding to those of
the air raid medical officers in England,
will include the following:-Arranging
courses and lectures to students in the
Medical School of Queen's University
and arranging for courses of lectures for
members of the medical, dental and
veterinary professions throughout Northern Ireland. These will be held, as a
general rule, under the auspices of the
local branch of the British Medical
Association.
He will also arrange for instructions to
be given to members of the nursing profession in Northern Ireland, mainly in
city and county hospitals; assisting the
various air raid schemes of local authorities from the medical aspect, and making
general arrangements for the provision
of hospital accommodation in Northern
Ireland for air raid victims.
We further hope to explain the various
types of gas that may be used, their
effects, and the remedies to be employed
against them. At the same time, the
Government would like to emphasise the
point Senator Major Dobbs has made,
namely, that up to the present there has
been too much apathy on this subject in
Northern Ireland, and the sooner local
authorities get to work the better.
.
Captain PERCEVAL MAXWELL:
I should like to know if the right hon.
Gentleman has anything to say in connection with evacuation schemes that are
going on in England. Is there anything
in this country comparable to them. In
Northern Ireland we have a Territorial
unit which is not under the control of
the Government, but I assume the
Government are in touch with the War
Office with regard to its possible use.
That Territorial unit consists, I understand, of a company of Royal Engineers
and a company of Heavy Artillery. I
understand that in England the air
defence is in the hands of two divisions,
one in the North and Midlands, and the
other in the South, and it is most unlikely that any of those Territorial troops
could be spared, in the event of war, to
give any assistance to us. We have a
great shipbuilding yard, and Messrs.
Short & Harland's aeroplane factory, and
it is quite probable we might have other
munition works which, perhaps, might be
a serious problem in the event of war.
Exceptional Distress Bill- 136
Air Raid Precautions
THE SENATE
[Captain Perceval-Maxwell]
Would it not be possible for our
Government to arrange with the War
Office to utilise the Territorial unit we
already possess for the purpose of the
air defence of Belfast and such other
vital spots as might be used as bases in
time of war. These Territorials are
trained in the use of artillery, and it
should not be impossible to give them
some further training, possibly by
attaching officers and some of their
men to some of these Territorial units
in England.
The only other question I wish to ask
is, would it be possible for some organisation to be carried out which would
enable Belfast to be evacuated at short
notice. I understand that in England
all persons who are not required to live
in congested areas are likely to be
evacuated. It does not seem to me that
that is a question entirely for one local
authority. If the Belfast Corporation
wished to evacuate half the population
of Belfast to County Down, for instance,
it would have to be done in conjunction
with the local authorities in that county,
and some steps would have to be taken
beforehand to ensure accommodation for
the persons evacuated. I merely draw
attention to this matter, because it is
one that will require a certain amount of
organisation beforehand.
Mr. ROBB: With regard to air raid
defence, I am not familiar with that
branch of Government work, as it is a
matter for the War Office, but, without
being familiar with the details, I may
say that that aspect of the question is
not being lost sight of. As regards the
question of the evacuation of large cities
like Belfast and Londonderry, that must
necessarily come before the Government
and the local authorities when plans are
being made.

Exceptional Distress (Transitional
Provisions) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: This is a short Bill and
really affects the Belfast Corporation.
About five years ago the Corporation of
Belfast borrowed a sum of £156,000 for
the purpose of affording outdoor relief
to able-bodied persons.
By an Act
passed in 1934 a period of five years was
allowed for the repayment of money
borrowed under such circumstances. That
period in the case of Belfast would expire
on 31st March, 1939. The balance remaining to be paid off at the present
date is £25,000, but in view of the heavy
commitments in other directions the
Belfast Corporation approached the
Government and asked that extended
time should be allowed for the repayment
of this sum. This the Government
agreed to, and accordingly under this
Bill it is proposed to allow a further
period of five years for the repayment of
the sum in question. Therefore, the
whole sum will be paid off within ten
years of the date of the original borrowing. I beg to move, "That the Bill be
now read a Second time.'
""
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn until Tuesday next."-(Mr.
Long.)
Adjourned
accordingly
at

Four o'clock until Tuesday
next.

Question to Minister.
LAW REGARDING MOTOR SIGNALS
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in
in the name of Major
Dobbs:
-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the Government would consider the
advisability of bringing the laws regarding motor-car signals up to date.
Major DOBBS: I am sure all hon.
Members will agree that anything that
can be done to stop the serious loss of
life on our roads should be done, and
that no stone should be left unturned to
attain that result. Our motor engineers
have given us very fine motor vehicles,
easily controlled, electrically equipped,
with four-wheel brakes controlled
hydraulically and otherwise; in fact, with
everything to give us a first-rate motor
car or motor lorry. On the other side
of the picture we have what to my mind is
a most extraordinary state of affairs. We
have got motor signals recognised by the
law which are perfectly antiquated. I
should like to deal with them one by one.
I am
The first one is the stop signal. The
motor car designer has given us quite a
good stop signal-a stop light showing
red when the brake is operated. If anyone is going to stop his car in that way
and a car behind runs into him, the law
does not recognise that signal; it
cognises only the hand signal.
quite sure that when Adam and Eve were
strolling through the Garden of Eden,
as no doubt they did; if Adam wanted
to prevent Eve bumping into him he
just raised his hand. Perhaps the
Leader of the House can give us some
authentic information on that point. If
there was any chance of treading on the
tail of the serpent Eve must have gone
in front and signalled. At any rate,
the signal used then was a hand signal,
and it is strange that that is the signal
which has been used down the ages and
is in use now. Thirty or thirty-five years
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
5 MAY 1938
ago it was perhaps a good signal, but
to-day it is absurd.
What happens to-day? If you wish to
act legally when slowing down to stop,
the first thing you do is to wind down
the window, put out your hand, make
the requisite signal, and hope it is seen.
Then you put on the brake, and if the
road is slimy you have a very good chance
of skidding, because one hand is off the
wheel and out of the window.
Take the other way of signalling to
stop, which is not recognised by law. If
you want to stop or slow down, all you
have to do is to put your foot on the
brake pedal and automatically and instantaneously a red light is shown at the
back of your car, informing the motorist
behind that you are about to stop. Both
hands are still on the wheel, and there is
no tendency to skid, because you are in
a perfectly natural and well-balanced
position, and you have a real opportunity
of preventing your car skidding. The
whole operation is instantaneous if it is
necessary to use it in an emergency, and
I cannot see why it should not be the
recognised means of slowing down.
Then we have the signal to turn to the
right or the left. There again the motor
car designer has given us a mechanical
signal, electrically operated and electrically illuminated, which can be seen at
night equally well as by day. It
should, of course, be in front of the
driver, so that he can see if it is working,
and the arms should drop either when the
car has finished its turn or after a predetermined period, but that is all done
for us by the designers. But the law
says the signal must be done by hand by
the driver. If it is done by hand the person on the left of the driver can equally
give the signal, and I strongly object
to that. I can give you an instance of
what happens.
I was motoring from the South to
Piccadilly. I drove up St. James's
Street, turned sharp to the left and then
to the right into Piccadilly. There is a
one-way road, and at the time there were
three lines of traffic, and we were stopped
to allow the Piccadilly cars to get
through. Just in front of me was an
open touring car, and a lady sitting on
the left of the driver thought she ought
to signal that her car would be going to
the left. Incidentally, it could not go
to the right or straight on; it could go
only to the left, but she thought she
ought to signal. She accordingly put
out her hand, and on her left was a taxi.
The taxi driver did not quite know what
to do, but, being a taxi driver, he did
the best he could and shook the lady
warmly by the hand.
That is comedy, but now let me give
you tragedy. I was on one occasion
motoring behind an armoured car near
Belfast, and a man sitting beside the
driver gave a signal to turn left. I should
in the ordinary course have gone to the
right, but I was a little suspicious and
refrained from doing so, and the armoured
car turned to the right. Had it not been
for the mercy of Providence my car would
have been smashed to pieces, so that I
strongly object to anyone but the driver
giving a signal. But according to the
law at present, anyone can signal, and it
must be done by hand. When a person
puts his hand out he may be signalling or.
he may only be knocking out his pipe, but
why cannot it be made perfectly safe and
simple by having a mechanically-operated
signal.
There is also a question regarding
trolly buses. I wish the Belfast Corporation in their wisdom had painted their
trolly buses a special colour. It is not so
bad at present, but when they run, as I
hope they will, down York Street there is
going to be potential trouble for motorists.
The trolly bus does not behave in the
same way as the ordinary bus. When it
leaves the side of the road the trolly bus
comes out much more sharply than the
ordinary bus. You can see it on the
Chiswick Road, London, and I hope the
Belfast Corporation will take this into
consideration.
Then there is the question of the towing
signal. I should like to know why there
is no towing signal to show that a car is
being towed. Nowadays we have some
big ten-ton lorries which tow a seven-ton
trailer, and when a motorist comes up
behind them at night, or even during the
day, especially on a narrow road, it is
quite impossible to tell whether what he
sees in front is a lorry or a trailer towed
by a lorry. Whether it is a broadside
of 22 feet or one of 45 feet is very important when you are passing it. I cannot
understand why we do not have a special
signal for towing. There are on the roads
to-day what look like twenty tonners,
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Major Dobbs]
although I do not think they are quite
that weight, but I should like to know
whether there is any regulation enforcing
a maximum dimension for motor-lorry
bodies.
Then there is the question of the lorry
with the very long over-hanging bar, a
steel girder, or something like that. When
viewed from the side it is very easy to
see, but in certain lights it is quite impossible to see from behind. It may overhang for ten or twelve or even fifteen
feet, and there should be some sort of
signal attached so that a motorist coming
up behind can definitely see what he is up
against. Then there is the question of
the horn and bell. I should like to know
if the bell is allowed here at the present
time. Scotland Yard prohibited it in
London in 1903, I think, and it was laid
down that a bell must not be used on any
motor vehicle except a fire engine in order
to allow the fire engine to have its distinctive sound.
I wish the horn was not used quite so
much, but only in an emergency. I also
wish something could be done about persons
chiefly young people driving small 5cwt.
vans, who when they see in front of them
a horse being ridden by a man or woman,
sound their horn and drive past at 30 or
40 miles an hour. I wish they could be
instructed that it is much better not to
sound the horn, but to approach quietly,
ready to pull up if there is any necessity
to do so.
Then we have the signals on roads.
First of all there is the cross-road signal.
The "Stop. Major road ahead" and the
"Slow. Major road ahead," are most
excellent signals, but is there anybody
to see that those signals are observed by
motorists? Only a few days ago my
daughter was driving a car near
Carrickfergus.
She was
on the main
road, and as she came to a cross-road
a lorry flashed across at about thirty
miles an hour. That cross-road had a
"Stop. Major Road Ahead" sign. From
her side of the road the view was all
right, but on the other side there was an
absolutely blind corner. There were
houses on either side of the corner and
no one could possibly have seen a lorry
coming across. The lorry had no right
to do so, and had there been a smash
it would have meant certain death for
someone.
Then we come to the robots, the red,
yellow and green lights. They are excellent except the one just north of the
City Hall. I think the authorities might
with advantage see what is being done in
Piccadilly Circus, London, and it might
help them to improve that signal. Another question that arises is, are these
red lights observed? Going home from
this building yesterday I saw, first, a
private motor car, and then a Belfast
tram-car deliberately go across when
their signal was at red, and that is not
a thing that seldom happens. With
reference to that red signal, I think
there ought to be some authoritative statement with regard to fire engines. Can a
fire engine go past the crossing when the
signal is at red? The question has been
asked before, and we ought to have an
authoritative answer.
We have also the Belisha Beacons. If
you have only one or two they are not
much good, but if you have a considerable number they are excellent in order
to allow pedestrians to get across the
road. But, on the whole, the beacons
in Belfast are put in the worst possible
places where they cannot very well be
seen. The beacon itself is used for purposes other than for allowing pedestrians
to cross, and I think that is a very great
mistake. Another point is that trams
are in the habit of stopping across these
beacon crossings, and that does not seem
to be right.
Then we have the white lines in the
middle of the road. They served an excellent purpose, but now they are becoming white and yellow. Is there any
significance in the fact that some are
white and others are yellow? In France
they have a white line on the road just
on the crest of a hill. It is an excellent
idea, and I wish we could adopt it here.
Here have a white line round a bend
you
and that is excellent; when you have it
along what I should like to call a straight
bend it is making an absurdity of it, and
motorists will not pay attention to the
white line when they should do so.
I wish we had the thirty-mile speed
limit here, because I should have liked
to have asked a question about the invisible ray which has been tried near
Smethwick, and what results have been
obtained from it, but that does not arise
here. In conclusion, I hope the authori
ties will lose their Adam and Eve
a
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
5 MAY 1938
complex and really do something to bring
the motor signals up to date. I am sure
it would do a great deal to prevent
serious accidents. When that has been
done, I hope we shall see a great deal
more courtesy on the part of pedestrians,
cyclists and motorists. We do not want
the man who has too much toujours la
politesse, because he becomes a nuisance, but we do want everyone to be
prepared to give way and not always be
rushing. As a slogan for motorists
might I suggest the last two lines of a
nursery rhyme some of us may remember:
Let us see if he is able
To sit still when he's at table.
Now the two last lines:
Let us see if Johnny can
Be a little gentleman.
I think that ought to be the slogan for
all motorists. I beg to ask the question
standing in my name.
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House): The
existing regulations require every person
driving or propelling a vehicle on a public
highway to signal his intention to slow
down, stop, or turn to the right. The
regulations do not prescribe whether the
signal is to be given by hand or by means
of a mechanical device, but the type of
mechanical signals which may be used
and the positions in which they must be
fitted, if used, are determined. They
are not, however, obligatory. To quote
the Minister of Transport-"Their recognition does not confer upon them any
greater adequacy in law than they may
be found to have in fact."
The position, therefore, is that every
person driving a vehicle is required to
give a signal that is in fact and in the
particular circumstances adequate to express his intention. It would appear to
be difficult to improve upon this provision, and no difficulties have so far
arisen in Northern Ireland as to the
giving of signals, but the matter is, of
course, being kept under observation.
Might I supplement that reply of the
department by answering some of the
observations made by the hon. and gallant
Senator. He concluded by saying that
he hoped he and all other motorists would
keep smiling. If, after the unfortunate
experiences he has had for many years
he can still keep smiling I am surprised.
It is not true to say that the law does
not recognise electrical or other mechanical signals. A case did arise where a
lady motorist stopped suddenly, and
alleged that she had given a signal by
means of a stop light. It was held in
that case that she had not given an
adequate signal. The court did not
decide that it was absolutely necessary
that she should have given a signal by
hand, but only that in the circumstances
the signal she did give was not adequate.
It is not, I think, true to say that that
case decided that the law did not recognise a mechanical signal.
It might be of advantage that trolly
buses should be of a special colour, and,
with regard to a towing signal, I agree
that if a device could be invented that
would give notice that a vehicle was being
towed it would be an advantage. I am
not familiar with the regulations, but I
think that if there is an overhang on a
towed vehicle there must be a signal, but
whether the mere fact that a vehicle is
being towed must be the subject of a
special signal I am not quite sure. With
regard to the maximum dimensions of
lorries, there is no regulation with regard
to length, but the width must not exceed
7ft. 6ins.
Major DOBBS: I asked particularly
about bodies.
Mr. ROBB: As regards width, there
is a regulation, but there is none as regards length. With regard to fire engines,
I believe there ought to be a regulation
that fire engines must observe the red
light as well as anyone else. With regard
to the lights at the Belfast City Hall
crossing, I drive through that signal
twice practically every day, and I
have never experienced any difficulty.
In addition to driving, I walk across it
generally twice a day, and again I can
say my experience has been anything but
the experience of the hon. and gallant
Senator. On the whole, I think it works
exceedingly well. Belisha beacons have
only recently been introduced here, but
I think that, on the whole, the people are
learning to use them fairly well. With
regard to the "halt" and "slow" signals
at cross-roads, the police do, in fact,
make inspections from time to time, but
it would be quite impossible to maintain
a constant watch at every cross-roads, but
I am aware that when they do detect an
offence being committed they are not
slow to prosecute.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Regarding the question of the fire brigade
and traffic lights, the Police Committee
of the Belfast Corporation have had that
matter under consideration several times,
and while they have issued no definite instructions to the fire brigade they expect
the fire brigade to recognise the lights as
far as possible. But if there is nothing
to prevent them crossing, and they are
satisfied there is no danger they are expected to cross against the red light. It
must be remembered that when the fire
brigade sets out it may be a case of life
and death if a few seconds are lost, and
the Committee leave it to the fire brigade
to cross if it is possible. I think that is
quite right.
Mr. MCALLISTER: The hon. and gallant Senator who asked this question
seemed to devote his entire speech to the
rights of motorists against other classes of
the community, so far as signals are concerned. We must all recognise that
pedestrians have a perfect right to use
the roads, and that these signals are a
protection to pedestrians. I understood
the hon. and gallant Senator to take
objection to these road signals, but surely
they are the best protection that pedestrians have. I speak with a longer
motoring experience than any
any other
Member of this House. I drove the
second car that came to my own district,
and I have no hesitation in saying with
the greatest deliberation that 70 per cent.
of the people driving motor vehicles of all
classes to-day have not the sense of duty,
the sense of right, and the sense of
humanity to be on the road with such a
dangerous vehicle.
I think more attention should be paid
to the rights of the pedestrian. They
should have the first preference. No doubt
they are not by any means perfect, and
some of them may be
a trouble to
motorists, but we as a House should not
restrict the use of the highway for the
benefit of motorists alone. If I interpret
the speech of the hon. and gallant
Member rightly he objected to these road
signals, but although they may not be
perfect they are some protection to pedestrians who have to pass along the public
highway. I think pedestrians should be
protected just as much as motorists.
Major DOBBS: May I make a personal explanation. I am very sorry the
hon. Senator should think I was speaking
entirely from the point of view of
motorists. I am afraid I did not make
myself as clear as I might have done. I
was speaking very much more from the
point of view of pedestrians than of
motorists. What I did want to show was
that these signals were not so good as they
should be. Regarding road signals,
Belisha beacons, and robot signals, I think
I said they were excellent because they
did protect pedestrians, but I did take
exception to the fact that they were not
properly observed. In England and
Wales there are plain-clothes police on
duty to see that these signals are observed.
I am very sorry if I did not make my
meaning clear.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I would not for
one moment suggest that the hon. and
gallant Senator would be guilty of puting forward any selfish point of view in
this matter.
Mr. LESLIE: I was going to ask the
Leader of the House whether he could
give us any definition of that expression,
"Slow. Major Road ahead." What is
meant by "slow"? Presumably it is not
"Halt," but even that is perhaps not
beyond cavil. Does "slow" mean five,
ten, or fifteen miles an hour?
Mr. ROBB: Whatever would be in
the circumstances reasonable.
Mr. LESLIE: And circumstances include the amount of visible traffic on the
cross-road?
Trade Boards Provisional Orders
Confirmation Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons
and read a First time, and ordered to be
read a Second time on Tuesday next.
Criminal Lunatics Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons
and read a First time, and ordered to be
read a Second time on Tuesday next.
Blind Persons Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Blind Persons Bill-
Committee
5 MAY 1938
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Robb.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Lowering of age at which
non-contributory old-age pensions may be
paid to blind persons).
Question put, "That the Clause stand
part of the Bill."
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: There is one
question I should like to have some information upon on this Clause. The various
County and County Borough Councils
already have a number of blind persons
in their charge. So far as the Armagh
County Council is concerned, it is their
unanimous wish that all blind persons
between the ages of forty and fifty who
will be eligible for pensions under the
provisions of this Bill should not have to
undergo another medical examination.
The position of the present pensioners is,
that they have already been examined by
a medical expert on behalf of the Council
and have already been accepted by the
Council for a pension. Is it the intention
of the Government that these persons
who are between forty and fifty should
have to undergo a fresh medical
examination?
Mr. ROBB: I do not really know, but
I would assume they would not have to
have another examination. Perhaps the
hon. Member would put down a question
on the Third Reading.
Mr. LESLIE: The passage in the Bill
which says: "This Act shall come into
immediate operation" would seem to solve
the difficulty. The right of the man of
forty to a pension will arise as soon as
this Bill receives the Royal Assent.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Duty of local authorities
to promote welfare of blind persons).

blind

assistance under the preceding provisions of this sub-section, the Council
shall take into account not only the
needs of the blind person, but also the

member who are dependent on him,
and the rules laid down in paragraphs
(a) to (e) of sub-section (3) of section

complied with in computing the resources of any person in order

Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to move,
in Sub-clause (1) to leave out the'
words "any members of " in line
24 on page 2, and insert instead "immediate relatives only in". I put down
this Amendment on the suggestion of the
Armagh County Council. I think it very
desirable to define the members of the
household of a blind person who are dependent upon him. I might have a
favourite or an adopted child or a distant
relative and bring them into my household in order to get the allowance for a
dependent, and I do not think that
should be permitted.
I
Mr. ROBB: I do not think any good
reason has been given for this amendment, and the Government cannot accept
it. The words in the Bill contemplate
those persons who are living in the household of a blind person and are dependent
upon him. I would think that an adopted
child is not an immediate relative.
think it is far better to leave the matter
as it is, because the intention of the
Government is that in considering the
amount of assistance that is to be given
the actual circumstances of the household
at the time are to be taken into consideration. If there is a person in the
Blind Persons Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Robb]
household who is dependent upon the
blind person for support, that person
should be taken into consideration in
estimating the amount of assistance to be
given. To limit it to immediate relatives
only seems an undue limitation to the
assistance which it is the intention of the
Government a blind person should receive.
For these reasons the Government cannot accept the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Sir JAMES WILTON: Who are the
judges of the dependents? Who decides
who the actual dependents are, and is
there any appeal against that decision?
Is it the county council, as suggested by
the hon. Senator?
Mr. ROBB: There is nothing in the
Act to regulate that. It will, of course,
be primarily a matter for the county
council. Whether there are regulations
for an appeal from the county council
I am not in a position to say at the
moment.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out the third paragraph of Sub-clause (1)
("The Council shall on or before the 31st
day of March . . .") and insert-
"(1A) The powers conferred by subsection (1) of this section shall include
the provision of facilities for the technical education of blind persons and of
other persons who, by reason of their
defective or failing eyesight, have
become, or are in danger of becoming,
unable to perform their ordinary work
and are capable of receiving and being
benefited by such education. For the
purposes of this sub-section such persons last aforesaid shall be deemed to
be blind persons and the expression
"ordinary work" shall be deemed to
include work of the character which
such person has been accustomed, or
has been trained, to perform.
(1B) The council of every county
and county borough shall, on or before
the thirty-first day of March, nineteen
hundred and thirty-nine, prepare and
submit to the Ministry of Home Affairs
a
scheme for the exercise of their
powers under the foregoing provisions
of this section (1C)."
The root of the trouble I am trying
to get at in this amendment is, of course,
the unduly harsh definition which is reproduced in Clause 4. I am not dealing
with that at the moment, because, as I
said the other day, we cannot touch that.
Let me begin at the end of my new
amendment, between that and the beginning of another. I start by asking
the Committee to leave out certain words,
and I will take them first and try to
explain why I want them left out. The
first reason, of course, is to reproduce
them somewhere else in the Bill. I do
not really want to condemn them utterly,
but there are amendments in these words
that I think we ought to make. They
will come in at the end of the rest of
my amendment, or perhaps in some other
part of the Bill. The words in the Bill
scrupulously follow the words in the
principal Act, the Act of 1920. They
are an exact copy, including the capital
C for council. They are as careful as that.
But we have used up the expression.
"The council already in line 22.
and I think it is desirable that we should
emphasise again what we mean. We
found in the past that the county council
were not too ready to take advantage of
their powers, and I think there can be no
harm in emphasising it further. That is
the first small point.
The second small point is the submis
sion to the Minister of Home Affairs.
That is still in accordance with the
original Act, and with the English Act
except that it is the Minister of Health
in England, while we have in line 7 the
Ministry of Home Affairs. We cannot
have the two expressions, I think, standing together. I am afraid that both expressions make sense, and it would require
formal amendment to alter either
of them. The third
regards the
section.'
a
two
point is

as
final words this
Is it really intended that
the scheme to be submitted and
applied is under what will be the new
Section 1 of the principal Act or is it
limited, as I think the intention is, to the
new Sub-section (1) and, I hope, the new
Sub-section 1A? I may be wrong about
that, but if I am we can make the correction later. But bearing in mind that 1A,
as it now appears before us, is something
quite new, and bearing in mind also the
words I am attacking at the moment were
not in the original Bill, but were inserted
in another place, I have come to the conclusion that I am right, and that these
words are not intended to extend to the
Blind Persons Bill-
Committee
5 MAY 1938
new Section, as a whole. That is a small
matter, and in the event of our inadvertently falling into error we can correct
ourselves at a later stage.
I pass to the main part of the Amendment, the words that I propose to interpolate. I repeat that the reason I have
moved in the matter is the very harsh
definition in Clause 4. That definition,
I believe, was not intended to apply
throughout the 1920 Act. I believe it
was limited to the first Section. That
was the Section which reduced the
age
of
pension. But if we pass Clause 4 as it
stands we shall be, so to speak, riveting
the chains of that Section on the necks
of people who are blind, but not so blind
as to be pensionable. If we leave it as it
is we shall be shutting out the man who is
a person who by reason of defective or
failing eyesight is becoming, or in danger
of becoming, unable to perform ordinary
work from those provisions.
The Clause or Sub-clause I am endeavouring to alter really speaks of three
things. It speaks of workshops, then of
technical education, and it deals with
hostels. It also gives power to the council
to do other things. My Amendment does
not touch the question of hostels. I am
not making any suggestion that the intermediate class, the people suffering from
defective eyesight, should be allowed to
come into the hostels. But what I do
want is to have them admitted as of right
into classes generally arranged for technical education which would be of some
use to them. I believe that the powers
really exist already. They exist under
the Education Act. It is Section 22 of
the Act of 1923. It shall be the duty of
the education authority to make provision
for affording persons resident in the
education area by means of day or evening continuation classes or otherwise
technical instruction as in this Section defined. That is as much as I want.
say there we have the dry bones of the
right or duty, at all events, to provide
technical instruction for persons, and
from that technical education the semiblind person is not excluded.
I
Again it will be remembered that under
part 9 of the Act dealing with afflicted
children we have direct power given. The
afflicted child, the Committee will remember, continues to be subject to the
requirements of elementary education
until it attains the age of 16 years,
instead of 14. Section 57 of the 1923
Act says it shall be within the power of
any local authority which may after such
child has reached the age of sixteen years
become wholly or partly responsible for
its care to make arrangements with the
education authority for the maintenance
and education of such child for such
further period beyond the age of sixteen
years as the Ministry of Home Affairs
may approve. So we really have semiblind persons of two classes, the person
who has been an afflicted child in that
sense and the man who has not. I think
we ought to provide for them technical
education if they are capable of benefiting by it.
I say it was the intention of the Act of
1920 that that should be provided. I
will, if I may, remind the Committee of
the provisions of that principal Act. The
passage I am about to read now is subsection (6) of the same Section 2. We
are told that:
Nothing in this section shall affect
the powers and duties of local education
authorities under the Elementary
Education (Blind and Deaf Children)
Act, 1893, or the Education Acts,
1870 to 1919, and local education
authorities in the exercise of their duty
to contribute to the establishment of a
national system of public education
available for all persons capable of profiting thereby, shall make or otherwise
secure adequate and suitable provision
for the technical education of blind
persons ordinarily resident in their
area who are capable of receiving and
being benefited by such education.
I
say that that sets the stamp of
further approval on the duty which
existed in England before 1920 of providing technical instruction. We had, of
course, Technical Instruction Acts of our
own, but these have been relegated to the
background by the big Act of 1923. It
is the definition of 1923 which is wider
than the definition that went before that
I want to see enshrined in the Bill before us.
What I am really now asking the Committee to do is to make it obligatory to
use the powers which I say the authorities possess to help the man who cannot
follow his ordinary occupation to learn
to follow an occupation which his limited
capacities in the way of eyesight enable
him to follow. I want the Committee to
Blind Persons Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
abstain from shutting out the man from
the beneficence of Parliament merely because he is not blind enough. If by
means of these amendments we can make
the dry bones of the Act of 1923 live
we shall do a great deal of good. I do not
think we are really imposing any new
liability on anybody, but we are taking,
or shall be taking, steps to ensure that
the powers which already exist are used.
The whole thing can be worked between
the councils and the education authority
under the scheme which is spoken of in
these words which for the moment we
are taking out. I think it is right to say
that the councils in future will be able to
perform a statutory duty and also benefit
a singularly helpless class.
Mr. ROBB: It is a little difficult to
know exactly what it is my hon. and
learned Friend wants to do. On the
face of it his Amendment seeks to give
powers to the authorities, the councils of
the counties and county boroughs, to train
people who are not blind within the
meaning of this Act, but who are becoming blind. So far as that is concerned the Government cannot accept
that for this reason, that there has been
a great deal of investigation by different
committees the Advisory Committee on
the Blind in particular, as to the extent
to which these Blind Persons Acts should
go, and the only extent to which it has
been recommended they should go in
England, and the only extent to which
the law is put in England is to deal
with people who are blind within the definition of the Act, that is, so blind
that they cannot do work.
The difficulties attendant upon framing a standard below that are very great,
and this Government is not prepared to
go any further than the Government in
England is, at the moment, in extending
the operation of these Acts to persons
who are partially blind. It is not that
the Government has not got sympathy
with these people, but that they do not
see in their own mind a scheme that
can be in all probability usefully applied
to people of that class.
There is one further point. The suggestion is that technical education should
be given in some form to a person who
is not yet blind, but the experts have discovered that if you train a person who
still can see to do a certain operation,
when he ceases to be able to see you have
to train him afresh. But these are not
all the difficulties. In no single area would
there be any large number of persons who
are going blind, and if there are only one
or two there are great difficulties in forming technical classes, on the ground of
expense, if nothing else. In addition,
you would require special teachers, and
it has not been shown to the satisfaction
of any Government department that it
would be feasible to provide what is asked
for here except in one or two schools to
which persons of this class would be
drafted.
That would mean taking them away
from their homes and putting them in
hostels or giving them some form of allowance. The Government does not see that
there is any feasible scheme for working
out this charity, if you like to call it
that. The numbers would necessarily be
small; the tuition would have to be
special; allowances would have to be
given, and there would be the difficult
question of decisions as to whether persons were in danger of becoming unable
to perform their ordinary work, and yet
were capable of receiving benefit from
technical instruction. These are all practical difficulties in the way of carrying out
a scheme of this sort.
It is quite true that all the hon. and
learned Member proposes is to confer
powers. The conferring of powers is a
very innocuous thing, but then he goes
on, by an Amendment to the last part of
the Clause, to impose upon county councils
and the county boroughs of Belfast and
Londonderry the duty of submitting to
the Ministry of Home Affairs a scheme
for carrying out this work. In the opinion
of the Ministry, the cost would be prohibitive considering the amount of good that
would result. It is not a question of lack
of sympathy with the object, but apprehension of the difficulties that surround
it. As the Ministry has not a workable
scheme in mind they do not consider it is
proper to insert any powers in the Bill,
at least until the time comes when someone will devise a suitable scheme. On
these grounds the Government cannot
accept the Amendment.
Sir JAMES WILTON: I can quite see
the difficulties there are in putting in a
Bill a definition of a blind person that
would be acceptable. In my opinion the
Blind Persons Bill-
Committee
5 MAY 1938
present definition is too harsh, but as regards the Amendment before the Committee it seems to me that it creates a
new class of persons. One of the objections mentioned by the right hon. Gentleman was that the Government could not
conceive of a suitable scheme to carry it
into effect. As I read the Amendment
any scheme submitted by a local authority
would be subject to the approval of the
Ministry of Home Affairs. What is the
right hon. Gentleman's objection to allowing the local authorities to have a shot at
it? The Ministry might be able to get
some sort of scheme from some council
that would be acceptable.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
afraid that if this Amendment were
passed there would be considerable confusion. We know there are a large number
of people with defective eyesight. In the
workshops there are people working with
the aid of very strong glasses, and it will
be very difficult to decide whether persons whose sight is defective will eventually become blind. It was quite proper
that education authorities should be given
the power to provide treatment for school
children whose eyesight was defective.
They were also given the power to provide dental treatment and treatment for
deafness, but there are very few persons
between forty and fifty who have not got
defective teeth.
I have had some experience of the
administration of the Blind Persons Act,
and I know the difficulties that we had
to contend with. The only thing we
could rely upon was the doctor's evidence,
and even doctors differ so far as the definition of blindness is concerned. In
many cases we had to send persons to the
umpire to decide whether they were so
blind as to be unable to perform work.
I am afraid the Amendment would create
even more difficulties than already exist,
and that it would throw a tremendous
burden on the local authorities. Girls
with very bad eyesight were able to take
domestic service, and the only successful
training centre I know of is the Workshops for the Industrious Blind, where
blind persons are trained in basket-making, knitting, and other similar work.
We found from the reports of the
superintendent that when a person who
was put to one class of work was a failure
he was put to another class at which he
did a little better, but these were blind
persons. and not persons who were likely
to become blind.
In my opinion this
Amendment would create great confusion
and throw a tremendous burden on local
authorities.
Mr. LESLIE: I did not quite appreciate whether Senator Cunningham
understood that I was not making any
request for making any allowance in
money to these people, nor was I dealing
with the question of pensions. I was
urging the admission of such people to
education classes. The Leader of the
House spoke of the number of centres that
would be required for the giving of such
instruction. I do not know how many
centres there are at the present moment.
Are there any at all outside Belfast?
Mr. ROBB: You referred to classes
created for these people. They would
have to be new classes in different centres.
Mr. LESLIE: Evidently the right
hon. Gentleman does not think it possible
to do anything at the existing technical
schools, and apparently the Ministry, not
being able itself to formulate a scheme,
is quite satisfied that nobody else can do
it either. That is an attitude which I
am afraid I have met before. The Mayor
of Derry, like myself, did not apparently
accept that as a panacea for all ills. The
more important point is to switch the
right hon. Gentleman back to something
he said the other day. He has reiterated
the sympathy of the Government, which
costs nothing and effects but little, but he
did make some sort of suggestion that
some legislation might be brought forward
in the future. Did he contemplate that
there is any probability of that legislation being brought forward during the
present session?
Mr. ROBB: No. What I had
in mind was this: that it was our
intention to bring the matter up before the Advisory Council for the
Blind and ask if they could recommend any method suitable for dealing with these people. When we get their
answer I will undertake that the Government will very carefully consider it.
Mr. LESLIE: I presume that if the
steps recommended by the Advisory
Council can be taken without legislation.
they will be taken without legislation;
Blind Persons Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
that is to say, by communication with the
technical education authorities, because I
believe they have got the power now. If
it means that we would be content to
leave it there.
Mr. ROBB: As Minister of Education
may I say that if anything is proposed
to the Ministry that we think can be
done within the limits of the existing
technical school legislation, I will undertake that an effort will be made to do it.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that on that
we shall be content to ask leave to withdraw the Amendment. What about the
last few words? They do really require a
little revision.
Mr. ROBB: I do not think they do.
At the beginning of the Clause it says,
"It shall be the duty of the council of
every county and county borough." Subsequently that is called "the Council,"
and I think those words are sufficient to
cover the matter.
Mr. LESLIE : The more important
point is the end. Do you mean the whole
Section, or the foregoing provisions?
Mr. ROBB: I mean the foregoing provisions. That is all that is left.
Mr. LESLIE: No. It is not all that
is left. You are adding 1A.
There are
other parts of the principal Act.
Mr. ROBB: The powers under that
section, the full section.
Mr. LESLIE: There are seven subsections. Perhaps we had better consider
that later on. I think I may ask leave
to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. SPEAKER: Does the hon. and
learned Member wish to move the next
Amendment?
Mr. LESLIE: Yes. That is a separate
thing altogether. That is an Amendment to the new sub-section la, which
it is proposed to insert in Section 1 of
the principal Act, the Blind Persons Act,
1920, which provides that the Council
of a county or county borough may pay
the expenses of the funeral of a blind
person ordinarily resident in
district.
their
I beg to move in sub-clause la
to leave out "or county borough" and
insert "county or other borough or
urban district." We are not given any
idea of the arrangements. As a matter
of fact, the time between the death and
the funeral is not generally very long.
It does not give time for much consultation with the authority as to whether a
contribution is or is not to be forthcoming. I did think at the time, and I still
think that power should be given not only
to the county council or council of a
county borough, but also to the other
rate-making authorities, that is, the
borough and urban district councils. I do
not see why power should not be given to
these rate-making authorities, and accordingly I put down the Amendment.
I ought to explain that there is power
given in one of those innumerable subsections of Section 2 of the Act of 1920
which runs as follows: The expenses incurred by a council under this section
shall be defrayed in the case of a county
council out of the poor rate. So that
whatever kind of council we take the
power is still there.
I beg to move.
Mr. ROBB: The explanation of it is
this. The scheme under this Blind Persons Act is for the county council or
corporation of a county borough to deal
with. The urban district council or
rural district council have nothing to do
with it. Consequently, the urban district.
council is not the authority and has
nothing to do with existing legislation.
It is the county and borough councils.
Accordingly this power is confined to
them.
Mr. LESLIE: Surely that is a most
hopeless attitude to take. Does the right
hon. Gentleman remember that when the
principal Act was passed in 1920, seventeen years ago, there were no such things
as borough councils at all? I do not
think that even Coleraine was a borough
council in 1920. There were, of course,
the counties of the cities, but since 1920
we have called into existence Coleraine
and Bangor, and we are about to make
boroughs of Newtownards and Ballymena,
and Larne is in the offing. Surely it is
not to the county and county borough we
should limit what is done?
Blind Persons Bill---
Committee
5 MAY 1938
Mr. ROBB: What he speaks of here
are the urban district councils.' There
have been urban district councils since
1899, and the point about what has happened since 1920 is not relevant to the
Amendment. There were urban district
councils since the 1st of April, 1899.
Sir JAMES WILTON: I understand
that Ballymena has been mentioned. Do
I understand that the urban district of
Ballymena has nothing to do with putting this into force? In other words, if
there are a number of people in Ballymena anxious to take advantage of this
is it to the county council they must
apply?
Mr. ROBB: That is right.
Mr. LESLIE: Then we are left in this
way, that when an unfortunate blind
person dies in Ballymena application must
be made by his relatives to the county
council, which has its office in the County
Courthouse, Crumlin Road, Belfast.
Surely the only hope of making it work
is to spread it as far as you can. I am
very much inclined to suspect it is the
intention to run this thing on the cheapest
lines possible and to prevent any expenditure that possibly can be avoided.
Speaking generally, in 1899 when the
urban district councils were formed I
doubt very much if there were many
urban districts with a population of
10,000. There certainly
boroughs. I think they will be capable
of dealing with this matter.
were
no
is
Mr. ROBB: As far as economy
concerned the economy will be the county
council's, and not the Government's. If
there is any niggardliness it is displayed.
by the county council and corporations of
the two boroughs. I do not see any
reason why this one small item in the
administration of the law for blind persons should be taken out of the hands
of the people who administer all the rest
of it, and have a new department set up
by the urban and district councils. It
was always confined to the county council
and the corporations of the two cities.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I have great
sympathy with the mover of the amendment, but I think the explanation given
by the Leader of the House is valid. The
blind persons at their death automatically
come in for funeral expenses. All that
is required is to communicate with the
secretary of the county council of the
area in which the blind person died. Even
if it were made the urban or district
council I am afraid the clerk would have
in the first place to get in touch with
the county council. Therefore, from the
point of view of expediency, the county
council is best.
Sir JAMES WILTON: My only reason
for supporting the Amendment is that the
effect of leaving out the urban council is a
temptation to the urban council to shirk
its work.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I can sympathise
and understand the point of view of the
hon. and learned Senator, but it occurs
to me that the Amendment would mean
that the county council would be charged
with this man during his lifetime, and it
would be only at his death he would pass
over to the urban council.
Mr. LESLIE: As the Bill stands there
is constant dove-tailing between the Bill
As our Bill is now
and the existing Act.
it will be the duty of the council of the
county or county borough to submit a
scheme for carrying out the powers under
the whole Section 1 of the 1920 Act,
and this new fig. 1A will be part of that
Section. Consequently, I presume the
county council will submit a scheme for
carrying out their duties and contributing
to the funeral.
Mr. ROBB: That is what the Section
means.
Mr. LESLIE: Then we can leave the
matter, and I ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause 2 ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

resident in that area so long as he con-

Amendment not moved.
Clause 3 ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE (4 (Interpretation)—
In the principal Act and in this Act
the expression "blind person" means a
person so blind as to be unable to perform any work for which eyesight is
essential; and in this Act the expression "medical assistance" includes the
supply of medicine.
There are two
I am
Mr. SPEAKER:
Amendments down to Clause 4.
afraid these must be ruled out of order,
because the effect of these Amendments
would be to enlarge the definition of
blind persons under this Bill, and, there-
1 fore, increase the number eligible for
State pension. Therefore, the Amendment is outside the power of this House.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I am sure it is
the anxiety of every Member of the Senate
to defend the liberty and privilege of the
House, and every Member of the House
desires to give you assistance. But lest
by chance you might be wrong in your
ruling I would like to ask you what are
the Sections of the Acts of Parliament,
and by what authority you rule this
matter out of order.
Mr. SPEAKER: By the Government
of Ireland Act the Senate cannot amend
Bill in so far as to increase the charge
any
on the public.
Mr. ROBB: It is Section 16 of the
Government of Ireland Act, and I think
it is Sub-section (3).
Mr. MCALLISTER: I suggest that it
is very doubtful whether that Section
applies to such a Bill as this. It is not
a Finance Bill we are dealing with. I
quite agree that we may have no power
to amend a Finance Bill, but I submit
that we have power to put down an
Amendment to a Bill of this description
and to discuss it.
Mr. SPEAKER: I would point out to
the hon. Member that the appropriate
Section of the Government of Ireland Act
does not refer to a financial Bill. It says
the Senate cannot amend any Bill so as to
increase the charge on the public, and
this Amendment would increase the
charge.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I move that this
House do now adjourn until we have
had time to go into the question of
whether or not this Amendment is in
order.
Mr. ROBB: Perhaps I might read the
Section. It is Section 16, sub-section
(3), of the Government of Ireland Act.
It reads as follows:
"The Senate of Southern Ireland or
Northern Ireland may not amend any
Bills so far as they impose taxation
or appropriate revenue or moneys for
the services of the Government of
Southern Ireland or Northern Ireland,
or for services administered by the

Council of Ireland, and may not amend

any
charges or burdens on the people.'

mean a financial Bill imposing taxation
or something of that sort. Surely it cannot be suggested that that Section covers
an
Amendment of this description. I
suggest, with great respect, that that
section relates to a a purely financial
measure which imposes taxation.
Mr. SPEAKER: As Speaker of this
House it is my duty to interpret the
Rules, and that is my ruling. Of course,
if the hon. Member disagrees with my
ruling he can put down a Motion of
Censure.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I am not going
to put down a Motion of Censure, but
at the right time I will move the rejection
of this Bill and expose the manner in
which the Government are dealing with
these people.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: While I appreciate your ruling, I think we all agree
that the present definition of a blind
person is very harsh. If under this Bill
blind persons are entitled to a pension
the public of the Six Counties are prepared to give them that pension.
Sir JAMES WILTON rose.

I have a promise that that question will

Motion for Adjournment.
THE TRANSPORT INQUIRY.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
House do now adjourn until Tuesday
next."
It
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
desire to raise a matter of some pressing
public importance. The Government
have appointed two Transport Commissions, one of which is being held in public,
to inquire into the working of the Rail
and Road Transport Act of 1935.
appears that some action may possibly
arise, though I do not wish at the
moment to suggest what that action will
be. I think it may be very important
that Members of this House should know
the facts and figures which are brought
before this Commission. These facts
and figures will no doubt appear in the
Press from time to time, but anyone who
has any experience of judicial proceedings
knows that facts and figures as they
appear in the Press-and I do not wish
to say anything about Press reporting;
it is very accurate so far as it goes-may
not be sufficient for our purposes as the
facts we may require to know may not
always have a news value.
Therefore I make the suggestion that
the Government should be in a position,
when the report is furnished, to produce
the evidence, and for that purpose to
have a verbatim report of the evidence
taken, so that if it is necessary for Members of this House to know what the facts
and figures are the Government can produce them. I think the Members of this
House ought to be at least in as good a
position as those persons who, having
nothing much better to do, are able to
go day after day and listen to the evidence given, while Members of this
House are too busy to be able to spare
all that time. It is reasonable to ask the
Government to put them in as good a
position as those who can listen to the
evidence.
Mr. ROBB: I have not had time to
find out exactly what is happening in
connection with this Commission, but I
know as a fact that a verbatim report of
the evidence is being taken.
Mr. McALLISTER: I can quite understand the Leader of the House being
unable to answer the question, but I
hope he will consider it in the meantime.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Five o'clock until Tuesday next.

Criminal Lunatics Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: This is a short Bill, and I
do not think the House will have any
difficulty in according it a Second Reading. In the year 1929, by the Criminal
Criminal Lunatics Bill-
THE SENATE
Second Reading
(Mr. Robb]
Lunatic Act (Northern Ireland) there
was allocated for the purposes of the
criminal lunatic asylum "the ground floor
tier of the main block of 'the Londonderry
Prison and the three exercise yards
adjacent thereto.'
The number of
criminal lunatics for which provision must
be made has shown no tendency to increase to any extent, but the premises set
aside under the Act of 1929 have proved
insufficient to allow adequate accommodation for recreation purposes
for the
patients, the majority of whom will in
all probability remain in the institution
for the rest of their lives.
During the greater part of the day in
winter, and at all times when the weather
is inclement, the patients have to remain
indoors, and under existing circumstances
the only room available both for dining
and recreation purposes is of some 330
This space
square feet superficial area.
is insufficient and has an adverse effect on
the health, physical and mental, of the
patients. Should Parliament pass the
Measure at present under consideration
the Minister of Home Affairs proposes to
make additional space available in the
disused hospital block of the prison which
is situated only some 20 feet from the
present recreation room.
It has been thought preferable to draft
the Bill so as to allow discretion to the
Minister of Home Affairs to utilise from
time to time such part or parts of the
prison as may be necessary for the
purpose, rather than to make a specific
amendment of the Schedule of the Act
of 1929. The numbers of the patients
to be provided for may alter from time
to time, and it is considered reasonable
that authority should be given to allocate
the necessary space within the prison by
a Ministerial Order rather than that the
House should be asked to pass legislation
on each occasion when a change in the
arrangements is rendered necessary,
has happened in this case.
as
I beg to move, "That the Bill be now
read a Second time.'
Mr. LESLIE: There is no objection,
obviously, to the Bill itself. It is a
perfectly normal and natural recognition
of development, but the method is, of
course, a little unusual. It has to be,
but I do not think there is any objection.
ment.
What the Bill really seeks to do is to
give the Minister power by notice to override a plain provision of an Act of Parlia-
It does that definitely, and I do
not know whether there is any precedent
for doing it in this way, but really I do
not think it matters. The new words
suggested in the Bill will become part of
the original Act, and, therefore, at the
next stage I shall suggest a slight alteration in the wording, the point being that
the insertion in the original Act will not
contain any information as to how the
notice is to be given. I think we ought
to put in the proviso which will go into
the original Act words to the effect that
the notice will be published in the Belfast
Gazette. That will obviate any possible
criticism.
The object of the Bill is admirable.
These unfortunate people are there, they
have not got enough room, and you want
to give them more. There is also the
possible difficulty that some of the accommodation at present provided might at
some time require repair or decoration,
and as the law stands there would not
seem to be any provision for putting these
unfortunate creatures anywhere else. As
I
say, the purpose of the Bill is admirable,
but I do think it requires the slight
amendment I have indicated.
Mr. ROBB: I would direct the hon.
and learned Member's attention to the
second sub-clause of Clause 1, which says
that any notice given by the Minister
of Home Affairs shall be published in the
Belfast Gazette.
Mr. LESLIE: But my point was that
the reference to the notice will not appear
in the original Act. That is where I
think it ought to go.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.

Transport Inquiry
10 MAY 1938
Motion for Adjournment.
REPORT OF TRANSPORT INQUIRY
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
House do now adjourn until Thursday."
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
would like to ask the Leader of the House
if he is now in a position to answer the
question which I put to him last Thursday
as to what steps the Government were
prepared to take to inform Members of
this House of the precise evidence, and
the facts and figures given before the
Commission now sitting on the Road and
Rail Transport Act, 1935.
Mr. ROBB: A verbatim report is being
taken of the proceedings of this Commission, and it has been arranged that two
copies of that Report will be left in the
Library for the use of Members of both
Houses.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Are Members of
both Houses expected to make use of only
two copies? Several Members may go
into the Library at the same time to study
the Report, and they will have to wait on
each other's convenience. I am afraid
that will not give Members sufficient
opportunity to study the Report.
Mr. ROBB: Frankly I do not think
there will be any great demand for this
Report, but if it is found that two copies
are not sufficient, I have no doubt that
arrangements can be made for additional
copies to be available.
Major DOBBS: Could not a copy be
distributed to every Member of the
House?
Mr. LESLIE: The point would seem
to be that it is not proposed to print
the verbatim report. I gather that these
copies will be either lithographed or typewritten. If they are in print there will
be no objection to circulating them freely,
but the expense of printing is perhaps
at the back of the right hon. Gentleman's mind, and that would be considerable. I do not altogether share his
views that there will be no great demand
to study this evidence. I think a great
many Members of both Houses will want
to see what has been said.
Mr. ROBB: I really cannot say
whether the report will be printed or not.
From time to time I have been present
at this Commission listening to the proceedings, and although it is possible that
the printed word may appear to be more
lively than the spoken word, anything
more dull than these proceedings I cannot imagine.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at half
after Three o'clock until
Thursday next.

This percentage would naturally be
somewhat larger than the percentage of

since eggs which were originally classified

Similar information in regard to

as
As regards the latter part of the
question, each "second" egg or "reject"
egg is, on being classified, marked on the
shell with the word "Second" or the
word "Reject," as the case may be.
"Rejects" are subsequently destroyed,
but "seconds" may be sold as such by
wholesale or retail. A considerable
quantity of "seconds" is sold to bakeries,
etc., for manufacturing purposes.
