the average ex-store prices at which
"seconds" were sold by licensed wholesale dealers during the past nine weeks
were as follows:

The Ministry has no information regarding the retail price of "seconds."
I should, perhaps, add that deductions
in respect of "seconds" have been made
each week at the rate of one halfpenny
per egg. It is intended that these deductions should be of such an amount as
would discourage producers from dispos
ing of their eggs otherwise than in a fresh
and marketable condition. If any
additional profit accrues to the dealers as
a direct result of these deductions this
factor is taken into account in fixing the
price of new-laid eggs.
177 Physical Training Bill-

12 MAY 1938
Second Reading
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the following Resolution, to which they desire the
concurrence of this House:-
Standing Orders relative to Local Bills.
-That there be inserted in the Schedule
of Fees-
"Provisional Order Confirmation Bills.
On Second Reading of a Confirmation
Bill a fee of £5 is charged to the applicants for each Provisional Order included
in the Bill.
The same fees are charged to applicants and opponents at the Committee
stage in the case of opposed Confirmation
Bills as in the case of Local Bills. No
other fees are charged."
They have passed the Superannuation
(Teaching Services) Bill, and the Weights
and Measures Bill, to which they desire
the concurrence of this House.
Superannuation (Teaching Services)
Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Ordered, That the Bill be read a
Second time on Tuesday next.
Weights and Measures Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Ordered, That the Bill be read a
Second time on Tuesday next.
Physical Training and Recreation Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: I think the object of this
Measure, which is to provide additional
facilities for physical
physical training and
recreation in Northern Ireland, will commend itself to every Member of this
House. I sincerely hope so indeed,
because the success of the Bill, and more
important still, the success of the movement to secure a healthier population, out
of which the Bill has grown, will depend
in no small degree upon the interest taken
in it by Members of both Houses of
Parliament.
The Bill is entirely permissive, and the
Government is relying upon the desire
of local authorities and voluntary
organisations, including public elementary and secondary schools, for its success.
Much excellent work is being
is being done
already by all of these bodies and, with
the financial encouragement given by the
Bill and the public interest in the
physical well-being of the population, an
interest which, it is hoped, will be continued and increased, I have no doubt
that Northern Ireland will be in the forefront of the campaign for physical fitness,
to the great benefit of the general happiness of the community.
The object of the Bill is to aid in the
provision of additional facilities for
physical exercise, and above all, to stimulate public interest in the advantage and
pleasure to be gained from the pursuit of
fitness. We in Northern Ireland are
fortunate in that every member of the
population is within easy reach of the
countryside, and the great majority
within easy reach of hills and sea. These
facilities for physical training and recreation are freely provided without Act of
Parliament, and I hope that the
campaign which has been inaugurated
will cause them to be used as freely as
they are given to us.
The Bill before the House follows in
great measure the Physical Training and
Recreation Act passed by the Imperial
Parliament in 1937. The differences,
which hon. Members will observe, between the two Measures are due to the
different needs of the two areas. The
1937 Act is practically confined to the
encouragement of the provision of facilities for the adult population and that
portion of the adolescent population
which has left school.
Northern Ireland, however, has considerable leeway to make up in providing
adequate facilities for the physical
pastimes of its school children, and
advantage has been taken of this opportunity to cater in some degree for them.
In view of this the Government decided
to make available a slightly larger sum
than that which would be strictly proportionate to the sum available in Great
Britain.
Another difference between the two
Measures is this. It was thought necessary in Great Britain to set up, in
addition to the National Advisory
Council, 22 area committees to encourage
the movement and assist in the adminis-
Physical Training Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Robb]
tration of the Act. The Government
have decided that in view of the comparative smallness of our area and population, one Council would be sufficient
for our needs. I would point out in this
regard that the areas having area committes in Great Britain are of considerable size and population, for instance.
London and Middlesex form one area,
Lancashire and Cheshire another, and
both of these have populations of over
six million persons. The constitution
and duties of the proposed Advisory
Council are set out in Clause 1 of the
Bill, which I think is self explanatory.
Clause 2 proposes the establishment of
a Grants Committee, with an independent chairman appointed by the
Prime Minister and, for the rest, consisting of a representative from each of
the Ministries of Education, Home
Affairs, Finance and Labour.
Although grants are to be made by
the Ministry of Education, they are
only to be made on the recommendation
of the Grants Committee, who, in the
cases of applications upon which they
desire advice, are empowered to consult
with the Advisory Council.
Clause 3 sets out most of the projects
towards the cost of which the Ministry
of Education may make grants. It will
be seen that the list is a fairly exhaustive one. Local authorities and
voluntary organisations may be assisted
by grants up to 50 per cent. of the cost
of gymnasiums, playing fields, swimming
baths, holiday camps, camping sites,
and generally of buildings and premises
for physical training and recreation.
When a grant is made to a voluntary
organisation for any of these purposes,
the Ministry may impose, conditions for
securing the continuity of the scheme
assisted. The total amount of all such
grants is limited by sub-section 3 of the
Clause to £65,000.
The Ministry may also assist local
authorities and voluntary organisations
in employing persons whose qualifications
are
approved by the Ministry, as
organisers or leaders of physical training
and recreation.
Clauses 4 and 5 are to enable local
authorities to make arrangements for
the provision, maintenance, equipment,
proper supervision and use of such
premises as they may desire to provide
under Clause 3. Clause 4 also enables
local authorities to co-operate with one
another or with voluntary organisations
in providing such premises.
Clause 6 enables the Ministry of
Education to contribute up to threequarters of the cost of training suitable.
persons as instructors or leaders of
physical training. This Clause is thought
to be necessary in that there are no suitable institutions in Northern Ireland for
the training of instructors and it is expected that it will be necessary to send
a certain number of persons to Great
Britain for training. How many instructors will be required we do not
know, or how many will offer themselves,
but we hope that with the encouragement the Ministry are providing
sufficient number will be forthcoming.
a
Clause 7 deals with the expenses of
administration, and Clause 8 is the
Interpretation Clause. The Government
hope that this campaign towards improving the physical condition of the people
generally, both the young and those who
are no longer young, may grow, and
provide in Northern Ireland a higher
standard of physical fitness generally, so
that people may not only be better able
to do their work but by the enjoyment
of better health they may be a happier
people. I beg to move, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I
must say that we are taking this
Bill perhaps a little quickly. We
read it a First time on Tuesday,
and this is Thursday. Consequently I
have not myself had time to study the
Bill as closely as I could have wished.
I did, as a matter of fact, read the Bill
when it was put into print after its first
appearance in another place, but I have
not, I am afraid, read the Bill at all
since it passed through the Committee
stage. Consequently I have got to speak
on the Bill with some reservation, perhaps, as to the accuracy of my observations. I should have hoped to have
been able to speak a little more decisively
on some points if I had had a little
more time. However, I do not think
there is any reason to suppose that the
House would refuse a Second Reading
to the Bill, and therefore any criticisms
that arise can be dealt with at a later
stage.
Physical Training Bill-
Second Reading
12 MAY 1938
There are some questions I want to
ask, and those, no doubt, the right hơn.
Gentleman will be able to reply to this
afternoon. He spoke of this Bill being
part of a campaign, and he also said it
followed very much on the lines of the
Bill which received the Royal Assent in
the United Kingdom Parliament on 13th
July last year. I should like to know
who has definitely asked for this Bill in
Northern Ireland; whether there has
been anything in the shape of a Northern
Ireland campaign for the application of
a similar Measure, or whether the Bill
has simply been brought in because a
Bill of the same class has been brought
in in England.
I rather gathered that the right hon.
Gentleman intimated that something in
the way in inquiry had been made
locally, because he drew attention to
certain differences between the Bill
before us this afternoon and the Act
that exists in Great Britain. We were
told-and indeed it is apparent from
the Bill itself that the Measure is
purely permissive, and I am very glad
that that is so, because the fact that it
is permissive allows action to be taken
under the Bill or action not to be taken
under the Bill, and I do not myself
think that the local authorities are likely
to take any very definite action at all.
I do not think that local authorities
generally, having due regard to the amount
of rates and the size of the rate, are at
all likely at present to embark on any
additional expense in spite of the grants
which are to be obtained. I do think
that the people who will apply for grants
are the voluntary organisations to which
reference has been made. I think the
hope of getting the Bill worked rests
rather there than amongst the local
authorities.
are of
Local authorities, of course,
different kinds. You have got the large
Corporation of Belfast, where a good deal
is done already in this direction and
where, I believe I am correct in saying,
there is no desire to do anything that
will increase the local rate. You have
other similar towns where again something is done, but then you get to the
rural district councils, and under this
Bill a rural district council, although
not a rate-making authority, is to be
encouraged to be a spending authority.
I do not myself believe that rural district
councils generally are likely to do much,
but that need not prevent voluntary
organisations from coming forward.
Another question I want to put is
about this question of money. Owing,
no doubt, to the rather cursory examination of the Bill to which I have
pleaded guilty I am a little in doubt as
to what the limit of expenditure is. We
have got a sum of £65,000 set out in the
proviso to the third sub-clause of Clause
3. Is that the outside limit, and is that
£65,000 to be spent at once, or is there
anything in the story that appeared from
the very brief reports in the newspapers
that the matter was to be spread over
three years. I cannot find anything in
the Bill about three years, but something was distinctly said in the newspapers about that.
Again, this £65,000 is to be a grant,
or rather is to furnish the money that
will be granted by the Ministry on the
recommendation of the Grants Committee, but is that the end of it, or is subclause (5) additional? Sub-clause (5) is
the one which extends the purposes for
which a grant may be made by the
Ministry of Education under the 1930
Education Act. It does not seem quite
clear whether anything spent in this
direction under the 1930 Act is to come
out of that £65,000, or whether it is to
be additional. That is a matter that
can be cleared up directly.
Taking the Bill by itself, there is
nothing to be said about Clauses 1 and
2, and Clause 3 I have just spoken
about. Then we come to Clause 4, which
deals with the purposes for which land
may be acquired and laid out and so on.
I think we have to ask ourselves very
seriously whether those powers are not
too wide, what exactly they mean, and
whether it is desirable that they should
be given to Northern Ireland.
I am
going to say that those powers go a
great deal beyond anything we have done
before. The House will remember that
the Education Act of 1923 contained a
provision giving powers which, until what
I was going to call the present fad for
further physical training manifested itself,
were thought to be ample. There are powers
there to take land for playgrounds and
so on. I do not know to what extent
they have been used, speaking generally,
but I was under the impression that
something material had been done here
and there throughout the Province.
Physical Training Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
At all events, there were powers for
the education authority given in 1923,
and they are still there. But again, in
this same Clause we talk of the acquisition and provision of suitable buildings
and so on, and clubs and societies and
organisations having social or educational
objects. We are not, apparently,
limited there to objects relating to
physical training. I thought for some
time, from one or other passage in the
English Bill and our own Bill, that the
intention was to limit the word "recreation" to physical recreation, but I am
afraid it is not so. So we have it, apparently, that a local authority, if it is
sufficiently complacent, and finds the
Ministry of Home Affairs sufficiently
pliable, can give money for the establishment of a club for playing darts as a
social object, and for the study of ancient
Gaelic as an educational object.
Sir JAMES WILTON: And shovehalfpenny.
a
Mr. LESLIE: The Mayor of Derry
suggests shove-halfpenny, but I am afraid
I do not know how much energy is required in the propulsion of the coin.
There seems no limit to the form of
amusement contemplated, but you have.
the great protection that a local authority
has got to do it, and I do not think there
is any serious danger that local
authority will incur the expense for some
of the matters mentioned. But we ought
to consider very carefully whether that
Clause is not rather too wide, and whether
we ought really to give the powers the
Bill proposes to give so far down as a
rural district council. It has not been
the practice hitherto to go below the
county council.
That brings you at once to the question
of the acquisition of land. The method
of acquiring land provided for in the Bill
originates with the old form of the Irish
Public Health Act of 1878, which, where
land was desired to be obtained under
compulsory powers, required, as the
House will remember, a Provisional
Order, to be confirmed by Parliament, for
which the same form of words is still used
in the United Kingdom Public Health Act
of 1935. But in the companion volume
to this Act, passed last year, approximately the same method is provided as
that which this Bill provides, and that
is the method depending on Section 24
of the Local Government Act of 1934.
That is to say, where a local authority
desires to acquire land for any of its purposes by compulsion, it may apply to the
Ministry for an Order, and the Ministry
may, after a public inquiry, if it thinks
it necessary to hold a public inquiry-and
it is not bound to do SO- - make a
vesting order. That has been found a
very convenient method of doing it, and
as a rule there has been no trouble,
because the necessity for taking land by
compulsion has not arisen.
un-
Here you have a suggested power to
take land for holiday camps and camping
sites. Those are purposes which a person
owning land may be not at all
likely to object to. Holiday camps are
not, in the country and seaside districts,
by any means universally popular. There
are objections made, sometimes on moral
and sometimes on sanitary grounds, but
taking it all round they are not very
popular. As the power to acquire land
will be taken by a local authority there
is some protection there, and the right
hon. Gentleman will be able to tell us
that in effect the powers of compulsory
acquisition will not be sanctioned except
in very extreme cases. If he is able to
say that he will be able to point to the
fact that the education authorities have
since 1923 had compulsory powers, and
how many times, if at all, those powers
have been exercised. I am not certain,
but I do not myself recollect that I have
ever heard of a case of the application of
compulsory powers at all, and we know
that the similar powers given to the
Ministry of Home Affairs for constabulary
barracks have been exercised once in the
last seven years.
Although I could not myself agree
wholeheartedly to any new extention of
the powers of compulsory acquisition of
land, I think that a statement of the kind
I have indicated would probably be
sufficient to prevent anybody objecting to
that part of the Bill on that ground.
There is, of course, the remaining power
of appeal to the court in the event of its
being sought to make a Vesting Order
not within the terms of the Statute, but
that does not arise. The protection of
the man who does not want his land
taken would seem to rest on the natural
185 Physical Training Bill-

12 MAY 1938
Second Reading
disinclination of a local authority to do
anything which would tend to annoy the
owner of the land, because it would not
be the owner's goodwill they would
acquire, and also the power of the
Ministry to refuse to sanction the
arrangement. I think, probably, that
will be all right, and nothing more
serious will arise there if the House is
of opinion that such power ought to be
given to a local authority.
Then again, this same Clause 4 gives
power to the Ministry of Home Affairs to
apply with or without modification
certain provisions of the Baths and
Wash-houses Acts. There are various
provisions in those Acts, as the
House will remember, but I should like
to be told how far the expression
"modification" is intended to go. If I
remember rightly there is a Schedule to
one of those Acts indicating what sort of
regulations are to be made. I think it
is prescribed that the Regulations made.
by the appropriate Ministry shall include
provision for the separate bathing of the
sexes, and for the prevention of children
over a certain age bathing except in the
manner indicated. I am not at all sure
whether in one of those Acts power is
not given to the authority to prescribe
something in the way of an order as to
the kind of clothing to be worn. I think
there is something of that sort, and those
are matters that might, perhaps, cause
discussion, although it does not, perhaps,
belong to what I am saying at the
moment.
I am coming to what does seem to be a
very serious difficulty in the Bill. It
arises under Clause 5. A local authority
is to have power to take land within or
without its own area. That power to
take land without the area of a local
authority certainly goes back to the
Public Health Act of 1878, and it would
be scarcely possible to manage without
such
a power. The same provision
occurs in the English Act of 1937, but
the English Act does not, if I remember
rightly, contain any powers such
those given by Clause 5 for the making of
bylaws. I think it is fairly obvious that
you will require bylaws for some of these
undertakings, but the difficulty that
occurs to me is this.
as
If your local authority takes land
without its district for the purpose of a
camping site how is it going to make
bylaws which are to be operative outside
its own area, and how is it going to avoid
any bylaws it may make clashing with
bylaws already in force in that other
area. I do not think that question has
had to be tackled in England, because
there is nothing in it, and there is
nothing in this Bill to indicate how it is
proposed to apply it here. It is obvious.
that such questions as regulating the
times of admission will have to be dealt
with by bylaws, and that raises the
question of whether these places are to
be open on a Sunday or not.
It might very well be that one local
authority might want a bylaw allowing
Sunday opening, whereas the local
authority within the area of the site
might be of a contrary opinion and want
the place closed on Sunday. Unless we
can do something there we may have endless difficulty. It might be possible to
insert a provision that bylaws applying
outside the law-making authorities are
only to be made after consultation with
the authority within whose area the site
happened to lie. If we do not I think
that if the Bill is taken advantage of at
all we shall have very considerable
trouble.
I do not know that there is anything
else that one sees at first sight about the
Bill, except the very great danger that
there may be of the powers given being
misused, and the very grave doubt I
have in my own mind as to whether such
powers ought to be committed to a rural
district council. I would rather have
kept them to the county council. All
that is without prejudice to the assistance
to be given to voluntary organisations,
from whom more help is to be expected
than from the local authorities.
I notice two cases in the Bill in which
it seemed to me there was something in
the nature of insufficient elasticity. I
may mention them, perhaps. but I should.
not put anything on the Paper about
them, because the money grant has been
made in another place, presumably with
the intention that the Bill should go
through enacting this particular restriction. Neither of them, I think, can be
described as large matters, but it might
be worth while to mention them. It is
as regards the grants to schools. There
are several powers taken, one of them
187 Physical Training Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
very admirable, to provide for the continuity of the instruction. Those of us
who live in the country have only too
bitter experience of this or that club
starting with a flourish of trumpets and
with all the faddists behind it to-day, and
a year or two later seeing what happens
to that hall or club. So the Government
is to be congratulated on importing from
England that provision regarding continuity.
But there is a provision that in the
event of a grant being given to a school
it shall be made a condition that the
facilities shall be open to outsiders. It
seemed to me that to prescribe that as
definite is perhaps a little rash. The
point is, of course, that again some of
us may be aware of cases where schools,
not public elementary schools, in Northern Ireland have certain facilities open,
perhaps, to old boys, and where the result
has been that the scholar of the day has
been, in fact, crowded out. However, I
suppose that the right hon. Gentleman
is satisfied that he can sufficiently protect
the pupil of to-day, who is the person
to whom his energies would naturally be
primarily directed.
I
The other question was as regards these
allowances for instructors. An allowance for training instructors is, apparently, only to be paid for training at an
approved college, which has to be in
Great Britain or Northern Ireland.
wonder whether it is wise to limit it
like that. The right hon. Gentleman
said they were in grave doubt as to the
extent to which that power would be
called upon.
It did occur to me that
when you are going in for physical training a good deal of instruction could be
obtained, I believe, still in Sweden,
which was the original country from
which it came, and also in Germany, and
I wonder whether it is wise to limit your
college to Great Britain and Northern
Ireland.
It may be that it is a very simple
thing, but if you really want to obtain
the best physical training it is not wise
to limit the places where you are going
to seek the instruction. That is a very
small point, and the Bill comes to us with
that limitation in it, and I should rather
hesitate to suggest making alteration,
although I shall hope to receive some
information on the points I have raised.
The only thing I should think likely to
require attention in Committee is the
rather difficult problem of the bylaw
made by the providing authority by
reason of something provided outside its
own area.
Mr. JOSEPH
CUNNINGHAM: I
would like to say how pleased I am to see
the Government introducing this Bill. I
believe that every person in Northern
Ireland who has any pride in his country
and its people is most anxious that our
people and we have a fairly good stock
-should compare favourably physically
with the people in England, Scotland and
Wales. We are all anxious to see them
receiving the physical training necessary
to develop them on the same lines as
across the water. The hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) asked who had asked
for this Bill. Some months ago quite a
number of people spoke to me and asked
me what I thought the Government were
likely to do as regards formulating a
physical training scheme for Northern
Ireland on similar lines to that in operation in Great Britain. I put down a
question in this House, and the reply of
the Government was to the effect that
they were watching the Bill in the
Imperial Parliament.
In Northern Ireland there is ample
scope for physical training. Some years
ago I served on a juvenile instruction
committee, and several Australians came
over here to interview candidates who
might be suitable to emigrate to
Australia. They told us they had
examined many fine young men, but.
they said, they were flabby; they had
indulged in no exercise, they were weaklings, and these Australians expressed
surprise that these young people had not
exercised themselves in some way. That
suggested to me that some scheme of
physical training was necessary, even in
the interests of the unemployed men and
girls in the Province, because I believe
that if the facilities were made available
there might be a craze for physical training, and there would be no necessity for
any compulsion.
In Northern Ireland we are fortunate
in having the best places possible for
hiking and other forms of physical training. English, Scotch and Welsh people
come here and hike through the country.
and surely that is physical training of
189 Physical Training Bill-

12 MAY 1938
Second Reading
the most beneficial kind. Then we have
hills and sea, and what better exercise
can
The
one have than swimming.
authorities in Belfast have provided
swimming baths, but in comparison with
Great Britain there are not enough
swimming baths in proportion to the
respective populations. Some of us who
were members of the Belfast Corporation
pressed for more swimming baths, but
the local authorities do not desire to
increase the rates, although I do not
believe there are many ratepayers who
would object to a penny rate for the purpose of providing better swimming
facilities than we have at present.
Better swimming facilities could also be
provided at Ballymoney and Ballymena
and other towns, and it would be a great
benefit to the younger people.
If this scheme is taken up thoroughly
by the authorities who will be responsible
for its administration, I do not think
there will be any need for compulsion,
and it will be one of the finest things that
could be provided for our young people.
I have a great pride in our young people
because I think they are second to none.
If they are enabled to indulge in this
physical training they will very soon
compare favourably with their brothers
and sisters across the water in this
respect. While we are very fortunate in
having hills and sea within our reach we
have not got the commons that they
possess in England for racing and
recreation, and therefore it is necessary
for the Government to have power to
acquire land compulsorily for the purpose of utilising it for physical training.
I have very much pleasure in supporting
the Second Reading.
Sir JAMES WILTON: I also welcome
this Bill, and I intend to support it. I
disagree with Senator Leslie in welcoming the fact that the Measure is only
permissive. I am afraid the fact that it
is permissive will lead some local authorities to ignore it completely. I am, however, hopeful that if one or two or three
or four local authorities take advantage
of the Act other local authorities also
will be forced by their ratepayers to put
the provisions of the Act into force in
their areas. The hon. and learned
Senator also objected to outsiders being
allowed to take advantage of the facilities provided by gymnasiums and other
.
recreational institutions. I welcome
that provision, because I have in mind
my own secondary school which is provided with an excellent gymnasium and
swimming bath which at present is used
by the pupils only in school hours
whereas it could be used by outsiders if
a grant was given and it was made
available.
Colonel SINCLAIR: May I add a few
words of congratulation to the Government on the introduction of this Bill.
It is a most useful Measure, and I hope
that when the Advisory Council and
various Committees are appointed to
administer the Act some of the Members
at least will be recruited from members
of the medical profession who have distinguished themselves in various forms
of sport. Clause 6, which provides for
the training of instructors, is, I think, a
very important Clause. It is very
necessary that the instruction for
physical training should be regulated
and controlled and carried out in such
a way that the greatest care is taken
that early adolescents, both boys and
girls, are not over-trained.
It is very important that there should
be some proper organisation to control
the training of these young people so as
to ensure that in trying to improve the
physical condition of the people generally
the lives of a number of very promising
boys and girls may not be blemished. I
have had a long experience of boys and
girls being permanently blemished
because they were allowed to carry out
their physical training more by their
own spirit and instinct than by a well
regulated method. The instructors
should be carefully trained at some
approved centre, because there is
apparently no plethora of
of training
instructors in this Province.
We can do with a great many more
physical training instructors than there
are at the moment. I hope that attention
will be paid to these points, and that
as a result of the passing of this Bill the
physical condition of the people of Ulster
will be immensely improved, so that the
condition of the youths referred to by
the visitors from Australia will be
exchanged for a more uniform physical
development, provided we do not overdo
it in certain directions.
Physical Training Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
While I
this
Sir ROLAND NUGENT:
welcome the introduction of
Bill I agree with Senator Leslie that
there are certain minor points that might
be looked into. Unlike most of the
previous speakers I am rather sorry that
there is not an element of compulsion in
this scheme. The two outstanding
successes of physical training schemes
are Germany and Italy, and in both
cases they are compulsory. I suppose
we must first
trial
give it a
voluntary basis, but at the same time
one cannot get away from the fact that
most physical training is very dull and
yet it is controlled physical training
under qualified instructors that is beneficial to one.
on a
We
I should like to support what was said
by the last speaker that this instruction
should be given only by qualified
teachers. In the Bill itself there does
not seem to be any provision regulating
the qualifications of instructors.
who have trained recruits know how
extraordinarily easy it is to strain a
young man by injudicious physical training, and how very skilled a teacher must
be to avoid that. I am rather alarmed
at the prospect of a partially qualified
person who many years ago was a staff
sergeant being appointed to carry out
this instruction, and I should like the
House to consider whether some method
of licensing instructors should be introduced to make sure that a teacher is
properly qualified.
Major DOBBS: I should like to ask
one question. I am not certain whether
I heard the Leader of the House
correctly. What I understood him to
say was that in the case of a local
voluntary organisation it would get only
a percentage of the cost of providing a
gymnasium and other necessary equipment. I hope I was wrong, because it
seems to me that if you are not going
to make this scheme compulsory you will
have to rely mainly on your local
voluntary organisations, and if you could
recoup them the whole expense it would
help them enormously.
I do not altogether care for that part
of the Bill which refers to camping sites.
The provision of swimming baths, playing fields, and so on is excellent.
Schemes of physical training should not
be confined to gymnasium work.
I was
at the Spring Show in Dublin last year
and I saw a physical display given by the
police to music and it was remarkably
good. What appealed to me tremendously was that they were all on their
toes.
Mr. ROBB: I would like to reply to
some of the questions that have been
raised. Senator Leslie asked a good
many. He inquired who had asked for
this Bill. There were some requests for
this Bill, but I think I may be allowed
to say that if there is any credit going
for initiating this movement the Government is entitled to it. It is quite true
that there were some inquiries made as
to what we were doing in this matter,
but, on the whole, we are entitled to any
credit that is to be got for the introduction of this Measure. Senator Leslie
also
asked how the expenditure of
£65,000 was to be borne, if at all. In
England what they arranged to do was
this: The Government agreed to give
a grant of £800,000 for each of three
years.
Mr. LESLIE: For three years?
Mr. ROBB: For each of three years
-£2,400,000 altogether. Our proportion
on a sum like that for our population
would be £60,000. We got the Minister
of Finance to stretch a little, and he
said we might have £65,000, but as the
campaign for physical fitness had really
not begun in this area it is not proposed
in our Bill to limit the expenditure
strictly within the three years. The idea
is that there shall be provided, possibly
within the three years, but not necessarily within three years, a total sum of
£65,000. Senator Leslie also asked did
the total amount include all the grants
under the Bill. There will be current
expenditure which will not be included.
Mr. LESLIE: There was a point as
to whether the grants under Section 10
of the Education Act, 1930, would come
out of the £65,000.
Mr. ROBB: No, they will not.
Mr. LESLIE: They are additional?
Physical Training Bill-
Second Reading
12 MAY 1938
Mr. ROBB: Yes. As to the question
dealing with the powers under Clause 4
it is proposed that the procedure in that
respect be by provisional order and not
by Act. With regard to the question
of the acquisition of land in the area of
another local authority a safeguard is
provided in that such land can only be
compulsorily acquired after a public
inquiry, held by order of the Ministry.
That, I think, would provide an opportunity for all objections that might be
raised to be ventilated should there be
any valid objections.
Mr. LESLIE: Do I understand the
public inquiry must be held?
Mr. ROBB: I do not say that the
public inquiry must be held, but the
procedure is that an advertisement is
issued saying that it is proposed to make
a vesting order. Any person who wishes
to object can, under the existing system,
send in his objections. It might be that
the objection sent in would obviously
be frivolous, and, if so, I am not prepared to pledge the Ministry to hold an
inquiry, but should there be any reasonable objection at all an inquiry is always
held. It is not necessary, however, to
hold an inquiry in all cases, because the
objection might obviously be untenable.
With regard to the bylaws, these
must, of course, be sanctioned by the
Ministry. In any cases that may arise
where there is a clashing of interests or
opinions the matters in dispute will be
duly considered. I think the Mayor of
Derry replied to the point that Senator Leslie made as to the use of facilities
provided in connection with schools outside school hours. The idea, as the Mayor
of Derry said, is that facilities such as
gymnasiums or swimming baths might,
with the assistance of a grant from the
Exchequer, be provided in connection
with the school. As the main object of
the Bill is not to deal with children,
but to deal with adults, it is proposed
that when a grant of that sort is made
for the provision of facilities for a school
conditions shall be made that these
facilities shall be made available for the
adult scholars of that particular school.
In reference to the point that possibly
it might be advisable to have these
trainees trained abroad, the view of the
Government is that if we are going to
spend money on training people and
there are facilities within our Own
United Kingdom for the training of
these trainees it is advisable that our
money should, as far as possible, be spent
within the United Kingdom. In reply
to the point made by Colonel Sinclair
may I say at once that the Government
hopes to have the enthusiastic and hearty
co-operation of the medical profession,
whose advice and help in one way and
another in the furtherance of the objects
of the Bill will be sought. We hope, on
the point made by Sir Roland Nugent,
that the training that will be given will
be given by persons who are fit for their
job. The grants that may be made out
of Government funds towards the
employment of leaders and instructors
are limited, as will be seen in line 15 on
page 4. That meets Sir Roland
Nugent's point.
Senator Dobbs wanted us to go further
than the Bill contemplates and to give
the whole of the cost of the provision of
facilities for voluntary organisations.
That would be entirely contrary to the
general theory underlying the Bill. The
Bill is not for the Government to provide
facilities; it is for the Government to
assist any local authority in providing
them to assist in the growth of a system
of improving by physical training the
health of the people. What we want to
do is to get the general body of the public interested in this subject, and so
interested in it that they are willing to
work for the end we have in view, not
to give a thing and say "Use it."
What we say is "You organise the thing
and we will help you." To give a grant
of the whole 100 per cent. of cost would
be entirely different from the idea
behind this Bill which is to assist the
people to help themselves.
Major DOBBS: In regard to the
amount I gather that you make a
distinction between the local authority
and the local voluntary association?
Mr. ROBB: A grant is not in all
cases to be 50 per cent. There is a limit
of 50 per cent. imposed. The amount
that will be given up to that limit will
be given on the recommendation of the
Grants Committee. It is not proposed
that there should be any difference in
the amount of grant, except the Grants
Committee may see fit to recommend
otherwise, as between the local authority
and the voluntary organisation.
D
National Insurance Bill-
THE SENATE
Second Reading
Major DOBBS: That meets the point.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
National Health Insurance
(Amendment) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: This is a short Bill and
it is introduced in order to rectify a mistake that was made in the legislation
that exists. The formula arises from
the method in which National Health
Insurance legislation and administration
have grown up. National Health
Insurance, both in regard to its
administration and finance, is in many
respects constituted on an United Kingdom basis. Many approved societies
operate in all parts of the United Kingdom and pay benefits from a common
fund built up from the contributions
paid by the members in each country,
and from the State Grant payable by the
Exchequer of the United Kingdom and
of Northern Ireland. There are also
certain funds, such as the Central Fund
which provides a measure of reinsurance
for approved societies, which have been
created by Act of the Parliament of the
United Kingdom, and in respect of
which our Parliament has no power to
legislate although our societies are both
contributors and beneficiaries. For
these reasons it has been found
convenient, and in some respects necessary, to legislate for health insurance in
Northern Ireland by Act of the Parliament
of the United Kingdom. The
health insurance statutes have been consolidated on two occasions in 1924 and
in 1936. On each occasion the consolidating measure was passed at Westminster, and repealed not only the
statutes passed by that Parliament up to
the date of consolidation, but also any
statutes affecting health insurance which
had been passed by the Parliament of
Northern Ireland.
It is customary, therefore, for a health
insurance measure in the Imperial
Parliament to contain a clause providing
for its extension to Northern Ireland on
provision being made to that effect by
an Act of the Parliament of Northern
Ireland, or by an Order of the Governor
of Northern Ireland in Council made in
pursuance of such an Act. When the
Amendment
is
is pressing, and it
essential that it should operate in Northern Ireland on the same date as in
other parts of the United Kingdom, the
procedure has been to pass an Act in our
Parliament empowering the Governor to
apply the United Kingdom Act by Order
in Council. When, however, as on the
present occasion, there is no urgency
about the legislation, the extension to
Northern Ireland is effected by our Act
itself without having recourse to Order
in Council procedure.
The United Kingdom Act, 1938, rectifies a mistake which was made in 1935
in dealing with a small class of persons
who were excluded from insurance in
terms which proved to be more drastic
than had been anticipated. The persons
concerned were those who, having been
in business on their own account, transferred their business to a relative and
became employed in it in order to qualify
for a contributory old age pension at 65.
This was an obvious abuse of the scheme
these people acted deliberately in
order to obtain a pension, and paid the
minimum of contributions by arranging
that the transfer of the business would
not take effect until they were approaching 60. It is necessary to be in insurance for five years before a pension can
be obtained at 65.
was
Their exclusion from insurance
effected by Section 10 of the National
Health Insurance and Contributory Pensions Act, 1935, passed at Westminster.
That Section provided that the persons
themselves were not to be deemed to be
insured persons within the meaning of
the Act. This had the consequence of
preventing them from becoming insured
even though they might have had a title
to voluntary insurance, and it has also
prevented them from enjoying the period.
of free insurance which is, on the average,
twenty-one months, granted to a person
who ceases to be compulsorily insured.
If the Amendment had been that the
employment itself was not to be insurable, the abuse would have been termimated without affecting any rights to
voluntary insurance or otherwise which
the persons concerned might have had.
The United Kingdom Act, therefore.
puts the position right in this respect and
includes the employment amongst the
classes of employment which are not insurable.
Criminal Lunatics Bill-
Committee
12 MAY 1938
Clause 1 of our Bill provides for the
extension of the United Kingdom Act to
Northern Ireland. There will be no
immediate cost as no application for a
pension has been disallowed in Northern
Ireland under the provision of the Act of
1935, and it is probable that the contingent liability will be small.
Clause 2 merely provides for the
amendment or suspension at any time,
as found desirable, of the scheme for a
sickness visitation of insured persons
which was satisfactorily introduced in
Northern Ireland on 1st January last
under Section 207 of the National Health
Insurance Act, 1936. It might be necessary to amend or suspend the scheme
already in operation, and there was no
power to do that under existing legislation. This is to rectify that omission. I
beg to move.
Question proposed, "That the Bill be
now read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: I rather welcome this
Bill as providing what is in the circumstances probably the best way of escape
from an impossible situation, but I also
welcome the Bill because the speech of
the right hon. Gentleman in introducing
it is a distinct indication of the great
danger to which the present schemes both
of health insurance and unemployment
insurance are exposed by reason of the
sharp practices of the participants in
those schemes. That is only another
argument in favour of the complete
change over of the basis of these schemes
to a scheme in which the man draws on
what is his own money rather than draws
on a common pool.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Criminal Lunatics Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.

resolve itself into Committee."—(Mr.
Robb.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Amendment of s. 1 of
20 Geo. 5, c. 19, as to the criminal
lunatic asylum).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: It may be remembered
that I suggested on the Second Reading
that I should move a merely verbal
amendment to this Clause in the Committee stage. However, looking at it
further, I came to the conclusion that if
I really wanted to achieve my object
I should have to move something more
than a short verbal amendment, and also
I rather gathered from the attitude of
the right hon. Gentleman that he was
anxious to get the Bill at a very early
date. It is really not worth while to
run any risk of delay in order to achieve
a very trifling object. Consequently I
do not move any amendment.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Short title, construction,
and citation) ordered to stand part of
the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered, That the Bill be reported
without amendment to the House.
Ordered, That the House do
resume.
now
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without amendment.
Resolved, "That this report be now
received." (Mr. Robb.)
Ordered, That the Bill be read the
Third time on Tuesday next.

Adjournment-
New Schools
THE SENATE
Motion for Adjournment.
NEW SCHOOLS AT LOUGHMOURNE,
SULATOBER, AND GREENISLAND.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, “That
this House do now adjourn." It has
been agreed to meet on Tuesday and
Thursday of next week.
a
new
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to draw the
attention of the House to three
schools that have been completed for a
considerable time, but up to the present
have not been opened. One is at Loughmourne. The new school there
has
been built to take the place of
the old school, which, I understand,
is in bad state of repair. Last
winter during
during the snow and rain
the teacher had to send the children
home for several days until the storm
was over. One of the local government
representatives visited this school yesterday, and he informed me that if you
were not careful in walking across the
floor you would probably put your foot
through a hole and break your leg. That
was not the worst of it. The boys, I
suppose, had broken the air grates
underneath the floor and the rats have
got in, with the result that they sometimes run amongst the children. He
told me that at the present time the
teacher was teaching the children outside on the green, because he could not
teach them inside owing to the smell of
dead rats underneath the floor.
If that is not a scandal in a civilised
country and among civilised people, I do
not know what you would call a scandal.
The fact of the matter is that the
farmers in Northern Ireland, since the
pig industry became valuable, have
better houses for their pigs than these
children at the present time. It is up
to the Ministry of Education to make
some inquiries and bring about the opening of the new school which has been
completed for several weeks. The workmen are all away, but I am told there
is a clerk of works there, and whether
he is keeping back the opening of this
school or not I do not know, or whether
he is using influence with the Regional
Committee to keep back the opening of
the school I cannot tell, but he is drawing £5 a week which the ratepayers of
that district will have to pay.
The second school to which I would
draw attention is at Sulatober. The
old school, as far as I can understand,
is not so bad as that at Loughmourne.
They have a new school built a short
distance from the old school, and the
children cannot be transferred until the
school is handed over. I would like to
know who is holding up the handing
over of this school. The new school is
complete for several weeks, if not
months.
The third school is at Greenisland
I know it has been completed since the
middle of March, but it is locked up.
It is a new school in a new district. A
great number of parents have children
ready to go to that school, and as they
do not want to send them to Carrickfergus or elsewhere, they are keeping
them at home waiting until this new
school is opened. I raise these questions
in order to draw the attention of the
Minister of Education to the facts. I
hope he will make inquiries as to why
the opening of these schools is being
held up.
I say that if drastic action is
necessary he should take it and see that
the schools are opened immediately.
Mr. ROBB: As I have had no notice
of this question I do not propose to
make any statement, but I am quite
various
willing to inquire into the
allegations the hon. Senator has made,
and if action is necessary I am prepared
to take it.
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
minutes before Five o'clock
until Tuesday next.

Mr. LONG: I beg to move, That there
be inserted in the Schedule of Fees-
"Provisional Order Confirmation Bills.
"On Second Reading of a Confirmation
Bill a fee of £5 is charged to the applicants for each Provisional Order included
in the Bill.
"The same fees are charged to applicants and opponents at the Committee
Stage in the case of opposed Confirmation
Bills as in the case of Local Bills. No
other fees are charged."
The object of this Motion is to insert a fee of five pounds in the Table of
Fees in respect of Provisional Order Bills.
This fee was inadvertently omitted when
the Standing Orders were first framed,
and it is now proposed to insert it in order
to make the Table of Fees complete.

The following Report was made from
the Joint Unopposed Committee on the

said Bill and examined the allegations
