Law Relating to Divorce.
Captain PERCEVAL - MAXWELL:
The Motion standing in my name reads
"That in the opinion of this House the
law relating to divorce in Northern Ireland should be assimilated to that of
England." May I say I used the word
"assimilate" because I thought it would
make for a certain latitude in drafting or
adding to the law of England such necessary amendments as might be required,
having regard to the special circumstances
of this country. But I now hear the word
"assimilated" has been understood by
some Members of this House to mean
adopted, and, therefore, as I do not wish
to suggest that the English law should be
adopted word for word I would like to
alter this Motion so that instead of the
word assimilated the words "brought into
line as far as possible with" should be
substituted. It is merely, to my mind, a
drafting Amendment, and I ask that these
words shall be imported into the Motion
as a substitute for "assimilated."
It may be a platitude to say so, but
of all the common actions of mankind
Law Relating to Divorce 204
Law Relating to Divorce
THE SENATE
[Captain Perceval-Maxwell]
marriage is, perhaps, productive of the
greatest happiness or the greatest misery,
yet it is one of the easiest of obligations
to assume. The legal qualifications for
marriage are so simple, and rightly simple,
that marriage is equally within the power
of all classes in this country, whatever
their condition-whether they are rich
or poor, fit or unfit. Yet, although this
relationship may be entered into almost
as a thought, although it is so easy to
assume, and is fraught with so much uncertainty, with so many possibilities for
evil as well as good, it has always been
one of the most difficult relationships to
dissolve. There is, at the outset, the
difficulty in considering this matter, that
there are two distinct aspects from which
marriage can be viewed. There is the
purely religious aspect, and there is the
political aspect. By the political aspect
I mean to refer to the influence of
marriage on the development of the
State independently of the State's
development from the ethical point of
view. While it must be admitted that
in some respects these views seem to
coalesce, in other respects they are undoubtedly divergent. Arguments based
on the sacramental nature of marriage
seem to me to be more within the
province of theologians than that of
politicians, and I do not think this is
the place or time to discuss this aspect
of the question, nor do I feel competent
myself to do so. On the secular side,
however, marriage confers a legal status
and affects rights in property. To the
man it may in certain circumstances give
a right to a life estate in his wife's land
if he survives her, known as an estate
by courtesy; to the woman marriage gives
a right to dower and free bench, and the
legitimacy of children affects their rights
of inheritance. These matters, what I
might call the political consequences of
marriage, are clearly in the domain of
politics.
When, however, you regard the social
consequences of marriage, its effect on
the continued existence of the race and
on the standards and education of future
generations, when you reflect that any
form of social instability or discontent
affects religion as well as politics and that
destruction of morals loosens the bonds
of religion, I think you will agree that
both the Church and the State are
equally interested in maintaining rational
marriage and divorce laws.
I would like to point out here that
notwithstanding opposition on religious
grounds, Churches and States have found,
even in the Middle Ages and at a time
when the Church was supreme in its
power, that some relaxation of the marriage bond had to be admitted, so the
State devised divorce laws, and the Church
adopted as the doctrine of nullity founded
on pre-contract, a pre-contract that was
often not above suspicion of being fictitious. It seems to me, therefore, that the
only difference really existing between
Church and State in this matter is one of
degree, not of fundamental principle. It
is one of what should constitute valid
reasons for severing the marriage tie and
of the name by which the severance should
be known.
Notwithstanding this view of my own,
I know that many people feel the
strongest scruple of conscience against
divorce, and I hope to say and do nothing
to belittle those scruples or the sanctity
of marriage, but I do urge that those
who hold those views will believe that my
reason for urging the reform of the
divorce laws is to strengthen marriage.
and not to weaken it, and to give less
excuse to those who break the marriage
law, and who may feel that under modern
conditions they are unable to keep faith
under their contract.
I would like to point out that it was
ecclesiastical influence that grafted on to
the Common Law those great and bene- |
ficent principles of equity that relieve in
cases of failure of consideration and unconscionable contracts. These are the
principles that make our law the admiration of the whole world for its fluidity, so
that it can be relaxed in proper cases. Is
it too much to ask for the same charity
and good sense to be applied to marriage
contracts? If divorce is fundamentally
wrong it should not be allowed. If divorce
is to be allowed at all it should be rational
and cheap enough to be available to the
poor as well as the rich. At present-I
am sorry I have to say it-it is neither
rational nor cheap.
A man can divorce his wife for simple
adultery, but a woman cannot divorce her
husband for the same cause unless the
adultery is incestuous or bigamous, or is
coupled with desertion of two years or
with cruelty. I think that it is impossible
205 Law Relating to Divorce

17 MAY 1938
Law Relating to Divorce
under modern conditions to justify this
distinction. There is a rule of law applicable to wills that death may be presumed
after a person has been absent and unheard of for more than seven years.
It
might be thought reasonable that that
law should apply also in the case of
marriage, but there is no such presumption in the law of divorce. A bigamous
marriage is void ab initio, and the children of such a marriage are bastards. If,
therefore, a man deserts his wife and lives
abroad for many years, then she hears a
report that he is dead, and on the strength
of that report she marries again; if the
report proves to be untrue and even without legal intervention of an interested
party, the second marriage is bigamous,
with all the consequences that the offence
of bigamy entails. I understand that
under modern conditions steps are taken
to relieve that position as much as possible, but it seems to me that it is an
injustice that a man, after deserting his
wife for many years, should be the cause
of creating a marriage that is unlawful.
As hon. Senators are as familiar as I
am with the recent legislation in England
on this matter, it is unnecessary for me
to presume on the patience of this House
by referring to this Act in further detail.
But I must ask for the indulgence of the
House for a few minutes more to enable
me to draw the attention of hon. Senators
to the injustice occasioned to petitioners
by the necessity which they are under of
promoting a Bill in Parliament before
they can convert a decree a mensa et thoro
into a decree a vinculo matrimonii.
This method of procedure is so expensive that poor petitioners cannot hope to
obtain a divorce. It is not true to say
that there is one divorce law for the rich
and another for the poor, but it is true
to say that the poor cannot pay for the
law so they cannot use it. I am
afraid that is the position of our divorce
law at the present time, and I think it is
not too much to say that this state of
affairs constitutes a scandal which brings
the law into contempt, besides being unjust.
It may have been all right when in
former times a committee of the House of
Lords, all great judges, sat to review
divorce decrees. I do not think it has
ever been known for a lay Member of the
House of Lords to sit on the Judicial
Committee, and to me it seems ludicrous
that a committee of persons who are not
lawyers should sit in Stormont to review
the decrees of the Lord Chief Justice of
Northern Ireland. The Members who sit
on this committee are, doubtless, very
able in conducting their own affairs, but
it is wrong that legal questions should be
submitted to a committee of men who are
not lawyers. It is a most complicated proceeding. As far as I can see it is quite
possible for a committee of this House to
upset the judgment of the Russell v.
Russell case. It is even possible that the
committees sitting on divorce cases might
not know anything about the Russell v.
Russell case, or appreciate the complicated legal questions involved in that
case.
Rationalisation of divorce should include speedy divorce. It may happen
that owing to a Parliamentary recess or
prorogation a Bill cannot be presented to
Parliament until many months after a
decree has been granted. Thus justice is
delayed, and a delay of justice is a denial
of justice.
I also believe that rationalisation of
divorce should be concurrent with increased efforts at matrimonial reconciliation. The report, recently issued by the
Home Office in England on social services,
emphasises the amount of work which is
being done in this direction by utilising
the probation service and the courts of
summary jurisdiction for this purpose, and
in this country the committee appointed
by the Minister of Home Affairs to enquire into the law relating to children and
young persons aimed at setting up a
system which would be well adapted for
reconciliation of matrimonial disputes.
This committee came to the conclusion
that crime in children was often due to
the influence of unhappy home conditions.
In cases where the parents are divided on
fundamental principles of life they should
be allowed to find a partner who will
agree with them.
I trust that I have now discharged my
duty to this House by convincing hon.
Members that there is a strong case for
adopting, so far as possible, the English
Divorce Law in Northern Ireland with
whatever amendments may be necessary,
and so I beg to move.
Mr. MCALLISTER: As one who was
taught in his youth that divorce cannot
be justified, and who conscientiously
believes that it is a mistake, and as one
also who belongs to a Church which
207 Law Relating to Divorce
Law Relating to Divorce 208
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
to me
teaches that, I cannot support this
Motion. At the same time I must recognise the obvious desire and intention
of the hon. and learned Senator who
moved the Motion not to offend any
section of the community or any Church.
in the community, but merely to propound to the House his own views on
divorce. I am sorry the Motion was
ever introduced, but I must say that it
could not have been introduced with
more moderation, and a more obvious
desire not to offend anyone or, what is
a conscientious conviction.
The hon. and learned Senator referred to an aspect of the question and
said what I entirely deny. He said there
was the sacramental aspect, and said, and
I agree, that this is not the place nor the
time, nor was he competent from that
point of view to deal with it. I accept
that and I do say this is not the place nor
the time, nor would I be competent or
entitled to deal with that aspect of the
question. I just take exception to one
statement he made. He said the Church's
opposition to divorce was a question of
degree and not fundamental. Well, on my
own behalf, and I think I can truthfully
say on behalf of the Church to which I
belong, it is fundamental; and it being a
fundamental objection prevents me or
anyone who conscientiously believes this
is a fundamental objection from recognising divorce at all. Therefore, having said
that, I am bound in duty to oppose a
Motion which would facilitate and render
more easy the matter of divorce. I hope
I have made my position clear to the
House, and I do not think it is the time,
nor am I the person to discuss an aspect
which is all-governing and fundamental
so far as I am concerned.
Mr. LESLIE: I have listened with
great attention and with great interest to
what I may presume to call the very able
and very scholarly speech which was delivered to us by the hon. gallant and
learned Gentleman opposite. It seemed
to me that that hon. Member was speaking with the fullest sense of responsibility
and with a very close regard to the various
issues involved in the problem to which
he directed the attention of the House. I
can only hope that on this occasion my
hon. Friend's observations will meet with
a more favourable reception, and with
greater success than those poor efforts of
myself made several years ago to get something in the nature of the English Married
Women's Summary Jurisdiction Act of
1895 accepted in this country or the
attempt I made in this month in the year
1933 by question to direct attention to
some of the points to which reference has
been made.
Passing from that to what might be
called the more practical difficulties before
us one of them has been alluded to by the
hon. Senator on my left. I think that we,
sitting as representatives, although indirect
representatives of the people, must remember that the people who marry are
divided into two classes. There is, first
of all, the class which regards the marriage ceremony as a sacrament of the
Church, and who regard that ceremony
as constituting and consecrating a tie
which is to endure for life and which
cannot be dissolved by any court of
merely terrestrial commission. I think
my Friend in front will subscribe to that
view, at all events. And even if not,
those who share the faith and practice
of my hon. Friend are by no means alone
in their opinion as regards that matter.
There are many others who hold that view
and hold it very firmly and conscientiously
indeed. To these people, the people who
hold these views, nothing that any Parliament can do in any way dissolving marriage can apply at all. Conscientiously
they cannot touch it, and, therefore, they
are, if I may put it that way, to some
extent outside the argument.
You have, on the one hand, the persons
who take what I am going to call the
strictly Christian view of marriage; people
who exalt the religious side of the
ceremony, and pay but little attention to
anything else, and you have the other
class, possibly greater in number, who lay
rather more stress on the civil contract
side of the business, and I think it is only
those who lay greater stress on the civil
contract that we can expect to reach by
whatever legislation we may pass. I do
not think my hon. Friend elaborated the
point of the interest the State has in the
proper maintenance of married life, but
he did say something about it. I think
he was, perhaps, unduly apprehensive of
wearying the House, and, therefore, did
not go into it as fully as he could have
wished. The fact remains that in my
view and in the view of many others--
certainly the view has been put forward
209 Law Relating to Divorce

17 MAY 1938
Law Relating to Divorce
again and again by judges of the high
court the State has a distinct interest in
the maintenance of the institution
known as matrimony. The State
interest arises partly from the question of the maintenance of the
children to which my hon. Friend referred
and partly from the interest in maintaining the honourable status of the married
woman. The last is a point which ought
not to be lost sight of.
But it has always been recognised that
for the sake of those people whom I put
in the second class it has been necessary
to make some provision for interference
with the contractual obligations of the
marriage tie. For a moment I want to
lay stress on what the hon. Member called
divorce a vinculo, such divorce as entitles
the parties or either of them to enter into
another union which the State will recognise as a legal union. He was not referring to the suits of nullity of marriage.
or judicial separation. Neither of these
minor matters was, I think, intended to
be comprised within the ambit of his
speech, and I am not going to say anything about them.
Coming back to the arrangements for
divorce a vinculo in this country, as the
House knows you never had that form of
divorce at all. Within recent times the
only method of obtaining relief from the
chain was an application to Parliament
for a private Bill. That, as we know,
now exists in this country. I have served
under you, my Lord, on many Divorce
Committees of this House. It may be
remembered that I was obliged to ask the
House to relieve me from further service
on the ground that for the second time the
Committee thought fit to recommend to
this House that a petition should be
granted which was founded solely on a
simple act of adultery by the husband.
I think I am correct in saying we only
have had two cases of that class since in
1925 this Parliament undertook the most
distasteful duty of dealing with an application for divorce. The first case was a
good many years ago, in which although
I thought about it a great deal at the
time I came to the conclusion that desertion in the legal sense of the word might
have been pleaded, but was not pleaded.
If it had been we should have been left
with one case alone.
The hon. Member pointed out to us
that we had no law or rather practicetwo
we have no law at all-which justifies us
in applying over here a doctrine which
was not accepted in England until 1923,
after the foundation of this
years
Parliament. Therefore our Committee is
left with the painful necessity of legislating in every case ad hoc. There is no
provision that their decisions shall be consistent one with another. The result is
to produce absolute chaos as regards the
grounds on which Parliament will be
recommended to grant relief. My hon.
Friend did not tell us he kept very well
away from detail-whether his idea was
that these matters should be confided exclusively to the courts. I think that was
in his mind. As the House may remember
when I raised the question in 1933 some
doubt was expressed as to whether the
powers conferred by the second of the
Miscellaneous Provisions Acts went
sufficiently far-whether they did, in fact,
enable us to confer on the high court
power to deal completely with such cases,
that is, to go from their present powers
and custom of pronouncing a decree a
mensa et thoro to the complete decree a
vinculo. The question was not raised as
to whether something analogous to the
English decree nisi should be introduced
and so on. Those were matters which
wisely we left for further consideration.
With regard to the change from the
word "assimilate,'
"that is not without
point. I may say I did not myself interpret that word "assimilate" as meaning
that we should produce an absolute photographic copy. I took it that he rather
applied it in the sense of approximation.
The point on which I do want to follow
the hon. Member particularly is the
absolute necessity for Parliament to lay
down definitely the lines on which this
relief-detestable as it is to my mindmay be granted to those people who seek
for it. If we do not define the grounds
of relief we leave it open to successive
Committees chosen out of successive
Parliaments to produce what I have described as utter chaos. As regards the
religious matter I do not really see that
those of us who hold most firmly the view
of the sanctity of marriage can possibly
presume to say there must not be legislation as regards the civil law for those who
think differently. This Parliament has
to legislate not only for people who hold
what I may call the somewhat narrow
view which I myself hold. We have to
legislate for the people who take different
Law Relating to Divorce
Teachers Superannuation Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
views. We legislate not only for various
shades of Christian thought but also for
the Jew, Turk, infidel or heretic. We
are not limited in any way. I hope the
House will accept the Resolution.
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House): I
am a comparative new-comer to this
House, and perhaps the House may think
it impertinent on my part, but I would
like to say that I have been greatly struck
with the fullness of knowledge and the
dignity with which this matter has been
discussed here this afternoon. I do not
want to make, on behalf of the Government, any other statement but this: that
although the Motion has been changed in
the manner indicated by the hon. and
learned Member who moved it, I would
advise the House to accept it. When I
first saw the terms of the Motion I recognised that it might be interpreted as a
Motion calling upon the Government to
bring the law here into line with the law
in England. I did not think that that
was a wise course. I consulted my colleagues, who agreed that they could not
sympathise with the Motion as originally
drafted, but that if it were modified in
the way it has been so as to leave this
Parliament, so far as this Motion is concerned, free to adopt such legislative provisions as our peculiar circumstances may
require, then they were prepared to ask
the House to accept the Motion, and on
thefr behalf I ask the House to do so.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
do not think I need say anything in reply
to the debate except to point out that I
was careful to say that as regards the
conflict between the Church and mundane
thought on the subject of divorce, I was
expressing only my own opinion that there
was very little difference of principle between them, although I recognised that
that opinion might not be supported by
those who hold the view the Churches
hold. I would also like to say that I was
expressing what I thought was the public
need for a proper divorce system, quite
irrespective of my
views in the
matter. I purposely did not go into
detail regarding the proposed legislation,
because I realised that under our system
of dual legislation, with the restricted
powers of this Parliament and certain
powers reserved to the Imperial Parliament, some steps might have to be taken
own
by the Parliament at Westminster before
our law could be put on an absolute footing with the English law. Therefore it
would not have been in order for me to
suggest that the Government take steps
which it would be outside their power to
take. Therefore when I found that the
Government took a rather strict view of
the wording of my Motion I readily
altered it in order to enable the Government to introduce appropriate legislation.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Superannuation (Teaching Services)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: As each of the first four
Clauses of this Measure is concerned with
a different matter from that dealt with in
the other three Clauses, I propose to give
a brief explanation of each.
Clause 1 is designed to implement a
promise given by my right hon. Friend
the Minister of Finance in his Budget
statement last year that teachers should
not suffer any reduction in their pensions
as a result of the reductions in their
salaries, which were effected in 1931 and
1932. Hon. Members will remember that
these reductions were abolished as from
July 1st, 1937.
The Clause further provides that the
lump sums to be paid to teachers on their
retirement will also be calculated on their
fully restored salaries.
Clause 2 provides that a scheme shall be
brought into effect to enable a teacher on
retirement to allocate part of his pension
to a dependant so that the dependant may
be provided for in the event of the teacher
predeceasing him or her. It is provided.
that the amount of such subsidiary pensions shall be calculated on an actuarial
basis so that the Exchequer may be at no
charge in respect of the operation of the
scheme. A similar arrangement is already
in operation as regards civil servants.
Clause 3 provides that civil servants
who transfer to the teaching services will
preserve their pension rights. The
arrangement is similar in principle to that
which has been in force for several years,
enabling teachers who become civil servants to carry their pension rights with
them into civil service.
Under Clause 4 it is proposed that
pensioners under the Teachers' Pensions
213 Weights and Measures Bill-

17 MAY 1938
Second Reading
Scheme shall be obliged only to furnish
an annual declaration of continued existence instead of a quarterly declaration as
is necessary under existing law.
I beg to move, "That the Bill be now
read a Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Weights and Measures Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: Hon. Members will be
aware that under the Government of
Ireland Act the powers and duties of the
Board of Trade in regard to the administration of the law relating to weights and
measures were made the business of the
Ministry of Commerce.
Inspectors of weights and measures have
not, however, been civil servants controlled by that Ministry, but police
officers who have qualified to undertake
the work by passing the examination of
the Board of Trade. There were some
disadvantages under this system.
Police candidates have had to be given
the opportunity of studying for examination; constables have had to be withdrawn
from ordinary police duties for periods of
six months and upwards, and classes
arranged for them. As the examination
is concerned with subjects remote from
ordinary police work the proportion of
failures has been substantial, and, moreover, the successful candidates have still
to look for promotion to ranks higher than
that of sergeant through their police
duties proper.
The Government have carefully considered the matter, and have found savings
can be effected both to the Exchequer and
to local authorities by the transfer of the
work of inspection of weights and
measures to civilians directly controlled
by the Ministry of Commerce, and the
Measure now before the House seeks to
achieve this end.
It is proposed that the existing inspectors shall be given the choice of
remaining in the police force or of transferring without loss of pension rights to
the Ministry of Commerce.
Under the Bill it is also proposed that
the provision of standards of weights and
measures and other equipment, the travelling expenses of inspectors and expenditure on office accommodation shall be met
by the Ministry of Commerce instead of
by county and county borough councils.
As a contribution towards the cost of these
items it is proposed that the sum of £1,000
a year from the Dogs Acts Account shall
in future accrue to the Ministry instead
of to these authorities. I beg to move,
"That the Bill be now read a Second
time."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not rise to object
to the poor unfortunate Dogs Fund being
once again plundered, but I presume we
may take it that the Government is satisfied that the fund can stand the extra
£1,000 a year easily enough. The point
I really wanted to ask about is, were the
local authorities consulted on this matter?
They have certain powers and duties at
present, and one would like to be assured
that they are not being transferred without their consent. It would seem from,
I think, the third Sub-clause of Clause 4,
that certain property of the local authorities is to be, I was going to say confiscated,
but perhaps I had better say annexed,
and transferred to similar use under a
different control. Is it intended to give
any compensation to local authorities?
Otherwise there is nothing to be done except to support the Bill. It is obvious
that in our small area this is a matter
that can be dealt with more economically
and more satisfactorily than at present,
although I do not know that any grave
fault has been found with the manner in
which local authorities have hitherto
carried out their duties.
Sir JAMES WILTON: The information the hon. and learned Senator has
asked for is the very information I had
intended asking for from the Leader of
the House.
Mr. ROBB: With regard to the first
point, the local authorities were consulted
and agreed to the proposal. The Government is satisfied that the Dogs Fund can
bear the extra cost of £1,000 per annum,
but as regards the question of the payment of compensation to local authorities
for weights and measures equipment that
is handed over to the Ministry of Commerce, I do not think that matter has
been considered.
Physical Training Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Physical Training and Recreation Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Robb).
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair].
CLAUSES 1 (Advisory Council
physical training and recreation), and 2
(Committee on applications for grants),
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
on
CLAUSE 3 (Powers of Ministry of
Education).
Question put, "That the Clause stand
part of the Bill."
Major DOBBS: I should like some information regarding the effect of Subclause (1) of Clause 3. Paragraph (a)
enables the Ministry of Education to
make grants on the recommendation of
the Grants Committee in aid of the provision of facilities for various types of
recreation, including playing-fields. Since
the Second Reading of this Bill I have
come across a case of a village not very far
from Belfast where up to now there has
been a playing-field. That field has been
used in the summer as a cricket ground,
and in the winter as a hockey ground. It
has been used for these purposes up to
within the last two or three months when
the landlord apparently decided that the
ground could be more advantageously
developed as building ground. What I
should like to ask is this: the money has
to be raised and is being raised to pay for
that ground. Would the grant under this
Clause be retrospective in any way? It
all points to future work. The provision
is for future playing-fields. I wish to
know whether playing-fields already in
existence can be helped by this grant.
Mr. ROBB: The Act is not intended to
be retrospective. I might suggest to the
hon. and gallant Senator that money
might be available under this grant for
the equipment of that field if the equipment came after the passing of the Act.
However, every case will be dealt with on
its merits by the Grants Committee.
Major DOBBS: Do I understand that
it would be better for them to let the
playing-field go and when that is done
they can apply and say, "Can you do anything to help us to get it back? We are
prepared to spend a certain amount of
money; will you give us a grant to help
us?"
Mr. ROBB: In another capacity and
for a fee I might give that advice.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Extension of powers of
local authorities).
*
*
(2) The council of a rural district may
provide public swimming baths and
bathing places, and the Ministry of
Home Affairs may, by regulations,
apply for that purpose, with or without
modifications, any provisions of the
Baths and Washhouses (Ireland) Act,
1846, or of any other Act relating to
the provision of public swimming baths
and bathing places by local authorities.
*
*
*
*
*
+
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out Sub-clause (2). This is a matter
which requires very careful consideration.
on the part of the Committee. At first
sight those who remember "Water Babies"
would be ready to consent to anything
which would make it a little easier for the
people of the class dealt with in the
fantastic story I have mentioned to bathe
and so on. That is all very well. But
we have got to deal with realities. We
have 32 rural district councils in Northern Ireland. How many of these rural
district councils have asked for powers of
this description? No doubt the right hon.
Gentleman has access to the report of the
extensive inquiries-which I should like
to have which were made before this Bill
was launched on an unexpectant and not
wholly receptive Parliament. Of the 32
rural district councils how many have
asked for power to provide swimming
baths and bathing places? How many of
these 32 rural councils have got water
suitable to fill the baths? A swimming
bath to be of any use at all would require
to be something about the size of a young
reservoir. My experience is that most
of our villages have not got any water
217 Physical Training Bill-

17 MAY 1938
Committee
supply at all, nor have they got any
system of drainage. If you are going to
fill a swimming bath with water you have
got to drain the water away somehow.
How many of these councils are equipped
in the necessary manner?
If this Clause is allowed to stand and
if any of our rural councils are, shall I
say, imprudent enough to put it into
operation the cost is going to be methow? As I make it out 50 per cent. of
the cost, perhaps, may be obtained if the
Central Fund is not exhausted by that
time. The other 50 per cent. has got to
be raised, as I understand, from a rate
which will fall on the whole of the particular rural district council. That is to
say, for instance, if Rasharkin decides to
establish a swimming bath the ratepayers
of Dunloy will have the privilege of contributing to the cost. These two places
are not, I suppose, less than eight miles
apart, and the prospect of Dunloy using
the swimming baths is not very great.
Then it is not only the first cost-you have
got to keep the swimming bath in order;
appoint caretakers, one male and one
female; and these people have got to be
paid.
That is a cost of £150 a year
irrespective of repairs. That is going to
fall, so far as I can see, entirely on the
rates of the rural district council.
Swimming baths are, no doubt, under
proper conditions, desirable things, but I
am not at all certain that the opinion of
medical officers in country towns is altogether favourable to them. I am not
sure from more than one aspect in regard
to health matters, but that is a matter
already entirely dependant on the extent
of the organisation and superintendence.
It will be remembered that part 8 of the
English Public Health Act, 1935, does
not apply to this country and, therefore,
if any bylaws should be made it is necessary they should go into, perhaps, greater
detail than would be otherwise necessary.
Again if you are going to put another
severe pull on the water supply of the
rural district you have got to watch that
you do not interfere with existing water
rights. There are various kinds of water
rights-fishing rights, power rights, water
to actuate a mill and so on. There is also
as the hon. Member reminds me-t
e-the
question of drinking water. The broad
fact remains that the rural districts have
not any water to spare. They have not
enough for existing sanitary requirements.
Again, they have very heavy rates, and
they are not anxious to add to them. If
you want to make a rate more unpopular
than ordinary let it be in any way connected with the Ministry of Education,
because the shilling education levy is not
yet receiving that degree of approbation
which it ought to receive, particularly in
the rural districts. The rural districts
do not want any more rates. I do suggest
that the only possible chance of success
with regard to swimming baths in or
about rural districts is to be found in
Sub-clause (3), that is, the power of a
local authority to contribute towards the
expenses of another, because you might
get a rural district on the immediate confines of a town to join with the town in
helping to pay the expenses. But to suggest a swimming bath in ordinary rural
districts seems to me preposterous. I do
not like to put in an Act of Parliament
something which I know is not going to be
put into operation, because, after all, if
you allow the thing to stand part it conveys the suggestion that Parliament desires
and expects it to be put into operation.
Further, it puts these authorities under
constant attack for not using these powers
which they did not ask for, and did not
want, and are not prepared to pay for.
Accordingly I suggest we should do well
to expunge this Sub-clause from the Bill.
Mr. ROBB: I cannot accept this
Amendment. If hon. Members will look
at the Clause they will see it deals only
with swimming baths and bathing places.
No doubt it is quite true the rural district
councils were not consulted with regard to
this, and they did not ask for it. But a
great part of the legislation embodied in
this Bill is intended to be stimulating, and
it is hoped that rural authorities, with
others, may do something to further what
is the object of the Bill. Now it is not
anticipated for one moment that one rural
district will build a swimming bath. But
there are many rural districts, even
Rasharkin, where bathing places may very
well be set up for the use of the inhabitants of the district to improve their
physical powers. Rasharkin is quite close
to the River Bann, and if the rural
district, in which Rasharkin is situated,
thought it would be useful and would help
the inhabitants, young and old, to erect
a springboard and bathing boxes on the
banks of the Bann, that would, it seems to
me, be a most excellent idea from their
Physical Training Bill-
THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr Robb]
point of view. It would not cost an extravagant amount of money, and it would be
directly in line with the aim of this Bill.
And there are many other places besides
Rasharkin where bathing places might be
usefully established for the youth of the
neighbourhood.
The learned Senator said quite truly
that the inhabitants of the rural districts
did not want the rates increased. That
feeling ought to be a sufficient answer to
his anticipations with regard to large
sums of money being spent on swimming
baths. The baths ought to cost very little
and ought to make a very great return to
the places for any small expenditure that
might be involved in making them, and
for that purpose the Clause really was inserted.
Mr. BARCLAY: I heard the Leader of
the House speak about Rasharkin, and I
would like to know if water for bathing
purposes could be had, and there was no
right-of-way to the water, will this Bill
enable the local people to obtain a rightof-way. At Greenisland the people live
within sight of the water day after day,
but those who own the land along the
waterside prevent them from going down
to bathe in the summer-time.
This question has been raised time and again by the
local committee. It would seem that
Larne Rural District has nothing to do
with it and Carrickfergus Urban District
can do nothing, and the people along the
shore at Greenisland have been trying to
get a way to have a bathe there. I would
like to know if this Bill will assist in any
way in providing bathing places for the
young people there in the summer-time.
Mr. ROBB: Under the provisions of
the Bill a right-of-way can be compulsorily acquired.
Major DOBBS: I do not want to be
hypercritical, but I do hope the district.
councils will be very careful with regard
to bathing places. A few years ago at a
university in the east of England some
undergraduates started bathing in the
river there. They were asked by some old
ladies to move a bit further down as they
could see them. These undergraduates
moved about threequarters of a mile
further down. Two or three days afterwards they were again asked by the old
ladies to move further down as they could
still see them with a telescope.
some
Sir JOSEPH DAVISON: I should
like
as to
further information
how it is proposed under this Bill to
acquire a right over a right-of-way. I
cannot see that the Bill gives any such
power.
I
If
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH:
must say that it does seem to me to be
an extraordinary power to give anyonethe right to walk over private property to
get to a certain place. In my opinion it
is a most serious thing. Take the case of
my own place at Lismara. On a Sunday,
particularly, the great difficulty we have
is to keep people going to the shore from
walking through my own grounds.
the Government is going to give people
the right to go over private property to
get to a bathing place they are taking
power which, in my opinion, they have
no right to have. While I say that I am
absolutely in sympathy with the objects
of this Bill, although I believe that those
who will mostly benefit from the grants to
be given under the Bill will be voluntary
organisations, such as the Boy Scouts and
the Churches, rather than the local
authorities. I should like to emphasise
that I most strongly object to the Government taking power to allow people to go
in and out as they like of any man's
private property in order to get to a bathing place or any other place of recreation.
Mr. LESLIE: Having some acquaintance with Rasharkin I should like to
point out to the right hon. Gentleman
that it is some little distance from the
River Bann. However, if the right hon.
Gentleman would be agreeable to leave
out swimming baths and confine the Subclause to bathing places it might meet the
objection.
Mr. ROBB: I am afraid I cannot
agree. Some area might want to provide
a swimming bath, and why should we not
allow them to do so?
Mr. LESLIE: I do not like hypothetical legislation, and I have not, as a
rule, been a supporter of legislation in
favour of the isolated case, more particularly when it is a purely hypothetical
isolated case. I am afraid I shall have
to take the opinion of the Committee on
the point.
Physical Training Bill--
Committee
17 MAY 1938
Sir JAMES WILTON: Is this not an
entirely permissive Clause?
Mr. ROBB: It is entirely permissive.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: At
the present time young people bathe in
the river in very dangerous places, and
why should not the rural councils be in a
position to provide safe bathing places,
which is all that is suggested by this subclause.
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: Might I ask
the Leader of the House whether he can
enlighten the Committee further regarding the powers to be given by this Bill
for the acquisition of rights of way. I
never heard of such a drastic proposal
being embodied in a Bill of this nature.
A right of way may come into existence
through long usage, but the compulsory
acquisition of a right of way should not
be slipped into a Bill dealing with a
totally different matter.
Mr. ROBB: This Bill gives the
Government power to acquire land compulsorily. In the compulsory sections of
the Public Health Act, the Land Clauses
Consolidation Act, and the Local Government Act of 1934 relating to a Vesting
Order, land is defined as including a
right of way. Supposing a bathing place
is wanted on the edge of a lake. There
is no use giving anybody power to take
that land unless they can have a right of
way to it, and if you have power to compulsorily acquire land you ought also to
give a person a right of way to get to
that land. All these powers can only be
enforced after a public inquiry, where the
merits of each particular case is gone
into.
a
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: I should
like to be quite clear about this matter.
If that is really the position it seems to
me to be the most monstrous suggestion
I have ever heard of. If you compulsorily acquire land to construct
bathing place it is quite reasonable to
acquire land to enable persons to get to
that place, but it is most unreasonable
to take power to compulsorily acquire an
existing right of way. If such a power
is put into an Act of Parliament of this
description half of one's property might
conceivably be compulsorily acquired. I
am afraid that most of us who are living
within a reasonable distance of Belfast
are suffering quite enough by people
wandering across our land and leaving
our gates open, and we do not want to
encourage them.
Mr. BARCLAY: I agree with what
the Lord Mayor of Belfast has said
regarding young people walking and
running across one's land and garden on
a Sunday afternoon. That is done all
along the County Antrim coast near Belfast, and that is one of the reasons why
those who have property at Greenisland
will not allow access to the shore through
their land. If there was a place set
apart for people to get to the shore, and
it was properly fenced, it would soon
become known, and people would use that
path and not tramp through people's
gardens.
Mr. LESLIE: There is another point.
Even as regards these little bathing
places, if the rural district council sets
apart another place as a bathing place
they assume certain very serious responsibilities. They may be liable, in the
event of an accident, to all sorts of
things, and they may have to provide
life-saving apparatus and things of that
kind. As regards the compulsory
acquisition of land, that seems to arise
from that Section 22 of the Local
Government Act of 1934, which is tucked
away in appropriate obscurity in one of
the sub-clauses of this Clause, and in
regard to which I have an Amendment
on the Paper. I shall have to take the
opinion of the Committee on this matter.
Sir JAMES WILTON: I would again
like to emphasise the fact that rural
authorities are not compelled to do any
of these things that some Members seem
so much afraid of.
Mr. LESLIE: They are not compelled.
but it is suggested they should do them.
Question put, "That this sub-clause be
left out."
The Committee divided: Contents, 4;
Not-Contents, 12.

CLAUSE 5 (Supplemental provisions
as to exercise of powers by local
authorities).
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie:
Clause 5, page 6, at the end of subclause (1) insert:
"Provided that where a local
authority has acquired, for the purposes of this Act, any lands or buildings situate without their area bylaws
relating to such lands or buildings shall
not be made without the consent of the
local authority in whose area such
lands or buildings are situate."
Mr. LESLIE: As I think you, Mr.
Chairman, are aware, there is a slight
alteration in the Amendment as it
stands on the Paper. The Amendment
suggests the insertion of certain words
after line 7 on page
It is proposed
.g
now to insert the words after line 43 on
page 5, at the end of the first paragraph
of sub-clause (1). It is also proposed to
make a slight alteration in the wording
as it appears on the Paper. Instead of
using the words: "shall not be made
without the consent of the local
authority," I now propose to say: "shall
not be confirmed without consultation
with the local authority." The point is
a simple one. You have one local
authority providing this or that convenience or social service or disservice
outside its own area, and therefore
necessarily in the area of some other
local authority. It is essential that you.
have bylaws, and you have a distinct
difficulty if you have the occupier of the
dominant tenement making a bylaw
applicable to the servient tenement. You
must bring the local authorities into
agreement with each other. The by-
Maguire, Mr. J.
Perceval-Maxwell, Capt. J. R.
Robb, Mr. J. C.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
laws will be of no effect until confirmed
by the Ministry of Home Affairs which
has powers distinctly limited as regards
bringing local authorities into agreement.
But at all events the new wording I
suggest will insure that the bylaws must
receive the agreement of both authorities concerned and the confirmation of
the Ministry of Home Affairs. The
bylaws made under this Bill are likely
to be of very great importance. I beg
to move the words on the Paper as
corrected.
Mr. ROBB: On behalf of the Government I accept that.
Amendment agreed to.
now.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to insert
at the end of sub-clause (2) "Provided
that notwithstanding anything in this
Act contained land for the purpose of a
holiday camp or camping site shall not be
acquired otherwise than by agreement."
This is a question dealing with the compulsory acquisition of land with due
regard to Section 22 of the Local Government Act of 1934 of which we spoke just
I know that such powers do exist
for other matters and that they have not
in practice worked inequitably at all. A
vesting order is the phrase the right hon.
Gentleman now adopts as opposed to a
provisional order. The procedure of a
vesting order really is rather convenient.
But when you come to the compulsory
acquisition of land you have got to be
very careful for what purpose you do it.
Hitherto Parliament has only given
power of compulsory acquisition for some
tangible object, something for the advantage of the public. First of all railways,
public roads, and then labourers'
cottages. These were matters where the
advantage to the public jumps to the
eyes.
But has power been given to acquire
land from one man against his will, not
Physical Training Bill-
Committee
17 MAY 1938
for a local authority to occupy or use but
to pass it on to somebody else? That is
what is sought here. The particular
point I am taking is the holiday camp
or camping site. Holiday camps are not
by any means popular. Very often a
generous land-owner has allowed a holiday camp one year but has not repeated
the invitation the following year.
That
is perhaps for a reason. Before this
land can be taken there is a public
inquiry. The right hon. Gentleman laid
stress upon that. I am afraid the question as to whether a public inquiry is or
is not to be held is one for the Ministry.
The Ministry is not bound to hold an
inquiry. The Ministry is the judge as to
whether the objection is untenable or not.
That is not altogether all that it should
be. I am afraid I do think that the
suggestion to take land for a holiday
camp or camping site is a
warrantable invasion of such rights as
are left to owners of private property.
A man may have a number of objections.
There are difficulties of water supply,
difficulties of gates being left open, and
a man may very well be excused for
desiring that a holiday camp or camping
site should be somewhere else, and I am
going to suggest that there are plenty of
somewhere-elses in Northern Ireland.
most
un-
If you want land for a holiday camp
you can get it by agreement, and you
have no need to resort to compulsion. If
you have compulsory power in the Bill
nobody would feel quite safe, and you
would most definitely discourage the
good-natured person from allowing a
camping site to be put on his place for
even one year.
I do not think any
ordinary Government that is not a
Government depending on Socialist
support would do this. And in this
country we have not got even a National
Government yet. We have a Government in power which is supposed to pay
some regard to the interests of private
property. I do not think this proposal
ought to come before Parliament. The
only point I am making is as regards
taking land compulsorily for a holiday
camp or camping site. In order to protect the public against that I suggest the
words on the Paper. The necessity for
these words arises solely from the
reference to Section 22 of the Act of
1934.
or
Mr. ROBB: I cannot on behalf of the
Government accept this Amendment.
The idea behind the resistance to the
Amendment is this. Supposing that
Boy Scouts or Girl Guides or any other
voluntary organisation
any local
authority for a service of this sort can
come to an agreement. They find a site
suitable for this purpose with water and
all other conveniences attached to it and
the site is 60 acres in extent. Supposing
they can agree with the owners of 40
acres of it. Unless there were compulsory powers to acquire the interest of
the owner of the balance, that owner
might prevent the project altogether or
hold them up and exact an exorbitant
price. That is the sort of case that may
arise and which is the foundation of my
resistance to this Amendment.
As I have explained before, the compulsory powers cannot be put in force
without a public inquiry. Any person
objecting to the acquisition of land compulsorily for any purpose under this or
any other Act and who shows reasonable
ground for objection is entitled to have
and gets in practice an opportunity of
stating his objections in public before an
inspector and having them weighed up
by an impartial authority who may be
relied on not to disregard reasonable
objections and who will balance these
objections against the public advantages
that are advanced by the authority seeking to put the powers in force. For
these reasons I cannot accept the Amendment.
I
Capt. PERCEVAL-MAXWELL:
am sorry that I must support the Amendment. I am sorry because I have already
received a very kind reception from the
Leader of the House. I do not think this
House is justified in giving compulsory
powers for the purchase of a holiday
camp. I may be wrong but it seems to
me that up to now compulsory powers
have never been given for the benefit of
a limited class of society. I think they
have been limited up to now to such
things as railways, waterworks, and so on
that are for the benefit of the whole community. With regard to holiday camps
I am in entire sympathy with them and
I should be very sorry to see a state in
which the landowners of the qountry
should band themselves together to prevent Boy Scouts or Girl Guides or any
other class of person from camping on

ex-
[Captain Perceval-Maxwell]
their ground. I do not think there is
the slightest chance of any organised
opposition to holiday camps. I know a
great many landowners and my
perience is they are all ready to give land
for the purpose of holiday camps, and
there are reasons why they might not like
to do so in certain particular cases.
these cases I think the owner of the
property should be the judge as to
whether it is suitable to have a holiday
camp on his ground. I certainly do not
think the Government have made any
case which would justify us in going to
the extreme limit for what is after all
a relatively small part of society.
or

Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
am afraid I will have to support the
Amendment. It is altogether different
even from a right of way from
acquiring ground compulsorily or the
building of new houses or making a new
thoroughfare. Here it is for recreation
grounds. You are going to go to an
individual who has 50 acres and say we
want 40 acres. You are bringing something home to that individual that he
certainly does not want. I am sure if
the Boys' Brigade or Boy Scouts want to
get temporary accommodation for playing

fields most people would give it.
go so far as to say the Government should

will vote for the Amendment.
Sir JAMES WILTON: I am rather
pleased to find so many hon. Senators
very anxious to get holiday camps, but
it is a wonder they still want to put
obstacles in the way by preventing any
power being given in the Bill. The hon.
and gallant Senator said he was sure
very few landlords would refuse to give
part of their ground for holiday camps,
and the Lord Mayor of Belfast expressed
the opinion that there should not be any
objection to it but still he will not give
powers to compel anybody. If they
have no power any person can destroy the
Bill completely by saying "No, no, we do
not want this." The Lord Mayor of
Belfast has referred to the fact that in
cities there is compulsory acquisition of
land for public purposes. As a matter
of fact this is for a similar purpose,
namely, the health and well-being of the
entire community. I intend to support
the Amendment.

Mr. LESLIE: On the Second Reading
I asked a little question as to the compensation, if any, to be paid to local

which was acquired by the Ministry of

any compensation to local authorities in
respect of this equipment. If this
property was put up for sale it would
probably fetch only the price of junk, and
as it is proposed to use it for the same
purposes as hitherto it is not proposed
to pay any compensation.
Mr. LESLIE: The articles are going
to be used for the purposes for which
they were procured, only under another
authority?
Mr. ROBB: That is so.

Belfast Water Bill.
Question put, "That this Bill be now
read the Third time."
Mr. MAGUIRE: With regard to the
Belfast Water Bill, I want to make a
few remarks. I wish to say that it is
my intention to oppose the Bill unless
the Leader of the House can give
me some guarantee regarding several
different matters I desire to raise.
In
the first place, there is within the City
of Belfast an area called Whiterock. At
the present time there is no water
supply to that district from the Belfast
Water Commissioners. The present
water supply to the houses at Whiterock is led through an open drain, and
the Medical Officer of Health for the
city has in the past condemned this
water supply. The difficulty is that the
Water Commissioners look for a return
of ten per cent. per annum on their outlay in providing a supply service to this
I feel that this is a great hardon an area within the City
area.
ship
Belfast. Compulsion should
be
of
brought to bear on the Water Commissioners to supply water to all residents
within the city boundary at least.
a
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry to interrupt the hon. Member, but I must point
out to him that this Bill has been before
Joint Opposed Committee of both
Houses. It is not a Government
Measure, and the Leader of the House
will not be in a position to answer any
questions on the Bill.
Mr. MAGUIRE: Am I not in order
in putting forward the reasons why I
oppose the Bill?
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member is
quite in order in doing that.
Mr. MAGUIRE: Plans for additional
houses for this area were prepared, but
owing to the fact that the Water Commissioners would not concede a water
supply building was not proceeded with.
This is a great hardship, and some effort
should be made by the Water Commissioners to give all the residents of Belfast
a proper water supply. In the second
place, I am informed that the Medical
Officer of Health for Belfast has no right
to enter the Commissioners' property in
order to take a sample of water from
the different storage reservoirs supplying the City of Belfast. He is occasionally compelled to write for samples to
the Water Commissioners, and he has
got to await their convenience for a
reply before he can test the water. I
think this is a very serious matter for
the residents of Belfast, bearing in mind.
the recent outbreak of typhoid
Croydon, due to the pollution of the
water supply.
at
I also wish to draw attention to the
fact that the Water Commissioners, in
their recruitment of clerks, do not hold
competitive examinations for these
positions. As a result, it is my opinion.
that the minority section of the community hold very few positions under this
Board. This is an injustice and a want
of fair play to the Roman Catholic
minority in the city, and in proof of
this I will give you some figures. Out
of 166 employees of the Belfast Water
Board, 12 are Catholics, representing
7.23 of the minority who are employed
Belfast Water Bill-
Third Reading
24 MAY 1938
I
by the Water Board. The annual earnings paid out to these 166 employees
amount to £37,163 2s 4d. Of that sum
the employees representing the Catholic
minority of the City of Belfast receive
£2,569 4s, a percentage of 6.9. Those
figures do not include the labourers employed by the Water Board. I have not
got the figures relating to labourers, but
I do know that of one squad of 46 men
only five are Catholics, which works out
at 11 per cent. of the total. I feel that
this is very unfair to the minority, considering that they represent about 27
per cent. of the population of Belfast.
For these reasons I wish to oppose the
Bill now before the House, and I hope
that as a result a change of front will
be shown by the Water Commissioners.
Mr. LESLIE: As you, Mr. Speaker,
have just reminded us, the Bill before
us is a Local Bill, and I would respectfully submit to the House that, as in
the case of a Public Bill we are bound
by the Title of the Bill, so in the case
of a Local Bill we are bound by the
Preamble of the Bill, which has been
approved by a Joint Committee of both
Houses of Parliament. I was not myself on that Committee, but I have read
the Bill, and I cannot detect any
reference in the Preamble to any of the
points made by the hon. Member.
Mr. McALLISTER: I am
sure the
revelations made by Senator Maguire
have created some little surprise amongst
han. Members of this House. There
seems to be no proper control over the
water supply of Belfast, and the obvious
thing to do would seem to be to give
the Belfast Corporation sole control over
the water supply. I have just heard for
the first time that the Medical Officer of
Health for Belfast has no right of supervision over the water supplied to the
residents of the city, and that is most
surprising. It is a state of affairs that
this House should not stand for. It
appears that people are living in newlyerected houses in the Whiterock area
with no proper water supply, with no
one responsible for the water supply,
and, above all, the Belfast Corporation's
Medical Officer is not allowed to test or
approve the water supplied to houses in
the city, many of which were built with
the aid of money supplied by the citizens of Belfast.
That is a state of affairs that I, personally, would accept no responsibility
for. New houses are built, and no
proper provision is made for a water
supply to those houses. I certainly
would not give my approval to such a
state of affairs. It is a shocking thing
to find that the water supply to new
houses is in such a backward condition as
described by Senator Maguire. In my
opinion the best solution would be to
merge the powers and duties of the
Water Commissioners in the Belfast
Corporation, which is responsible for the
health of the citizens. I certainly
would not support the Bill as it stands
at the present time. There seems to be
no provision for the public health, and
no provision made for any water supply
for these houses. I think it is a criminal state of affairs to occur in the city
at this age. On these grounds, unless
some provisions are made by the water
authorities for examining and carrying
out a water supply to be conveyed to
each of the houses, I would not support such a Measure.
Sir JOSEPH DAVISON: I would say
that the matter of the supply of water to
these few houses is a question of cost just
the same as the Electricity Department of
the Belfast Corporation will not supply
electricity to an area in which there are
only a few houses. The cost of taking
the supply from the nearest mains to
these houses would be prohibitive to the
Belfast Water Commissioners, and the
income would not justify them in supplying it. They have asked for a guarantee
of 10 per cent. The Electricity and Gas
Departments also ask it, and they are a
service to the community as is the Belfast
water.
I am sorry to hear the attack made by
the hon. Senator on the other side with
regard to those employed by the Belfast
Water Commissioners. This is a private
Bill and the Belfast Water Commissioners
have no person here to represent them,
and I think the attack is scarcely fair.
I know, as a matter of fact, that on all
occasions on which there are a number of
houses to justify a supply going to those
houses the Water Commissioners never turn
them down. In fact they have gone outside the area with the approval of the
authority to supply other districts.
am sure in a mountainous area like
Whiterock the Senator can understand
I
Belfast Water Bill-
Third Reading
THE SENATE
[Sir Joseph Davison]
it would be very expensive to supply
water. There are only a few houses. If
there was a greater demand I am sure
they would supply it.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL:
This is a question that has been discussed
from both sides, and it does not appear
that it is within the power of this Bill
to alter the circumstances. I, therefore,
move that the House proceed to the next
business.
Not
Dr. GIBSON: I would like to make a
few observations on the speeches that
have just been delivered. When there
was an Opposed Committee consisting of
Members of both Houses, why did these
hon. Senators not come before that Committee to make these complaints.
having done so, they ought to be out of
court with regard to the complaints they
have made. As to the medical officer of
health not being permitted to take
samples of water from the various
reservoirs, I know as a fact that the
Belfast Water Commissioners have the
most competent authority that could
possibly be obtained for examining the
water. Samples are taken every week.
I venture to say that the medical officer
of health for the city of Belfast has never
applied to the Commissioners to take
samples from the reservoirs, and if he
had applied he would have been
permitted.
This whole thing, I think, is got up to
discredit the Water Commissioners. A
lot of people have the idea that the Water
Commissioners should be merged in the
Corporation. I disagree with that entirely. I think the Board regulating the
water for Belfast is one of the most competent Boards in Belfast. They have
had vision. They have gone out to the
Silent Valley and produced a water
supply second to none in the British
Isles. If they had not had that foresight and that vision we to-day would
have been in a very bad way with regard
to a supply of water. Then the dragging in of the religious question is totally
absurd and unjustified, and I think the
House should take no notice of it.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: It is
surprising to me to learn of any mismanagement by this Board, especially
The
when you take note of the fact that this
Board is elected on the popular franchise,
exactly the same as the Corporation, and
there are scarcely ever any contests.
people of Belfast seem quite content with
their representation on the Board at the
present time. With regard to the complaints made by Senator Maguire, I am
a little surprised, because I have never
heard any complaints at the Corporation
about the want of water on the Whiterock site. I think it has already been
explained that probably on account of
the high level and the cost the Commissioners expect some remuneration
from the people who own the property.
So far as the employees are concerned
that is, of course, the usual complaint
made by our friends. They never have
enough of that particular persuasion on
any Board, so that it is not the least
surprising. However, I would like to
point out that if there was any justification the city of Belfast representation on
the Water Board would be changed.
is only a few months since there was a
nomination for a representative on the
Water Board and there was
opposition.
It
no
Mr. MCALLISTER: It is gerrymandered.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM :
There is no gerrymandering. It is
exactly the same as the Corporation
franchise, and there are no complaints
whatever about the elections for the city
of Belfast.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Henderson's Divorce Bill.

Business of the House.
Resolved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking the Second
Reading of the Solicitors Bill and the
Appropriation Bill."-(Mr. Robb.)
Message from the Commons.
The Commons agree to the Amendment made by the Senate to the Blind
Persons Bill without Amendment. They
have passed the Solicitors Bill, and the
Appropriation Bill, to which they desire
the concurrence of this House.
Solicitors Bill.
Resolved: "That this Bill be now read
a First time.”—(Mr. Leslie.)
Mr. LESLIE: When I rose last year
to move the Second Reading of the
Solicitors Bill, which I have again the
honour to present, I think I told the
House as then constituted that I was unable to follow the great example of
brevity set by the late Lord Macnaghten,
who moved the Second Reading of the
Solicitors Bill in 1898 in the Imperial
House of Commons, because, as I then
pointed out, his Lordship had the advantage, among others, that I had not, of
introducing a Bill which had already
passed the House of Commons. That,
last year, was not the case in respect to
the Bill before us. This year, however,
the situation is changed. The Bill I am
now asking the House to give a Second
Reading to has already passed the House
of Commons.
The House may remember that the
Bill last year had to some extent a rather
chequered career. The circumstances of
the time were not altogether favourable.
I introduced the Bill in June a little
hurriedly as regards the printing part
of it, because of the elections to the
Senate, which took place in the middle
of that month. We left it over the long
vacation in order to give everybody time
to consider the proposed alterations
which it made. And when we took the
Commitee stage last autumn and subsequently the Third Reading, by the
time it got to the House of Commons
there really was scarcely time to examine
it properly. Consequently the Bill did
not proceed beyond the First Reading.
Moreover, it will be remembered that
during last autumn legal changes were
in contemplation, and it was obviously
very desirable that the Bill before it
received the Royal Assent should have
the advantage of the approval of the
Lord Chief Justice. Since then a new
Lord Chief Justice has been appointed,
and I am glad to be able to tell the
House that he concurs in the principles
of the Bill before us.
But as I introduced the Bill last year,
and as the House then gave a Second
Reading and put it through its final
stages it is, I venture to submit, unnecessary and even, perhaps, undesirable that I should detain the House this
afternoon by any elaboration of the
details of the Bill. I need not, for
instance, again draw attention to the
fact that Part 3 of the Bill, which is
intended to afford further protection to
clients in respect of money, is based, or
rather more than based-it is almost a
word for word copy of the English
Solicitors Act of 1933. That is only one
example of the matters to which I do
not propose to refer in detail. On the
contrary, I want only to draw attention
this afternoon to two main changes which
have been made in the Bill, the first of
which deals with the question of the
Universities. It may be remembered
that some little difficulty was experienced
last year, particularly outside this House,
as regards the method by which this Bill
proposed to deal with the Universities.
All the matters bearing on the case have
been considered in the meantime, and
this year it has been decided, and the
House will be asked to agree in that
decision, to put the Universities referred
Solicitors Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
to in a separate schedule to the Bill. I
venture to think that that will be found
convenient
objectionable.
in
every
in
way
and
un-
The other change has been due to the
appearance since last year in the constitutional firmament of a new constellation, a constellation which we hope will
be constitutional and also Imperial, that
is Eire. We have had to substitute
reference to Eire for the former references to the Free State. I am hoping
that the House, which has already had
the indication made by the right hon.
Gentleman
suspending Standing
Orders, will follow in that part and allow
this Bill a swift and easy passage. In
particular do I hope there will be no
attempt made to interfere with the
elasticity of the discretion the Bill proposes to confer on the Council of the
Incorporated Law Society. I am particularly anxious that the Bill should get
through before the Summer Adjournment, because it is desired to bring it
into operation on the 1st January next
year, and consequently unless we get
the Bill through before the Summer
Adjournment the time left for making
such arrangements as have to be made
relating to the Bill-the preparation of
regulations and so on-will be unduly
short. I venture to think, having regard to the fact that the Bill has already
passed through all its stages in this
House and also passed through all its
stages in another place, that there will
be no difficulty experienced in accelerating its progress to the Statute Book.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL :
When this Bill came before this House
on a previous occasion I was one of the
Members whose names were on the back
of the Bill, and I still remain in support of the general principles of the
Measure. It has the support, I understand, of the Council of the Law Society,
and I believe not only is it in the
interests of the solicitors' profession that
a Bill on these lines should be passed,
but it is pre-eminently a Bill for the protection, if I may say so, of the layman.
I have been for many years in contact
with the solicitors' profession, and I wish
now to pay a tribute to the way in which
they protect the interests of their clients.
It is to their credit that not only are
they very jealous of the interests of their
clients, but they are also naturally very
jealous of the interests of their profession. Therefore they, as
well
as
everyone else, realise that it is in the
interests of the profession that the layman should be adequately represented
and carefully protected.
The Members of this House will see
that there are a great many provisions
in the Bill relating to the education and
qualification of solicitors, and I think
they will agree that it is of the utmost
importance that anyone standing in the
confidential position that a solicitor does
with his client should be a man fully
qualified for that purpose, and for that
reason a great deal of credit is due to
the Law Society for having taken this
step, which will bring the practice of the
profession in Northern Ireland in this
matter into line with the practice in
England. No one who had, as I had, a
small part in some negotiations that
went
on during the time the Law
Property Acts were passed in England
could fail to observe the very great
interest which the profession took in
those Acts, because they found, although
in some ways it was against their own
interests, it was in the interests of their
clients, and it could never have been
worked in England but for the selfsacrificing action of the solicitors' profession.
I feel that in coming forward
as the Law Society have done on this
occasion, to place the profession on the
same basis of qualification as English
solicitors, they are taking a step in this
country that is comparable to the high
standard of public interest which the Law
Society in England evince. For this
reason I ask the House to give its support to the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday, 7th June.
