Milk Bill-
24 MAY 1938
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill be now committed."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: "That the Bill be read the
Third time on Tuesday, 7th June.
Question to Minister.
REVISION OF PUBLIC BODIES
ORDER.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Sir Roland
Nugent:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether the Ministry of Home Affairs
have recently considered the certificate
required to be given by the chairman of
the Finance Committee of a County
Council on the treasurer's advice note,
under the Public Bodies Order, 1904,
Articles 14, 15, 16 and 17, and similar
certificates required from chairmen and
members of other public bodies, and if
not, whether they will do so with a view
to amend the wording.
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: The question standing in my name on the Order
Paper may possibly appear slightly
mysterious to those Members of the
House who have not sat on the Finance
Committee of a County Council, and I
think I should give a few words in explanation. At every meeting of a
Finance Committee of a County Council
a document, known as the treasurer's
advice note, is submitted to the chairman.
In this document, among other things,
there is the following certificate:
We hereby certify that we have carefully examined the annexed list (or
lists) of payments and checked the total
and find it correct, and we sign this
advice note on the part of the majority
of the Finance Committee.
That has to be signed by the presiding
chairman and two members of the committee. The point is this, that the list
referred to in the certificate normally
consists of some thousand or more
separate items for payment. These
amounts are only completed the night
before the meeting, and the unfortunate
chairman cannot see them until just
before the meeting. They cannot be
presented sooner. If he does not sign the
certificate at that meeting the whole
business of the county will be held up.
You are asking the unfortunate chairman
to do in a few minutes what it would
take a trained accountant a couple of
days' hard work to do. It is hardly in
the public interest that a large number
of presumably honest men should be
asked to perjure themselves twice a
month in this manner. I put the
question down in the hope that the
Minister of Home Affairs may be able to
clarify the position.
Mr. ROBB: The Ministry of Home
Affairs has under consideration the
provision of the Public Bodies Order,
1904, and the Articles mentioned by
the hon. Senator are among those which
the Ministry recognises require to be
revised.
Milk and Milk Products Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: I beg to move, "That
the Bill be now read a Second time."
I asked the Minister of Agriculture to
come himself to this House and explain
the provisions of it in order that Members may have from the horse's mouth
a proper explanation of the Bill.
Accordingly, I content myself with
merely moving the Second Reading.
Question proposed: "That the Bill be
now read a Second time."
The MINISTER OF AGRICUL-
TURE (Sir Basil Brooke): As most
hon. Senators are already aware of the
contents of the Bill I do not propose
to
weary them with a detailed exposition of the provisions. The object of
the Bill is two-fold: to enable bonus
and equalisation payments to be made
to suppliers of milk to factories engaged
in the manufacture of processed milk,
and to make administrative changes in
the collection of butter and margarine
fees. On the latter question there is
not a great deal to be said, and it might
be convenient if I dealt with it first,
so as to leave the way clear for a full
discussion of what is really the core of
the Bill, namely, those provisions
which relate to milk supplied to processed milk factories.
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Sir Basil Brooke]
Hon. Members may be aware that
under the 1934 Act fees were payable
for the sale of margarine and butter in
so far as it affected the retail trade,
the fee being 4s 8d per cwt., but if it
was for manufacturing purposes a mere
nominal charge was made. It was found
that this concession was being seriously
abused and there was no remedy which
the Ministry could find whereby any
check could be made to see whether the
butter which purported to have been
bought for manufacturing purposes was
in fact used for such purposes. In
many cases it was found it was sold for
retail purposes. At a meeting of butter
merchants in the city a system was put
before them, which at first they agreed
to, namely, a form of rebate, that is to
say, the full licence fee would be paid,
and, subsequently, having satisfied
the Ministry that the butter had been
used for manufacturing purposes they
would receive a rebate for that amount
down to a nominal penny per cwt.
On
further consideration the butter merchants felt that they would like to go
back to the arrangement that existed
prior to January 1st, that is to say, that
a certificate be issued certifying that the
butter had been used for manufacturing
Of course
purposes.
that was really
going back to the system under which, as
I have explained already, there were
abuses.
We have, therefore, put down in the
Bill certain safeguards so that the
Ministry can
itself that the
satisfy itself
butter has been properly used. In fact,
it can take proceedings against any
man who gives a certificate that the
butter had been used for manufacturing
purposes when it had been sold for retail
purposes. The general object of this
concession is to
that
ensure
local
merchants who make their sales within
the jurisdiction of this Parliament shall
not be prejudiced when in competition
with firms, say, in Great Britain who
make their sales outside the jurisdiction
of this Parliament. I should also like
to state that if a Second Reading is
given to this Bill it is proposed on the
Committee stage to bring in a series of
drafting Amendments which will have
the effect of deleting from Clauses 5 and
6 the references to the interpretation of
the expression "manufacturing purposes" and will make the corresponding
provision for the interpretation of this
expression in the interpretation Clause.
I now come to the more important of
the two parts of the Bill, the substance
of which is to be found in Provision 1.
In the past the manufacture of milk
products in Northern Ireland was confined practically and entirely to the
manufacture of butter and cream, with
the result that the 1934 Act made no
mention of processed milk factories. It
was obviously intended at that time that
the benefits that suppliers would receive
in the way of equalisation payment and
bonus would be made to suppliers of
any factory whether it was a creamery
or processed milk factory, as in the case
of this particular factory in question. I
should like to say that in England milk
sent to such factories as Messrs. Nestlé's
qualifies for assistance, and all this Bill
does is to bring us into line with the
practice in Great Britain.
I would like to make it quite clear,
because there has been a great deal of
misunderstanding on that point, that
the Bill is not being introduced to benefit Messrs. Nestlé's or, in fact, any
other undertaking of the same type. It
is solely and simply to
and simply to benefit the
farmers of this country. If the Bill is
rejected it is not Messrs. Nestlé's but
the farmers in the particular areas in
which these factories are set up who will
suffer, because they will be deprived of
benefits which other farmers receive.
There have been other misconceptions.
It has been stated that this particular
factory has been placed in a favourable
position as against the creameries, that
is to say, they will be processing milk
and the suppliers will be benefiting
from the equalisation payment while the
factory will not be subscribing anything
to the Milk Fund. I think it only
right to point out to hon. Members that
there are already several concerns in this
country which are taking their milk
from the farmers. Those farmers are
receiving the equalisation payment and
bonus payment, while the concerns pay
nothing towards equalisation payment.
I refer, of course, to those creameries
which are almost altogether concerned
Iwith the cream trade.
In addition to this there is also a very
large amount of milk which is converted
into cream in the ordinary factories, and
they, of course, pay nothing. There is no
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
24 MAY 1938
levy and there is no licence which would go
to the Milk Fund, so that in all equity the
farmers who supply milk to this creamery
should be placed in the same position as
the farmers who supply milk to the
creameries where those creameries manufacture that milk into cream, and which,
as I say, pay no licence and so contribute nothing towards the equalisation
payment. Further than that,
licence on the sale of butter is on sale
not on manufacture, so that no hardship falls on the creamery itself.
the
Various representations were made to
my Ministry on one point which we
have covered in Clause 2, namely, that
creameries are restricted in their areas
and that any fresh enterprise which
wishes to start in an area has to apply to
the Ministry before it is allowed to start,
because it is reasonable to conceive that
factories may start up in a district and
compete unfairly with a creamery already
in existence and which has been able to
draw on all the available supplies in
that district. We propose, in order to
cover that point, to inaugurate a system
of registration designed to prevent the
establishment of milk product factories
where a sufficient supply of milk is not
available. That brings any new factories
which are set up into line with the
creameries.
we
We
are
I wish to conclude by saying that the
policy my Ministry have in view is that
should process
all our raw material.
After all, agriculture produces the only
raw material in Northern Ireland and
it should be processed here.
carrying that policy through, and this
Bill is a new attempt on the same lines.
It is calculated that when this factory
gets going it will employ from 200 to
300 men. I feel in this case, as in many
others, that agriculture will be coming
to the assistance of industry and helping
to solve the very grave problem of unemployment.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I desire to ask
the Minister of Agriculture if in connection with this Bill he consulted the
Farmers' Union and on what he based
his opinion that there would be supply
of milk to justify the starting of a new
business under this Measure. On that
point he has given us no information up
to the present. I would like to know
did he satisfy himself before the introduction of this Bill that there would be
sufficient milk to supply the existing
creameries as well as this new factory.
It seems to me it would require a very
big increase in the cow population in
Northern Ireland to carry out the intentions of the Bill, and I think the
House should have the data on which
the Government decided to introduce it.
After all, we have no information to
justify the Bill. I would also like to
ask the Minister if he has consulted the
heads of the various farm and agricultural societies in Northern Ireland, and
whether they all agreed with him or
whether they expressed disagreement.
Will he let the House know on what
basis those who disagreed with him
did so?
If there is not a sufficient cow population in Northern Ireland to produce
the milk required for a factory of
this description and for the existing
creameries there is going to be created
a very serious state of affairs. The
creameries will have to go. I would like
the Minister to show us that this Bill
is not going to destroy the existing
creameries in which the farmers have
put their resources and which they have
carried on for years. I would like him
to show that it will not put them under
such a serious disadvantage that they
cannot carry on.
I have just been informed that the
present creameries cannot get sufficient
milk to carry on the work they would
like to carry on. If that is so, I would
like to know the data and information
on which the Minister has come to the
conclusion that he can sanction, with the
present cow population, a Bill of this
description. It is not a matter of personal information. I want to know on
what grounds he formed his opinion so
that the House may come to some conclusion as to whether or not they should
support this Bill. All the creameries in
Northern Ireland are financed by the
farmers, and this House is entitled to
the information I have asked
whether the cow population is sufficient
at the present time to justify this
Measure without doing very serious
harm to the existing creameries.
would like an answer to that question
before I could see my way to support
this Bill.
as to
I
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
a
Mr. DUFF: The Bill now before the
House has caused a lot of controversy,
particularly in country districts where
creameries are working. In my native
village of Coagh we
established
creamery in 1896. I was elected a
member of committee at that time and
have continued to act up to date, having
been president for the past ten years, so
I know something about the creamery
system.
we
We receive the full milk from the
farmers, extract the cream, and return
the skim milk which they use to feed
calves, pigs and poultry. After
started the creamery we competed at
different shows until we won ten first
prizes and a championship gold medal,
so we were able to command a good price
for our produce; and since the Ministry
established the "Red Hand" brand we
have steadily produced that standard,
our only drawback being the scarcity of
milk, as we could handle double the
quantity with very little extra cost.
When the creameries were started we
were all engaged in the production of
butter, but latterly the larger creameries
have been developing the cream trade.
We sell all the cream possible, which is
about one-third of our production, and
the balance we manufacture into butter.
During the winter months when milk
is scarce, to supply our customers we
are compelled to buy butter, principally
Empire, and for every cwt. of butter
we manufacture and sell for retail, we
pay a levy to the Ministry of 4s 8d per
cwt. The new milk factory will retain
all the milk, so no skim milk will be
returned to the farmers. The factory
will make no butter and, therefore, will
pay no levy and, as well as that, they
will receive all the benefits of a new
industry.
Under the equalisation scheme their
suppliers will receive the same benefits
as ours, although they have contributed
nothing. To assist our creameries to
meet this competition I would ask the
Ministry to refund to the creameries the
levy they now pay on all butter which
they manufacture from cream produced
by our farmers in Northern Ireland. As
it stands at present an old industry is
being taxed to the advantage of a new
one.
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: I had not
intended to intervene in this debate as
I am only a businessman who has come
very late in life and very reluctantly to
be a farmer. There seems to me to be
something extraordinarily curious from
a purely business point of view about the
argument that has been advanced by
hon. Senators. I understand them to
say that because there is not the supply
of milk, therefore you must not encourage a fresh demand. Surely, unless
you encourage a fresh demand you will
never get a fresh supply. Being a
businessman, I would never think of
laying down plant to supply a nonexistent demand any more than I would
think of getting more cows to supply a
non-existing demand, but for the same
reason I was delighted when I heard that
people were coming here to take 10,000
or 12,000 more gallons of milk. Here
was a demand for my product, and I
might even get a better price for it.
Anyhow, it would be worth while producing the supply, which is my job as
a farmer. It may be that my mind,
running from business reasons to
farmers' reasons, has slipped a cog
somewhere, but I find it very difficult to
believe that an increased demand for my
product can possibly be a permanent
disadvantage to me. I fully realise that
the interests of those who have created
the present demand should as far as
possible be safeguarded, but I understand the Minister has been meticulously
careful to do that.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
too am a farmer who has started farming
somewhat late in life. It does seem to
me that there is a very great need for an
increase in the number of channels
through which milk can be consumed.
One hon. Member of this House has complained that there will be no skim milk
for farmers from this new processing
factory. I do not know that that in
itself will be a very great injury to
farmers. My experience of skim milk is
that unless one has a large number of
potatoes or some other stodgy material
to use, skim milk is not of a great deal of
use. One of the great objections that
farmers have to breeding calves is that
their money is tied up for such a very
long time. I look upon this business
very much as a business man would look
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
24 MAY 1938
upon a by-product which would enable
him to increase his turnover.
The Ministry of Agriculture has
fostered these creameries and has done a
great deal for farmers. I know of no
Ministry that has done more for the
people of this country than the Ministry
of Agriculture. I really do think that
the farmers owe a great debt of gratitude
to the Ministry, although, like other
farmers, I get very much annoyed when
I have to fill up a great many forms.
Now is the Ministry going to set itself
out to ruin the greatest trade, perhaps,
in this country-the milk trade and the
butter trade? No, it certainly is not
going to do anything of the sort. It is
perfectly clear from this Bill that the
Ministry has met all the reasonable
demands of the creameries. It has been
stated in this House that there is not
enough milk produced to supply this processed factory and to supply the
creameries, but anyone who goes into the
markets and fairs, as I do, must be
struck by the fact that a great many,
an increasing proportion of, heifers are
being turned into beef. Anyone who
tries to buy them can get them. They
must be aware that the subsidy scheme
has done a great deal of damage to the
maintenance of heifers for the purpose of
milk production. The reason for that is
that under the subsidy scheme you can turn
over your product much more quickly
than in the case of a milch cow.
fore, I think it is to the benefit of
farmers that they should be encouraged
not to turn their heifers into beef, but
that some other means of quick turnover
should be given to them.
There-
It is a very great hardship on the
small farmer, who may not have a great
deal of land, that he should be discouraged in any way from maintaining a
dairy cow. We in this country used to
be great producers of dairy cattle. Now
the tendency is I know this from the
Agricultural Committee of my countyto let the breeding stock die out. I
think it will be a very great pity, both
from the point of view of the prosperity
of the farmers and from the point of
view of the general prosperity of the
country, if our breeding stock should die
out.
I think that if the farmers would
encourage the processing factories they
would
very quickly increase their stock
when they found that they would get a
reasonable return on their money. For
these reasons I beg to support this Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me that
the main point of this Bill-that is, the
payment of the bonus and equalisation
payments, if there are any, to a somewhat
wider circle-is a natural and equitable
consequence of the proposed establishment of the milk processing factory in
Northern Ireland, and that these
factories should be established seems to
me to be desirable, because they will provide they must provide or they could
not exist- -an alternative resource for the
disposal of milk. As I understand, the
demand for Northern Ireland butter and
cream in Great Britain, our best
customer, is not a constant demand that
can be relied upon. We have no control
here over the arrangements made across
the water for importation from the
Colonies, for instance, and therefore, it
seems to me we are always liable to a
sudden reduction in the demand for our
products. If we can turn our milk to
some other use, especially a use which
means something which will be sold under
world-wide conditions of sale, not limited
to Northern Ireland or even to the
United Kingdom, but something that
goes throughout the world, we shall
materially assist our own trade to start
with, and incidentally, it seems to me
the trade balance of the United Kingdom.
As regards the question as to whether
there will be enough milk, I think we
can rely upon the fact which has demonstrated itself time and again, that the
demand will create the supply. If more
milk is wanted more cows will come forward in due time. It will take time.
The time may be longer owing to what
has been alluded to, that is the destruction of heifers, but sooner or later the
cow population will be equal to the
demand. I cannot myself see that there
is anything in the Bill which ought to
cause any objection.
The administrative provisions, to which
reference has been made, are of course
minor matters for a later stage. I shall
have a small Amendment myself, but I
must mention it this afternoon, because
the right hon. Gentleman (Mr. Robb)
has already an Amendment of his own
dealing with the expression "manufacturing purposes. It may very well be that
in preparing his own Amendment he
""
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
I
may think fit to bring in the little
Amendment I am about to mention.
am going to ask the Committee, if
.necessary, to amend sub-clause 6 of what
at the moment is Clause 5-
In this section the
expression
means
"manufacturing
purposes
manufacture into articles by way of
trade, and includes cooking in connection with meals or refreshments provided at an hotel, restaurant or other
place of refreshment.
I think that we should follow the precedent we set in the last Marketing of
Eggs Bill, and include clubs and boarding houses. The point in regard to clubs
is a small one, but the boarding houses
may be of some slight importance,
because in the country districts the line
between the small boarding house and the
small hotel is so fine that there is a
possibility of doing some injustice. It
cannot be a serious one, but I think it
might be advisable to bring in the boarding houses. That is the only change in
the administrative provisions that, so far
as I can see at the moment, is likely to
be required.
Mr. MAGUIRE: The question has
been raised as to whether there will be
sufficient milk produced in Derry and
Antrim to give full supplies to the existing creameries and to Nestlé's processing
milk factory. At the present time there
is no great inducement to the farmers
to increase the number of milk cows.
The creameries pay 5d a gallon in the
summer and 6d a gallon in the winter,
and now Nestlé's come along with a price
of 7d a gallon to operate all the year
round. I contend that that is not a
sufficient inducement to the farmers to
increase the number of milk cows. If I
might suggest a solution to the Minister
of Agriculture, I would put forward the
idea of establishing a milk pool, similar
to that in operation in England. It may
be put to me that there is not sufficient
liquid milk in this area to justify that
step as compared with the quantity
available in England. It is said that
70 per cent. of the milk produced in
England is sold as liquid milk. I want
to refer to an answer given to me by the
Leader of the House a short time ago in
regard to the creameries in Northern
Ireland. The right hon. and learned
Gentleman informed me that only 20 per
cent. of the milk produced in Northern
Ireland was supplied to the creameries.
What becomes of the remaining 80 per
cent.? Of course a good deal is used by
the farmers for household purposes, the
feeding of calves, et cetera.
I agree with the hon. and gallant
Senator opposite (Sir Roland Nugent)
that it would be good policy to
create a demand, but in the present
circumstances it is very difficult to supply
the demand. There is no inducement to
the farmer to supply it, and unless there
is a great improvement in price I do not
see that there will be any great change in
this direction. The hon. and learned
Senator said that skim milk is not very
valuable, but in my district most farmers
place a good deal of value on it. I feel
that there is a great mineral content in
the skim milk that is returned from the
creameries, and that it is very much
appreciated in the feeding of pigs,
et cetera.
The creamery that I supply only
carries on business two or three days per
week during the winter period. It is
only since Thursday last that the milk is
taken daily. That is a great hardship
upon the farmers whose supplies
naturally run short in the winter period.
It certainly is a drawback that a man
cannot get his milk away each day. It
also prevents the farmers getting into
the different grades, Grade C and Grade
D. If it were possible to create a milk
fund I believe that it would satisfy the
farmers all over the Six Counties, for
each farmer would get the same price
for his milk, as is done in England at
the present time.
Mr. McHUGH: There are one or two
questions I should like to ask the Minister of Agriculture. There appears to be
some misunderstanding in regard to the
factory which is to be established in
Derry. The farmers are under the
impression that the Ministry will allow
this factory to canvass for milk over the
whole area of Northern Ireland and in
that way destroy the creameries. That
is a very important thing. We were
under the impression that the area in
which they could collect milk would be
one of 35 miles. If they are allowed an
unlimited space in which
to collect
milk they will eventually destroy the
creameries. The inducements which
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
24 MAY 1938
they are offering by paying higher prices
for milk will mean the destruction of
the creameries and creamery butter, if
they are permitted. In other words, if this
happens people will have to buy Empire
butter because there will not be sufficient
milk to produce the necessary butter for
the requirements of this area. I trust
that the Minister, in his reply, will give
an assurance that this factory will not
be allowed unlimited space in which to
collect milk.
Mr. BARCLAY: While I support
the Second Reading of this Bill there.
are one or two questions I should like
to ask the Minister. I understand a
factory is to be established in the City
of Derry. There is nothing in this Bill
to prevent farmers in County Donegal
from bringing their milk to this proposed factory. What I want to know is
this. Will farmers in County Donegal
who supply milk to this factory receive
a subsidy or a bonus? If they do, from
what source will this subsidy or bonus
be paid? It has been stated that the
creameries in Northern Ireland are
asked by the Ministry to pay a levy of
4s 8d per cwt. on all butter manufactured in Northern Ireland. If it is
from this source the farmers are to be
paid a bonus I protest, because it would.
not be fair to tax the consumers with
a levy of 4s 8d per cwt. and then give
a subsidy or bonus to those who live outside Northern Ireland.
I
I have no objection to Free State
farmers bringing in milk to the City
of Derry, but I do object to any subsidy or bonus being paid to them from
the levy placed upon the creameries.
speak for the consumers, and those I
wish particularly to speak for are the
poorest of the poor. It would be unreasonable to ask these poor people,
many of whom are unemployed, others
being old-age pensioners and others
widows and orphans, to pay this levy.
I support the Second Reading of the
Bill.
Major DOBBS: So many points have
been put forward that I will be as brief
as I possibly can. There is one point I
should like to make. The question has
been raised whether there will be enough
milk to meet the demands, and also as
to whether the price will be right.
Surely the question of supply depends
upon the number of cows in the country.
If the farmers realise that they can
supply not only the creameries but these
processing milk factories they should be
satisfied that there will be a demand for
supplies for five or six years. If they
realise this it will mean that they will
go more and more into the milk trade,
and that instead of fattening their cattle.
they will increase their milking herds.
The hon, and gallant Senator (Captain
Perceval-Maxwell) pointed out that the
less fattening of heifers that is done the
better for the country.
I now come to the question of price.
The price the farmers will receive from
the factories will be practically the same
as they receive from the creameries at
the present time, because the question of
the skim milk returned to them from the
creameries has to be taken into account.
There is one point in regard to the milk
supplied to creameries that I should like
to have made clear. Will the processing
factories take into account the amount
of cream in the milk, or will the milk
be paid for at so much per gallon? I
have great pleasure in supporting the
Bill.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I will first
deal with some of the questions that
have been asked, and then I will deal
with the larger issue that has been
raised. Senator Dobbs asked whether
the quantity of the cream content of the
milk would be taken into consideration
in regard to payment. That is already
done so far as the creameries are concerned. That is to say, the farmer is
not paid his equalisation payment on
gallonage alone, but on the cream content. I understand that the same
system will apply in regard to the processing factories.
Senator Barclay asked if a bonus
would be paid to those who supplied
milk from outside Northern Ireland. It
is true that the factory, if it is established in Derry, will be prepared to take
milk from Donegal or any other place.
No bonus, however, will be paid by this
Government.
I find it rather difficult to argue about
this Bill because, as hon. Senators will
have ticed, the points raised vary
from the
the high price which Messrs.
E
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Sir Basil Brooke]
Nestlé's are going to pay to the low
price paid by the creameries. Surely
it must be left to the farmer to decide
whether the price is too low.
If he
values the skim milk which is returned
to him he will naturally go to the
creameries. If he wants cash in his
hand to buy feeding stuffs for his cattle
he may decide to give his milk to Messrs.
Nestlé. I hope I made it clear in my
Second Reading speech that this Bill has
got nothing whatever to do with Nestlé's
or any other factory. All that it seeks
to do is to ensure that farmers who
supply milk to these processed milk
factories will receive the same treatment
as the farmers who supply creameries.
Whether it is manufactured into butter
or anything else has nothing to do with.
the factory. I will go further than
that. Neither the Ministry nor the Government could prevent Messrs. Nestlé's
from coming into this country any more
than they could prevent a grocer from
starting a shop. All the Ministry can
do is to see that the men who supply
milk get a fair crack of the whip.
Senator Duff raised a point which has
frequently been raised-there is perhaps some substance in it-that this
factory has been designated a new industry. The Government have not
power to say what is a new industry.
The Advisory Committee set up by the
Ministry of Commerce decide whether a
new factory is a new industry or not,
and in the case of Messrs. Nestlé's they
stated that they were starting a new
industry. It would be quite impossible
to refund the levy paid on butter because the creameries do not pay that
levy. The levy is made on sales and not
on production.
Senator McAllister asked whether the
Ministry consulted the various interests
concerned. The Ulster Farmers' Union
wrote to me two years ago and asked me
to get into touch with Messrs. Nestlé.
They were present at the discussions
which took place and took part in them.
It is very difficult for me to believe that
the Ulster Farmers' Union, of which I
happen to be a past president, would
oppose a Measure which will help the
farmers. They could not do anything
to prevent Messrs. Nestlé's coming in.
Senator Leslie put forward a strong
argument. He stated that there was no
guarantee at the present time that
butter would remain at the high price
which it commands to-day. That is a
possibility that must be borne in mind,
for with the end of the present drought
in Denmark the price of butter may fall
considerably. Therefore, it might be a
very serious thing if we had, so to speak,
all our eggs in one basket.
In
I think I have answered all the questions except that of supply, in which I
think all hon. Senators are interested.
Perhaps if I read something which I
have in my hand it might explain the
position. This is an investigation which
was carried out in June, 1937. The
inquiry showed that the butter made on
farms was equivalent to 27 million
gallons, that is, made on farms and not
in the creameries. I have the figures for
the rural districts of Ballycastle, Ballymoney, Coleraine and Limavady.
Ballycastle district the butter made on
farms amounted to 177,380 lbs., which
is equivalent to 514,402 gallons of milk.
I do not think it is necessary for me to
read the whole document. The total
gallonage available was 3,546,906.
Thus, over 3 million gallons of milk
were used for butter-making on farms in
the area during the year ended June,
1937, which, for 312 working days in the
year, works out at 11,300 gallons per
day. Messrs. Nestlé's only require 3,000
gallons per day, and it is expected that
there will be an increase in the cow
population.

Adjournment.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That
this House do now adjourn."
Mr. LESLIE: I presume until Tuesday, 7th June?
Mr. LONG: Yes.
Mr. LESLIE: And on 7th June I
imagine we shall be taking the Committee stages of the two main Bills we
read a Second time this afternoon, the
Milk and Milk Products Bill and the
Solicitors Bill. I may take this opportunity of saying that I shall not myself
have any Amendments to the Solicitors
Bill on the Paper at all, unless something unforeseen occurs in the meantime.
As regards the Milk Bill, I understand
there may be some Amendments.
Mr. ROBB: There seems to be very
little business left, and it may be that
on the Order Paper for 7th June there
will be a Motion to suspend the Standing
Order to take the remaining stages of
outstanding Bills on that day, so that
we may complete our business on 7th
June. There will be only some formal
Amendments to the Milk Bill, and I do
not expect there will be any Amendments
to the Solicitors Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Ten minutes before Five
o'clock until Tuesday, 7th
June.

The Senate met a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.

Laid before the House (pursuant to

Business of the House.
Resolved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking the remaining
stages of the Solicitors Bill and the Milk
and Milk Products Bill."-(Mr. Robb.)
Question to Minister.
LEASES IN ARMAGH CITY.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
McLaughlin :
To ask the Leader of the House if
he is aware that in the city of Armagh
a large number of houses are in bad
repair and a number of houses closed
down owing to the shortness of
leases; whether for this reason it is not
worth while for the lessee to do repairs;
whether the landlords in many cases
refuse to renew leases without an extra
payment of a large renewal fine; and
whether the Government will consider
introducing legislation to remedy this
state of affairs.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: The question I
have put down refers to the city of
Armagh to a very large extent. There
are a large number of houses there, the
rents of which range from 3s to 5s a
week, and they are being condemned by the
sanitary authorities owing to the fact that
the lessee has been unable to put them in
a proper state of repair on account of
the shortness of the lease. In a great
many instances leases are given for a
period of about 39 years, and at the end
of that time there is a large renewal fine
on the lessee, with the result that he has
got to allow the property to go into a
state of dilapidation. I myself know a
number of houses which the lessee is not
in a position to put into a proper state
of repair such as the sanitary authorities
require, owing to the fact that his lease
will expire in a very short time. He also
fears that on the expiration of the lease
the head landlord will impose a heavy
renewal fine, so that it will be impossible
for him to continue to maintain the
property.
I have been informed of five houses
in the city of Armagh that are at present closed owing to the fact that the
lease has but a short period to run, and
the lessee cannot see his way to spend
money on them. If the head landlord
would renew the leases for a sufficient
period the lessee would be quite prepared
to put the houses into proper order so
that they could be inhabited, and they
would let at from 3s to 5s a week. When
requested to do so the landlord agreed to
renew the leases on condition that the
265 Leases in Armagh City

7 JUNE 1938
Leases in Armagh City
lessee paid a very large renewal fine, and,
not satisfied with that, he inserted in the
lease a clause compelling the lessee to
spend a sum of £650 on these houses.
When the lessee went into the question
he found that if he carried out the landlord's demands the money he would receive in rent would not have paid the
interest on the money he would have had
to expend on the property and the renewal fine. The result is that these
houses are lying derelict at the present
time.
If the new Housing Bill which the
Government have promised to introduce
in this Parliament is to be a success the
Government will have to keep its eye
on this state of affairs in Armagh. That
Bill should include a Clause providing
that leases, as they fall in, should be
renewable without the payment of any
renewal fine. If that is not done I am
afraid that, as far as the city of Armagh
is concerned, the Bill will have very little
effect, because under the present state of
affairs between 100 and 200 houses in the
city of Armagh will have to be closed
owing to the fact that the leases are about
to fall in and the landlord will not renew them without a renewal fine, and the
stipulation that a large sum of money
must be spent on them. If the lessee
wishes to put his houses in order he has
got to go to the bank to raise the money,
and no bank will advance money on such
short leases.
a
Even the urban council, under the
existing conditions, cannot grant
subsidy or make any advance on these
houses owing to the shortness of the
leases. Therefore, I suggest the Government should keep this matter before them
when drafting their new Housing Bill,
and if they cannot see their way to compel landlords to renew the leases of
property without a renewal fine, at least
they will see that these houses will share
in
benefits that be given under
may
the Housing Bill, even though the leases
have only a short time to run.
any
Mr. MCALLISTER: Before the Leader
of the House answers the question I would
like to supplement in a general way what
Senator M'Laughlin has said. The rank
and file of the public do not understand
the difference between the position of
landlord and tenant in the city of Belfast and in the provincial towns.
In
Belfast there are a large number of landlords, with competition between them,
and a tenant seeking a lease has the choice
of a number of landlords. In almost
every provincial town one landlord has
a monopoly of the ground, and this single
landlord has the people entirely at his
mercy.
This is not by any means a new question. It has been raised in this House
and in another place time and again, but
the Northern Government is entirely unsympathetic owing to the fact that it is a
landlords' Government. This question
first arose in 1906 when a Bill, the Town
Tenants Bill, was introduced in the
Imperial House of Commons by Mr. J. J.
Clancey, M.P. Mr. Clancey has sent me
a draft of his Bill as it passed the House
of Commons. At that time the House
of Lords was all powerful, and dominated
the political situation in England. The
House of Lords was not then subject to
the democratic control it is subject to today, and when the Bill went to the House
of Lords they introduced a rather ingenious amendment which deprived lessees
of practically all the benefits the Bill was
intended to confer on them. The Lords
inserted a Clause declaring that the Bill
was not to apply to any lease with a
covenant for a valuable consideration.
That amendment cut the whole ground
from under the Bill.
The result is that to-day tenants in
Northern Ireland are compelled to accept
a 61 years' lease containing a building
covenant to expend a certain amount of
money on their premises, and, further, if
the tenant happens to be a man of substance, the landlord can, when the lease
is about to expire, sue him to put the
premises in repair if they are not in good
repair. As I have said, tenants in provincial towns have, in the great majority
of cases, only one landlord to deal with,
and they are completely at his mercy.
There are many people in Northern Ireland who have spent their whole life in
building up a business, and then, at the
expiration of their 61 year lease, they
find themselves absolutely at the mercy
of the landlord.
I would ask the right hon. Gentleman
the Leader of the House, to give me a
definite answer to this question. Can he,
on behalf of the Government, justify this
position, that when a tenant has spent,
say, £3,000 on a building, and when the
Leases in Armagh City 268
Leases in Armagh City
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
61 year lease has expired, the landlord
can put him out, or charge him 600 per
cent. more rent, and generally deal with
him as he will. I ask the Leader of the
House to say if the Government justify
that, and, if so, on what grounds they
do so.
I remember raising this question
about twelve years ago in another place,
and a member of the legal profession
admitted that in Scotland no tenant would
build unless he held the ground in perpetuity or had a grant in fee-simple. Why
should the tenants of Northern Ireland be
put in an inferior position to the tenants
of either England or Scotland? I sat
on a committee that considered this matter
about twelve years ago, and since then
the report has been ignored or shelved. I
admit that the right hon. Gentleman
(Mr. Robb) was on the side of the
Majority Report. I, as representing the
tenants, while acknowledging the rights
of landlords, but not justifying the abuses
that exist, was one of those who brought
in a Minority Report. I think it is high
time that the public in the provincial
towns should know that the Government
throughout all these years have been in
sympathy with the landlords; they are a
a landlords' Government, and have made
no effort to ameliorate the lot of the unfortunate lessees in Northern Ireland.
Mr. ROBB: The Ministry is aware that
a large number of houses are in an unsatisfactory state of repair in the city of
Armagh, and it is a fact that the Commissioner for the urban district has
condemned a number of houses; but no
closing order has been enforced where no
alternative accommodation is available.
The Ministry has no information as to
any action of landlords refusing to renew
leases without an extra payment of a
renewal fine.
The Government has already intimated
that it proposes to introduce legislation to
encourage the building of houses for the
working classes and to facilitate the carrying out of repairs to such houses, and its
proposals on the subject have been prepared and circulated to local authorities
for their observations.
I have no doubt that if special circumstances apply to Armagh those special
circumstances will be brought to the
I notice of the Government. Senator
McAllister has asked me how any person
could justify a particular state of affairs,
and instanced a tenant spending a large
sum of money upon a holding. The
answer, of course, is that since the year
1860 the relations of landlord and tenant
are founded upon a contract, and if a
tenant takes a lease or buys the remainder
of a lease he knows that his rights depend
upon the contract that is in the lease.
One can only assume that if he spends a
sum of £3,000, as the Senator has suggested, upon the premises he does so with
a full knowledge of what his rights are
under his contract. If he does that, he
does it, I assume, as
a business man,
calculating that he will have a reasonable
return for the money he is going to spend
during the balance of his lease. If he
is ignorant of the terms of his lease or
makes a bad calculation I do not think
the law is to blame. It is upon the
foundation that the relationship of landlord and tenant is one of contract that
the present state of affairs exist. Whether
in certain respects it might not be advantageously modified I am not saying at
the moment, but the foundation of the
present state of affairs is that there is a
contract between the landlord and his
tenant, that their rights are set out in
a document, that the tenant knows them
just as well as the landlord, and if he
makes a bad calculation as to the amount
he spends upon particular premises I do
not think he can blame anyone but himself.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I know you will
not permit me to reply now, but I will
put down a Motion which will enable me
to comment on the statement of the
Leader of the House.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Might I suggest
that there are
houses at present in
Armagh
Mr. SPEAKER: Does the hon. Member wish to ask a supplementary question? He knows that without the leave
of the House he is not entitled to make
a second speech.
So.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I am not doing
The Leader of the House stated that
he had no information with regard to
houses in Armagh. I respectfully sug
gest that he should ask the town authorities to inform him as to the number of
houses in Armagh that cannot be put
into a state of repair owing to the attitude taken up by the landlords.
Solicitors Bill-
Committee

Solicitors Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill."-(Mr. Leslie.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSES 1 (Power of the Society
to make provision as to education,
apprenticeship and qualifications), 2
(Admission to apprenticeship), 3 (Restriction on number of apprentices), 4
(Regulations as to apprenticeship), 5
(Candidates not to present themselves for
final examination until expiration of
apprenticeship), 6 (Repeal with saving of
ss. 12 to 15 of the principal Act, and s.
12 of the Irish Universities Act, 1908),
7 (Exemption from preliminary examinations), 8 8 (Special exemption from
preliminary examinations), 9 (Examination in lieu of preliminary examination),
10 (Default of solicitor in certain respects not to disqualify his apprentice),

Mr. LESLIE: Before making a formal
Motion, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time," I cannot refrain from expressing my humble thanks to the House
for the facilities they have afforded to
me, and for the easy passage which on this
occasion has been given to the Measure
before us. I venture to think that by
passing this Bill into law our Parliament
will earn the gratitude not only of the
Milk Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
Incorporated Law Society and of its constituent members, the solicitors of Northern Ireland, but of the general public in
whose services the honourable profession,
to which I have referred, gives unremitting attention. It may be recalled, perhaps with satisfaction, that this Bill
made its first appearance in this House on
the 7th of June of last year. We cannot,
therefore, be accused on the one hand of
undue haste, or, on the other hand, I
venture to think, of undue delay. I beg
to move, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time.'
""
On Question, Motion agreed to.
"Resolved, "That this Bill do now
pass."(Mr. Leslie.)
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any amendment.

to

CLAUSE 7 (Interpretation).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in
Clause 7 the Amendment which was
originally put down for Clause 5, page 6,
line 8, and I am going to ask the House
to leave out the words "an hotel" for
the purpose of inserting "a club, hotel,
boarding-house." The new words which
I suggest are the words which were
adopted in Section 16 of the Eggs
Marketing Bill of 1936, and I move them
really more with the intention of clarifying the Bill than for any other object.
The fact, I think, is that constructively
a reference to a boarding-house is there
already, because it seems to me that a
boarding-house is, as the right hon.
Gentleman, the Leader of the House,
would say, ejusdem generis with hotel,
and if you put "hotel" in and follow it
with other places of refreshment I rather
think my boarding-house is there already,
whether you insert the words or not.
It would clarify things and would also
assimilate this Bill to the Eggs Act, to
which I have already referred, if we
inserted these words. Accordingly I
move in the first instance to leave out
"an hotel," and if the House decide
they would like to stop there, that is, to
leave out the words "an hotel" and not
trouble about putting in the other words,
I am not sure that would not be a rather
satisfactory solution of such difficulty as
arises. In any case the whole thing is
of a most paltry character, and it is very
unlikely that any claims from any hotels,
boarding-houses, or clubs will be received.
Still they are there and it would be
difficult to make a separation.
It may
be that the House would prefer to stand
simply on the words "restaurant or other
place of refreshment," but if they do not
want to do that, and if they still want
to keep in the words "an hotel," I think
Milk Bill-
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
the words I suggest ought to be inserted.
Accordingly, in the first instance, I beg
to move to leave out the words "an
hotel."
The MINISTER OF AGRICULTURE
(Sir Basil Brooke): I regret that I cannot
accept this amendment. The object of
Clause 5 is to grant a concession in regard
to licence fees in respect of butter or
margarine sold for what are described as
"manufacturing purposes." It has been
represented to the Government that if
such a concession were not given, many
persons who purchase butter or margarine.
in considerable quantities might be driven
to make their purchases outside Northern
Ireland, and that, as a result, a certain
amount of trade would be lost to butter
and margarine merchants who carry on
business within the jurisdiction of our
Government. Moreover, if any manufacturer decided to make his purchases
here, and so was required to pay at a
higher rate for his supplies of butter or
margarine than would otherwise be the
case by reason of the imposition of the
licence fees, that manufacturer might be
prejudiced in competition with competitors who purchase their supplies outside
Northern Ireland.
-
It will be evident, therefore, that a
typical example of the kind of manufacturer whom it was desired to assist
would be the baker, the pastry-cook, or
the biscuit manufacturer who might be
selling biscuits in competition with cross-
Channel firms. It cannot, I think, be
argued that, say, a boarding house
keeper is a "manufacturer" in the same
sense as a baker; the business of a
boarding-house keeper is not the manufacture of foodstuffs, though he must,
incidentally, be engaged in the cooking
of food, and this may often include
the baking of cakes and pastries.
It was found, however, that many
restaurants conducted a home bakery
in connection with their business, and
that it would be unreasonable to
refuse the concession to the bakery merely
because of the existence of the restaurant,
or, alternatively, to require separate
records to be kept of the butter or
margarine used in the manufacture of
cakes, etc., consumed in the restaurant as
distinct from those sold over the counter.
Somewhat similar considerations apply in
the case of hotels, and the Government
has been somewhat reluctantly compelled
for administrative reasons to extend the
concession to "hotels,' restaurants and
other places of refreshment."
""
While it is true that it is difficult to
draw distinctions in cases of this kind, the
Government believes that a boardinghouse-and the same considerations apply
to a club-should be regarded as being
more nearly comparable to the large
family household than to the restaurant
which, incidentally, receives the concession largely because it is so often associated
with a business which, it is agreed, is
entitled to the concession in its own right.
In the Government's view, Section 16
of the Marketing of Eggs Act (Northern
Ireland), 1936, cannot be regarded as a
precedent since the main object of that
Section was to relieve boarding-house
keepers, etc., of duties, such as the holding of an egg retailer's licence, which are
properly applicable only to persons
engaged in the sale of eggs by retail, and
not to persons who only sell eggs incidentally as part of a meal.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I cannot
express myself as altogether convinced,
but I do think there may be some trouble
in the future between the boarding-house
and the small hotel; but, as I say, the
thing is very trivial and is certainly not
worth dividing the House on. I think
any boarding-house keeper who feels a
grievance must be left to rely on the
second shot, that is to say, that the words
are constructively in the Bill already.
Therefore I beg leave to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Resolved, "That the Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Mr. Robb.)
Resolved, "That the Bill do now pass.
-(Mr. Robb.)
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill with
some amendments, to which they desire
their concurrence.

Resolved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking the Second
Reading of the Finance Bill and the
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill."-(Mr.
Robb.)
Question to Minister.
DAMAGE BY BLACK-HEADED
GULLS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether any representations have been.
made to the Government to the effect that
black-headed gulls are indirectly very
detrimental to the maintenance and increase of trout in some of the rivers and
streams of Northern Ireland, and, if so,
whether it is proposed to take any action
in the matter.
Mr. LESLIE: I venture to think that
the question on the Paper requires a
word or two of explanation. It was
represented to me some time ago that
the gull, commonly called the blackheaded gull-although ornithologists, I
believe, claim that he is not entitled to
that epithet throughout his life is doing
very material damage to trout streams in
various parts of Northern Ireland.
was suggested that the damage was
distinctly confined to particular areas
and was not widespread. As the matter
It
was of some importance, partly in the
interests of trout fishing and partly in
the interests of tourist development, I
thought it worth while to put down a
question some months ago so as to allow
time for ample inquiry to be made. I
therefore now ask the Government to tell
us whether any complaints have been
made to the proper quarter, and, if so,
whether any action has been taken in
the matter.
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House):
As a result of representations made by
various fishery interests to the Government the question of the future protection
of the black-headed gull came under
consideration in the year 1936.
The Wild Birds Advisory Committee
appointed under the Wild Birds' Protection Act were asked to consider the question and it was their recommendation,
owing to the very considerable damageespecially to young trout-which they
were satisfied was caused by this bird,
that the protection at that time afforded
to this species should be completely
withdrawn.
Accordingly, the Minister of Home
Affairs made an Order on the 9th
February, 1937, under which the protection afforded to this bird, its eggs and
nest, was withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: Thank you.
University and Collegiate and Scientific
Institutions Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Robb.)
National Health Insurance (Amendment) (No. 2) Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Robb.)
Finance Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Robb.)
Mr. ROBB: I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now read a Second time." The
purpose of this Measure is to effect the
reductions in taxation announced by my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Finance, in his Budget statement.
Clause 1 and the Schedule to the Bill
will bring the rates of licence duty
Expiring Laws Bill-
Second Reading
18 OCTOBER 1938
charged on goods and hackney vehicles
into line with those imposed in Great
Britain. I should, perhaps, add that the
reductions in respect of hackney vehicles
apply only to the larger types as the taxation rates for the smaller classes carrying
eight or fewer passengers are already the
same as those applying in Great Britain.
Clause 2 will do away with the fee of
5s in the case of motor-cycles and 10s in
the case of other motor vehicles, which is
now charged when the surrender value of
a licence is refunded.
Clause 3 abolishes the licence duty of
£2 now imposed on persons hawking
goods in horse-drawn and motor vehicles.
The abolition of the tax has been made
mainly in view of the anomalies arising
under the present law whereby persons
hawking some classes of goods have to
procure a licence while persons hawking
other classes of goods are not under this
obligation. Also it has been found
difficult to determine what is hawking and
what is not.
By Clause 4 the limit of exemption
from estate duty of annuities left by
deceased persons to their dependents is
extended from £25 to £52 a year, and
modified rates of duty are imposed on
annuities between £52 and £104 a year.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."(Mr. Robb.)
Mr. ROBB: This Bill is in the same
form as last year when it received the
assent of this House without discussion.
I do not think it is necessary for me to
explain in any detail what it does, and
I therefore move, "That this Bill be now
read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I must say
that my recollection does not quite
coincide with that of the Minister of
Education. I was under the impression
that some remarks were made about the
Bill last year, with particular reference
to a statute which now appears in the
Second Schedule. I cannot, of course,
discuss the matter to-day, but I was
anxious to give notice that I intend to
direct attention to the second Schedule on
the Committee stage. I shall suggest an
alteration which will not, of course, do
violence to any existing arrangements, but
I thought it right to give notice in order
that hon. Members might avail themselves
of the opportunity of referring to the
particular Statute included in the Second
Schedule, and also to the debates in this
House of 8th December, 1921, and 6th
December in another place. I do not, of
course, oppose the Second Reading of the
Bill.
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. and
learned Member refers to Part 11 of the
Schedule?
Mr. LESLIE:
Yes.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I intend to oppose
the Second Reading of this Bill on a
matter of principle. In the first place,
we had no notice at all that the Second
Reading of this Measure would be taken
to-day and it does not appear on the Order
Paper. Yet we are asked to read it both
a First and Second Time in one day. I
suggest that that course cannot be justified
and I further suggest that the Second
Reading should be postponed to give
Members an opportunity of considering
the Bill.
Mr. ROBB: I was under the
impression-I may be wrong that this
Bill was circulated last week to all Members of this House. Moreover, on the
Order Paper for to-day, notice is given
that I would move the suspension of the
Standing Order to enable the House to
take the Second Reading of the Bill today.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I do say it is
creating a very bad precedent to take the
First and Second Reading of a Bill on
the one day, especially when the Order
Paper did not contain, in a separate
paragraph, a notification that the Second
Reading would be moved to-day.
Mr. ROBB: What better notice could
have been given than that which appears
on the Order Paper, I do not know. The
very first Order on the Paper is a Motion
in my name to move the suspension of the
Standing Order, to take the Second
Reading of the Bill to-day.
Mr. McALLISTER: I do not suggest
that there has been any discourtesy on the
part of the Leader of the House, but I do
suggest that on the Order Paper, the
Expiring Laws Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
First and Second Readings should have
been set out in separate and distinct
paragraphs.
Mr. SPEAKER: When the Motion
to suspend the Standing Order to enable
the House to take the Second Reading of
this Bill was put, the hon. Member did
not vote against it, as he could have
done.
Mr. BARCLAY: I received a copy
of the Order Paper and of the Bill last
week, and I knew the Leader of the House
proposed to move the suspension of the
Standing Order for the purpose of taking
the Second Reading of the Expiring Laws
and Finance Bills. Perhaps the hon.
Member (Mr. McAllister) did not get
these documents.

Mr. ROBB: Apparently fault is now
found with the form of the Order Paper.
Any objection the hon. Member has to

SCHEDULE.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Part
II of the Schedule to leave out of column
3, in line 32, the words, "The Whole
Act," and insert, "So far as it relates
to any Order made before the thirtyfirst day of December, nineteen hundred
and thirty-eight." I do not want, and
I do not think it is necessary, at all
events at this stage of the debate, to say
very much about the Amendment standing in my name. For one reason, the
Committee may remember that when
speaking on the Second Reading the
other day I suggested that hon. Members
who were with us in 1921 should refresh
their memories, and those who joined us
since should look into the matter. I
suggested a reference to the debates in
1921, the debates of 6th December in
another place, and of the 8th December
in this House, and I should like to think
that my invitation has been accepted. I
am sure that those who repaired to the
sources of information indicated will
have seen some rather illuminating
observations which were passed in one
Chamber or the other, particularly perhaps in the other Chamber.
But apart from all that, I do not
think it is very desirable or necessary to
recall the actual considerations which led
to the passing of the particular Act for
this purpose,
the Local Government
(Emergency Powers) Act of 1921. There
is no point in once more pillorying the
unfortunate county council whose misdemeanour led to the passing of that
Act. It is sufficient to say that if the
same circumstances recurred this House
would not hesitate to take similar steps
again. However, I am of opinion that
that emergency that arose in 1921 is now
a thing of the past, and that therefore the time has now come, after
seventeen years, when for the future
at all events we can rely on the ordinary
law. The ordinary law, as the Committee will remember, dealing with the
suspension, and so on, of boards of
guardians, goes back a hundred years.
I think the first hint of the power of
suspension is to be found in Section 56
of the Poor Law Act of 1838, and there
is also power possessed by the Court
under our own Local Government Act
of 1898, corresponding, of course, to the
Act of ten years earlier across the water.
The Court has certain powers in the
event of mal-administration and so on,
and I am suggesting that the time has
come when we should go back to that
and rely on permanent Statute and permanent courts rather than on emergency
Statute.
If I am wrong the Leader of the House
will correct me, but I think I am right
in saying that these extraordinary powers
-and that expression was applied to this
Act in another place at the time of its
introduction-have only been put into
operation twice during the last ten years.
One of the Orders has been withdrawn,
and the other Order is still in existence,
and on that existing Order I shall have
to speak again in a few minutes, not as
regards the Order itself, but with the
necessity for securing its continued
existence until that also comes to an end
in due course. But if we have only
had to exercise this power twice in ten
years I do suggest that the time has come
when the question of keeping an emergency Statute on the Statute Book is a
little out of date.
If we are not satisfied with our existing law, the provisions of the Local
Government Act and the Poor Law Act
I have referred to, the proper course is
to bring before this House a new Statute
intended to have permanent effect, dealing with the situation as it may be at
the time, and making such arrangements
for the future as may seem advisable to
Parliament. I venture to say that any
such Bill brought in to-day would not
follow exactly the wording of the emergency Statute of 1921, for I cannot think
that now we should think it right to give
the Ministry of Home Affairs power to
suspend the operations of the Corporation of Belfast or the County Council of
Down, both of which things can be done
under the emergency Statute. But that
is for the future.
Let me pass now to what it is I propose to do, because the whole arrangement is somewhat unusual. I have already
said that it is necessary to keep the one
existing Order in existence until it ex-
293 Expiring Laws Bill-

25 OCTOBER 1938
Committee
pires in accordance with the Act of 1921.
How are we going to do that? It is
obvious that it would be absurd to repeal
the Act of 1921 in October, because it
would normally expire at the end of
December next, and it is a little doubtful as to what would happen to that
existing Order. I am not sure that it is
not rather a matter for forensic
argument as to whether the well-known
repeal Section of the Interpretation Act
applies, but if it does not and the Act
is repealed the Order would expire at
once. On the other hand, we might do
nothing at all and leave the Act to expire on 31st December next, as it will
do if Parliament does not take steps. In
that case I am satisfied, from such
authorities as I have been able to consult, that the existing Order would be
out of date at 31st December, and we
should have succeeded in producing in a
mild way a state of chaos.
One cannot suggest that and I do not
think the Committee would be with me
if I did, and, accordingly, I am suggesting that we should so adjust matters as
to provide that this Emergency Powers
Act of 1921 shall continue in operation
so far as it relates to any Order made
before 31st December, 1938. That is the
date when it will expire if Parliament
does not interfere. That leaves the existing Order unaffected, and I have given
the Minister the chance of making new
Orders between now and the end of
December, if unfortunately that should
prove necessary. The terms of the
Amendment, I think, follow the precedent which the Committee will remember as regards the Courts Emergency
Powers Act which disappeared last year,
and, in conclusion, I should like to
repeat that the continuance of this one
existing Order is safeguarded.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I rise to support the Amendment moved by the hon.
and learned Senator. It may be remembered that four years and eight months
ago a Commissioner was appointed for
the City of Armagh, and the urban
district council was abolished. At that
time I objected very strenuously to the
appointment of a Commissioner and the
abolition of the council. Any irregularities that had occurred had at that
time been cleared up, and all I want to
say is that if this Government calls itself
a democratic Government there is not
very much democracy in depriving the
citizens of Armagh of their local govern.
ment franchise for a period of four years
and eight months. The local council will
have been abolished five years in March
next, and surely the time has now come
when the people of Armagh, and particularly the majority of the citizens
whom I represent, should have their local
government franchise rights restored to
them. While I speak as a representative
of these people I also speak as a ratepayer of Armagh, and as a ratepayer I
object to the continuance in office of a
Commissioner from the financial point
of view. He costs the ratepayers 4d in
the £, whereas we could elect a council
of ratepayers who would give their time
and services free and do their best for
the welfare of the city and its people.
It is time that the Commissioner went,
and the people of Armagh allowed to
elect an urban district council.
Major BOYLE: I also am interested
in the city of Armagh, and as the last
speaker has said, the local council was
dissolved some four years ago. The Ministry of Home Affairs at that time considered it necessary to take some steps
to get the council to carry out their
ordinary functions. A state of chaos
existed, and I understand that irregularities were rife. The Ministry sent
down inspectors and took over the duties
of the council, and I believe that one
of their first actions was to discover
money deficiencies. As a result the rate
collector was arrested, and was eventually lodged in His Majesty's prison for
twelve months. In addition, the clerk
of the markets was short to the extent
of £200 in his lodgments, and I think
the sum of these shortages amounted to
between £3,000 and £4,000, a part of
which was afterwards lodged by the
securities, but the ratepayers lost to the
extent of 1s 6d in the £, and the rates
were raised from 13s 6d to 14s 6d in the
first year the Commissioner was there.
The rates have since fallen considerably,
and I think that instead of the Commissioners costing the ratepayers 4d
in the £, as suggested by Senator
McLaughlin, he has saved us a great
deal. The rate is now 10s 8d in the £,
and I hope that when the new rate is
struck it will be even less. The city
has undoubtedly been better managed
since it came into the hands of the Commissioner, and I hope the Committee will
not pass this Amendment.
295 Expiring Laws Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
Mr. ROBB: With regard to the Commissioner for Armagh, the Minister of
Home Affairs, speaking in another place
on the Second Reading of this Bill, said
that the condition of affairs in Armagh
has steadily improved, and he hoped that
in a not very long time he would be
able to restore the local council to its
work. Therefore there is a prospect of
the urban district council of Armagh
coming into existence again soon.
Regarding the Amendment moved by
Senator Leslie, it is quite true, as he said,
that only twice in ten years has the Act
been put into force, but the mere fact
that it has been put into operation with
general public approval shows that there
was some necessity for the existence of
this power. The Ministry has found this
power useful in the past and the Government think it is a power that should be
preserved for the present. Therefore I
am unable to accept the Amendment.
The
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I think there is
no one in this House who knows the
affairs of the city of Armagh better than
I do. I happened to be the chairman of
the urban district council at the time it
was dissolved, and I want to say this,
that a great deal of the laxity was due
to the Ministry of Home Affairs.
affairs of the council had been practically
cleared up when the Commissioner was
appointed, and there was practically no
reflection on the existing city council.
The city council requested that a public
inquiry should be held into the affairs of
the council. We had nothing to hide, and
we were perfectly satisfied to go before
any public inquiry.
I might mention that if the urban
council as it then existed had received
the assistance of Senator Major Boyle's
party in the council possibly things
would not have gone so far, so far as
the rate collector is concerned. A
motion was put forward to allow the
chairman and some other members to
investigate certain things, but Senator
Major Boyle's party, with the assistance
of one or two others, got that resolution
turned down and refused to allow any
investigation into the affairs of the rate
collector. Two days afterwards she was
under arrest. Therefore, it was no fault
of the council, which requested an
inquiry into what took place.
Mr. LESLIE: In rising to reply to the
debate, might I explain once more that
I said nothing about the particular Order
as such. I was dealing with the matter
on broader lines. I am sorry I seem to have
failed to impress on the mind of my hon.
and gallant Friend that the effect of
this Amendment would not be to destroy
this Order in relation to Armagh of
which we have just heard, but that is
by the way. The Amendment will not
affect that Order one way or the other,
except that it will keep it in force after
31st December if the Ministry so decides.
I am afraid I must join issue at once
with the Minister of Education. He is
apparently desirous of grafting on to
our permanent Statute law an emergency Statute, and that is a thing I
object to. It must be remembered that
before these powers can be put into force
something in the nature of a dispute between the Minister of Home Affairs and
the particular council concerned must
have arisen. If that is so, it is not in
accordance with our notions either of
equity or justice that the Minister should
be judge in his own cause. That is what
it comes to. A difference of that sort,
regrettable though it may be, ought to
be decided by the King's Courts, not
by the Minister.
Again and here I think I am rather
following Senator McLaughlin-the direct
responsibility of all these councils is to
their ratepayers. It is not to the Minister of Home Affairs. Of course, they
have a certain responsibility to Parliament to carry out the instructions of
Parliament, and it is for the Minister
of Home Affairs to exercise certain
powers of supervision in order to see that
those responsibilities are carried out. That
is a rather different matter, but I do not
think we can allow the Minister of Home
Affairs permanently to override the
Local Government Act of 1898, and that
is what in effect these Orders do. As I
said before, for myself at all events I
am not prepared to leave in the Minister
a power to summarily suspend the
Belfast Corporation or the councils of
any of our counties. The short point is
whether Northern Ireland is to be
governed by Parliament or by bureaucracy. We have courts, and we ought
to be content with them. We cannot
allow any abuse of the complacence of
Parliament. If the habit grows we may
297 Expiring Laws Bill-

25 OCTOBER 1938
Second Reading
pass Emergency Powers Acts and carry
them on until Dora has become a member of the domestic staff. That is
entirely contrary to my idea of government, and accordingly I am not prepared to withdraw the Amendment.
Question put, "That those words be
left out."
