University and Collegiate and Scientific
Institutions Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: The purpose of this Bill
is to authorise the making of larger
grants by Government to the Queen's
University of Belfast and to the
Governors of the Armagh Observatory,
and the making of a grant to the Magee
University College, Derry.
For the past ten years the Government has assisted the work of the
Queen's University
University by grants-in-aid
amounting annually to a total sum of
£40,000. It is proposed to increase this
sum by £20,000 per annum, so that the
total annual grant will amount
£60,000, the increase to have effect as
from 1st April, 1938.
to
The Government are satisfied that the
importance of the work done by the
University and the need for providing
additional facilities for the carrying on
of this work amply justifies the proposed
additional grant from public funds.
It is also proposed, by means of this
Measure, to make a grant of £2,500 per
annum in aid of the funds of the Magee
University College, Londonderry.
The college originally consisted of a
scientific and theological department,
and for that reason the Government was
unable to assist it in its work. The
endowment scheme governing the college.
was amended, however, in 1934 in such
a way as to enable a literary and
scientific department to be run entirely
separately from the theological department. The literary and scientific
department is undenominational, and
the grant-in-aid will be applied solely
to it.
Hon. Members will observe that
Clauses 3 and 4 of the Bill provide for
the separation of the accounts of the two
departments, and for the representation
of the Government on the governing
body.
It is intended to increase the grant
made in aid of the funds of the Armagh
Observatory. The amount of the grant
is not laid down in the Bill, but it is
the intention of the Government that it
shall be fixed at £300 per annum for the
next five years. Since 1927 the amount
of the grant made by the Government
has been £100 per annum.
Universities Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Robb]
The Government are satisfied that the
increased grant is necessary if this, the
only observatory in Ireland, is to carry
useful work under modern
out
ditions.
con-
At present the governing body consists of the Archbishop, the Dean and
Chapter of Armagh, and two laymen
co-opted by them. They have agreed
with the provision made in Clause 7
that the number of lay members be
increased to five, one of whom shall be
appointed by the Government and one
by the Queen's University. I may say
that since this grant was suggested the
astronomer at Armagh Observatory has
been appointed lecturer in astronomy in
the Queen's University of Belfast, and
a close connection will thus be preserved
between these two bodies, a connection
which, I am sure, the House will very
gladly approve.
There is provision made in Clause 7
for the observatory to sell or lease land
belonging to the observatory. The
observatory was constituted in the
eighteenth century by Act of Parliament, and it is the possessor of a certain
amount of land. Already one portion
of land has been granted for the purpose
of building a school, and that required
a special Act of Parliament. It is now
proposed to give power to the governing
body to sell or lease any part of the
remaining land, but there is a safeguard, and that is that it can only be
done with the consent of the Ministry
of Finance. I beg to move, "That the
Bill be now read a Second time."
Colonel SINCLAIR: As this Bill is
to all intents and purposes a Money Bill
it is not competent for a Member of this
House to move an Amendment to it,
although I have no such intention. I
would merely ask the indulgence of the
House for a short space while I explain
a few points that deserve a wider
publicity as to the facilities offered by
the University and its limitations. I
feel that if these were better understood
by the public a still greater amount of
sympathy would be directed to the
University, which is the only University
we have in the Imperial Province.
In the first place, let me express the
gratification of the University authorities for the handsome addition to the
revenue was
resources of the University made by the
Government of Northern Ireland, and
given generously at a time of depression.
trade and
This
agriculture.
additional
very sorely
needed for new buildings, extensions,
and modern scientific appliances and
their frequent and costly renewals. It
should be realised that in some departments the professor is obliged to deliver
the same lectures in one day three times
over owing to the limitations of classroom accommodation. That congestion
is still greater in the laboratories. The
rapid advance of science in the thirty
years of the University's life involved
a heavy demand upon resources that
were deemed sufficient thirty years
before.
Moreover, it should be recalled that
by an unfortunate oversight in the drafting of the Irish Universities Act of
1908-09 no provision was made for
Queen's sharing in the annual grants of
the University Grants Commission which
bestows sums from £2,000 to £5,000
annually to British universities. Therefore, we have to endeavour to compete
with rival universities of comparable
size with this heavy financial handicap.
We do not receive one penny from this
University Grants Commission. Although
it is not embodied in this Measure, I
hope that in time this handicap will be
removed, and that we will be placed on
the same footing as universities across
the water in regard to this annual grant.
The public, and particularly the
parents and guardians of intending
students, should understand that a
student pays for little more than onethird of the cost of his education at
Queen's, the remaining two-thirds being
supplied by the University. Our fees
are the lowest in the United Kingdom.
and we are desirous of keeping them
low by as rigid an economy in keeping down expenses to as low a figure
as is compatible with efficiency. We
are very proud of our 1,650 students,
and we will challenge comparison with
British modern universities as to the
achievements of our graduates in all
parts of the Empire. We have quite a
large number of officers in the Services,
and many of our graduates occupy the
highest positions in various walks of life.
I was delighted to hear that in another
place this Bill was favourably received,
!
Universities Bill--
Second Reading
25 OCTOBER 1938
and I am sure this House will be equally
favourable in passing the Measure.
Particularly gratified was I when two
hon. Members in the other House-the
Member for Pottinger and the Member
for Woodvale-applauded the Bill. They
suggested that the University should provide evening lectures for working men
and women ambitious of educating themselves up to the highest standards. In
answer to the hon. Members for
Pottinger and Woodvale their wishes in
this regard have been in being since
1910, and only require a wider publicity
to increase the numbers. Students
attending these evening classes
can
obtain the degree of Bachelor of Commercial Science in four years. Again,
the close association of the university
with the Technical College of the Corporation enables students to proceed to
the degree of Bachelor of Science in
four years. Further, a joint committee
of the University and the Workers'
Educational Association, a most admirable body, arrange for classes for adults
in general cultural and scientific subjects.
These classes are conducted by university
professors and lecturers, and most of
them extend over two and three years.
I do not desire to strain the indulgence of the Senate unduly, but there
is one other matter I must mention. We
are anxious to have a Faculty of Architecture as soon as possible at the University.
All our architects are keen to have such
a Faculty, and although it would cost a
large sum to endow a chair and assistant
lecturers, running into five figures, we
shall live to hope that the Imperial
Province of Ulster may, in time, add
this useful and cultural subject to the
equipment of its only university. As
the Leader of the House has pointed out,
astronomy has already been provided for,
and the University is now linked up with
the Armagh Observatory. An
plished lecturer has been appointed in
the person of Dr. Lindsay, and already
four students have been enrolled, so that
astronomy is safe in the keeping of this
joint association of Armagh Observatory
and Queen's University.
accom-
I thank the House for allowing me to
dilate on a few points that deserve
wider publicity, and I hope the public
will think well of the University and take
a proper pride in it. The advance of
science is such that we always require
more and more money for the proper
equipment of our University, and I can
assure the House that we shall be able
to make very good use of this latest
grant from the Government, for which
we are deeply grateful.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure we are all
very glad it has been found possible to
find some extra money for the University
and I am also sure we all hope that the
persuasive eloquence of the hon. and
gallant Senator who has just spoken will
not be without its effect in another
place, where the purse strings are somewhat tightly held. Having congratulated the University on their success in
picking up the not inconsiderable trifle
of £20,000, I should also congratulate
the Government on the ingenuity they
displayed in keeping the grant to Magee
College within the terms of a well-known
Act of Parliament.
I should now like to ask the Leader
of the House whether he is quite satisfied with the wording of Clause 7, or
whether in his judgment anything further is required. We are giving notice
of application, authorisation, and direction, but we are not in terms giving any
power to act in accordance with that
authorisation or direction. I am not
particularly fond of Amendments or of
dividing the Committee except on questions of principle, and therefore it might
save time if an answer could be given
now.
Major BOYLE: I would like to say
a word about the Armagh Observatory.
Probably very few Members here know
why there is an observatory in Armagh.
It was built in the year 1791 or 1792
by the then Lord Primate, Richard
Robinson, who afterwards became Lord
Rokeby, a great benefactor to Armagh
and the neighbourhood. He built and
equipped this observatory at his own.
expense, and laid out twenty acres of
ground. He also endowed it out of his
own private purse. The income from
endowment was very considerable in those
days, and enabled the astronomer to live
comfortably in the house built for him.
Unfortunately for the present astronomer
it was invested in land which fell under
the axe of the Land Act, with the result
that the endowment has now sunk from
several hundreds a year to very little, so
that there is practically no money to
keep up the observatory.
Universities Bill-
General Debate
THE SENATE
[Major Boyle]
The observatory was vested in the
Archbishop, and Dean and Chapter of
Armagh Cathedral, and it was vested in
trust for ever as a Corporation to
be "useful to the public." The
Archbishop also has the appointment of
the astronomer, but the Corporation,
"if the astronomer continues obstinate,'
have the power to remove him. I think
it is of interest to know that none of the
astronomers have up to the present been
removed for being obstinate, and I am
very glad the Government are helping
this old institution.
Mr. ROBB: I would only like to say
that I have considered the wording of
Clause 7, and I think it is sufficient for
its purpose.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.
National Health Insurance (Amendment) (No. 2) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: Briefly, this Measure provides that the full capitation rate of nine
shillings shall be paid as from 1st of this
month to all dispensary doctors in respect
of
their National Health Insurance
scheme patients.
As Hon. Members are aware, dispensary medical services are the responsibility of boards of guardians who pay
dispensary medical officers with the assistance of substantial grants from Government funds.
The dispensary system was, of course,
in existence for the provision of free
medical services for the poor before medical benefits were provided in 1930 as part
of
the National Health Insurance
Scheme.
It was anticipated that the introduction of medical benefit under the Insurance Scheme would reduce the amount
of work done by the dispensaries and an
enquiry made as to the occupations of
the persons treated over a period in the
dispensaries showed that about half of
them would be eligible for medical benefit under the National Health Insurance
Scheme.
The Government proposed that, in
view of the reduction in the work of the
dispensaries, which would take place on
the introduction of the new scheme, that
dispensary salaries should be reduced.
The doctors preferred, however, that
they should be paid on a lower capitation
rate in respect of the patients treated by
them under the new scheme. Their
reason was, I understand, that they were
afraid that their conditions of service
and superannuation rights might be prejudiced if their salaries as dispensary
doctors were to be reduced.
As a result a reduced capitation fee of
7s 6d was fixed for dispensary doctors
who also practised under the Insurance
Scheme as against the 9s fee payable to
other medical practitioners under the
scheme.
The saving was paid into the Exchequer, which was thus placed in the
position it would have been had the
salaries of dispensary doctors been reduced and decreased grants paid to
boards of guardians.
The work of the dispensaries has, however, grown steadily so that it is now
much the same as it was before the Insurance Scheme came into operation. One
reason has been that owing to the slump
and trade depression many people could
not afford to pay for the doctors they
had been accustomed to have, and on
that account the work of the dispensaries
has increased. On a recent investigation
it was found that in fact, so far from
the work of the dispensary medical
officer having been reduced, it has in
fact in recent years been increasing, and
consequently there would have been no
justification whatever for the reduction
of the dispensary medical officers' salary,
and there was no justification for a lower
figure being paid to the dispensary doctor
in respect of insured persons. This
claim has been strongly urged upon the
Government by the dispensary medical
officers, and the Government is satisfied
that their claim is just. This Bill
accordingly proposes to amend what was
done at the time of the introduction of
the scheme in 1930, and the fee of 9s
will in future be paid to dispensary doc
tors as well as to other practitioners.
That is the whole effect of this Bill, and
I beg to move, "That the Bill be now
read a Second time."
305 Architects Registration Act

25 OCTOBER 1938
Adjournment
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for tomorrow.
Architects (Registration) Act, 1938.
Message from the Commons.
That they have passed the following
Resolution to which they desire the concurrence of this House:
-
"That an humble Address be presented to His Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland as follows:-
Your Grace :
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Commons of Northern Ireland in Parliament assembled,
do by this humble address pray that
it may please His Majesty to make
provision by Order in Council under
sub-section (1) of section 6 of the
Architects (Registration) Act, 1938,
that the said Act shall extend to Northern Ireland, subject to such adaptations in its application to Northern
Ireland as may appear to His Majesty
in Council to be necessary.
Ordered, That the said Message be
taken into consideration to-morrow.
Adjournment.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
House do now adjourn."
Mr. LESLIE: Could you give the
House any idea of the business for tomorrow?
Mr. ROBB: We propose to take the
further stages of the Bills on the Paper
to-day, and the Second Reading of the
Hairdressers Bill.
at
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly
fifteen minutes after Four
o'clock.
Private Business
Hairdressers Bill-
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 26th October, 1938.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
New Member.
Mr. SPEAKER informed the House
that he had received a certificate from
the Clerk of the Crown that Henry
Taylor, Esquire, of 105 Ballygomartin
Road, Belfast, was, on the 11th day of
October, 1938, duly elected a Senator to
serve in the Parliament of Northern
Ireland in the room of Mr. Hill Dickson,
deceased.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Marks' Divorce Bill.
The following report was made from
the Joint Committee on Divorce Bills:-
That they had considered Marks'
Divorce Bill, and that the averments in
the Preamble had been proved to their
satisfaction, and that they had gone
through the Bill, and directed the same
to be reported to the House without
amendment.
McMullan's Divorce Bill.
The following report was made from
the Joint Committee on Divorce Bills:-
That they had considered McMullan's
Divorce Bill, and that the averments in
the Preamble had been proved to their
satisfaction, and that they had gone
through the Bill, and directed the same
to be reported to the House without
amendment.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Holidays
with Pay Bill, to which they desire the
concurrence of this House.
Holidays With Pay Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now
read a First time."-(Mr. Robb.)
Ordered, That the Bill be read a
Second time on Tuesday next.
Hairdressers Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LESLIE: The little Bill which
I have the honour to present for the consideration of the Senate this afternoon
is, perhaps, not altogether a Bill without a history, but I hope that it is not
going to be an example of the fact that
that which has a past has no future.
The history of the Bill really is that for
some years
the
past
Hairdressers'
Association, which consists both of employers and employees, have been working, slowly perhaps, but not altogether
covertly, in order to obtain some
amelioration in respect of certain
matters incidental to their calling. I
think they have been proceeding, if I may
say so, not only energetically but wisely,
and that I shall endeavour to indicate
later on.
Perhaps the most important matter to
which attention has been directed is the
matter of public health. We all know,
unfortunately, that unclean brushes and
other implements of the hairdressing
trade are not infertile producers of
diseases of various kinds, and most
diseases dermatitis, and things of that
sort are not attractive. It is hoped
that one effect of this Bill will be to
minimise the risk of anything of that
nature in the future. I hope that the
efforts which have occupied some years
are now about to be crowned with success. The hon. Member who represents
the Falls Division of Belfast in another
place has piloted the Bill through the
more or less troubled waters which prevail in that other place, and it has now
been put up to me, so to speak, that I
should put my hand to the tiller and
endeavour to persuade this House that
it would be a good thing to do to pass
the Bill.
I must tell the House at the commencement that I, myself, am not responsible either for the drafting or the
ararngement of the Bill before us. I
am in no sense the architect or even the
constructor of the Bill. Indeed, I must
tell the House at the outset quite
definitely that unless certain Amendments are made to the Bill I shall be
unable to ask the House to give it a
Third Reading. The necessary Amendments are matters of machinery, but I
think of very essential machinery, and
I will mention them more specifically
Hairdressers Bill-
Second Reading
26 OCTOBER 1938
when I ask the House to accompany me
through the Clauses. I may say at once
that most of the suggestions I shall have
to make will find their counterpart in
the Ice-Cream Act, which we passed last
Session, because that happens to be the
last general Act empowering local
authorities to register, and in some
senses the two cases are distinctly of the
same class.
The Bill, of course, is purely permissive. It rests entirely with the local
authority whether it shall or shall not
be put into operation in their area, but
part of the spade work which has been
done, and to which I have referred, has
been to obtain information from the local
authorities throughout Northern Ireland,
and I have seen and taken note of fully
a score of letters from local authorities
in different parts of Northern Ireland,
running from Portrush and Ballycastle
in the North to Tandragee in the South,
with branch lines taking in Newtownards
and Omagh, and all have expressed
approval of the principle of the Bill.
Also the Hairdressers' Association,
through their very energetic secretary,
has approached the Ministry of Health
in Whitehall and has received from that
Ministry a very helpful set of model
by-laws. I shall not trouble the House
with these this afternoon, but they will
no doubt be of use to the Minister of
Home Affairs at a later date, when the
construction of by-laws dealing with
registration comes to be considered.
One point the House will require
definite assurance on is whether the Bill
will have any prejudicial effect on the
small man. My information is that the
small man will not be prejudiced in any
way, and also it is that small man who
is likely to form the mainstay of the local
committees of the Hairdressers' Association, which will be appointed
approach local authorities to see whether
the Act should or should not be put into
operation in their districts.
to
Now I must ask the House to accompany me through the Clauses. The first
Clause is the important Clause. It
enables the Act to be put into force by
order of the Minister of Home Affairs
on the application of the local authority.
I shall have to ask the House in Committee to leave out the two words, "owner
or," so as to cast the duty of registration on the occupier of the premises.
The second Clause indicates the
matters in which the local authority
may make by-laws. They may make
a by-law requiring inspection of the
premises, but hon. Members will see that
as the Bill stands there is no provision
for an inspector to do the inspecting.
That is a matter to which again I must
invite attention later on. The provision
for securing the cleanliness and proper
lighting and ventilation of the premises
goes almost entirely without saying.
is the main object of the Bill to see that
these premises are
and
kept clean
properly ventilated. The following
paragraph is on the same lines, prescribing the precautions to be taken against
infection. It is
It
necessary that the
people who employ themselves as barbers
should receive some sort of training as
apprentices before they embark on what
may be the somewhat dangerous undertaking, so far as the patient is concerned, of shaving or applying electric
irons for giving ladies' hair a permanent
wave, so that their instruments shall not
be used recklessly and with considerable
risk.
Again, there is this question of insurance against accident. There have been
too many cases in the courts of injury
inflicted, mostly on women, by the
injudicious use of the apparatus intended
for producing what I hope I am correct
in describing as a permanent wave. It is
obvious that if heavy damages are given
against a small hairdresser he is not
likely to be able to meet the liability
unless he is supported by insurance.
Then we come to the usual application
of sections of the Public Health Act
which deal with by-laws, and there will
be the place where we must add words
about the inspector in the terms of the
usual protection to see that he goes about
his duties unopposed. Then we come to
penalties for offences, which are
are very
small, and we must add a definite provision enabling the court which convicts
a man to cancel the registration of the
premises. It seems to me that that is
an imperative requirement, and I shall
ask the Committee, in due course, if the
House passes the Bill to-day, to allow
me to insert that.
Then we give the ordinary summary
jurisdiction procedure, and I have no
remarks to make until we come to the
end of Clause 7, where it will be
Hairdressers Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
necessary to insert another formal subsection to which we are accustomed, providing for the construction of any
enactments of the Parliament of the
United Kingdom. We almost always do
that, and in my view this is not a case
where it ought to be left out.
As to the commencement of the Act,
there is no suggestion in the Bill, and I
do not propose to make any, but if any
hon. Member does think we ought to
provide a definite date for commencement, it is a matter that should be considered. I do not, myself, think it is
necessary, but there might well be Members who think it advisable. That is all
I have to say about this Bill, and I beg
to move, "That the Bill be now read a
Second time.”
Captain
per-
PERCEVAL-MAXWELL:
Many years ago the professions of
surgeon and barber were carried on by
the same man, and it appears to me that
this Bill is the beginning of an attempt
to apply to the barber a similar system
of regulation as is applied to other professions, in which dangerous and
haps harmful instruments are used. I
must confess that in reading through this
Bill grave misgivings have been raised
in my mind as to whether the Measure
is not applying the doctrine of using a
sledge hammer to crush a nut. Anyone who has gone into the premises of a
country barber in some out-of-the-way
district cannot have failed to have noticed
that his equipment and plant are very
often not as up-to-date as those of a
barber in our big urban areas, and it is
inevitable, I think, that this should
be so.
I agree with what Senator Leslie said
about the necessity of cleanliness and the
necessity for a fair knowledge of some of
the more dangerous instruments that are
used by hairdressers, but what has
puzzled me about this Bill is, who is to
be the judge? No one would suggest
that a country barber in an out-of-theway country district should have tiled
floors and tiled walls, nor should he
have the same standard of spotless
cleanliness as a barber's shop where the
turn-over is enormous in comparison, and
where all the most modern precautions
are taken against the risk of contamination. Therefore, I think that the officer
who carries out the inspection of barbers'
premises under this Bill will require to
be a most broad-minded man; otherwise
great hardship might be done in country
districts.
Then it appears that under this Bill
it is quite within the power of the
inspector, indirectly, not to handicap a
man in business, but to put him out of
business altogether. That, I think, is
a very dangerous weapon, a weapon which
might have serious consequences, if not
for the man himself, for the unfortunate
people who have to rely on a barber to cut
their hair. There are not a large
number of applicants for the business of
barber in a country place, and it might
be that a barber would be put out of
business because the inspector thought
the premises were not clean, and there
might be no one to take his place.
When Senator Leslie is considering his
Amendments to this Bill I think he
should lay down very clearly the type of
inspector who will be used. I can
imagine a case going before a local magistrate. I assume some one will have to
give notice to the local authority that he
considered his hair was not being
properly cut, or that the premises were
untidy. The inspector would then come
in, perhaps at a busy time of the day
when the local fair was on, and he would
perhaps see unsavoury material on the
floor brought in from the local fair, and
he would immediately cause a prosecution to be taken against the local barber.
In circumstances like that it might be
a most unfair thing to do, because no
barber can be responsible for the cleanliness of his customers. They follow
different occupations, and perhaps for the
remainder of the day that barber's shop
is in a dirty condition. With, perhaps,
only one assistant, the barber is unable
to brush the sweepings of hair from the
floor, and yet he has to be labelled as
being an unclean barber, and he may be
fined and have his registration cancelled.
All that will be given in evidence by
the inspector appointed by the local
authority, and perhaps the barber will
not be able to deny the facts stated.
All he can say is that this happened on
fair day; that it was very difficult to cope
with the number of people who came in,
and he may ask for leniency. It is
questionable whether the magistrate will
apply leniency in a case like that. It
puts him in a difficult position, as well
as the inspector.
Second Reading
Hairdressers Bill-

26 OCTOBER 1938
That is one of the most difficult things
about this Bill-that a person may come
into a barber's shop with some infection
of the hair, on which a brush is used.
That brush
be taken away and
may
analysed, and then the barber will be
pilloried simply because he has had an
unclean customer. In the case of a
surgeon, his patient is put in a bath and
washed and treated in the most sanitary
way before he is brought into the operating theatre, so that if there is any fault
it lies with the hospital authorities. But
if a man comes into a barber's shop from
the fair you cannot take him and wash
him, or ask him if he has any skin infection, and wash his hair with carbolic
soap. No barber who attempted to do
that would stay in the trade very long.
I think this Bill will throw an impossible
burden on the barbers. A barber may
wash his hair brushes every night, and
first thing in the morning a dirty person
comes in to have his hair cut. Later an
inspector visits the premises and sees
that the brushes are dirty. The onus
of proof will lie on the hairdresser, and
it will be very difficult for him to prove
that he took all reasonable and proper
steps to keep his brushes clean.
There is another provision which quite
rightly compels barbers who use expensive and dangerous instruments for
hairdressing to be skilled in their use.
I agree that the man who uses instruments without proper skill is putting his
customers in a dangerous position, and he
is open to serious penalties, but as this
Clause is drafted it will apply to anyone
using an instrument, even if it is only
a comb or scissors. I have had my hair
The
cut so badly that I was ready to put
the barber in prison, but it is a serious
thing to take away a man's business
simply because the inspector may think
he cannot cut hair to suit him.
inspector might be a man who has very
little hair on which the barber can
practise, and in those circumstances he
would have to rely on the way the barber
cut the hair of someone else. Is a man
who is a sanitary inspector the man to
decide whether a person is qualified to
cut hair or not, or would you use a man
who is accustomed to making up roads.
There is no inspector attached to the
local authority who is qualified to decide
these things.
How are you going to decide whether
brushes are clean without taking them
away and submitting them to chemical
analysis? They may have some material on
them which seems to be dirt but is merely
oil used on someone's hair, and it does
not necessarily follow that it is dirty.
As I understand it, the definition of dirt
is material which is in the wrong place,
and if you have hair oil on a brush or
comb which has just been used on a
person's head it cannot be said that it
is material in the wrong place. Neither
can you tell whether it will cause contamination until you put it under
chemical test. I agree that there are
cases in which a man coming into a shop
might say, "This is a dirty shop, and it
shall not be allowed to exist in this
town," but I am willing to assert that
the greater number of barber's shops in
this country would be border-line cases
under this Act. Therefore, I hope that
when Senator Leslie comes to consider
his Amendments he will be very careful
to eliminate as many border-line cases
as possible.
I do not propose to move the rejection
of the Bill out of hand, because I think
the House will agree that, like Mr.
Kipling's "Shipwrecked Mariner," the
hon. and learned Member is a man of
infinite resource and sagacity, and I have
every confidence that when he applies
his mind to the Amendment of this Bill
he will produce something which,
although it can never be an ornament
to the Statute Book, will at least bear
fewer of the blemishes that it contains
in its present form.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
support the Second Reading of this Bill,
and I think the public at large will
approve of it. We have just listened to
a very gloomy prediction of the effects of
this Measure, and the suggestion has
even been put forward that a man should
take off his boots to get his hair cut.
That is the first time I have ever heard
of that, and if it is done in the country
it certainly is not done in the cities or
towns. I am afraid the hairdressers the
hon. and gallant Member has been in
the habit of patronising have not been
very up-to-date. In Belfast they are
ceasing to use a comb and scissors to
cut hair, and instead they are employing the latest electrical appliances, as
they do in every other business. I do
think there is every possibility of this
Hairdressers Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
Bill making a considerable improvement,
especially as regards the unclean places
in back streets, and I think its effects
are only likely to be fully felt in Belfast,
Londonderry, and other large towns.
The hairdresser in a country district who
cuts only one man's hair a day or two
in a week will not suffer by the introduction of this Bill. He is probably a
man who has learned very little about
haircutting, because he is possibly an exsoldier who started by cutting the hair
of his comrades in the Army. In my
opinion the Bill will be of great benefit
to the hairdressers in the city. There
are places which require inspection, and
the knowledge that they may be visited.
by an inspector will cause barbers to keep
their premises and equipment in a clean
and proper condition.
I
I also think the Measure will benefit
the public at large. To my mind the.
most important part of the Bill is that
dealing with third party insurance.
have not yet had an opportunity of
noticing how women's hair is treated, or
what particular process they go through
to obtain that very nice wave, but I
understand there is an electric crown put
on their head. It is not very long since
a woman told me that her daughter had
gone through this operation, and as a
result she lost her speech and was in
hospital for some time. I do not think
that girl or her parents received any
compensation. The fact that under this
Bill hairdressers will be compelled to
insure against third party risk will be
a great protection to the public, because
the insurance companies will see to it
that every precaution and every safeguard is taken.
Sir JAMES WILTON: I rise with
considerable satisfaction to support this
Bill. For quite a number of years members of the hairdressing profession with
whom I have come in contact have been
very anxious to have a Bill such as this,
and during the past two or three years
my own barber was very anxious to
know why this Bill was not introduced.
I do not expect any of the difficulties to
arise that have been mentioned by the
hon. and gallant Senator opposite. I do
not see why either an inspector under
the Shop Hours Act or a sanitary inspector under the medical superintendent
officer of health would not be competent
to inspect barbers' shops. I do not think
there will be any insistence on luxurious
premises and equipment, but the only
thing that will be required will be
ordinary cleanliness, not only of the
person of the barber himself, but his
surroundings and his instruments.
The one difficulty I can conceive is,
what will be considered efficient instruction. Will barbers require to have
served a certain term of apprenticeship
with another barber? There might be
some difficulty with regard to that, and I
hope that Senator Leslie will insist on
the insertion of a Clause providing that
where there are repeated breaches of the
Act the registration of the premises will
be cancelled, similar to what is done
under the Ice-Cream Act. I welcome
this Bill, and I think it will do quite a
lot of good. I do not think it will
cause the hardship the hon. and gallant
Member opposite anticipates, because,
after all, breaches of the Act will be
dealt with by resident magistrates, who
have legal knowledge, and not by lay
magistrates.
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: I am afraid
I am in agreement to a large extent
with the remarks of Senator Perceval-
Maxwell. In my opinion this Bill is
not merely a steam hammer to crush a
nut; it is a whole mass of elaborate
inspecting machinery to kill one louse.
The whole point of the Bill is to protect the public. The public may be
injured in several ways. A man may
be injured through the carelessness of
the barber, and he may be injured by
someone with an infected head who
occupies the chair before him. I think
the real difficulty about this Bill is the
impossibility of inspection. You are
going to ask your inspector quite frequently to decide things which only a
skilled analyst with laboratory facilities.
can decide, and there will be a very
grave danger of injustice if any less
efficient system of inspection is adopted
than that adopted in certain other cases
where it is desirable to protect the public in a rough and ready way, and it
is not claimed that this Measure will do
more than that. Where it is not desired
to have very elaborate inspecting
machinery, quite simple steps are taken.
Insurance against third party risks is
made compulsory under this Bill, and
the rest is left to the insurance com-
Hairdressers Bill-
Second Reading
26 OCTOBER 1938
panies. I would suggest that the hon.
and learned Senator who has moved the
Second Reading should consider whether
a great deal of this Bill might not
disappear by placing reliance solely on
the insurance Clause, and on the
insurance companies, as is done in
various other cases, including civil aircraft. Where compulsory insurance is
in operation the court does seem inclined
to award very heavy damages. I am
genuinely anxious that you should not
place on the Statute Book something
that might defeat its own object, and
I think my suggestion to rely on the
insurance Clause might be a way out
of the difficulty.
Mr. LESLIE: It now falls to me to
defend the Bill, and I should first of all
like to thank Senator Joseph Cunningham and the Mayor of Derry for their
support of the Measure. Now I must
endeavour to deal with the criticisms
that have been passed. First of all I
think the hon. and gallant Senator
opposite did not attach sufficient weight
to the fact that local authorities might
be relied upon to exercise a certain
amount of discretion. That is to say,
we have no right to assume they will
pass foolish by-laws, and not only have
we that protection as regards the by-laws,
but those by-laws have to be submitted
to and approved by the Ministry of
Home Affairs.
Again, I do not know that quite
sufficient weight was given to
my
reference to those model by-laws which
were issued from the Ministry of Health
in London, and which are in the hands
of the Hairdresses' Association. They
are merely suggestions, but they lay a
good deal of stress on this matter of
cleanliness, and go into such detail as a
new shaving cup being provided for each
person. I am afraid I have no doubt
that, whatever the consequences to the
bristles of the hair brush may be, if
the hair brush has been used on a man
with a dirty head it is the duty of the
hairdresser to have it washed before
using it on another person.
As regards the question of using a
sledge hammer to crush a nut, we might
of course have dealt with this matter
by an extention of the Public Health
Acts, but that would have been
troublesome job to undertake, and it
a
would have run counter to the idea that
this Bill should be permissive. If you
start tinkering with the Public Health
Acts in regard to a particular trade or
business you have to consider the possibility of local authorities having their
own Acts of Parliament. I do not
know-perhaps the Lord Mayor could
tell us whether there is anything in the
Belfast Acts of Parliament which
enables some supervision to be exercised
in these matters, but what about the
borough of Bangor and so on? You can
scarcely work through all the local Acts
in operation throughout the area, and
I think we get over that sufficiently
by leaving this matter to the local
authorities.
.
Again, something was said about the
liabilities cast on a man, and I must
really remind the hon. and gallant
Senator that there is no liability without negligence, and the liability is
there whether or not there is an
insurance company to protect you.
You cannot establish a claim for
damages unless you can prove negligence or some other misfeasance akin
to it. The object of insurance is to
enable the small man to have at his
back the means of meeting a successful
action for damages.
As regards those border-line cases, I
hope this Bill will turn those cases over
the border on the right side. When
this Act is in operation and its effects
become disseminated throughout the
Province the doubtful man will put his
house in order and all will be well. I
agree there is not unlikely to be some
little difficulty about the inspector, but
again I think you must rely on the local
authorities. The Mayor of Derry suggested it might be the Medical Officer
of Health or the Shops Act Inspector,
and I think the authority can make
arrangements as regards that. As
regards the insurance companies carrying out this inspection, I think that if
the insurance companies are not satisfied
they will send their own men to look
at the premises, and they can refuse
to renew that man's insurance.
The Bill, I may perhaps again say, is
intended for the registration of the hairdressers premises; not for the registration of the customers who resort thereto,
National Health Insurance Bill 320
Expiring Laws Bill-
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
I think that on the whole the House
need feel no hesitation in giving the Bill
a Second Reading.

Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.

re-

should be given to the position to which.

minute there is something in the nature

King's Courts deliberately conferred by

feeling of uneasiness. I do not oppose
the Bill, and in any case it would be
ridiculous to object on Third Reading
to Bill which deals with sundry
matters on account of one particular
detail.

26 OCTOBER 1938
321 Architects Registration Act
Architects Registration Act, 1938.
The following Motion stood on the
Order Paper,
"That an humble address be presented to His Grace the Governor of
Northern Ireland, as follows:-
Your Grace :
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Commons of Northern Ireland in Parliament assembled,
do by this humble address pray that
it may please His Majesty to make
provision by Order in Council under
sub-section (1) of Section 6 of the
Architects (Registration) Act, 1938,
that the said Act shall extend to Northern Ireland, subject to such adaptations in its application to Northern
Ireland as may appear to His Majesty
in Council to be necessary.
Mr. ROBB: In presenting this Resolution which has come to us from another
place, I beg to move as follows:
"That this House do agree with the
House of Commons on the said
Address to His Grace, and do insert
the words 'Senators and' before the
word 'Commons' in the said Address."
The effect of this Amendment is of
course to identify the Senate with the
House of Commons in the Resolution.
The purpose of the Resolution is to provide for the application to Northern Ireland of an Imperial Act-the Architects
Registration Act, 1938. This Act was
passed recently by the Parliament of the
United Kingdom. As it stands, it does
not apply to Northern Ireland, but provision is made in Section 6 (1) of it
whereby it may be extended to Northern
Ireland by an Order in Council of His
Majesty, passed in pursuance of this
Resolution.
Hon. Senators will recollect that a
similar procedure was adopted in the
case of the Architects Registration Act,
1931, which is the basis of this particular
code of legislation. Under the 1931 Act
an Architects Registration Council for
the United Kingdom was set up, and
when the Act was applied to Northern
Ireland that merged the registration of
our architects with that of the architects
in the United Kingdom. This is a procedure which is in the public interest as
well as in the interests of the architectural profession.
Architects Registration Act 322
The procedure of applying Acts of this
kind to Northern Ireland by means of
Orders in Council is not a very common
one, but it was devised to meet cases of
this kind-that is to say, where we in
Northern Ireland desire in any matter
to take advantage of procedure and
machinery provided in Great Britain.
It might at first sight appear that we
should pass an Act in this Parliament
on the same lines as the Act passed at
Westminster. But that presents one
difficulty because this Parliament of Northern Ireland could not impose any
duties on, or give any functions to, the
Architects Registration Council in the
United Kingdom, over which we have no
jurisdiction.
As hon. Senators will, however, see
from the English Act-a copy of which
has been circulated-while this Act is
primarily applicable to Great Britain, it
has been drafted with a view to its convenient application to us in Northern
Ireland. Throughout the passage of the
Bill at Westminster our Government has
been closely in touch with the promoters
of the Bill and the authorities in Whitehall; and the Ulster Society of Architects, taking as it does a very deep
interest in the matter, has been in close
co-operation throughout. I may say
that the Bill was carried in the Imperial
Parliament by very large majorities
and recived the support not only of the
architectural profession but also of
organisations representing the building
trade.
The Act is a very short one. There
are six sections in it, but it is to all
intents and purposes a one-clause Act.
Its main provision is to protect the title.
of "architect." In this it extends the
provisions of the original 1931 Act which
protected only the title "registered
architect."
The object of that Act in doing so was
to protect the public from unwittingly
employing as architects persons not
adequately qualified to undertake architectural work. It is not perhaps
altogether surprising that this purpose
should have failed when it is borne in mind
that the fact that a profession or trade is
registered by virtue of a statutory provision by no means implies that some
responsible body is charged with seeing
that the persons SO registered are
properly qualified. For instance, money-
F
Architects Registration Act
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Mr. Robb]
lenders have to be registered but there
is no provision for investigation as to
qualification or standing.
On the other hand, in the case of
practically every other profession of the
skill of which the ordinary member of
the public stands in need, statutory protection is afforded to the commonly used
professional title. For example, no one
but a duly registered person may call
himself or herself a physician, surgeon,
a solicitor, dentist, a veterinary surgeon,
a chemist, or a midwife.
The relations of the public with the
architectural profession are of just as
close a nature as they are with many of
these professions and while it
be
may
true that in the past architects were
seldom employed except by persons of
wealth who might be expected to be well
informed on such matters as professional
qualifications, this certainly is not the
position now, when houses are being
built by almost every class of the
community.
The 1938 Act, that is the Act just passed
and which this Resolution would apply
to Northern Ireland, protects the title
of "architect" by providing that no person shall carry on business or practise
under that title unless he is a registered
person. I would, however, like to make
it clear that it does not in any way prevent any person from designing or supervising the erection of buildings, nor
would it in any way hinder any person
from recovering any fees he may have
earned in connection with such work,
even if he should not be a registered
architect, nor, I may add, would it in
any way affect the submission of plans
for houses to local authorities. It
merely secures that a person shall not
hold himself out to be an architect and
invite the public to employ him as such
unless he is properly qualified.
That is the main provision of the Bill.
The other provisions are directed to
securing that it will not operate harshly.
These provisions are of a simple but
effective character and I think I need
not occupy the time of the House with
them, but hon. Senators will observe that
in the Appeal Tribunal that has been
set up in that connection very generous
representation has been given to Northrn
Ireland.
As I think hon. Senators are aware,
our local architects are most anxious that
the Act should be applied to Northern
Ireland, and I venture to express the
hope that this House will pass the
Resolution.
It does not come into operation until
August, 1940, and I think that gives due
time for any person not registered to do
so. I do not think I need trouble the
House with the various provisions of the
Act. Our local architects
are most
anxious that the Act should be applied
to Northern Ireland, and I hope the
House will pass this Resolution.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Address,
and have inserted the words, "Senators
and" before the word "Commons" in
the said Address.
Adjournment.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That
this House do now adjourn until Tuesday next."
I may say that the present
intention is that we shall meet next week
on Tuesday and Thursday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Ten minutes past Four
o'clock until
next.

Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House): I
have to report that a Privy Councillor of
Northern Ireland had waited on His
Grace with the Address from the Senate
of October 26th, to which His Grace was
pleased to return the following answer:—
I have received your Address Praying that it may please His Majesty to
make an Order in Council under subsection (1) of section six of the
Architects (Registration) Act, 1938,
that the said Act shall extend to Northern Ireland.
I shall be pleased to forward the
Address to His Majesty's Secretary of
State for the Home Department, who
will bring the matter before His
Majesty in Council.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Queen's
University of Belfast Bill, the Appropriation Bill, and the Factories Bill, to which
they desire the concurrence of this House.
Queen's University of Belfast Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Ordered, That the Bill be read a Second
time on Thursday next.
