Education Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MINISTER OF EDUCATION
(Mr. Robb): I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now read a Second time." The
object of the Bill is to extend the age of
compulsory school attendance from 14 to
15 years.
I am one of those who
would have preferred that legislation in
this direction should have gone
on the lines of the English Education Bill introduced by Mr. Fisher
1918, whereby boys and girls
of this nation would have been given
part-time education up to the age of 18.
That Bill, however, did not find favour
with the majority of people, and was
never in fact put into force. Instead,
legislation has proceeded on different
lines, extending the compulsory school
age from 14 to 15.
Education Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Robb]
In this country compulsion in respect
of attendance at school began with the
Irish Education Act of 1892. That Act
made attendance at school compulsory
from the age of six to 14, but it contained
provisions for exemptions for children
over 11 who had obtained a certificate of
proficiency in the fourth class.
It was
confined to boroughs and towns having
town commissioners, and urban districts,
and it provided that when
that when county
councils were inaugurated in Ireland
those county councils might make provision for the adoption of the Act in
rural districts. By the year 1923 there
were 81 areas in which this Act was in
force. They included Dublin and
Belfast, 29 urban districts, and 50 rural
areas, but the provisions of the Act,
although ostensibly enforced in 81 areas
were really effective only in the two cities
of Dublin and Belfast. In 1923 the
average daily attendance of children at
school was only 75 per cent. of the average enrolled in the schools.
Following upon the recommendations
of the Lynn Committee, the Act of 1923
was passed in Northern Ireland. It provided that attendance at school should be
compulsory between the ages of six and
14, without exception. That applied to
all areas, and it had a most beneficial
effect. It raised the average daily
attendance to 86 per cent., a considerable
advance, due possibly to the greater
interest that was aroused in education
generally by the discussions that took
place when that Act of 1923 was passed,
and possibly also to the more strict
enforcement of the law by the education
authorities through their school attendance committees and officers. It is
however recognised that the
daily attendance of 86 per cent of the
total enrolment is not a satisfactory
peak, and that it is something which
can and ought to be improved upon.
average
Now we come to a further extension
of the school leaving age. This Bill provides that the ages
between which
attendance at school shall be compulsory
shall be six to 15, instead of six to 14.
The Bill is not without exemptions, with
which I shall deal in a moment. It
further provides that the obligation of
attendance can be satisfied either by
attendance at a day school, some school
recognised by the Ministry as giving
efficient education, or it can be satisfied
by attendance at technical classes
for 15 hours
classes
a week, those
being held in the day time and not later
than six o'clock in the evening. It further
provides that the obligation can be fulfilled by attendance partly at technical
classes and partly at classes held under
the county agricultural committees.
The exemptions from the obligation
imposed by the Bill are in three classes.
First of all, exemption can be given for
beneficial employment. Such exemption
is only obtainable on a certificate given
by the local education authority within
whose area the child to be exempted resides. Such certificate can only be given,
in the first place, on evidence that the
parent desires that the child shall be so
employed; secondly, that the education
committee, after consultation with the
local committee for juvenile employment,
if there is one in the district, and after
consideration of the health and physical
condition of the child, considers that the
employment would be beneficial to the
child; and, thirdly, that arrangements
shall be made for the child to attend some
appropriate school or institution for
further education, where facilities for
that purpose are available, until he attains the age of 15. Such attendance
must be not less than six hours in each
week during which the school or institution is in operation, and not later than.
six o'clock in the evening.
The issuing authority is by the Bill
required to take into consideration, in
determining whether a certificate shall or
shall not be given (1) the nature and
probable duration of the employment, the
wages to be paid, and the hours of work;
(2) the time available to the child for
recreation; and (3) the value in relation
to the future career of the child of any
training or other advantages afforded by
the employment. The latter condition is
one to which the Ministry attaches considerable importance. In addition to
exemptions in respect of beneficial employment, the Bill provides by Clause 7
that there may be an exemption granted
by a local education authority where they
are satisfied that by reason of the circumstances existing in the home of the child
who has attained the age of 14 the child
ought to be allowed to give assistance in
the home at domestic or agricultural work.
Education Bill-
Second Reading
1 NOVEMBER 1938
If they are so satisfied the authority
may, after consideration of the health
and physical condition of the child, grant
a certificate upon such conditions as they
think fit, permitting the parent to withdraw the child from school.
It is recognised that in our area we have many
small farmers to whom the assistance of
their children at that age may, especially
in particular seasons, be very useful.
There may also be homes where the assistance of a daughter of that age may be
not only helpful, but almost necessary,
and where those conditions are fulfilled
the education authority may grant a certificate of exemption. A certificate so
granted may be for only a limited period,
but is renewable.
local
There is a third class of exemption, and
that is where there is no school or educational facilities available for the child. It
might possibly be that a child of 14 lives
in a remote area where there is only a
very small school, and where the child
would be a very considerable distance
from any suitable centre of educational
instruction. In such cases the
education authority may grant a certificate of exemption, but in this case it
must be approved by the Ministry of
Education. The Bill further provides
that if a parent is dissatisfied with the
decision of the local education authority
which refused to grant a certificate of
exemption on any statutory ground, the
parent may appeal to the Ministry, and
in that event the decision of the Ministry
is final. These are the provisions of the
Bill. I do not think I need commend the
principle of the Measure to the House,
because I am sure it will meet with
general approval.
I may be asked, "What results do you
expect from this Bill?" Frankly, any
forecast of the results must be merely a
guess. The number of children attaining
the age of 14 every year in our area is
about 20,000. It is not possible without
experience to know how many of those
children will be exempted under the three
classes I have mentioned. Only experience can tell us what the results of
this Bill will be. In SO far
this Bill
an increased attendance at the elementary schools the
cost of that increased attendance will be
practically the increased salaries to
teachers. In that event the whole of the
cost will fall upon the Exchequer, as the
whole of the teachers are paid by the
causes
as
Exchequer. Should a large proportion
of the children who come under this Bill
and are not exempted, attend technical
classes, that may result in an increase in
the number of teachers required in
technical schools. The salaries of
technical teachers are paid, as to their
basic amount, by the education authorities, and the incremental portion is
borne entirely by the Exchequer.
Towards the basic salary the Exchequer
makes an attendance grant, and it is
probable, although it is not certain,
that the attendance grant in respect of
the attendance of these children at
technical classes will cover the larger
part of the basic salary. I should also
say that the Bill provides that no fees
are chargeable by the education authority
to a child attending these technical
classes under the provisions of the
Measure.
It has been suggested that one of the
results of this Bill may be that it will
be necessary to enlarge technical schools
either generally or in particular places.
Only experience can show whether that
will be necessary or not, but I might
say that in the technical schools in our
area there are roughly 10,000 places,
and the day attendance at these technical
schools is only 3,500. The difference
between those two figures ought, and
probably will, provide for the attendance
of children under this Bill. Whether
that will be so or not no one can say with
any degree of certainty, but that is the
probability as it appears to those who
have investigated the matter. These are
the provisions of the Bill, with the
principle of which I do not think any
person is likely to cavil. I hope also
that the details will not be found unacceptable.
Mr. LESLIE: I cannot claim to be
an enthusiastic supporter of the Bill
before us, but I do not propose to make
that customary Motion which is
tantamount, if not to rejection, at all
events to postponement. There are
four reasons why I do not propose to
take the course of asking for a postponement. The first of those reasons
is that this Bill comes to us from another
place where it has presumably been
examined carefully, both in principle and
in detail, and it has finally received
approval. The second reason is that we
are only some seven or eight months from
Education Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
a General Election, and prior to that
General Election something in the nature
of a manifesto was issued by or on behalf
of the Prime Minister, and in that manifesto reference to raising the school age
was distinctly mentioned. Therefore it
may fairly be claimed that, to that extent,
the proposal was before the public.
The third reason is to be found in
Clause 2 of the Bill, which seems to me
to contain some really very admirable and
well thought-out proposals which, on the
face of things, might reasonably be expected to serve the purpose for which they
were introduced. The fourth reason is
that I believe the Bill is capable of
amendment in one important matter, and
if an amendment on those lines is introduced and carried I believe we shall have
a very good Bill, indeed. As I have
stated, I do not propose to vote against
the Bill at the moment, but I must say
that if anybody else can bring forward
reasoned argument for the postponement
of the Bill I should have to listen to the
debate very carefully and consider very
carefully whether I should not support
that hon. Member.
The fact is that I cannot help thinking
that it would be easier to support this
Bill if it were, so to speak, in two parts.
That is to say, it would be easier to support the Bill if its effect was limited to
the urban districts, because that is one
of our root difficulties. We know that,
to some extent, the Bill before us follows
the Statute recently passed in the United
Kingdom, but conditions are not quite the
same. A Bill which, on the average, is
suited to Great Britain may very well be
passed, but it does not follow that the
average in Great Britain is the same as
that in Northern Ireland. The main
point of difference is that even yet the
larger portion of our industrial population
works in the rural districts. They are
blending more and more with the towns,
and it is a regrettable feature. The fact
remains that there are two distinct
problems in Northern Ireland very
clearly marked, the town and the
country.
I am sure we all have every sympathy
with a contention which was put forward, certainly in another place, although
I do not think the Minister mentioned it,
and that is the contention that the young
people of this Province should be as well
equipped for the battle of life as those
across the water. That appeals, I am
sure, to all of us, but it seems to me that
the question is whether an additional
year at school, even with the alternatives
offered by Clause 2 is necessarily the best
way of securing that equivalent education. Can we be sure that the additional
year of education is really going to be of
more value than a year's earlier entry
into the battle of life. That is the
problem.
The right hon. Gentleman himself seemed
to admit that the future was rather on the
doubtful side. If that be so, or indeed if
it be not so, what authority have we got
for a further interference with parental
duty and responsibility? The right hon.
Gentleman admits that the result is
rather a gamble. If so, is it not a gamble,
if one may use the expression, with other
people's property without their consent.
It is, to my mind, for the parents of the
children of Northern Irleand to say
whether or not they desire an additional
year's education for their children. It is
all well for the State to insist, as has
very
now been done for
that
years,
child
every
shall be taught to read and write, and to
allow it, perhaps, to remain at school up
to 14. We have done that. Since 1923
we can claim to have been fairly successful
in enforcing it, but what have we got to
show for it.
Our educational system is costing our
Exchequer between £2,000,000 and
£2,250,000 a year. What have we got to
show for it? Are we giving the taxpayers
value for their money? I am afraid there
are many taxpayers who hold most
distinctly that we are not. I must insist
on that question of interference with the
parental authority. What right have we
got to do that? Can we guarantee these
young people who have had their additional year's education that they
will get jobs when they have finished
it. Can we guarantee that their
future will be safer than it would have
been if they had been turned loose at 14
to follow what has been described as a
blind alley occupation. Is that not entirely on the lap of the gods, and, if so,
what right have we to interfere?
There are other criticisms that might
be made of the proposals. One of them
that occurs to me is something in the
nature of unfairness to what I might
call voluntary schools, and by voluntary
Education Bill-
Second Reading
1 NOVEMBER 1938
schools I mean not only those schools
which do not accept even the four-andtwo committees of which we hear so
much, but those of even lower standing
who do not accept those committees.
Those schools were, as a rule, started by
people acting from conscientious motives,
and are usually retained, with or without committees, for the
same conscientious reasons. Rightly or wrongly
they feel they must maintain those
schools, and now there is some risk of
injustice there if they are not large
enough for the increasing population. I
know that that injustice can be mitigated
to a very great extent by the powers
with which Parliament endowed the
Ministry under our Act of 1930, and I
am quite sure that that power will be
as generously exercised in the future as
it has been in the past. There is also
another point. Many of our country
schools, and some, I believe, of our town
schools, continue what is called a mixed
education of the sexes. That is going to
be much more difficult if you are going
to raise the age from 14 to 15.
I think I may leave that, and pass
for a moment to what seems to me to be
another objection. That is the further
burden which this Bill will cast on the
local authority. That burden will be, as
I see it, partly a burden of money and
partly a burden of time. I will take
money first. According to the memorandum affixed to the Bill it is unlikely
that the total extra cost to the local
education authorities will exceed £17,000
in one year. That, I think, is perhaps.
not a very heavy addition, although the
burden of local taxation is already, to
my mind, stretched even beyond the
breaking point.
The other question is that of the
technical schools, and I am bound to say
that the right hon. Gentleman seemed to
make out a very good case for the
technical school. Having regard to
the fact that there are 10,000
places with only 3,500 children to occupy
them, I do not think there is any serious
danger in that respect. As far as the
local authorities are concerned, the mere
question of money does not seem to me
to be of very great importance. But
there is also the burden on the time of
the local authorities, and the burden on
the time of those people who work with
the local authorities as the education
authority. There is also a burden on
the employers. We have got to think of
whether these new proposals will make it
any easier for the young person of 15 and
upwards to get employment than it is
at present.
There is another detail, and it relates
to what I have just said about the burden
on the time of the local authority. The
Bill offers exemptions, and it deals with
exemptions at some length, in some three
or four Clauses. It must be presumed,
therefore, that in the minds of the
authors of the Bill it is intended that
these exemptions shall be made effective.
Sir JAMES WILTON: We hope so.
Mr. LESLIE: The first question we
have to ask ourselves about that is, where
is the exemption to be obtained from?
According to the Bill it is to be obtained
from the education authority. What
do we
mean by the term "education
authority"? I have read it two or three
times, and occasionally the expression is
qualified with the words, "education
authority of the area." The right hon.
Gentleman introduced a further qualification. He spoke of the local education
authority, but as the Bill comes before
us I can only read the expression,
"education authority" in the terms in
which that expression is used in Section
(2) of the Act of 1923. That is to say,
an education authority in the case of a
county council means the Regional
Education Committees if there are any,
and in the case of a county of a city
means the education committee. As I
read the Bill it seems to me very doubtful whether that is the intention, but
for the moment it does not matter, because I understand I am correct in saying and you, my Lord, will be able
to correct me if I am wrong that the
County of Down has only one Regional
Education Committee.
If these applications for exemptions are
to be made to the entire county council,
how are the unhappy parents to go there
and make their case? We know from
experience in the country that it is hard.
enough to get parents to attend the local
school education committees, which in my
part of the world are held at different
points within the North Antrim
Regional education area. It is very
difficult to get people to come.
Has the
Education Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
unfortunate woman who wants her
daughter at home for domestic purpose
to leave her home-and in charge of
whom? Not the particular daughter, who
is presumably lawfully at school. Has
she to leave her home to go and hang
about where, and for how long, to put
her case for exemption? And is the
employer going to wait while all these.
steps are taken before he engages little
Willie? Surely it is unworkable. The
time involved in getting exemptions is
almost endless, and, as they say in the
country, the row in getting them would
not be ordinary.
Educational faddists will complain
bitterly if exemptions are granted too
freely, and the public who want exemptions will complain even more bitterly if
they do not get them. We have got to remember that the people who are likely
to be anxious to obtain exemptions are,
on the whole, likely to be parents of
those children who are least likely to
profit by the additional year's education.
We must be quite clear as to what we
mean by the education authority, and,
therefore, if the Bill gets a Second Reading, I propose to put something on the
Paper which will enable us to discuss
that question.
Again, there is this question of appeal
to the Ministry. We are told nothing.
of the procedure of appeal. Is there to
be a solemn session of the Ministry, or
is the decision to be taken behind closed
doors? If the Ministry is going to hear
the case and the parent is to attend, how
is the parent going to get from, say,
We
Ballycastle, Omagh, or Newry?
want to know a great deal more about
that, but it is really detail which might
be explained at a later stage. On the
whole, in spite of the desire that our
children should be equipped educationally
to the extent to which they are able to
profit by education, which is not a
uniform matter on the part of
children, in spite of that feeling,
in my opinion, the Ayes in favour
of the Bill have not really got
it. I am afraid that the case for extending the compulsory school age in Northern Ireland has not, in my judgment,
been made out.
our
Sir JAMES WILTON: I can quite
understand the Leader of the House being
said,
certain to be a
very reluctant to prophesy the results of
this Bill. In my opinion the results are
very much in the air, and I think they
will depend, to a very large extent, on
the manner in which exemptions are dealt
with. If the exemptions are not dealt
with very strictly and gone into in detail
it is quite possible the Bill will be rendered
practically null and void. As the
hon. and learned Senator has
there is almost
large number of applications for
exemptions which will require a great deal
of investigation, and the success or otherwise of the Bill will depend upon how
they are dealt with. I am afraid I cannot be as optimistic as was the Minister
of Education about the principle of this
Bill receiving the general approval the
Minister anticipates. However, I am in
favour of the principle, and I propose to
vote for the Bill.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
propose to support this Bill, and I do so
mainly on the ground that when children
between the ages of 14 and 15, and, perhaps, a little older, leave school, in the
urban districts at any rate, they are apt
to drift into blind alley occupations. This
House has read a Report by a Committee
that was formed by the Minister of Home
Affairs to consider the question of juvenile
crime, and I think that one of the results
of the findings of that Committee has led
to the conclusion that one of the disadvantages of these blind alley occupations that
children get into is that they are very
seldom full-day jobs. Very often it is
the job of running errands and during
the rest of the day that child is at a loose
end. Children who have spirit very often
get into trouble, without intending to do
so, because they have nothing else to do.
I believe that keeping them a little longer
at school is a partial solution of that
problem. It is a very difficult question
which has been causing great concern not
only to judges and magistrates, but also
to juvenile workers.
I do see the force of the argument put
forward by the hon. and learned Senator
regarding the country districts, and I
would ask the Government to consider
most carefully whether they can do something in reference to these appeals to the
local education committee, which, I presume, is the regional education committee.
I believe that one of the worst tendencies
of this age is the drift of the country to
Education Bill
Holiday Pay Bill
1 NOVEMBER 1938
the town, and I believe that over-education in book learning of many of our young
people has definitely added to this drift.
A boy between 14 and 16 is becoming
strong and able to take an active and intelligent part in farm work, and unless
he goes on to the farm at that age he will
not, as a rule, go on to the farm at all.
The farmers are suffering very severely
from lack of labour, which is due, to a
great extent, to the tendency of young
persons to drift to the towns where they
can get more amusement and more company.
I believe the only thing that keeps
young people on the farm is getting to
know and to like farm work at an early
age. I have had that experience with
boys in my own neighbourhood. If you
once get them to take an interest in the
farm, when they leave school they will
have cultivated a liking for the outdoor
life, but if you let them drift away into
the towns and into shops you will find
that they tend to stay there. What I am
a little bit anxious about is that these
exemptions may be made too difficult to
obtain. Where a boy is fond of animals
and country life, and is keen on farm
work, he should definitely be encouraged
to take up that work. I can see from the
Bill that it is the intention of the Government that he should be able to do
that, but what I am anxious about is that
the Bill seems to make it a little difficult
for his parents, particularly if they are
ignorant, to get the exemption. Subject
to that, I think this Bill is a good one,
and should be supported.
Mr. ROBB: I would like to reply to
only one point mentioned by Senator
Leslie. The only thing in his speech that
really troubled me was that he seemed to
foreshadow that there might be some injustice to voluntary schools under the provisions of this Bill. I think it can be
quite safely predicted that in the condition in which we find ourselves in Northern Ireland with regard to an increase in
the attendance at voluntary schools
affected by this Bill it will not be such
as to call upon the owners of voluntary
schools to incur any additional expenditure.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Arising out of
that point, if in any particular district
a voluntary school finds it is so crowded
that it will have to build additional
accommodation, I hope the Government
will give an assurance that they will bear
the cost of such additional accommodation.
Mr. ROBB: I am not prepared to give
any such undertaking, but I would point
out that one of the grounds for exemption is that there is not a suitable school
in the district for the children to attend.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time') agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
of the
Holidays With Pay Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: The purpose
Measure which I now have the honour of
presenting to this House will commend itself, I know, to all hon. Members. It is
to provide facilities for a substantial
section of the people to obtain the privilege of a paid holiday. To many workers
and, particularly, to those with young
families, whose needs make saving next to
impossible in a normal week, a holiday
without pay is accepted with reluctance,
and is a time of anxiety rather than of
recreation.
The Bill follows where possible the
Holidays with Pay Act, 1938, which has
been placed upon the Statute Book in
Great Britain to give effect to the Report
of a representative Committee under the
Chairmanship of Lord Amulree, which
completed a close examination of the
subject in April of this year.
The Act
relating to Great Britain empowers trade
boards, agricultural wages committees,
and the Road Haulage Central Wages
Board, to direct that any workers for
whom a minimum rate of wages is fixed
by these wage-regulating bodies shall be
entitled to holidays with pay for periods
not exceeding in the aggregate, one week
in any period of 12 months.
The Act also authorises the Minister of
Labour for Great Britain to assist in the
administration of voluntary schemes which
may be established in other industries, by
way of issuing sums as holiday payments
on behalf of employers, subject to recoupment of the sums issued and the expenses
incurred by the Department in connection.
with any such scheme.
Holiday Pay Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr Robb]
The Bill before the House differs from
the Act passed by the Parliament at
Westminster in that, there being no agricultural wages committees or similar
wage-regulating machinery for the road.
transport industry in Northern Ireland,
agricultural and road transport workers
are not included in the Bill.
Clause 1 of the Bill provides that a
trade board, after they have inquired into
conditions in the industry, shall have
power to direct that workers for whom
they have fixed or are fixing minimum
rates of wages shall be entitled
to be allowed up to one week's
holiday in
in the year. The holiday
must be additional, unless the Board
otherwise direct, to any other statutory
holidays to which the workers may be
It further provides
already entitled.
that an employer who does not comply
with the direction will become liable to a
fine not exceeding £20.
Clause 2 provides that the holidays.
granted under Clause 1 must be holidays
with pay and empowers trade boards to
lay down the times at which and the conditions according to which, holiday pay
shall be given. The directions as to time
and conditions may also provide that an
employer shall be liable to pay a worker
who has ceased to be employed by him
the amount of holiday pay which accrued
to him during the period of his employment.
Clause 3 makes the procedure under
the Trade Boards Act (Northern Ireland).
1923, applicable to directions made by
the trade boards under this Measure and
enables the Ministry of Labour to make
regulations necessary to the working of
the Measure. The regulations must be
laid before both Houses of Parliament.
Clause 4 enables the Ministry of
Labour, if so requested by the organisations representing workers and employers
in an industry, or branch of an industry,
to assist in administering a scheme,
agreed by these organisations, for securing holidays with pay for the workers
in it.
The Clause has been included mainly
for the reason that in industries where
the employment provided is of an intermittent kind-dockers and builders'
labourers are cases in point-and covers
many employers in the course of the
year, some system such as the stamping
of employees' cards may be necessary in
order that a holiday with pay may be
enjoyed by
by them. The Employment
Exchanges provide a machine capable of
working such a system.
I commend this Bill to you. Already
the movement to provide holidays with
pay has led to the concluding of
voluntary agreements in many branches
of industry, and this Bill will establish
means of assisting the spread of this
movement to the benefit, I hope, of many
thousands of families whose breadwinners are employed in one or other of
the eighteen industries covered by Trade
Boards.
I beg to move, "That this Bill be
now read a Second time."
I
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
would like to support the Second Reading of this Bill, because I recognise
probably better than anyone the great
benefit it will be to the workers.
think the hardship on the wage-earning
class has been recognised for a good
number of years, although nothing has
been done in the nature of legislation.
For many years in different centres in
England they have generally organised
clubs to relieve workers
workers at holiday
periods. I am glad to say that in Belfast one of the big firms, the Ropeworks,
recognise the hardship that falls upon
the workers. They organised clubs and
the workers contribute weekly towards a
fund which helps them in the holiday
period. As the Leader of the House has
said, it is no benefit or pleasure for a
working man to be allowed a week or
ten days' holiday if he has no means
of enjoying the holiday. Furthermore.
it is a great hardship when he goes back
after the holiday to have to pull up the
arrears.
a
I was just thinking on reading the Bill
of the tremendous improvement in the
lot of the wage-earning class during the
past number of years. I can look back
some forty years when I became
journeyman engineer. In those days, I
suppose, it was the old story that a
prophet had no honour in his Own
country, for generally you had to leave
the firm in which you served your
apprenticeship and go somewhere else to
get a journeyman's wages. It fell to me
to travel from Belfast to the Clyde, where
I got the magnificent sum of 33s 10d as
Second Reading
Holiday Pay Bill-

1 NOVEMBER 1938
a journeyman. It is a remarkable thing,
but at that time a man had no State
benefit if he fell out of employment,
only some 10s a week for fourteen weeks
from his society to help him to exist.
A man is now entitled to benefit for a
long time, especially if he has a family.
Then, further, the hours of labour have
been reduced. It was a subject of
agitation at that time and for a number
of years by the trade union people who
advocated an eight-hour day. I was one
of those agitators myself, because I did.
not like rising at five o'clock in the
morning to start work. That has been
granted.
this is
what
a
I think
considerable
concession to the working classes. I
know
do not
may come, or
what we may expect in the near future.
There is only one other scheme I would
like to see put into operation and it would
be of great benefit. I have mentioned it
before. I think something should be
done to assist the man with a big family.
An effort in that direction is being made
on the other side of the Channel. In
fact, some firms have already recognised
the duty of making a contribution to a
man with a large family. I do not see
anything impossible in it when we have
insurance schemes. You can pay pensions at 65 out of insurance schemes and
also widows' pensions. I see no difficulty
whatever in giving a man with three or
more of a family something extra for
each child.
Mr. LESLIE: What about the wife?
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: What
was that?
Mr. LESLIE: I was thinking of the
wife.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: You
can hardly do without a wife if you are
going to have a family. As a matter of
fact, a married man has to make provision to keep a wife and two or three
children. It is a hardship for a man with
more than three, four or five of a family.
The man is in difficulties to provide for
them. I think it is the only solution
there is, to give to the man with a larger
family than his neighbour something
extra out of the health insurance scheme
or some other scheme. We are complaining at the present time about the reduced
No
birth rate and reduced population.
doubt it is serious, and I hope some wise
person will give the matter careful consideration and see if something cannot be
done in that direction. I do want to
admit that the position of the wage-earner
has considerably improved during the last
20 years.
It is much more comfortable.
He has not the nightmare he had about
falling out of employment. I remember
Mr. George N. Barnes, in the Imperial
Parliament, claiming that the workers
should be provided with work or wages.
You have almost given that to them. It
was looked upon as ridiculous at that
time. Now a man is almost provided with
I have great
a wage if out of work.
pleasure in supporting the Second Reading.
Mr. LESLIE : I am not going to
oppose the Bill, because I have great
sympathy with it. I think everybody
has sympathy with it. But I cannot
refrain from expressing my opinion that

voluntary agreement would be a
much better way of dealing with a
matter of this kind. It seems to me that
the Bill endeavours to provide, with the
sanction of my lords of the trade
boards, that the employer shall give
holidays. But the Bill fails to tell us
how long the worker has to work before
he is entitled to a holiday. He is to
have not less than a week in twelve
months. And if he comes to work for
you for a week, as people do in the
country, when you are unfortunate
enough to have to go to the Labour
Exchange, are they entitled to a share
of the holiday. Again, although the
Bill provides that employers must give
the workmen a holiday there does not
seem to be any provision that the workman shall take the holiday. There is
such a thing as a busman's holiday.
What is to prevent a man who gets a
week from employer A starting off and
going to work for employer B?
Then again, what about that man's
wife? If the man is to make proper use
of the holiday it is obvious he must go
away to the seaside.
Is he going to take
his wife with him or how is she going to
get a holiday? The whole thing is difficult and very much better left to voluntary agreement. I should have preferred
if this Parliament had waited to pass this
until they had seen the English Regulations in type. I do not know whether

Hairdressers Bill.

In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Premises to be registered
with the Local Authority).
or
In the district of any local authority
in which this Act is in force any
premises in which the trade or business
of barber or hairdresser is carried on
shall be registered by the owner
occupier thereof with the local
authority in such manner as the
Minister of Home Affairs may by
regulations prescribe, and any person
using or causing to be used for any
such purpose
as aforesaid any
premises not duly registered for that.
purpose shall be guilty of an offence
under this Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out "owner or." It has often been
found in dealing with matters of this
kind that the use of the expression
"owner" gives rise to difficulties. You
have the case of the freeholder, first
leaseholder and any number of sublessees. The person we want to get at
here is the man actually occupying the
premises for the purpose of the particular
business of hairdresser or barber. Therefore I propose, with the approval of the
Committee, to cast the responsibility of
registering directly on the occupier. In
order to do that I must get rid of the two
words owner or."
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out Minister and insert Ministry."

""
This is simply to correct a lapsus calami,
which occurred at some stage.
If you

look at Clause 5 you I will see the expression used is Ministry not "Minister."
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Local authority may
make by-laws).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move at the
end of Clause 2 to add:-"(3) Any officer
authorised by the local authority shall
for the purposes of this Act have power
to enter and inspect at reasonable
any
time, and on production of his authority,
any premises which such officer has reason
to believe are used for the purpose of
carrying on the trade or business of
barber or hairdresser."
This is part of the necessary equipment
of an inspector with authority to inspect.
Without a sub-clause of this sort and the
sub-clause I am suggesting should follow,
the Bill would be really useless and unworkable. The words on the Paper follow
the accustomed form.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move at the end
of the Clause to add-"(4) Any person
who obstructs or impedes any such officer
in the exercise of his powers under this
Section shall be guilty of an offence under
this Act."
This, of course, is merely complementary to the last. It is to protect the inspector in the exercise of his duties.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Penalty for breach of
by-laws).
Any person guilty of an offence under
this Act shall be liable on conviction
to a fine not exceeding five pounds,
and to a fine not exceeding two pounds
for each day on which any such offence
is continued by the said person after
conviction.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move at the
end to add:
"(2) The court by which any person
is convicted of an offence under this
Hairdressers Bill
Universities Bill
1 NOVEMBER 1938
Act or against any by-law made thereunder may, in addition to imposing a
penalty, by order cancel the registration of the premises in relation to
which the offence was committed; and
as from the date of the order the
premises therein referred to shall be
deemed to be premises which are not
duly registered under this Act and the
local authority may amend their
register accordingly."
If a man is
This again is necessary.
convicted of an offence, and a pretty bad
offence, it is obviously necessary that the
court which tries him should have power
to cancel the registration. There is
nothing to prevent the man whose registration is cancelled from amending his
ways and starting a fresh registration.
As long as he conducts himself properly
he is not unduly interfered with. I do
not think there is anything unduly penal
in it. After all, it can only be put in
operation by the local authority.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 4 (Prosecution of offences),
5 (Power of Ministry of Home Affairs
to extend Act), and 6 (Expenses of local
authority) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 7 (Definition).
In this Act the expression "local
authority" means the council of any
county borough, borough, urban
rural district in which this Act is in
force.
or
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move at the
end to add:
"(2) References in this Act to enactments of the Parliament of the
United Kingdom shall be construed as
references to those enactments as they
apply in Northern Ireland."
The words proposed to be inserted are,
as the Committee knows, purely formal.
They are almost always inserted, and in
this case, where the Bill contains references to Section 219 of the Public Health
Act, it was obviously impossible to omit
them.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Short title) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.

Mr. LESLIE: Can we have any idea

Mr. ROBB: The Committee stage of
the Education Bill and these two Bills,
the Second Readings of the Queen's
University of Belfast Bill, the Appropriation Bill, the Air Raid Precautions
Bill. The Factories Bill and the Marketing of Potatoes Bill I do not think we
will take on Thursday.
Mr. LESLIE: It is rather hurrying
the Committee on the Education Bill.
Mr. ROBB: We will take it on Tuesday. We might put all these other Bills
on the Paper for Second Reading on
Thursday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Half-past Four o'clock
until Thursday next.

3 NOVEMBER 1938
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 3rd November, 1938.

Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Marks' Divorce Bill.

the Third time."
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill, as

without amendment.

Unemployment Insurance (Additional
Benefits) Order (Northern
Ireland), 1938.
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House): I
beg to move "That the Order in Council
made by the Governor of Northern
Ireland on the 30th March, 1938, under
Section 65 of the Unemployment Insurance Act (Northern Ireland), 1936, for
increasing the maximum number of
additional days of benefit, other than
agricultural benefit, payable in a benefit.
year, and for augmenting the increase in
the weekly rate of benefit other than
agricultural benefit, payable in respect
of adult dependents, be hereby
approved.'
Under Section 65 of the Unemployment Insurance Act (Northern Ireland),
1936, His Grace the Governor may apply
to Northern Ireland, by Order in Council,
the provisions of any Order made by the
Minister of Labour for Great Britain on
the recommendation of the Unemployment Insurance Statutory Committee to
amend the Unemployment Insurance Acts
in force there.
In their report on the financial condition of the Unemployment Fund in
Great Britain at the 31st December, 1937,
the Statutory Committee estimated that
there was a surplus on the Fund available
for disposal, and they recommended that
this should be utilised to make two
important changes in the General Scheme
of Unemployment Insurance. These
changes were, firstly, an increase in the
maximum number of days of benefit payable in a benefit year to applicants with
a good industrial history, and, secondly,
an increase from 9s to 10s per week in
the rate of benefit payable in respect of
an adult dependent.
With the approval of Parliament an
Order, giving effect to these recommendations was made by the Minister of Labour
in Great Britain on the 25th March,
1938, and the provisions of that Order
were made applicable to Northern Ireland
by the Order in Council, which is the
subject of the Motion now before the
House.
In accordance with the provisions of
the Unemployment Insurance Act
(Northern Ireland), 1936, the Order
would cease to have effect on the expiration of twelve months from the 30th
March, 1938, the date on which it came
into operation, unless approved by Parliament in the meantime.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Unemployment Insurance (Additional
Benefits and Reduction in Contributions) (Agriculture) Order
(Northern Ireland), 1938.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move :
"That the Order in Council made by
the Governor of Northern Ireland on the
30th March, 1938, under Section 65 of
the Unemployment Insurance Act
(Northern Ireland), 1936, for
(a) modifying the conditions of requalification for agricultural benefit
following exhaustion of benefit
rights;
(b) reducing the number of days at the
commencement of a continuous
period of unemployment after
which agricultural benefit becomes
payable;
Unemployment Insurance
Question to Minister
THE SENATE
(c) reducing the weekly rates of contributions payable in respect of
employment in agriculture by
persons over the age of 18 years
and their employers; and
(d) increasing the weekly rate of agricultural benefit payable to young
men between the ages of 18 and 21
years,
be hereby approved."
In reporting on the state of the agricultural account of the Unemployment
Fund in Great Britain at the 31st December, 1937, the Unemployment Insurance
Statutory Committee considered that the
Agricultural Unemployment Insurance
Scheme had not yet been in operation
sufficiently long to enable precise conclusions to be drawn. The Committee
were, however, definitely of opinion that
the resources of the scheme were surplus
to its requirements, and they recommended that this surplus should be disposed of by making the following improvements in the scheme :-
(a) The abolition-except where the
full statutory maximum of 300
days agricultural benefit in a
benefit year has been drawn-of the
condition requiring a claimant who
has exhausted his agricultural
benefit rights in a benefit year to
show the payment of ten further
agricultural contributions subsequent to the exhaustion of benefit
as a requalification for benefit in
a future benefit year.
(b) The reduction from six to three
days of the "waiting period" during which agricultural benefit is
not payable at the beginning of a
"continuous period of unemployment."
(c) A reduction of a halfpenny per
week in the rate of agricultural
insurance contributions payable
respectively by workers over the
age of eighteen years and their
employers.
(d) An increase from 10s 6d to 12s per
week in the agricultural benefit
rate payable to young men
between eighteen and twenty-one
years.
An Order giving effect to these changes
was made by the Minister of Labour in
Great Britain on the 25th March, 1938,
with Parliamentary approval, and its
provisions were applied to Northern
Ireland by the Unemployment Insurance
(Additional Benefits and Reduction in
Contributions) (Agriculture) Order
(Northern Ireland), 1938, for which the
approval of the House is sought. Failing
Parliamentary approval, this Order would
cease to have effect on the expiration of
twelve months from the 30th March,
1938, the date on which it came into
operation.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Question to Minister.
JUNIOR AND SENIOR
CERTIFICATE EXAMINATIONS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Joseph
Cunningham:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
in future arrangements can be made to
have the results of the junior and senior
examinations made public with less
delay, in order to give the unsuccessful
pupils an earlier opportunity of making
arrangements for their future scholastic
career.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I put
down this question in order to draw the
attention of the Minister of Education to
the very serious delay that takes place
in making known the results of the junior
and senior certificate examinations.
From information I have received, I
understand that many parents are put
to considerable inconvenience and a good
deal of anxiety owing to this delay. I
know of one particular case that occurred
this
year where a young girl went through
the examination, and she did not receive
the result of the King's Scholarship
examination until well on in August.
The girl was not successful, and her
father was anxious to let her take the
domestic economy course at the Belfast
College of Technology, but on the date
he received the result of the examination
he found that the classes in the College
of Technology were full, and he was badly
handicapped in making arrangements for
the girl's tuition. In many cases parents
make considerable sacrifices to give their
children as good an education as they
possibly can, and it is not reasonable to
impose any additional inconvenience on
them.
Question to Minister-

Certificate Examinations
allowed each examiner in which to complete the marking and return the marks
to the Ministry. This time varied from
about two to four weeks, according to
the number of exercises to be marked.
Subsequently the marks were checked and
tabulated in the office of the Ministry
and were then printed.
3 NOVEMBER 1938
Mr. MCALLISTER: I have had this
question under consideration for quite
a considerable time, and I support the
plea of the hon. Senator who has just
spoken. I do, however, appreciate the
difficulties that stand in the way of the
results of the examinations being published earlier, because I recognise that it
is not practicable for two or more inspectors to mark the papers in the same
subject, as the standard of marking might
vary. But I should like to point out
another aspect of this question. It does
not alone affect the unsuccessful pupils,
but all those who enter for the
examinations.
The examinations are held about the
middle of June, and the results are not
known until a few days before the schools
open again at the beginning of September. The intervening period is not in
any sense a holiday for those young
children who are so anxiously awaiting
the result of the examinations throughout the holiday period. However, as I
have said, I do realise the difficulties of
the Ministry, because it is desirable that
one examiner should mark all the papers
in his subject, but I would ask the Minister to consider the matter with a view to
seeing whether some compromise can be
arrived at. In the best years of their
life children should be free from
the anxieties of waiting throughout
their holidays for the result of their
examination.
Mr. ROBB: The secondary certificate
examinations are held from about the
middle of June to the end of that month.
This time has been selected to meet the
wishes of the principals of secondary
schools that the examinations should be
held close to the end of the school session.
From the first every effort is made by
the Ministry to perform as rapidly as
possible the work leading to the issue of
the results. The work involved is, however considerable. In the present year
there were over 1,300 candidates in the
senior and over 2,900 in the junior
examination, and more than 16,000 senior
exercises and 32,000 junior exercises had
to be marked by examiners. The
exercises were collected by the Ministry
and sent to the examiners within three
days after the examinations in the
various subjects, and a definite time was
The greatest care is necessary in every
stage in dealing with the papers and the
results, and I am clear that it is not wise
to attempt to hurry the examiners more
than at present. The hon. Senator may
be assured that this matter is always
before the Ministry.
May I add, in reply to Senator
McAllister, that it is not possible to have
all the papers in one subject marked by
one examiner. We have more than one
examiner, but we ensure that there shall
be a common standard of marking. There
is a chief examiner in each subject, and
in some subjects, though not in all, there
are assistant examiners. The chief
examiner consults with the assistants
after a few papers are marked, and a
common standard of marking is established. That takes a little time.
There is also the difficulty of being
absolutely accurate in the setting out of
the marks of each candidate in each subject, and in the checking of those marks.
That takes quite a considerable time. It
has been suggested that that latter part
of the work might be got through more
quickly by employing a number of temporary clerks, but I may say that it has
not been found advantageous to have this
very minute examination of marks done
by temporary clerks without the supervision and assistance of the permanent
officials who are accustomed to the work.
Since this question was put down, I
myself personally examined from start
to finish the whole procedure, from the
examination of the papers to the publication of the results, and as the answer
says I am not satisfied that any effort
to hurry or any change of procedure
would be satisfactory.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
recognise the great difficulty if you
want to be strictly fair to competitors,
but surely if it is on account of the
great number of students if there were
more examiners appointed the results
could be made known a little earlier.
Question to Minister-
Certificate Examinations

Something should be done.
There is
no doubt matters cannot be allowed to

be done to expedite the publication of
results.

Queen's University of Belfast Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The
Con-
Mr. ROBB: This is a short Measure
introduced at the request of the authorities of Queen's University to change the
constitution of the Senate of the University. Under the Universities Act of
1908 as Amended in 1928, the constitution was there settled. It can only be
changed with the approval of the
Senate, the Academic Council and
Convocation of the University.
Senate, Academic Council and
vocation have requested the Government
to alter it in two respects. The first is
to make the honorary treasurer an exofficio member of the Senate and to make
the secretary of the Academic Council
if he is a member of that Council an
ex-officio member of the Senate. They
find it will be of advantage in the working of the University. Accordingly this
Bill provides for that. I beg to move.
Factories Bill-

On Question, Motion agreed to.

Appropriation Bill.

Mr. LONG: This is a purely financial

Factories Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: By this Bill, which is
based largely upon the Measure of the
same name passed at Westminster in
1937, it is intended to consolidate and
amend the considerable body of legislation which since the passing of the last
consolidating Act of 1901, and together
with that important Act, forms the
present tangled factory code. The
function of the Bill in clearing away
the difficulties which beset the administrator and interpreter of factory law
cannot obscure the importance of the
new requirements which it will make
respecting the well-being of the factory
worker.
I will enumerate some of these new
requirements. For the first time standards of cleanliness and of lighting are
laid down and a clause introduced
requiring the medical supervision of
workers in certain circumstances, all
highly desirable requirements from the
point of view of the prevention of accident and disease and the maintenance of
a high standard of efficiency.
For the first time it is required that
the amenities of available drinking
water, washing facilities and seating
accommodation must be provided for
every factory worker. Hitherto these
amenities have been compulsory only in
a few special trades. The employment
of young persons for the moving of
excessive weights is forbidden; normal
hours are established for all female and
juvenile factory workers and the hours
of young persons in certain unregulated
employments are controlled.
Another novelty, if I may so describe
it, in the Bill is that it seeks to place
the onus on the vendor of new
machinery for the provision of certain
specified safety devices.
I shall not be expected I hope to go
through the 162 clauses of the Bill, but
an explanation of some of the new provisions, particularly of those which may
increase the costs of production are I
think necessary.
I should like to say at this point that
the Ministry of Labour consulted in
advance representatives of all the
interests concerned and secured a very
satisfactory measure of agreement and
support for the new proposals made.
In Clause 2 are
provisions for
increasing the existing legal minimum
of room space for each worker from 250
cubic feet to 400 cubic feet. This
increase will not involve the very considerable cost that might appear
necessary as
factories where the space allowed is so
little as 250 cubic feet and a temporary
saving Clause is included in order that
removal, or discharge, of workpeople
may be avoided where for want of space
it is impossible to meet this requirement.
in fact there are few
Another Clause which breaks new
ground is Clause 12, which empowers
the Ministry in certain circumstances
to require that arrangements shall be
made for the medical supervision of the
workers.
The intention is that where a new
process is introduced or is carried on
which is, or may be, or is suspected of
being, injurious to the health of the
workpeople engaged in it, the Ministry
will be able to take immediate steps to
see that the workers are properly
protected.
Before I leave the subject of medical
welfare as dealt with in the Bill I should
mention the prohibition of the employment of women within four weeks after
confinement, and the provisions for maintaining and improving the present system
under which young persons under 16 years
of age must, on being taken into employment, be certified as fit for employment.
Another part of the Bill deals with
hours of employment. Limitation. of
hours is only applied to women
and
young persons but it has been found in
Factories Bill
Hairdressers Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr Robb]
are
a
practice that restrictions applied to the
working hours of these workers restrict
also the working hours of men in the
same factory. The main features of the
new scheme
normal maximum
weekly limit of 48 hours, reduced to 44
working hours for young persons under
16 but subject to certain possibilities of
exemption; a daily maximum limit of
9 working hours, and a maximum period
of employment (inclusive of meal and
rest times) of 11 hours to be fixed by
the employer between 7 a.m. and 8
p.m. (6 p.m. for young people under
the age of 16). On Saturday the limits
are 7 a.m. till 1 p.m.

In
Provision is made, subject to conditions, to enable overtime to be worked
up to 100 hours in a calendar year.
the
of women, but not young
persons, the Ministry can increase this
time to 150 hours a year in factories
liable to seasonal or other special
pressure of work. No overtime will be
permissible for young people under 16
years of age. Provision is made for
the first time to regulate the ordinary
hours of work of messengers and van
boys employed in outside work for
factories or
in or about docks and
warehouses.
The reductions which the Act makes
in working hours are already applied
very generally in industry and although
to give them legal sanction will impose
monetary loss on some firms I feel that
they are reasonable. Indeed I believe
the reaction of most people to this Bill
will be that it marks a welcome and
reasonable advance in factory legislation.
There are numerous other provisions
in the Bill to cover, for the first time,
works of engineering construction and
building operations even where there is
no machinery in use. These are the
main lines on which the Bill proceeds.
It is a very long Bill and is practically
the same as that in England. I beg to
move.
Question proposed, "That the Bill be
now read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: I suppose we may take
it that the ordinary farm and farm buildings used in connection with the farm
are not included in the somewhat expansive definition of a factory?
Mr. ROBB: Farm buildings are not
included, but a scutch mill is.
