Marketing of Potatoes Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: I beg to move "That the
Bill be now read a Second time." The
purpose of this Bill is to apply legal
sanction to potato marketing methods and
restrictions which have already been
applied voluntarily with a considerable
degree of success. The Bill has the warm
support of the Potato Merchants' Association and of the Ulster Farmers' Union.
The Agricultural Marketing Acts passed
by this House in 1928 and 1934, which
have resulted in the application of prescribed standards of quality and proper
packing of potatoes exported
exported from
Northern Ireland to Great Britain has
enabled Northern Ireland potatoes to
secure a privileged position on the British
market, where their reliability has enabled
them to be sold when local British supplies were unsaleable owing to glut. Not
until 1934, when the Potato Marketing
Board was set up in Great Britain, however, was it possible to attack the grave
problem of the trade that of undercutting prices. As soon as possible after
the Potato Marketing Board was set up
the North of Ireland Potato Marketing
361 Marketing of Potatoes Bill- 3 NOVEMBER 1938

Second Reading
Association and the Ministry of Agriculture entered into negotiations with them
to ascertain what steps could be taken to
stabilise prices by orderly marketing.
The outcome was an arrangement
whereby the Executive Committee of the
North of Ireland Association, composed
of merchants, two farmers' representatives and a representative of the Ministry, should issue each week to all Northern Ireland licensed shippers, during the
potato shipping season, a list of prices
to which they were expected to keep during the week following. It was also
agreed, in order, as far as possible to
prevent glut, that in a year of surplus in
Great Britain, exports from Northern
Ireland should be limited to 200,000
tons; the restriction to be immediately
and completely removed if any foreign
potatoes were imported into Great
Britain. These agreements were, of
course, entirely voluntary, as the pricefixing body had no statutory authority.
Nevertheless the arrangement had valuable results for the trade, and in the light
of the experience gained all the interests
concerned agree that it is most desirable
that the voluntary system should be replaced by one based on a Statute of
Parliament.
I now come to the Bill. Clause 1,
accordingly, provides for the framing of
a scheme by the Ministry of Agriculture.
The scheme to be drawn up in consultation with representatives of licensed
merchants and of producers. Clause 2
makes provision for the appointment
under the scheme of a Potato Marketing
Committee, composed of representatives
of licensed merchants, of producers, and
of persons appointed by the Minister of
Agriculture. The scheme is to state the
proportion in which each class should be
represented, but the Ministry is to have
power to increase the representation of
producers if, at any time, a plan for fixing the prices to be paid to growers, as
distinct from shippers, were
to be
instituted. Clause 2, sub-clause (6) empowers the Ministry to examine the
accounts of licence holders. Clause 3,
sub-clause (1) provides for the financing
of the scheme. This will be effected by
the payment of contributions by licenca
holders of such amounts as may be
necessary to cover the expenses of the
scheme. The contributions would be on
the basis of tonnage shipped, and would
not exceed 2d per ton. These contributions will cover the whole cost of the
scheme, and no charge will devolve upon
the Exchequer. The fund into which
the contributions would be paid would be
administered by the Potato Marketing
Committee.
Sub-clause (2) of Clause 3 is permissive, and enables provision to be made
under the scheme for setting up an agency
to receive and disburse moneys payable
in respect of consignments shipped to
Great Britain, and for requiring such
consignments to be invoiced through the
agency. Such an arrangement would be
an additional insurance against price cutting and might develop a system of
centralised selling. The sub-section
would also enable the Potato Marketing Committee to advise and assist the
Ministry in regard to the production and
marketing of potatoes, and as to the encouragement of research and education in
relation to these.
Clause 4 would enable the Ministry to
regulate the export of potatoes into Great
Britain. This power is necessary in order
that the Ministry may support the committee in the operation of the scheme.
The requirement under Clause 5, that
application for a licence must be made
on or before 30th September in each year,
is made to put an end to the practice
among persons with whom potato marketing it is a sideline, of waiting until the
shipping season is well under way and
then deciding to take, or not to take out
a licence according to the profit of the
trade. Such persons only encroach
on the trade when prices are good, while
the regular shipper must carry on his
trade whether prices are good or bad.
Clause 6 would enable the Ministry, after
consultation with the Potato Marketing
Committee, to refuse to grant new
licences if satisfied that the existing
merchants are sufficient to carry on the
trade. This arrangement could apply
either to the whole or to a part of Northern Ireland.
There is no intention of withdrawing
licences from existing merchants, and it
is provided that any person who held a
licence on 1st July, 1938, will not be
prejudiced by the arrangement suggested.
The reason for the provision is that exporters, with whom potato merchanting
is only a small part of their business,
enter the trade with little knowledge of
Air Raid Precautions---
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr Robb]
it, so that their activities are a source
of weakness to the trade generally.
Furthermore, an increase in the number
of merchants renders effective control
more difficult. Clause 7 of the Bill is
a secrecy clause. These are the main
provisions of the Bill.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would like to know
what effect this Bill is going to have
upon the farmers of those districts which
are scheduled and prohibited from sending their potatoes outside those areas.
There is one district in County Down
and another in County Derry. I do not
know the size of the district in County
Down, but I know well the size of the
district in County Derry. It is a very
large district, which probably produces
the best potatoes in Northern Ireland.
It runs from Minnimore to Tubbermore
and from Draperstown to Toomebridge.
Ten or twelve years ago black scab was
found in two different parts of this
district, and it was scheduled. From
that time until now no more black scab
has been found in the district, but the ban
still remains, and farmers are prevented
from sending their potatoes into the city
of Belfast. I was wondering how these
farmers will fare under this Bill. Will
it benefit them in any way? Will they
be able now to find a market outside their
district for their potatoes? If not, I
would like the Minister to take particular
notice of this and have the ban removed
from the two districts as soon as possible.
Mr. ROBB: This Bill does not affect
them in any way.
On Question, Motion agreed to..
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Air-Raid Precautions Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: The problem with which
this Bill deals is by no means a new one,
for during the Great War, as we know,
many parts of Great Britain were visited
by hostile aircraft, but it has been
calculated that in all those four years only
300 tons of bombs were dropped, whereas
a low estimate of the possibilities to-day
shows that a far greater tonnage of
bombs could be dropped within a space
of 24 hours. First, I think, we must ask
ourselves what types of danger may result
from attacks from the air, and then what
steps can we take either to repel the
attackers or to minimise the effects of
their attacks.
Now the types of bomb which might
be dropped are high explosive, incendiary
and gas.
It will, I think, be admitted
that it would be quite impossible to effect
any safeguard against a direct hit by a
high explosive, but what we can do is
to provide protection, not, as I have said,
against a direct hit, but against blast
and splinters, and it was for this purpose
that trenches were dug in so many public
parks in Great Britain during the recent
crisis. Trenches, if properly constructed
and sandbagged, can effect a very real
saving of life from this danger.
The incendiary bomb also presents
great difficulty, because they are so small
that a great number of them can be
carried by one machine, and a medium
sized bomber could start no fewer than
150 separate fires in this way. It is
essential that we should guard, therefore,
against attack of this sort, for although
each of the fires started by a thermite
bomb may not result in damage, yet a
percentage is bound to start large conflagrations. It is to deal with this situation that the fire brigade system in Great
Britain is being, for purposes of
emergency, augmented by the addition of
trailer pumps which can readily be moved
by hand, or by being attached to an
ordinary motor-car, from place to place,
and thus deal with small fires before they
become serious.
The
The gas bomb-the third class is the
bomb that is directed against the morale
of the country. It does little or no harm
to property, but can weaken the resistance
of any country if the population is not
taught in advance how to safeguard itself
from the effects of this bomb.
methods which the Government are adopting are the provision of respirators free
of cost, and the erection of decontamination centres and first-aid posts. Those
on whom the duty will fall of dealing
with infected areas or persons will be
provided with special protective clothing.
I have mentioned now the nature of
attacks which may be expected, and
briefly some of the steps which can be
taken to minimise their effects.
Air Raid Precautions-
Second Reading
3 NOVEMBER 1938
The next question which naturally
presents itself is how are these air raid
precautions to be organised. In the
United Kingdom we have, I think,
always prided ourselves on our system
of voluntary as opposed to compulsory
services. The decision, therefore, to
organise air raid precautions on a
voluntary basis was deliberate as also
was the decision to place the duty upon
the elected local authorities of the
country. Anyone reading the Bill will
realise that the duty of preparing
schemes for the safeguarding of life and
property in their areas has been placed
upon the local authorities with the
exception, I may say, of the county
councils. In an area like Northern
Ireland it will, it is thought, be much
more satisfactory for all local authorities
to deal direct with the Ministry of Home
Affairs than, as has been the practice
in Great Britain, for them to submit
schemes through the county councils
resulting in delay and in some cases
strongly divergent opinions and views.
May I say at once that it is not the
intention of the Government to press
local authorities in the less vulnerable
areas to prepare expensive and intricate
schemes. Rather will they be asked to
deal with three matters-the storage
of respirators for the public residing in
their areas, the provision of training
and lectures to enlighten those who
cannot be expected to have much
knowledge of the dangers of attack from
the air and what each may himself do
to minimise their effects, and, lastly,
the provision of first aid posts at which
will be kept the materials ncessary for
decontamination and other things.
I believe the House will
with
agree
me that air raid precautions have come
to stay, and no matter how remote any
of us may think we are from points of
danger, we must recognise that in times
of war we may have to give our services
or find ourselves working at civilian
duties in other parts of the United
Kingdom, and it is for this reason that
it is so essential that everyone should
learn by attending lectures something
of the dangers of and remedies for
aerial attack.
There is one other matter to which
local authorities must look and this is
the fire service. It will be unnecessary
to expect all local authorities to provide expensive fire fighting apparatus,
but even in peace time and all the more
in time of emergency it is essential that
all local authorities should either have
their own fire fighting apparatus or have
an arrangement under which the brigade
of a neighbouring authority will come.
to their assistance. This aspect of the
matter is at present engaging the
attention of the Ministry of Home
Affairs. In the more vulnerable areas
such as, for instance, the City of Belfast
and its surroundings, precautions of a
more extensive character are necessary,
and it has been a matter of satisfaction
to the Government to notice the great
progress which is being made in this
respect by the Corporation of the city
of Belfast, and indeed several other
local authorities. May I just repeat
what has been said several times already
that with the ever increasing speed and
range of aircraft no one
can say that
Northern Ireland is outside the range
of attack from Continental countries.
It will be noticed that a provision is
included in the Bill under which local
authorities may co-operate and assist
each other, and this Clause can be used,
for instance, by rural authorities who
agree together to store respirators in one
central place and share the cost.
I come to the financial implications of
the Bill which are set out very clearly
in Clauses 7 and 8 and in the Schedule.
Shortly, it amounts to this that 70 per
cent. of expenditure incurred by Belfast
County Borough Council in connection
with any scheme for air raid precautions
approved by the Ministry of Home
Affairs will be recouped by the Government, and similarly 75 per cent. of such
expenditure to all other local authorities. Should a local authority have to
incur such expenditure-and I may say
that I think this is most improbablewhich after payment of the grant,
involves the raising of a 1d rate, then
any expenditure beyond this amount will
earn grant at the rate of 85 per cent.
Provision is also made for the relief from
rates in respect of certain air raid protection works, and this Clause follows
very closely on the lines of similar
legislation passed in England and Scotland recently. A penal Clause has also
been added to the Bill, and I feel sure
that the Members of this House will
agree as to the desirability of including
Air Raid Precautions-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr Robb]
such a provision in view of the reported.
misuse to which respirators were put by
many misguided people after the last
crisis. These respirators are provided
for the people of this country free of
charge, and the least they can do is to
endeavour to follow the instructions
issued by the Home Office as to the
proper care and attention which they
should receive.
I should like to express the thanks of
the Government to the Joint Committee of the Order of St. John of
Jerusalem and the British Red Cross
Society in Northern Ireland for the
splendid work which they have been
doing during the past two years. Many
thousands of people have attended their
admirable courses.
The Government have been assured of
the co-operation of the British Medical
Association Branch in Northern Ireland
in considering the medical aspect of
schemes, for it is essential in an area
such as this that a wide view should be
taken of this problem which must
include the question not only of firstaid posts but of hospital accommodation
generally.
can
The subject of air raid precautions
is a very large one and I have not
endeavoured to touch on all the aspects
of the matter, but rather to confine
myself to those which concern us most
in Northern Ireland. Recent events
have shown how quickly a crisis
arise, and I would, therefore, appeal not
only to those local authorities who have
not yet done so to start the preparation
of their schemes as soon as possible, but
also to the citizens of Northern Ireland
as a whole to assist in the carrying out
of such schemes by attending classes and
learning something about the subject,
and if possible by offering their services.
in a voluntary capacity for the furtherance of this work.
in
I should like to add that the Government fully realise that the recent
emergency has shown that the system
of protection for the civilian population
Great Britain has its weaknesses.
Inquiries are now proceeding to
ascertain how these weaknesses can be
overcome and if, as a result of these
inquiries, it is found desirable to take
other precautions than those possible
under this Measure the Government will
have no hesitation in bringing before
Parliament an amending Bill. I beg to
move.
Question proposed, "That the Bill be
now read a Second time."
Dr. GIBSON: I rise to support the
Second Reading of the Bill. I want
specially to express my appreciation of
the remarks made by the Leader of the
House with reference to the Joint Committee of the Red Cross and St. John,
of which I have the honour to be
Director-in-Chief. We have worked
very strenuously for the last two years
training and preparing people, especially
in connection with first-aid and home
nursing. The admirable officer who has
been appointed by the City Council,
Major Eastwood, has expressed the view
that the one bright spot in Ulster is the
Joint Committee.
our
We have performed what we considered a public duty in beginning the
scheme of air raid precautions. We
have sent quite a number of men to
London, and they have been trained
and have efficiency certificates from the
various schools there. They have now
come back and have held classes in con-
The
nection with air-raid precautions.
Home Office have given us the greatest
encouragement to proceed with
work, and when classes are established
here for instruction in decontamination,
first aid, and home nursing, we can, I
think, supply teachers and instructors
for all the classes that can be got up
in
What
the city of Belfast.
required at
time
the present
volunteers. We want the whole civi
population to volunteer to do all they
in connection with this
possibly can
matter because, as the Leader of the
House has said, air raid precautions
have come to stay.
are
are
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: I cannot
help feeling that the Measure which has
been brought before the House to-day
is one of the least fortunate examples
of what is sometimes known as step-bystep legislation. At the
At the moment its
elder sister in England is losing the
last shreds of her reputation. It is not
too much to say that the English Act
has been as near as possible a complete
failure for reasons which are inherent
Air Raid Precautions--
Second Reading
3 NOVEMBER 1938
are,
in this Bill also. Those reasons
first, that it has to be a weight upon
the local authorities of a nature which
it is practically impossible for them to
carry. I know that when the English
Act was drafted there were strong
reasons for that.
Some time ago the general expert
opinion was that air-raid precautions
would be of very little value, and
reliance was placed almost entirely
on what is known as active defence,
chiefly counter-bombing. Since that
date it has been possible to get a very
large amount of experience from Spain
and some, less valuable, from China.
We collated that experience and we
realised that so far from being something to keep the civil population quiet,
air-raid precautions efficiently carried
out could enormously minimise casualties, even under the severest conditions.
That is one reason why the English Act
was brought in as it was. It was for
what was really not regarded by
experts as a matter of very great
importance, and consequently the
Government, being very busy, pushed
it off on to the local authorities.
In
The second reason, which does not
apply in this Province, was that it was
realised that anti-air-raid precautions
workers would have to be kith and kin
so to speak with the police forces.
England the police forces are local, and
it would be very difficult to run a
general scheme and use police forces not
controlled from a national centre. This
Bill proposes to hand the matter in the
first instance to the local authorities.
We all realise that anti-air-raid precautions are of enormous importance, and
in this country we have not the same
difficulty as in England because we have
a centralised police force. That clears
the ground.
Let us look at the thing with a little
commonsense. Anti-air-raid precautions
schemes are matters which require very
expert knowledge in drawing up.
Knowledge of what is possible and what
is desirable is still changing. For
instance, we are told by experts that
the relative importance of gas, incendiary bombs, and high explosives has
entirely changed within the last year or
so. Relatively the experts are now
barely bothering their heads about gas,
and, again, the incendiary bomb, I am
told, is regarded as less important than
high explosives. Here is a thing requiring
expert planning, with the expert
knowledge continually increasing and
developing, yet plans are to be drawn
up by local authorities all over the
country.
Does a sensible man, who is going to
build a house, draw up the plans and
submit them to the architect for approval?
Surely it is the other way round? Surely
the Home Office should have the experts;
they should draw up the plans? They
could then have the necessary consultations with the local authorities. This
Bill is putting the whole thing the wrong
way round. Of course there are certain
places where it would be possible to do
the other. The city of Belfast can afford
to engage experts. I know it has engaged a very able one. But the smaller
local authorities cannot do that.
If you go on with the Measure that
you have at present, what will happen?
Exactly what happened in England.
Some local authorities, either more zealous
or more nervous than others, will produce
very complete schemes, perhaps too complete in comparison with the danger to
which their areas will be exposed. Other
possibly more dangerous places, but with
stronger nerves or less zeal, will hang fire
with their schemes. Their schemes will
be produced slowly and will probably be
unsatisfactory. I know that a great deal
will be done by the advisers, but why
not put the horse before the cart in the
usual way? Why not make the drawing up
of plans a job for the experts, and then let
them consult with the local authorities?
I should really like to impress upon the
Leader of the House that in my opinion
we are following Great Britain step by
step, but in this case, like Great Britain,
are doing things the wrong way round.
I do not think I have very much to add.
That is the most important point. I
should like to pay my tribute to the work
of the St. John organisation. In Great
Britain they have followed a very bad
policy, and they have had to pull out of
retirement the greatest administrator of
our time to try to put things right.
I
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH:
should like to say that I very heartily
support this Bill. It carries out all that
was promised by the Government, and
consequently I welcome it. As the House
knows, we are part of the United King-
Air Raid Precautions-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
are
[Sir Crawford McCullagh]
dom, and as such are scheduled for protection according to the danger we
calculated to incur. If a war unfortunately did break out Belfast will get
the same protection as any
other part
of the United Kingdom, and will be provided with anti-aircraft guns. We in
Belfast have taken the matter seriously,
and the work is going on well.
I want to pay a tribute to-day to the
voluntary workers in Belfast. In the
week in which we had the real scare the
people came forward to offer their
services voluntarily. I, myself, went
round the different centres where the gas
masks were being assembled, and really
it was a treat to see the way the people
were working and doing their level best
to cope with the emergency. Again, the
employers of Belfast did their part. They
sent their workers to help, and where it
was necessary to pay the workers the
different firms paid them. That was the
spirit that was displayed during that
awful week.
The hon. and gallant Member behind
me (Sir Roland Nugent) takes another
view. I take it that he is thinking more
of the country districts. But we all
know this, that if an air raid were to
come and it is quite possible in the case
of war-the place that the enemy would
certainly attempt to get at would be the
city of Belfast, not the country. Belfast
would be attacked because of its great
shipbuilding yard, its aircraft factory,
and the great grain elevators. In the
case of an air raid the first thing that
would have to be done would be, as far
as possible, to send the children into the
country. We would provide shelters in
the city. We attempted that during the
crisis, and the work would have gone
on but for the fact that the necessity
ceased to exist. It would only be by
pure accident that any country town or
district would be bombed. Why should
they be attacked? In my opinion, the
greater amount of attention will have to
be given to the city of Belfast, and not
So much to the country towns and
districts.
I do not think at the moment that
we can criticise this Bill, which is carrying out all that was intended. I should
like to say that as far as Belfast is concerned there is a gas mask for every
citizen. A great deal of valuable work
has been going on without the public
knowing anything about it. The Corporation, the Harbour Board, which is a
separate authority, and the Government
had a conference, at which the three
parties agreed to work together in perfect harmony in carrying out the spirit
of this Bill.
Apart from gas, one of the greatest
dangers will be incendiary bombs. The
Corporation of Belfast have to protect,
and will protect, every citizen as far as
it is their power to do so. The city has
a great organisation, with a very capable
official at its head, and, looking ahead,
it has made provision for dealing with
fires anywhere in and around the city
and at the harbour. The brigade is
there for the service of the community.
We are pushing on; we are doing all we
can. We have a very capable official,
and we are working in perfect harmony
with the Government, which approves
of what we are doing. I, for one,
strongly welcome this Bill.
Major DOBBS: I want to support,
with all the power I can command, what
has been said by the hon. and gallant
Senator, Sir Roland Nugent. When I
read this Bill I felt numb. I felt that
we were dealing with unrealities. I will
tell you why. If Germany thinks it
would be wise to go to war
with us on New Year's Day she
will
go to war. May I try to
envisage what she would do. She would,
without any declaration of war, and without any warning, send her aeroplanes to
bomb
are
us, say 500 planes to bomb
London, 100 for Southampton, 100 for
Bristol, 100 for Birmingham, 100 for
Newcastle and other centres in England,
and, perhaps, 50 for Northern Ireland.
The 50 for Northern Ireland
going to come
this
against
piece of country round here. They
are not going to bomb Kilroot Fort,
Carrickfergus Castle, or Holywood
Barracks; they are going to bomb Belfast.
They are not going to bomb Victoria
Barracks, or the City Hall, or Harland &
Wolff's; they are going to bomb the
poorest parts of Belfast, because the
houses there are the worst built and are,
therefore, the most dangerous from the
point of view that they would take fire
most easily in the case of an air attack.
The poorest parts of the city are the
most populated.
3 NOVEMBER 1938
Air Raid Precautions--

Second Reading
men, but by technical experts, first for
the evacuation of the children, and,
secondly, for billeting and feeding them.
These schemes absoutely broke down in
London a few weeks ago. Again, if
there should be bombing there is no use
preparing a scheme after it takes place.
A scheme to provide for those who would
be rendered homeless should be prepared
so that they might be looked after and
fed. I do not envy the Ministry their
task, but they should not shelter behind
the local authorities.
It will be remembered that in the last
war the policy of the Germans was to do
the greatest amount of damage in the
shortest possible time, and not to spread
the killing of people over a long time.
This terrible decimation of the people in
London and the other English cities and
in Belfast is going to take place at the
same time. There is no reason why
bombs should not be dropped here and
in London and elsewhere at the same
time. We are going to have people
killed; we are going to have poor people
killed. All sorts of things will happen,
possibly rioting. I do not want
to go into this aspect of air raids,
but it has to be considered. It
is quite possible that the Germans
may go near to winning the war on the
first day. I have seen men killed, as
others in this House, doubtless have, by
high explosive; I have seen men suffering
from the effects of gas; but, thank God,
I have never seen children and women
suffering in that way. That is what is
going to happen in the next war. It
is not the men in the trenches that the
enemy are going to get at; it is the civil
population, men, women and children.
The first Clause of this Bill says:
It shall be the duty of each local
authority, other than a county council,
to prepare and submit to the Ministry
of Home Affairs . . a scheme.
The futility of it! What on earth do
members of local authorities know about
high explosive and about gas? Surely
the right people to undertake this work
are the Ministry of Home Affairs, and,
there again, what do they know about
high explosive and about gas?
I am going to ask the Leader of the
House to take this Bill back and to redraft, at any rate, the first part of it
I ask the right hon. Gentleman to ask the
Ministry of Home Affairs to prepare a
scheme for Belfast, and when they have
done that to deal with the other towns.
As the Lord Mayor of Belfast has said,
Belfast is the important centre.
scheme should not be prepared from the
ideas of amateurs, but from those of
technical experts, those who have seen
or studied what has been done in Madrid,
Barcelona, and other Spanish towns,
towns that have been attacked by
German bombs, possibly for practice.
The
I would ask the Ministry to get
schemes prepared, not by their draughts-
I had the good fortune a few days ago
of discussing this question of A.R.P.
with several gentlemen in London.
Although they were of opinion-they
could, of course, only speak about what
they had personal knowledge of that
things were fairly good outside London,
that is near it, at some places, Bath
especially, they were bad. In England
the cry is to tell them what to do and
they will do it. We want a scheme,
one which has been thoroughly thought
out; we do not want a hotch-potch one.
We want a scheme that will stand the
test of time and of bombing.
Mr. LESLIE: I am very much in
sympathy with what has been stated by
the hon. and gallant Senator on my left
(Major Dobbs), and also by the hon. and
gallant Senator opposite (Sir Roland
Nugent). Like them I can only regard
this as a make-shift Bill, and I hope it
is only intended that it should be a
temporary one. The hon. and gallant
Senator has suggested the rejection of
the Bill. Certainly I do not propose to
do that for it is, of course, impracticable
to reject it however much we disagree
with some parts of it. We have to get
the Bill through because of the
regularisation of certain expenditure
already incurred. I think that is really
necessary. I entirely concur with the
view that you are not going to get from
local authorities, speaking generally, a
scheme worth very much.
The Ministry should do something to
build on, rather than shake, the foundations of the Bill by preparing a model
scheme. They could then send it round,
rather like a skeleton map for the school
children, to the various local authorities,
and ask them this or that question.
They could also help them with the
scheme which may be called the local
375 Air Raid Precautions-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
authority scheme, but which will really.
be the scheme of the Ministry made in
consultation with experts. If that were
done you might get a proper scheme.
I agree that Belfast is the most
vulnerable point. I cannot agree, however, with what has been said about
rural districts where the difficulty of
storing gas masks, to come down to small
matters, is very great. I cannot agree
that the rural district is a negligible
quantity in this matter, nor can I agree
that the condition of the rural district
would not be changed during the course
of a war. The risk to be run by a rural
district depends to a large extent upon
what is in that district. It is not the
farm, but it is much more likely to be
the mill. A mill at present engaged in
the scutching of flax would not attract
hostile bombers, but during a war the
mills will be used for other purposes.
Almost every mill in Northern Ireland
is a potential munition factory, and all
such factories would, of course, be
legitimate targets.
Again, almost every rural district is
served by a railway. Railway stations.
are necessarily exposed to attack because
to damage a station or a railway line
interferes with transport. The transport business might be the transport of
troops, and, indeed, Northern Irelandit is remote from potential interference
shall we say-might be used more and
more as a training ground for troops if
a war lasted for any length of time.
Precautions and arrangements should be
made as far as is practicable for the rural
districts. The precautions should be on
well ordered lines, discussed and considered by experts who would have
regard to their particular circumstances.
We can do something under the Bill
before us. Mostly the matters I am
thinking of are items of detail. Some
of them are of more importance than
others. In any event that will fall for
discussion at a later stage.
Clause 5 relates to the duty of local
authorities to prepare schemes for the
evacuation of the civil population. It
says:
It shall be the duty of all local
authorities to furnish to the Ministry
such information as may be demanded
by it for the purpose of assisting the
preparation by His Majesty's Government of plans for any necessary
transference of the civil population
I should take that as an excellent
example of an invitation to the local
authorities not to lock the stable door
until the horse has been stolen.
Apparently it is so drafted as to require
the preparation of plans for necessary
transference in the event of hostile attack
from the air. In London the other day
the evacuation began long before the
hostile attack from the air. It never
got that length. These plans must be
made in advance, and I can see no
reason why in this respect this Bill
should not be altered. That could be
done without doing any harm to anything, and still more without incurring
delay.
We shall, I am afraid, have to consider very carefully whether we must
not take a further step in regard to the
protection of public property. We are
endeavouring to protect the gas mask of
the individual, but we are apparently
leaving Government property, obtained
for the purpose of this Bill, very much
to the mercy of the Malicious Injury
Acts. I propose we put on the Paper
a rather better worded Clause so that
something shall be done in the matter.
That is a matter of detail.
I do not think there is anything more
that I can usefully say at the moment.
I should like to repeat that I look
forward to a more comprehensive Bill
at a not too distant date. In the meantime we must pass this Bill, subject to
any such alterations as can be effected
on the Committee stage.
Mr. ROBB: I agree with what Sir
that our
Roland Nugent has said,
knowledge of air attack is incomplete, it
is in a state of flux, and that from day
to day the possibilities of defence against
air attack are changing both in scheme
and in width. I think I concluded what
I had to say on this Bill by giving an
assurance that should any development
take place, any suggestions be made for
protection against air attack from the
inquiries proceeding all over the world
into methods of protection, the Government will not be slow to introduce an
amending Measure in Parliament. His
Air Raid Precautions
3 NOVEMBER 1938
Adjournment
suggestion that the whole of this work
could be more easily done in this area
than in England, owing to the unification
of our police force, was most interesting.
As matters stand at present, the plan
that is under consideration in effect has
been adopted, and, as Senator Leslie suggests, will meet the case for the moment.
A model scheme, founded upon the best
expert advice that the Ministry of Home
Affairs could get, has been drafted and
will be sent to the different local authorities. These authorities will be visited by
the Air Raid Precautions Officer and
representatives of the Ministry, who will
explain to them the general scheme that
it is proposed they should adopt. They
will consider with the local authorities
what modifications, extensions or adaptations can be usefully made for each particular area. At this stage of our
knowledge probably that is the best
method by which we should proceed.
Everyone agrees that we do not know
enough to be sure that there is any satisfactory precaution against air attack.
As Sir Roland Nugent has said quite
frankly, the only defence at present
seems to be counter-bombing. Counterbombing is not within the province of
this Government, but so far as protection
can be given or devised at home, the
Government presents this Measure with
the promise that if knowledge extends
and further developments arise they will
not be slow to adopt them and to do their
best to carry them out. In the meantime, I commend the Second Reading to
this House.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Adjournment.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn."(Mr. Long.)
Adjourned accordingly at
a Quarter to Five o'clock
until Tuesday next.
379 Question to Minister-

THE SENATE
First Aid for Teachers
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 8th November, 1938.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Finance:
Census of Population of Northern Ireland, 1937. County of Fermanagh.
Appropriation and Consolidated Fund
Services Accounts, 1937-38.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
The Winter Assize Order, 1938.
Ministry of Agriculture:
The Pigs Marketing (Northern Ireland) (Special Levy) (No. 6) Order, 1938.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Question to Minister.
FIRST AID AND HOME NURSING
FOR TEACHERS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Dr. Gibson:
To ask the Leader of the House how
many elementary school teachers are employed under the Belfast Education Committee, how many of these teachers hold
certificates of efficiency in first aid and
home nursing, whether there are any
schools in the Belfast area where no teacher
holds such a certificate, and where there
is no teacher qualified to render first aid
in cases of accident or sudden illness,
occurring among the pupils, and, if so,
how many; whether he will consider the
advisability of including in the final
examination of teachers in training a
knowledge of first aid and home nursing,
and, in view of the fact that the school
age is to be extended, whether he will
prescribe first aid and home nursing as
part of the final year's curriculum.
Dr. GIBSON: I think it is admitted
on all sides that the evacuation of the
children in case of a threatened air raid
is the first thing to be attended to, and
perhaps the most important. For the
successful evacuation of children three
things are necessary. The first is a place
where they might find asylum; the
second is the question of how they are
to be fed and looked after; and the
third is the question of who is to take
charge of them. The first and second of
these requirements are naturally the
affairs of the local authority, but the
third, the taking charge of these children,
is of supreme importance. I have come
to the conclusion that that duty could be
performed by the elementary and
secondary school teachers. I imagined
that when the children were being
evacuated to avoid danger the school
teachers would immediately volunteer in
large number to go with them.
In order to be able to take charge of
an evacuation party a person requires
certain qualifications. These parties
will not be entirely immune from the
possibility of a bomb dropping in their
neighbourhood. It may be a high explosive, incendiary, or gas bomb, and
those in charge of such a party should
be qualified to render first aid in cases
of broken limbs or bleeding arteries, or
burns from an incendiary bomb. Then
again, they ought to be trained so that
they can detect gas, and they ought to be
capable of putting on a gas mask. Therefore such persons should hold a certificate
in first aid, and a certificate of efficiency
in air raid precautions.
I have been in communication with
the Air Raid Precautions Officer for
Belfast, and he gives me a rather alarming report of the attitude of school
teachers towards helping in the matter
of the evacuation of school children. He
says they have not volunteered in any
large numbers, and have attached such
conditions to their volunteering that it
would be impossible to satisfy them. He
adds that there might be great difficulty
in getting properly trained parties to
take charge of these children.
you have heard the figures given by the
Leader of the House in reply to the
question I have put down you will be
better able to judge whether these people
are discharging their public duty, and
their duty to the children whom they are
teaching.
When
With regard to the last paragraph but
one of my question, I think we are all
agreed that this is not a temporary
B
Question to Minister-
8 NOVEMBER 1938
First Aid for Teachers
Measure, but that it is one of protection
that will extend over years. We must
be ever prepared for attacks from the air,
and we must keep up our preparations
year after year. Therefore, I have
asked whether the Minister will consider
the advisability of including in the final
examination of teachers in training a
knowledge of first aid and home nursing,
and I might add to that air raid precautions. In the last paragraph I ask
the Minister whether, in view of the fact
that the school age is to be extended, he
will prescribe first aid and home nursing
and air raid precautions as part of the
final year's curriculum.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: Before the Leader of the House replies I
would like to say that I think it would
be a great advantage if, as we are extending the school-leaving age by a year,
girls were taught nursing and boys instructed how to deal with a wound or
other injury sustained in an accident.
The Duke of Buccleuch's son sustained a
wound when shooting and bled to death,
and it was then stated that if the gamekeeper who was with him had known any
thing about first-aid treatment his life
might have been saved. As a result of
that fatality the attendances at the St.
John Ambulance classes greatly increased, and I think it would be a good
thing if, in their final year at school,
girls were taught something about
nursing, and boys instructed how to deal
with wounds.
The MINISTER OF EDUCATION
(Mr. Robb): May I point out that the
question, as framed, does not refer to
anything except first aid and home.
nursing.
The number of teachers employed at
the present time in provided and transferred public elementary schools under
the management of the Belfast Education
Committee is 904. In addition, 421
teachers excluding members of religious
communities in schools paid under the
capitation grant system-are employed in
Belfast in public elementary schools
under voluntary management.
The Ministry has no information as to
how many of these teachers may hold
certificates of efficiency in first aid and
home nursing from voluntary organisations which grant such certificates, and
is, therefore, unable to say whether there
are any schools in Belfast where no
teacher holds such a certificate.
With regard to the suggestion that
students in training as teachers should be
examined at the end of their course in
first aid and home nursing, the present
position is that a course of domestic
economy, which includes some instruction
in first aid, forms part of the programme
for women students in the training colleges, on which they are examined at the
end of the session in which the course is
taken. The Ministry has under consideration an extension of this instruction
in first aid for women students at Stranmillis, by the formation of a class to be
taught by specialists in the subject. This
class will, it is expected, be in operation
in the current session, and the whole
question of the place of first aid and home
nursing in the training of teachers will
be examined from time to time.
In reply to the last part of the hon.
Member's question, I would say that the
proposal that first aid and home nursing
should be taught to children remaining in
public elementary schools after the age of
14 will be considered in connection with
the drafting of curricula suitable for such
pupils.
Dr. GIBSON: Arising out of that
answer may I sayis
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry to interrupt the hon. Member, but in putting a
question hon. Senators are not entitled to
speak more than once. They may speak
when putting the question, or they may
put the question formally, which
equivalent to making a speech, but after
the question has been answered they cannot make a second speech, although other
Members may speak. The Member who
puts the question may, however, ask a
supplementary question.
Dr. GIBSON: I desire to put a supplementary question. Is the right hon.
Gentleman aware that of the 1,325
teachers in the schools in Belfast, only 95
have enrolled for warden duties, and 120
for first aid; that the majority of these
teachers stipulated that they could attend
classes only between four p.m. and five
p.m., and that they refused to attend any
of the 52 classes being held in the evenings? I should like to know what the
right hon. Gentleman thinks should be
done about that matter.

Education Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Robb.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair].
CLAUSE 1 (Raising of compulsory
school age to 15).
(1) Subject to the provisions of this
Act, the age of 15 shall be the age up
to which parents shall be required
under the Education Act (Northern
Ireland), 1923 (in this Act referred to
as "the principal Act") to cause their
children to attend school unless there is
some reasonable excuse.
(2) The amendments set out in the
Schedule to this Act (being amendments consequential on the provisions
of the last preceding sub-section) shall
be made in the enactments specified in
that Schedule.
(3) This section shall not apply in the
case of children born before the first
day of July, nineteen hundred and
twenty-five, and the principal Act shall
have effect with respect to those children as if this Act had not been passed.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move at the
end of sub-clause (2) to insert the following sub-clause :-(3) The parent of a
child to whom this Section applies may, at
any time during the 12 months next preceding the day on which such child would
attain the age of 14 years, serve on the
education authority a notice under his
hand stating that it is his desire that the
child named in the notice shall cease to
be required to attend school so soon as
that child attains the age of 14 years.
The education authority shall record the
notice and cause the parent to be informed accordingly, and then, notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in the Education Acts (Northern
Ireland), 1923 to 1938, or any of them,
there shall, after the child named in the
notice attains the age of 14 years, be
I think I may claim to have indicated
with some precision when speaking on the

I need scarcely point out that the terms
of the Amendment are strictly within the
title of the Bill, which is to amend the
law with respect to the school-leaving age
and so on, and says nothing about compulsion, either universal or partial.
laid stress on the question of the exemp
tion to be given in respect of children. I
intimated fairly plainly that had it not
been that some attempt was made in the
Bill to provide for exemptions I should
not have been able to support the Bill.
I spoke several times as regards the
exemption question, as to which, of
course, the Committee will refer to
Clauses 4 and 7, the clauses which deal
respectively with exemptions for the
purpose of employment and for domestic
purposes. I must ask the Committee to
look at the details of these clauses and
see how it is proposed that exemption
should be granted. It is admitted, of
course, that before exemption is granted
under these clauses, the question would
have to be considered by somebody somewhere, and a decision taken on such lines
as seem reasonable as well as equitable. It
must also be admitted that we have no
indication anywhere in the Bill as to the
method by which it is hoped to secure
something in the shape of uniformity as
regards such decisions. Whoever makes
the decision, it would appear, is entirely
free from considering the exemptions
granted by other bodies in like cases.
of a
Again, the question arises as to how
in practice these exemptions are to be
granted. Are they to be granted, as
may appear from the Bill, by the education authority, which we agree is the
education committee in the case
county borough and the education committee of the county council as regards
the county council, the county council
people, of course, acting through the
regional education committee?
bodies have necessarily to sit
where, and the difficulty I laid stress on
was, and is, how are the parents of the
children to get to the places where that
authority sits, and how are they to come
back when the exemption requires re-

some-
I

newal, and how are they to keep the
offer of employment open until a decision
has been reached? These are the main
difficulties. Meanwhile, trying to find
a way out, I put the Amendment on the
Paper which is now before you.
I must ask the Committee to look at
the Amendment and see what in fact it
does. First of all, it puts the responsibility on the parent of the child. It will
be observed from sub-clause (4) of
Clause 4 that certain duties are laid on
what they are calling the issuing
authority-matters relating to the future
well-being of the child, which in the
ordinary way would be regarded as
matters within the discretion of the
child's parents.
The Amendment puts
the responsibility on the child's parents,
but it requires a decision to be taken by
the parent, exercised deliberately in
writing, and it does not make it irrevocable. I suggest to the House that the
main responsibility in a matter of that
kind does rest on the parent. It is his
child, not the child of the regional committee or of the State, and I also think
that we should find an amendment on
these lines likely to meet all those
difficulties on which I have laid some
stress. Also to some extent, I think the
insertion of the Amendment would
satisfy those numerous bodies throughout the Six Counties who have protested
against the main purpose of the Bill, that
is, the raising of the school age from 14
to 15.
The right hon. Gentleman knows better
than I do how many complaints have
reached him. I
the
see
Coleraine
education authority have put in an objection, and the Dungannon committee also
object. No doubt there is a great deal
of objection here and there throughout
the country to the raising of the education age, and these objections come
mainly from country districts, being
based on the fact of the increasing urban
population, which is not desirable. The
feeling is growing that the interests of
the country were not sufficiently studied
in the preparation of the Bill in relation
to this most important matter of exemption. I cannot claim that the Amendment would satisfy the extreme party,
the people who say that to stop education at 14 is absurd, and it is a good
thing to carry it on further. That is
all very well, but it depends on particular
cases.
There are some children who will
not derive any advantage from the additional year.
Which is the larger of
these classes I do not know. The right
hon. Gentleman admitted the other day
he did not know either. The whole
thing was to some extent a lottery. A
decision cannot be reached for many
years yet.
Consequently we ought to do everything we can to make the path of the
man reasonably requiring exemption
easier. It seems to me that this Amendment offered a way out of it, and prevented us from being under the necessity
of using these children-there are some
20,000 between 14 and 15-as pawns
against the wishes of those parents. It
also avoids putting those parents to an
unreasonable difficulty in the way of
obtaining exemption. The exemption
clauses would remain if my Amendment
were accepted. They would still be
operative in respect of the child whose
parent did not put in notice. Meantime I can only say that this is a matter
on which I recognise opinions must differ
widely. Everyone wants to do the right
thing. I do not think any of us knows
what is really the right thing. I offer
the Amendment as the best solution I
can think of.
Sir JAMES WILTON: Do I understand that the purport of the Amendment is that the parent of a child merely
serves notice that he does not wish the
child to attend school after 14, and that
is sufficient ground for exempting the
child from going to school? If that is
so, I must respectfully suggest that it

will destroy the Bill completely.
investigation of the claims is really the

Mr. MCALLISTER: I rise to discuss
the Amendment merely from the legal

on the wording of the Amendment is this:
The parent at a certain time notifies
the education authority that he does not
intend his child to attend school after
he attains the age of 14 years. That ends
the matter. In other words, the parent
has the sole responsibility either to make
the child attend school or not. I am

Amendment entirely deprives the parent

Mr. ROBB: The Government cannot
accept this Amendment for the reasons
that have already been stated by the last
two speakers. If this Amendment is
carried there is no point in having the
Bill at all. Parents would serve notice
that they were not going to have the
children attend after 14. That is not
the object of the Bill. Its object is to
continue attendance of children who are
not entitled to exemption.
With regard to the point the hon. and
learned Senator made as to there being
no information how uniformity in connection with exemption will be arrived
at, may I say it is the intention of the
Ministry, if the Bill passes, to summon
a conference of education authorities in
order, with consultation with them, to
evolve a common procedure. With regard
to the resolutions passed by authorities
throughout the Six Counties against the
principle of the Bill, they seem to the
Ministry to show that those who passed
the resolutions did not really consider the
terms of the Bill nor examine the
exemptions possible under it. They are
practically all based upon the idea that
the small farmer is going to be deprived
of the assistance of his elder children in
the working of his farm. There is
specific exemption in the Bill whereby
in the event of necessity for assistance
upon the farm or in household duty of
any child between 14 and 15, the child
will not be compelled to attend school.
The weight of these objections has been
convey to me that those who
passed the resolutions had not really
studied the exemptions in the Bill. The
main result of adopting this Amendment
would be to pass an Act for compulsory
attendance at school up to 15, but any
child's father could say, "I do not care.
My child is not going to conform to
this." To do so would be to cut across
the whole Bill, and the Government is
unable to accept the Amendment.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
agree with the principle of this Bill. I
think the principle of the Bill is sound.
It is that a certain number of childrenperhaps the majority of them—should be
educated later than they are at present.
It is not so much that the education of
children between 14 and 15 is necessary,
but it is that during that period they are
going through a period of change, and it
is very important that they should be
under some form of control. I had the
advantage of sitting on a committee which
the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister
of Home Affairs, appointed for the purpose of considering the question of child
welfare. It was impressed on me very
strongly there that an increase of a year
beyond 14 years for educational purposes
would be a way of dealing with the question of juvenile delinquency. Some people
think that that should be extended for a
much longer period. I myself thought
there was a great deal of evidence in
favour of it, but one has to remember
that there is much hardship sometimes in
homes where there are big families.
The
I think this extension of one year is a
moderate way of dealing with the problem
and it will make it very difficult for anyone who complains that their means are
reduced unreasonably by the Bill.
increment which comes in to some very
poor homes through boys and girls
employed in different occupations is very
considerable, and although there are
many advantages and many arguments
which could be used in favour of extending the school age for more than one year,
I think this period of one year is a reasonable method of dealing with the matter.
It will prevent hardships arising owing
to the loss of increment in many poor
homes. For that reason I will support
the Amendment.

It
I shall be glad if the Minister would
consider whether the Bill gives the parent
who wishes to have his child exempted a
reasonable opportunity of stating his case.
I am not altogether sure that it does.
is a matter that could possibly be decided
in consultation with the education committees. I think it is very necessary that
cases in which parents are concerned
should be placed before a committee, the
members of which are more or less resident in the area in which the parents
reside. Some of these people have no
experience and are ignorant, and find it
difficult to state their case before a
number of men and women they do not
know, whereas they would not find any
difficulty in stating their case before
local people whom they do know. That is
a matter which the right hon. Gentleman
might consider later on. I am not one
who believes that exemption ought to be
given in every case, but there may be
cases where a parent should get exemption
because of the necessity of the increment
gained from the child's labour. I think
the extension of the school age to 15 is a
very desirable thing, and may minimise
the amount of child delinquency now so
prevalent.
Mr. LESLIE: The debate has taken
very much the course I expected it would,
and it emphasises the difficulty we all
know about this matter. If I may say
so, the most helpful thing we have heard
is what the right hon. Gentleman said
about his proposal to call a conference of
education authorities. It is quite agreed
that if the education authorities and the
Ministry put their heads together they
may produce something which would meet
my difficulty. I must point out that if
the Bill is passed it would possibly be
proved that a large proportion of parents
do not want it. The question of summary jurisdiction does not come into the
matter. The only jurisdiction that the
magistrate has is in cases of disobedience
of school attendance orders. I do not
think that matters. I do think we may
leave the matter in the way it has been
suggested. Let the conference be called
and an agreement, if possible, arrived at.
If some reasonable and convenient working cannot be reached further action will
have to be taken. For the moment we
must accept the new slogan as regards the
parents of children that "Ulster must
be driven it cannot be led." I ask leave
to withdraw the Amendment. We may
get a little more light when we come to
discuss the next Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Mr. ROBB: That is one of the matters

Mr. McALLISTER: I suggest that
under the Clause the Ministry have.
taken too much responsibility on themselves. At the present time if a child is
found fault with for not attending school
the education authorities make a ruling
that before a penalty can be attached or
any compulsion brought to bear on the
parent or guardian of the child he is
brought before a court of summary
jurisdiction. I suggest no matter how
well-minded the Ministry may be they
are taking on too much. The courts of
summary jurisdiction as constituted are
composed of skilled, trained men, who
are able to judge facts and who could
decide these cases. I suggest that if the
Bill is to secure the confidence of the
people the Ministry should not be the
final arbiters on vital questions affecting
so many families. The Minister should
consider the desirability of amending the
Bill in that respect. I am sure he is
anxious that it should secure the support
of the country, but if the final say is
to lie with the Ministry, he will not have
the support of the people as a whole. I
am not discussing the merits of the Bill.
I am merely speaking from what I know
and feel should be done. No fault has
been found with courts of summary
jurisdiction in the past. They have had
the final say in matters of this kind, and
the public are satisfied that they get
from them an impartial and generous
interpretation of the Act and a fair ruling as far as cases are concerned.
Mr. LESLIE: I may say I have got
to plead guilty to a lapse of memory.
The Clause we are talking about refers
to regulations made by the Ministry
under the principal Act of 1923. My

Mr. ROBB: Yes.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that will dispose of any difficulty as regards the Bill.
Regulations will be made after this conference, which is the better way to get at
it, and laid before Parliament. If we
Mr. ROBB: As regards what Mr.
McAllister has said, this Section says:
(1) Where the parent of a child is
dissatisfied with a decision of the
education authority to refuse, in the
case of that child, an employment
certificate, certificate of domestic necessity, or certificate as to the absence of
suitable school accommodation, or to
attach any conditions to any such certificate granted by the authority, the

against the decision.

upon any appeal under this section
shall be final.
On those particular subjects referred
to in sub-clause (1) I really think, on the
whole, the Ministry is better qualified to
give a decision than any court of summary jurisdiction or resident magistrate.
Of course, the resident magistrate and
court of summary jurisdiction are not
ousted in any regard from their particular jurisdiction, which is that if a
parent does not see that his child attends
school he may be brought before the
court and convicted and fined.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I am not casting any reflection on the Ministry. But
there has been no complaint as to the

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Subclause (1) after "that is to say" to insert:
'Education authority' means a
""
committee established under section two of
the principal Act to exercise and perform
the powers and duties of the council of a
county borough or of the council of a
county, and includes also any sub-committee appointed by any such committee
for the purposes of this Act."
This is an Amendment which I do not
myself very much like, and I rather hope
the Committee will appreciate why I do
not like it. We discussed the point on
the Second Reading as to what "education
authority" did mean, and arrived at the
conclusion that it means exactly what I
have said in the first part of the Amendment. What I do not like is repeating in a
Bill to be construed with an older one a
definition which is already there. I do not
like the first part of the Amendment at
all, but I think it is justifiable because
of the second part. The second part is
that "education authority"
includes also any sub-committee appointed by any such committee for the
purposes of this Act.
That is my trouble. It may be remembered that when we established the school
management committees, which I think
was done by the Act of 1930-I am still
speaking from memory-we substituted a
new Section 3 for the old Section 3 in the
Act of 1923, and so we gave our educa.
tion authorities the distinct power that
they had not before of establishing school
management committees. Now when we
are giving power to the education
authorities to appoint sub-committees I
am for putting something in the Act of
Parliament about it. I am assuming, of
course, that the sub-committees to be
appointed for any of the purposes of this
Bill will not be school attendance committees. They would not seem quite the
right people for reasons the Mayor of
Derry and Senator McAllister gave just
now. They will apparently have to be
committees of the education authorities,
acting, perhaps, in very minute districts,
and there will be the difficulty of getting
people to serve on them. In the meantime can we without inserting words in
the Bill provide for them? I confess I
am rather in a difficulty about it. If
we do not put something in here implying
direct power to appoint a sub-committee
for the
of the new Act we should
purposes
rather put a strain on the words "in this
Act" in the Act of 1923. Therefore, for
the purpose of discussion I submit this
Amendment to the Committee.
Mr. ROBB: When I saw this Amendment my own opinion was that it was
unnecessary, but since then I consulted
those in a position to advise me on these
matters, and they agree that it is quite
unnecessary.
Mr. LESLIE: Unnecessary?
Mr. ROBB: Yes. This Bill has to be
read with the Act of 1923, and in Section
2 of that Act an education authority is
defined as the council of each county or
county borough which is to act through
an education committee, and in accordance with the Fourth Schedule the scheme
provides for sub-committees. I think
that this Amendment, although it does
draw attention to a point which is worthy
of notice is unnecessary. That was my
own opinion, and I am so advised by
those in a position to advise me. Consequently, I cannot accept the Amendment.

Holidays With Pay Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill."-(Mr. Robb.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Holidays).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move 1JR
Clause 1 to add the following new subclause :
"(6) If any worker wilfully works
for reward during a holiday allowed
him under this section such worker
shall be liable on summary conviction
in respect of each offence to a fine not
exceeding five pounds, and any person
abetting such worker in the offence
I
Holiday Pay Bill-
Committee
8 NOVEMBER 1938
shall be liable similarly to a penalty
of like amount."
On the Second Reading I pointed out
that although we were making elaborate
arrangements to ensure that an employer
should give an employee a holiday, the
Bill was rather lacking in a provision
that an employee should take a holiday.
I pointed out there was such a thing as
what was commonly known as a busman's holiday. However, thinking it
over, it seemed to me that as we were
making it a punishable offence on the
part of an employer not to give a holiday to an employee, on the principle
that what is sauce for the goose is sauce
for the gander we ought to make it a
punishable offence on the part of an
employee to work for someone else and
draw pay during the time he is on
holiday.
After all, the principle at the bottom
of a holiday is that it improves the health
and, consequently, the efficiency of the
worker, and therefore if a man gets a
holiday and does not take it he is defrauding his employer. There is the particular
case of a young person, a member of a
family, who gets a holiday and is put out
by, let us say, a furious stepmother to go
and earn a little more for the family
pot. We are already getting reports
from England as to difficulties arising in
the matter of unemployment pay and
allowances during the holiday period,
and I do not think we want to encourage
that state of affairs arising in this
country. If a man is given a holiday
and paid for it he ought to take that
holiday, and he ought not to work for
anybody else in order to get second pay.
If he does that it ought to be made a
punishable offence, and this is the course
I suggest we should take. The penalty,
you will observe, is a very small one.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
should like to ask how a person who
engages a holiday maker stands in
relation to this Bill? If he employs
such person unknowingly, would he be
subject to a fine, or would he have to
prove that he was not aware of the fact
that the person he employed was on holiday with pay? Take the case of a young
gardener who comes along and says that
he is just taking a rest, although in fact
he is on holiday with pay. If I engage
him, would I be subject to the penalty
contained in this proposed Amendment?
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I should explain that there is such a thing as a
guilty mind, and that the absence of a
guilty mind has a wonderful effect on
the courts.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I do
not know what attitude the Leader of
the House is likely to take up on this
Amendment, but I do not like it. The
hon. and learned Senator must recognise
that this Bill does not give the workers
holidays. They always had holidays, and
sometimes the holidays were far too long.
What this Bill proposes to do is to give
the workers holidays with pay.
It requires the employer to pay for the holiday that the worker is compelled to take.
Is this House going to tie things up to
such an extent that, say, in July, the
longest holiday of the year, if a man is
sent out on Saturday, and something
happens on Sunday either in his own
firm or in some other firm, and he is called
upon in order to have machinery ready
for starting again, he is to be held guilty
of a crime?
Mr. LESLIE: No.
Mr.
JOSEPH
CUNNINGHAM: I
hope the hon. and learned Senator does
not think that the workers are going
away on a cruise. A holiday of that
kind is not possible, because they have
not the means. Some of these men,
although on holidays with pay, may be
kicking their heels at home, and simply
because they are called upon to do something, or there is something they would
like to do, and they happen to receive a
reward for it, is that to be regarded as a
serious offence? I think a great deal of
harm will be done if an attempt is made
to put an Amendment of this kind in the
Bill. I hold that it would be better to
work the Bill as it stands, and that it
will be found much more satisfactory
without restrictions of the kind contained
in this proposed Amendment. This
would handicap the people who are on
holidays with pay, and perhaps result in
throwing a great many more persons out
of work. I hope the hon. and learned
Senator will not insist upon this Amendment.
Mr. ROBB: I was interested, I may
say, in the example of parental responsibility which the hon. and learned Senator
gave in moving this Amendment. As
regards this Amendment itself, the Gov-
Holiday Pay Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Robb]
ernment is unable to accept it.
When
this matter of holidays with pay was considered by the Committee appointed by
the Minister of Labour in England and
presided over by Lord Amulree, this
question came before the Committee on a
draft of a Bill drawn up by the International Labour Organisation. The provision
in that draft was not the imposition of a
penalty, such as the hon. and learned
Senator suggests in his Amendment, but
that if a workman given a holiday with
pay should work during that holiday and
receive pay
for his work he should not be
entitled to demand from his regular
employer payment for his holiday. It
was a much milder form of punishment
than the one contained in the Amendment
now before the House.
The Committee considered the question
very fully, received a lot of evidence upon
it, debated it for a considerable length of
time, and came to the conclusion that it
was inadvisable to incorporate any such
provision in this Bill. The Government.
in England adopted that recommendation
and we have to follow that here. I do
not think I need go through many of
the arguments that they suggested, but
it was the unanimous opinion of this
Committee, representing all sections,
employers and employees and social
workers, that it was not advisable to
make any hard and fast rule with regard
to a matter of this kind.
The Bill was designed in order that
people might have a holiday with pay, and
might be supported during that holiday,
and if circumstances should arise whereby
a man might be usefully employed in part
of his holiday they came to the conclusion, at any rate, that the instances
would be so few and might be subject to
such special conditions that they would
not incorporate the provision suggested
by the International Labour Organisation. Accordingly, the Government in
England did not include any such provision, and we have not included it here.
We do not think it advisable that it
should be included.
Mr. LESLIE: In view of the report of
the Committee I am inclined to agree
with what the right hon. Gentleman says,
and I think, perhaps, I should not insist
upon the Amendment. I must point
out to the hon. Senator on my left (Mr.
Joseph Cunningham) that he did not

quite appreciate the argument; he missed,
if I may say so, the word wilfully,"
and he missed the words "under this
section." We need not go into that,
however, and I ask leave to withdraw the
Amendment. Does the right hon. Gentleman expect any regulation to be made
that will help in this matter? The man
I want to get at is the fraudulent fellow.
We have a great many of those, far too
many, appearing before the courts in
Northern Ireland, even more than in the
days when the local magistrates adjudicated. Everybody knows that there is
any amount of fraud, or attempted fraud,
amongst the workers.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

power to make regulations in connection

(Short title) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

CLAUSE 1 (Making of scheme for

(7) The validity of an order approving, amending or revoking a scheme
shall not be called in question except
in proceedings duly begun during the
period of six weeks beginning with
the day on which the notice of the
order having been made is published
in accordance with this section, and,
unless the final decision in proceedings
duly begun during the said period is

shall be conclusive evidence that the
requirements of this Act have been
complied with in relation to the
making of the order, and that the
order and any scheme or amendment
approved thereby have been duly made.
and approved respectively and are
within the powers conferred by this
Act.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 1, sub-clause (4), to leave out from
the word "with" ("with any committee') and to insert the words "the
Potato Marketing Committee.'
""
Again I am in something of a
difficulty. Reading the Bill I stuck at
the words I propose to leave out, and I
started to read through the Bill in order
to find out what committee was in contemplation and what it was going to do.
I finally came to the conclusion that the
only thing it could refer to was the
Potato Marketing Committee, which
does not come into life until Clause 2 is
reached. On the whole, I think we might
as well take out these words and say
what we do mean, the Potato Marketing
Committee. There are other ways of
doing it, but this seems to me the shortest
and the most direct.
Question proposed: "That those words.
be left out.
""
Mr. ROBB: I do not think this
Amendment is necessary. If any
Amendment is to be put in here, I think
the words proposed by the hon. and
learned Senator are not sufficient. The
Bill says:
The Ministry of Agriculture may at
any time, after consultation with any
committee which may be established
in accordance with the provisions of
the scheme
In the next Clause, on the next page, it
says that the committee is the Potato
Marketing Committee. I do not think
anybody could have the slightest doubt
at any time as to what the committee
mentioned in sub-clause (4) of Clause 1
is, If the hon. and learned Senator insists
upon the insertion of these words, then
I suggest that after the word committee
he ought to add the words, "as hereafter
in this Act defined."
I think, on the whole, it would be
simpler to leave the Clause as it is.
Mr. LESLIE: I turned that over in
my mind, but I came to the conclusion
that leaving it as it is makes a ragged
end. There is a chance of some other
committee being appointed under the
scheme, but if the intention is to appoint
only this Potato Marketing Committee,
then I think we should insert these
words, with the addition, "as hereafter
in this Act defined.'
Mr. ROBB: I have no objection.
Amendment agreed to.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to
move,

Belfast Gazette."
I am afraid this is a necessary
insertion.
Mr. ROBB: I am prepared to accept
the Amendment, and thank the hon. and
learned Senator.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie:
Clause 1, sub-clause (7), to leave out

evidence") and to insert the word
"sufficient."
Having regard to the lateness of the
evening and to the fact that a similar
question was considered in this House
before, although I entirely disagree with
the word "conclusive," I content myself
with a protest and will not move the
Amendment.
The following Amendment also stood
on the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :-
Clause 1, at the end of sub-clause (7), to

powers conferred by this Act."
Mr. LESLIE: This Amendment is
bound up with the last one. I will not
move it either-resentfully.
I beg to move, in Clause 1, sub-clause
(8), after the word "published" ("is published'') to insert the words, "in the
Belfast Gazette.'
Mr. ROBB: The Government accept
this Amendment.
