Air Raid Precautions Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Robb.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Duty of certain local
authorities to prepare and submit air
raid precaution schemes).
1.—(1) It shall be the duty of each
local authority (other than a county
council) to prepare and submit to the
Ministry of Home Affairs (in this Act
referred to as "the Ministry") a
scheme (in this Act referred to as an
"air-raid precaution scheme") making
provision as to the arrangements to be
made in their area-
(a) for minimising the injury and
damage to persons and property
likely to result from air raids; and
(b) for the extinction of fires likely to
result from air raids;
and as to the authorities and persons
by whom such arrangements are to be
carried out.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Major Dobbs:
Air Raid Precautions--
Committee
8 NOVEMBER 1938
Clause 1, delete sub-clause (1) and insert "The Ministry of Home Affairs will
prepare model or standard schemes for
air-raid precautions as follows:
(a) for the protection of life against
air-raid attack;
(b) showing the precautions
that
should be taken to minimise the
injury and damage to persons and
property likely to result from air
raids ;
(c) showing the precautions that
should be taken against fire;
(d) and the measures to be taken for
decontamination.
The Ministry of Home Affairs will
also consider which of the above schemes
the various local authorities of Northern Ireland will be well advised to
take cognisance of and will inform each
local authority accordingly.
Upon receiving these schemes and
the accompanying advisory report (as
it affects each local authority) local
authorities shall take steps to put the
scheme into effect or shall furnish the
Ministry of Home Affairs with alternative schemes for their approval."
Major DOBBS: It is very appropriate
that we should be discussing the question
of air-raid precautions at a time when
our own bombers, as we read in this
morning's papers, have carried out such
a
wonderful achievement. I entirely
disagree with the first Clause in the Bill
because it places the initiation of
measures of this kind on local authorities. It also seems to me that the Government are only
only intent on taking
precautions so that when the damage is
done it shall be cleared up. They are
trying to limit this Bill to the duties of
first aid parties, fire-fighting squads, the
preservation of order, and so on. I
think the Leader of the House and myself are at cross purposes.
graph (a) in the Bill as it stands. It
reads:
Take parafor minimising the injury and damage
to persons and property likely to result
from air raids.
Now, take the paragraph which I ask
should be inserted in its place.
For the protection of life against airraid attack.
To put it crudely, the Leader of the
House wishes to take precautions so that
if I am wounded I will be well looked
after. I want precautions taken so that
I will not be wounded. If the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) talks about
law he speaks with authority; if the
hon. Senator (Dr. Gibson) speaks about
the Red Cross he also speaks with
authority, and I am sure that if I speak
on this matter the Committee will give
me credit for being sincere. I want
rather more than that; I want the House
to realise that I speak on this special
subject with a certain small amount of
experience. I ask the indulgence of the
Committee and of you, Mr. Chairman,
for digressing slightly in order to show
my credentials and at the same time to
make one or two points.
On Easter Monday, 1917, the battle of
Arras and Vimy Ridge was fought. On
the following Wednesday I received
orders to look out for a position for my
battery further forward. On the afternoon of that day I reported to the
Brigade Major of Heavy Artillery where
I proposed to take my battery. He was
marking it down on his map when he
said it was in front of a line behind which
the batteries ought to be. I told him it was
the only place I liked, and he said, "You
have come up from the Somme and you
realise what you are doing and you are
taking complete responsibility." Might I
digress for a moment. My battery had
been brought up from the Somme and
we were entrusted with the task of
cutting wire entanglements at a distance.
of about five miles with a new instantaneous fuse.
For two or three weeks we pounded
away at the wire entanglements under
aeroplane observation. On Sunday I got
a message from the General of Heavy
Artillery-an aeroplane apparently had
been sent up to see what damage was
done-which read as follows: "Airmen
report your target blown to hell. Congratulate you." Next morning at 5
o'clock the Canadians went over and they
drove the Germans back a distance of
three miles. They made an encircling
movement through the wire, which had
been cut, and captured 45 German
howitzers. That was the first big capture of enemy guns during the first part
of the war.
411 Air Raid Precautions-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Major Dobbs]
That night-I am sorry for digressing
-my battery took up its position, and
on the following night a sixty-pounder
battery came in on my right, while a
South African six-inch howitzer battery
took up a position on my left. I had
special reasons for taking up the position
which I did. Under no circumstances, if
I had a free hand, would I have taken
either the position on my left or right.
What happened? Within a week those
two batteries were shelled out of their
positions. From that I wish to make
this point. The matter of A.R.P. is one
dealing with life and death, and, therefore, you must have the best advice possible. You cannot leave it to local
authorities.
My battery was for weeks in that position, and it was the only battery in that
sector which was able to go on firing without interruption. We did not have a
single casualty. We fired anything from
20 to 500 shells a day, that is between
two and 50 tons of high explosives. That
is one side of the picture. Let me show
you the other. In Belgium, in November
of the same year, my battery was subjected for 15 minutes to concentrated fire
by 16 German batteries. There was not
a single serious casualty except in regard
to the guns.
We were taking precautions
all the time; we did not wait until we
were fired upon before we took them.
That is a point I want to rub in. Precautions should be taken in order to
minimise the number of killed and
wounded.
Might I make a few remarks about the
types of bombs and amplify, if I can, Mr.
Robb's interesting Second Reading
speech. I will take gas first. I think
this has got to do with the fact
that gas has taken such a hold on the
public imagination. In 1916 cloud gas
was a real terror, but it must be remembered that at times there was no ammunino
tion for the guns. Towards the end of
and in 1917 when we had quanthat year
tities of shells the first sign of a gas
cloud would have meant its being
blown to pieces by shell fire. That
is the reason why we had
gas
clouds after the middle of 1916. Gas and
high explosives do not go together.
have had considerable experience in regard to gas shelling, and though it may
be unpleasant and annoying I am sure its
effect was greatly overrated. So far as
I
gas bombs are concerned they are not the
terrible weapons that the popular mind
seems to think.
With regard to high explosive bombs I
am sorry to say I have heard a lot of
drivel talked about them during the past
few weeks. I should like to say that it is
quite easy to get more or less used to
them. A trench gives absolute protection, except in the case of a direct hit. I
really do not think anyone need worry
about a direct hit.
I now come to incendiary bombs. As
the Leader of the House pointed out,
they are light to carry; but I cannot see
that in themselves they are going to do
much damage to life. Definitely, however, you want to take precautions to deal
effectively with them. I do want to make
this point. A high explosive if it catches
you unprepared is going to kill, but incendiary bombs are not going to kill,
though they may cause
cause big financial
damage.
This Bill deals with hostile attack from
the air. What is going to be attacked?
No hostile bomber will come here except
under definite orders. He must be given
a definite target; he will not be sent out
like a man with a dog and a gun to shoot
any rabbit he sees. A bomber must have
a definite target. That target must be
so important that it is worth while risking the bomber to hit that target. That,
to my mind, eliminates the probability of
a mill, even a prospective machine shop
for making munitions, being a target.
Surely six bombs on the village where the
munition makers live would do far more
to delay production than even a direct hit
on the mill itself.
Again, it is not a bit of good for a
bomber to come over to Northern Ireland
to drop bombs on a railway junction.
Rails can be repaired; any damage done
would be only of a temporary nature.
Drop bombs and kill, you cannot bring
men back to life. To risk sending an
enemy bomber over to Northern Ireland
would mean that there was a very important target. Why? Take the map.
come here a bomber would have to cross
England or Scotland.
map
Το
I
When you are studying the
should like you to consider the size of
cities and towns in England and Scotland
over which a bomber would have to pass.
I am afraid I have got the population of
Air Raid Precautions-
Committee
8 NOVEMBER 1938
the various places from a rather old reference book, but there will not be any
appreciable difference in the figures.
London has a population of 7,500,000;
Birmingham, 900,000; Bristol, 350,000;
Liverpool, 800,000; Sheffield, 490,000;
Leeds, 450,000; Edinburgh, 420,000, and
Glasgow, 1,000,000. Let us turn to
Northern Ireland. The population of
Newry is 12,000; Lurgan, 12,000; Ballymena, 10,000, and Belfast, 350,000.
Surely with these immense targets in
England and Scotland it does not seem
practical politics to imagine that a bomber
would have orders to take the extra risk
of attacking the comparatively insignificant towns of Newry, Lurgan, or
Ballymena.
But there is one plum they might
strike for, and that is Belfast. I want
to point out that an attack on the poorer
parts of Belfast would mean a very
serious loss of life, and for holding up
the building of ships and the making of
munitions it would be far more efficacious
than the actual bombing of the shipyards and munition works. I want to
impress upon the Government that they
should take immediate steps to provide
proper precautions for the poorer parts
of Belfast. They should consider whether
that precaution can be best obtained by
the making of trenches, and I would suggest that they should consider the possibility of making deep, narrow passages
running under the houses themselves.
am speaking purely of the poorer districts of the City of Belfast.
I
With regard to anti-aircraft guns, do
not put too much trust in them. I have
seen a tremendous lot of firing by antiaircraft guns at aeroplanes, but I have
seldom seen an aeroplane brought down.
Anti-aircraft guns may, however, keep
the bombers up high. They will certainly not be a complete precaution, and
in any case you cannot get any
for months. With regard to our own
aeroplanes, it is going to be almost impossible for them to stop the bombers
coming here, though they may put them
down on the way back.
I have tried to put matters in their
proper perspective, and I hope I have
shown that it is absolutely necessary for
this subject to be tackled by the Government itself and not by the local authorities. They must carefully consider the
possibilities and probabilities of air-raid
attack, and then form plans and see that
those plans are carried out. They should
differentiate between precautions against
attack and precautions that play a part
when the attack has taken place.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I
am afraid I can claim to know comparatively little about the matters of
which the hon. and gallant Member
opposite has spoken from his personal
experiences in the Great War, as I came
in only in the latter part of the war.
But looking at this Amendment from the
drafting point of view I see several
objections to it. One of them is that the
hon. and gallant Senator is really
attempting to redraft the Clause, and I
rather prefer the original draft, because
it is more inclusive and much shorter. I
also think he is attempting to do what
Senator Sir Roland Nugent is doing in
a later Amendment. He has decided
that there are four different forms of
attack which aeroplanes are likely to
make, and therefore he quite rightly suggests that it would be a waste of money
to lavish a great deal of time and
attention on safeguarding the Slieve
Donard and other such places, which are
not likely to be attacked to the same
extent as Belfast would be.
I think every hon. Member will agree
that it would be unreasonable to spend
a great deal of money on safeguarding
places that are not likely to be attacked,
but although I agree with that part of
his Amendment I do not like sterotyping
all the different possible forms of attack.
If it is necessary I would much rather
leave it to model rules to be made in
conjunction with the naval, military, and
air force authorities. I hope the Government will treat this as a matter of
strategy, because, after all, war is war.
A local surveyor may know all about
building roads, but he knows absolutely
nothing about the effects of high
explosive shells. That is a thing the
Government ought to deal with on an
entirely different footing to ordinary
local authorities. If I am to support
any of these Amendments I would much
rather support those of Senator Sir
Roland Nugent.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
should like to make a few remarks with
reference to the position of the City of
Belfast. We have listened to an ex-
Air Raid Precautions-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Sir Crawford McCullagh]
cellent speech from Senator Major Dobbs
giving his experiences during the Great
War, and his suggestions as to what the
Government ought to do. The Amendment moved by the hon. and gallant
Senator really means that he wants the
Government to become entirely responsible for air-raid precautions. I have
every sympathy with him in that respect.
In the month of October our Air Raid
Precautions Committee instructed our
Town Clerk to write to the Home Office
and point out that in their opinion the
Government should take the sole and
entire responsibility in this matter.
What happened? Since then we have
discovered that the Bill which is now
before the House is modelled on the Act
which was passed by the Imperial Parliament. That Act puts the responsibility
on the local authorities, and when you
come to examine the position there is a
good deal to be said for it. The
Northern Government say they will pay
at least 70 per cent. of the cost of
air-raid precaution schemes, and the only
comment I have to make on that is that
our friends in the country are to get
75 per cent. I am not going to oppose
that. I say, "Good luck to you. Get
as much as you can."
The Government say in reality you are
entitled to take some responsibility. They
are paying at least 70 per cent. There is
a great deal to be said for that, because
local authorities know more of local circumstances and know more how the
matter should be treated than even
Government officials. If you carry out
what is suggested, what is going to
happen? You are going to set up an
authority controlled entirely by Government officials. You will have a good
many officials appointed, and very little
work done. Consequently, while
we
objected to paying for it, we went this
far, and I can go this far to-day.
Whatever the Government is doing or
whatever is included in the Bill, we as
a local authority in Belfast will do our
level best to carry out the scheme for
the benefit of the community.
It has been said that the Government
should bring forward a scheme and hand
it over to the local authorities to operate.
I want to tell the House that the Government have prepared a scheme, and
there is one I have in my hand prepared
by the Imperial authorities. You can go
and buy it at the Stationery Office for
2d. It is a complete scheme, and is
You
absolutely good.
can take that
scheme and fill it in to suit the locality,
and, in my opinion, you will have as
good a scheme as possible. It has been
said again that the local authorities have
not carried out the work. I admit they
have not. They did not appoint an experienced official in charge. They undertook to run it themselves. The first
thing they did was to hand out gas
masks to the children. I saw photographs in the Press of children running
up and down the streets amusing themselves. There was another case where a
woman put on a gas mask to peel onions.
All she said was that she was surprised
people did not find the mask out long
ago. In another case a boy put on a
gas mask and went to the gas stove and
turned it on. He paid for his experience
for he was found dead.
These gas masks deteriorate. With the
experience we have gained nobody would
dream of handing out these gas masks to
the people. You have got to store them
and look after them. We, in Belfast,
with Government approval-they pay 70
per cent.-bought a building in which to
store the masks. I think if this Bill just
stands as it is you will find it will work
out all right. It has been said by the
hon. and gallant Member that they
might drop bombs, say, on Carrickfergus.
Experience has shown that if they come
over to bomb a city or town they go
over that town and try, to hit a target.
The thing is nearly impossible. It altogether depends on the height and the
rate at which the machine is travelling.
If they liberate the bomb a fraction of
a second out they will not hit the target.
If the machine goes a bit to one side the
aim is missed. You cannot count on
hitting the target. They may come over
in formation and get over the city, say
Belfast. They simply drop bombs. What
happens? There is no such thing as
hitting a target. They might hit the
City Hall. They drop the bombs all at
once and you never know what is going
to happen.
As far as this Bill is concerned,
although I would much prefer the Government would take full responsibility
and pay for all, as things stand to-day
we gladly accept the Bill and will do our
best to protect the citizens from any
trouble.
I
417 Air Raid Precautions-

8 NOVEMBER 1938
Committee
a
Mr. LESLIE: The only remark I
would like to make is, I think it rather
mistake to deal with precautions
against air raids as though a raider made
itself unpleasant only in dropping bombs.
In case of mills you could scarcely hit
one with a bomb, but you can do a great
deal of damage by machine-gun fire.
is
Mr. ROBB: The Government
unable to accept this Amendment. As
a matter of fact there is not a very great
deal of difference between the two
Clauses, but in so far as it may be read
as having the meaning that it is the
duty of the Government to provide
schemes suitable to each different locality,
the Government is not prepared to do
that. What the Government is prepared
to do is to supply model schemes and to
discuss them with the local authorities,
but the local authorities are the people
who know the peculiarities and necessities
of their own area, and the Bill throws
upon them they will be given every
assistance in consultation with the Home
Office the duty of adopting a scheme
suitable to their conditions and carrying
it out. That is the principle upon which
they have gone in England. It is the
principle the Government has adopted
here and which the House approved on
the Second Reading of the Bill.
Accordingly, as this is the only possibility of difference between the Amendment and the Clause the Government is
not prepared to take the risk of anybody
thinking it is the Government should prepare these several schemes and not the
local authority. They cannot accept the
Amendment.
Major DOBBS: I put this Amendment
down in order to draw attention to the
matter. I would like to say something
in answer to the Lord Mayor of Belfast.
I do not quite understand what he meant
by mentioning Carrickfergus. I do not
think there is the slightest chance of any
bomber bombing any place in Northern
Ireland except Belfast, and as to a target
surely to goodness Belfast is the finest
target one could possibly have, because
any bomb dropping in Belfast must do a
tremendous amount of damage to life and
property. There is no reason why a
bomber should miss Belfast. It is true
that if they are coming over they may
be about 25,000 feet up, but why on
earth should they not be only 5,000 feet
In
up considering that we have no anti-aircraft defences or anything like that.
such circumstances you could make an
extraordinarily good hit at anything. I
think that the Lord Mayor and other
Senators are rather confusing the question. They say to take precautions
against gas and see that people who are
hurt shall be attended to. My chief
point is to see that precautions are taken
so that people will not be hurt. I beg
leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendments stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie : :-
At end of line (“made in their area ")
insert" or any part thereof."
After "areas" in sub-clause (3) insert
or any part thereof."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not propose to
move these Amendments. It is thought
they would not really help us.
Amendments accordingly not moved.
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: I beg to
move to insert after sub-clause (2):-
(3) It shall be the duty of the
Ministry to submit to local authorities
for their guidance model schemes varied
to suit the different circumstances and
different degrees of danger applying to
different districts.
I need say little in regard to this
Amendment. The matter has been explained by the Leader of the House on
the subject raised by the hon. and gallant
Senator. The only point I want to make
is as to what is obviously the real danger.
Targets for bombers vary enormously and
one model scheme would be unsuitable
for all cases. I have suggested that the
Ministry in their wisdom should draw up
three or four different model schemes to
choose from. The scheme which would
suit Belfast would be ridiculously excessive for rural parts. I should like to
assure the Lord Mayor that I am not concerned about Belfast, which is rich enough
to look after itself.
I am more concerned with the smaller urban areas
and country areas which are also
open to very considerable danger. You
may have noticed a report in the
London "Times' of a black-out in Hertfordshire. Patrolling bombers were flying
at a height of 12,000 feet on a bright,
moonlight night-a sort of night that
Air Raid Precautions-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Sir Roland Nugent]
It
only comes ten times in the year.
was stated that if it had not been for the
lights of the surrounding counties the
pilots would not have been able to identify
Hertfordshire. They frequently mistook
a village with a wood behind it and
bombed it. You see there is considerable
danger even in the case of rural areas. I
think the principle of the Amendment is
good.
Mr. ROBB: As I have stated, the
Ministry of Home Affairs has prepared
and is preparing and improving from day
to day as information comes in and guidance is given, model schemes to suit
different circumstances and different
degrees of danger applying to different
districts, but to put into an Act of
Parliament that it is to be the duty of
the Ministry to submit to the local
authorities for their guidance model
schemes to suit the different circumstances
and different degrees of danger applying
to different districts without, in some
way, defining who is to decide the
number of these model schemes-it seems
to me that is not going to help matters
very much. The sub-clause proposed to
be added to the Clause is so indefinite in
these regards that it does not seem to me
to add anything to the usefulness of the
Bill. As I have said, the Ministry will
do its best to assist the local authorities
and advise them. The local authorities
themselves are to decide what particular
measures are suitable for their particular
districts. On the whole, although the
Ministry recognise that they have a duty
which they intend to perform-to supply
model schemes-the Government would
prefer that it should not be so put into
the Bill in this form, which is too vague
to be of any real use and too indefinite
to help very much any person who has to
I do not propose to
turn up the Act.
accept the Amendment.
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: I would have
preferred to have had this Amendment
inserted in the Bill, and I think the right
hon. Gentleman has missed my point. I
did not lay down the number of schemes
that would be necessary, because I am
not an expert on air-raid precautions. The
Ministry of Home Affairs presumably are
and I left it to them. I think there is a
point that should be made, and I hope it
will be borne in mind by the Ministry.
It is that very often a local authority
will not be in a position to assess its own
value. The danger will depend on military considerations of a secret character,
and in this the Ministry, being a governing body, will have to lend a guiding
hand.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: I beg to
move to insert after sub-clause (2)—
(4) It shall be the duty of the
Ministry to provide a staff of technical
experts on air-raid precautions, whose
advice shall be at the disposal of local
authorities in the preparation and
execution of schemes.
The point in this Amendment is fairly
obvious. Few local authorities, not even
the richer ones, may be able to afford
the cost of experts. Sir Samuel Hoare, in
the House of Commons, said that a considerable number of months ago he had
urgently requested local authorities to
report on existing buildings and other
structures that might be suitable for air
raids. He had had no answers, whatever.
Sir Samuel Hoare added that the Home
Office had appointed a special expert staff
to carry out that investigation, thus
showing that it was clearly his opinion
and the opinion of the Government that
they had asked the local authorities to
do something which was beyond their
capacity. Many more examples might
be taken, but I do not want to weary the
Committee. I hope that the principle of
the Amendment will be accepted by the
Government.
Mr. ROBB: For practically the same
reasons I do not feel inclined to accept
this Amendment. The Ministry of Home
Affairs at the moment have a general airraid precautions inspector and a medical
inspector. The Ministry depends for the
larger part on the guidance of the
Imperial Home Office, and the Government have been assured of the cooperation and help of the Home Office in
London in all these matters. It is almost
certain there will be further appointments peculiar to this area in regard to
air-raid precautions, but what their
number or their specialities will be we do
not know. As I say, the Government have
been assured of the co-operation and
assistance at all times of the experts,
such as those of the architectural supplies
or fire brigade departments in the Home
Office. I may say the Ministry of Home
421 Air Raid Precautions--

8 NOVEMBER 1938
Committee
[Mr Robb]
Affairs have always found them most
ready and desirous of giving every
assistance in their power.
As I have intimated, a beginning has
been made with the appointment of a
staff, and should further appointments
seem to be necessary the Government will
not hesitate to make those appointments,
but at the same time the advice of those
technicians will be at the disposal of the
local authorities in the preparation and
execution of whatever schemes may
ultimately come to be adopted in this
area. I am not very enamoured of
putting this sub-clause in. It does not
define what the staff is to be or who is to
say of what members the staff is to be
composed. The Government prefer that
they should be left a free hand, such a
free hand as the Ministry of Finance will
allow in this matter. They much prefer
that this sub-clause should not be added
to the Bill.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: It
is very important that local authorities
should have technical experts nearer
than the Home Office in London. I do
not think the right hon. Gentleman,
the Leader of the House, went far
enough. I do not think the Government are going far enough. It
is no good taking the best expert
advice if it takes too long to get it. I do
not honestly believe that the local
authorities in this area,
or any other
area, have sufficient experts to deal
with high explosives. Nobody can be
satisfied that the English scheme has
worked well so far, and unless some new
scheme is developed by the Home Office
in England we have no guarantee that
it will work any better. I do not think
the Government here should rely entirely
on the advice received from the Home
Office, though naturally they will make
use of what advice they can get.
There is a very great feeling of dissatisfaction in England at the present
moment about the precautions that were
taken in the past, and I feel that the
Government should at any rate have some
technical expert over here who understands the business of protecting buildings from high explosive. I understand
that a great many of the casualties in
Barcelona were caused not by the bombs
themselves but through buildings falling
was
on shelters. It is one thing to protect
a large number of people from bombs,
but it is quite another thing to protect
them not only from bombs but from the
collapse of big buildings in their neighbourhood. I heard the other day that in
one place in England with a large
population, when it
desired to
protect a thickly populated area that
was done by digging a few trenches in
the Market Square. Anyone who knows
anything of high explosive knows that
one bomb would have destroyed them.
They would be in danger not so much
from falling bombs as from falling
buildings round the Market Square.
not.
accus-
That sort of thing ought to be avoided,
and a proper person to advise the local
authorities would be someone of the type
of a Royal Engineer, someone
tomed to dealing with fortifications, who
understands the type of shelter that is
safe from bombing and the type that is
Not only is the type, but the
position of the shelter important. Those
are things which no local authority in
England, or any other country, knows
anything about. There must be some
person appointed by the Government to
advise them on these technical matters,
someone with engineering knowledge. I
think the Government ought to consider
whether it would not be desirable to
have some person who can keep in touch
with the Home Office, but who would not
at the same time be depending on what
might emanate from that very efficient
body. I think that something more
ought to be done than the Government
have done up to the present.
Mr. LESLIE: The hon. and gallant
Senator has said very much what I
was going to say. I want to impress on
the Government the necessity for acting
at once, because if you wait until a war
is actually on, or is imminent, I do not
think there will be very many experts
on any subject connected with warfare
to spare for Northern Ireland. It is
surely incumbent upon the Government
to have a skeleton staff, at all events,
available now.
Mr. ROBB: The Government have
not neglected that.
Mr. LESLIE: Very good.
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: I am
afraid I must still press my Amendment.

THE SENATE

I
Major DOBBS: In support of what
the learned Senator said just now,
would like to say that I received a letter
from a gentleman who has taken a great
deal to do with A.R.P. work just outside
London. He ends his letter with these
words: "As a rule, I believe it has been
impossible to get answers from the Home
Office."
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: I attach
such importance to this Amendment that
I must press it.
Question put, "That these words be
there inserted.
""
The Committee divided: Contents, 4;
Not Contents, 7.

Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Approval,
amendment of air-raid
schemes).
effect and
precaution
I beg to
Sir ROLAND NUGENT:
move in Clause 2, after sub-clause (2), to
insert:-
("(3) If any local authority shall
fail to submit a scheme within two
months of the passing of this Act, or if
any local authority shall submit a
scheme which is not approved by the
Ministry in accordance with the provisions of this Act within two months
of the submission of such scheme to the
Ministry, the Ministry shall produce a
scheme for the area under the control
of such local authority, and such a
scheme on receiving the approval of the
Ministry of Finance shall be deemed to
be a scheme in force under this Act.")
I put this Amendment down on the
Paper in the hope that in the wisdom of
the Leader of the House and of the Committee a better way of doing this would
be produced on the Report Stage. It
has been the experience in England that
a certain number of local authorities for
one reason or another have not produced
a scheme or done anything else. In some
cases in England I am afraid it has been
a genuine lack of patriotism-political
things over-ride it. I do not think that
would happen here, but it is possible that
in an odd case a local authority might
be unable to put forward a scheme owing
to disagreement amongst its members as
to what kind of scheme should be
adopted. If it does put forward a scheme
that scheme may not be considered by the
Ministry to be sufficient, or, again, it may
refuse to put forward any scheme. These
are difficulties which I think would be
very rare. They have occurred in
England and have occasioned serious.
delay. If a war started it would mean
that a certain number of people in
England would have no form of protection. We do not want anything like that
to happen. I have, therefore, drafted
this Amendment, but I may say
I am
not in love with its exact form.
Mr. ROBB: I am entirely in
sympathy with the object the hon. and
gallant Senator had in moving this
Amendment, but I think it is unnecessary for this reason, that we have in this
area a power that does not exist in some
other areas. Where a local authority
refuses to do what it ought to do and
proves recalcitrant to the end, there is
power in this area to supersede it, and
should the necessity arise the Government will not be slow, or at any rate
too slow, to put that power into force.
In the first place I do not think this
Amendment is in suitable form, and, in
the second place, I do not think it is
really necessary in this area.
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: In view of
the right hon. Gentleman's explanation
I withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 3 (Duty of local authorities
to co-operate) and 4 (Acquisition of
land) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Air Raid Precautions-
Committee
8 NOVEMBER 1938
CLAUSE 5 (Duty of local authorities
to assist arrangements for evacuation of
civil population).
It shall be the duty of all local
authorities to furnish to the Ministry
such information as may be demanded
by it for the purpose of assisting the
preparation by His Majesty's Government of plans for any necessary transference of the civil population in the
event of hostile attack from the air.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in
Clause 5, to leave out from the word
"for" ("plans for") to the end of the
Clause, and to insert the words:
"the transference of the civil population in the event of such transference
being deemed advisable as a precaution
against the possible effects of hostile
attack from the air."
I think that this Amendment explains
itself. If the Committee will be so kind
as to read over the Clause——
Mr. ROBB: Perhaps I may intervene
to say that the Government will accept
this Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: Thank you.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 6 (Expenses of local
authorities), 7 (Exchequer grants), 8
(Expenses payable out of moneys provided by Parliament), 9 (Investigation of
working of financial provisions), and 10
(Regulations and orders by the Ministry) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 11 (Relief of air-raid protection works from rates).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the
end of the Clause to insert the following sub-clause :
"(6) In this section the expression
'purpose of affording air-raid protection' shall be construed as including
instruction and training in connection
with air-raid precaution
precaution and protection."
Mr. ROBB: The Government accept
this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 12 (Penalty for damaging
equipment, etc.).
The following Amendment stood on
the Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie. At
the end insert the following sub-clause:
"(2) If any person wilfully damages.
or causes or permits to be damaged any
premises, equipment, appliances or
material used or intended to be used
for the purposes of this Act and the
property of the Crown, any Ministry
of Northern Ireland or
any local
authority, such person shall be liable
on summary conviction to a fine not
exceeding fifty pounds, and in addition
to such fine to pay such sum as the
court by which he is tried thinks just,
for the purpose of making good any
such damage."
Mr. LESLIE: I must make a little
explanation here. I understand that the
principle is conceded, but it is thought
better, so to speak, to marry the new
sub-clause I propose to the sub-clause
already in the Bill. It is also thought
that the ceremony had better take place
on the Report stage, and, therefore, at
the moment I am not in a position to
do more than put up the banns. I will
not move the Amendment to-day; I will
move a new one on the Report stage.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 13 (Interpretation) and 14
(Short Title) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

Preamble and Title agreed to.

Ordered, That the House do now

House resumed accordingly.

Question to Minister-
Old Age Pensions
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 15th November, 1938.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Agriculture:
The Exported Animals (Compensation)
Acts (Northern Ireland), 1928 to 1938
(Suspension of Charges) Order, 1938.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act), and ordered to lie on the Table.
Question to Minister.
INCREASE IN OLD AGE PENSIONS.
Mr. BARCLAY: My reason for asking the question which stands on the
Order Paper in my name is this: As I
move about amongst the people I find
there is a great deal of poverty and hardship experienced by these old-age pensioners.
We, in this House, have passed
legislation for the purpose of helping
another section of the community, that
is, the farming section. Boards of all
kinds have been set up, prices have been
improved, with great benefit to those for
whom the Acts were passed. But there
has never been anything done for the consumer, and as the old-age pensioner is a
consumer he has to go to the same
market as any other person who has more
money coming in than he has. These
people tell me that 10s a week is not
sufficient to buy the necessaries of life,
such as food, clothing, and so on. No
matter how they try to spin it out they
cannot get it to cover all the things they
require.
There are three articles of food that
are very essential to the well-being of
the old-age pensioner, namely, milk,
butter, and eggs, and they cannot get
them owing to having no money to purchase them. Milk has gone up in price,
eggs have gone up, and butter has gone
up, and the result is they have to fall
back on something else-tinned milk for
instance. They have got to do without
eggs and use margarine for butter. I
think something should be done for these
old people, and I have not been unreasonable in what I ask. I have asked
for 5s increase in the pension. As you
are aware the pension is 10s a week, and
5s more would make it 15s. I believe
15s would enable them to live a kind of
way in comfort. I think it is our duty
to do something for the old-age pensioners, these men and women who have
borne the burden and heat of the day.
The men have brought up large families,
and during their lifetime of labour they
have produced goods that have been of
benefit to the State. I think those who
have given a lifetime of hard labour with
small remuneration, when they come to
the end of life, should have something
done for them so that they might live in
ease and comfort.
The second part of the question is
asking for 10s a week for the wives of
the contributory pensioners, both compulsory and voluntary. My reason for
doing so is this. I have come across
numbers of men who have been employed in certain industries and when
they come to be 65 years of age they
have to cease work and go on pension.
Some of these men have been earning
anything from 25s a week to £3, and
they have felt very much losing this
10s
wage and going upon
a week.
The wives of these men are generally
from two to five or six years younger,
and receive no pension until they are 65
years of age. So if it is possible to give
these women of 60, 10s a week, that
with the 15s would be 25s and they
could live very comfortably on that, and
when these women come to be 65 they
would be entitled to the full pension.
•
My reason for asking for the extra 5s
is that I read in the Press some time
ago that a deputation of old-age
pensioners waited on the Minister of
Labour in the Imperial Parliament. The
Minister gave them a sympathetic hearing and said that he would see what
could be done for them. He set up a
committee of inquiry, and this committee inquired into the cost of living and
reported that it had gone up to such
an extent that 10s a week was not
sufficient and they advised 5s more,
making it 15s. So I thought if the
Minister of Labour here in Northern
Ireland would get into communication
with the Minister of Labour across the
Bills Read Third Time 430
Education Bill
15 NOVEMBER 1938
water perhaps between them they might
be able to suggest some plan whereby
means could be found to finance this
extra 5s a week. Therefore I beg to ask
the Leader of the House whether the
Government will consider amending the
law so as to increase the old age pension
to fifteen shillings a week, and to secure
for the wife of a contributor, whether
compulsory or voluntary, a pension of
ten shillings a week at the age of sixty.
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House):
The Government has every sympathy
with the proposal to make increased provision by way of pensions for elderly
people, and when circumstances allow,
as I hope they will, for an increase in
the rate of pension or a lowering of the
qualifying age in the United Kingdom,
the Government of Northern Ireland
will, in accordance with its declared
policy, introduce legislation to ensure
that the rates of pension will be the
same in Northern Ireland as in Great
Britain.
For reasons which must be obvious to
the hon. Member, the Government cannot undertake to introduce legislation
on this subject in advance of corresponding legislation in Great Britain.
Education Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Mr. ROBB: I beg to move, "That
this Bill be now read the Third time.
Question proposed: "That the Bill be
now read the Third time."
Mr. LESLIE: We can only say this
Bill is a great experiment and we hope
it will be justified by its success, and it
may very well be that as the Bill and
the principles of the Bill advance towards
success they may attain a degree of
popularity which at present the proposals
in the Bill do not enjoy. However, a
great deal rests on the Ministry of
Education. When these regulations come
to be framed-and I may remind the
House it was admitted in Committee the
other day that the regulations would
have to be laid before Parliament-and
after the Minister has had the proposed
consultations with the education authorities it may very well be that some
apparent rough places will be smoothed
over, or, at all events, rendered considerably less rugged than they are at
present.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved, "That the Bill do now pass."
-(Mr. Robb.)
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any amendment.

Mr. ROBB: I beg to move, in Clause
4, to leave out the words, "The
acquisition of land for" in the first line
of sub-clause (2). This is purely a

Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 12 (Penalty for damaging
equipment, etc.).
If any person wilfully damages or
causes or permits to be damaged any
equipment, appliances
material
or
supplied to him or under his control for
the purposes of this Act, such person
shall be liable on summary conviction
to a fine not exceeding five pounds,
and, in addition to such fine, to pay
such sum as the court by which he is
tried thinks just for the purpose of
making good any such damage.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 12, to leave out from the word
"any" (any equipment) in line 2, and
to insert instead, "premises, equipment,
appliances or material used or intended
to be used for the purposes of this Act,
and being the property of the Crown or
of any Government department or local
authority or being in possession or under
the control of such person."
This is the revised form of the Amendment which was dealt with in Committee.
It will be remembered that we had a
Clause which it was thought did not go
far enough, and it was at first proposed
to insert another sub-clause. Then it
was thought that the two had better be
joined together and let one do the work
of two, and the result of that is now
before the House.
Mr. ROBB: As indicated on the
Committee stage, further consultation
has taken place between the hon. and
learned Member and the Department,
and the present Amendment is an agreed
one, and is, I think, a definite improvement on the original Clause.
Amendment agreed to.
in
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move,
Clause 12, to leave out the word "five"
(exceeding five pounds) in line 5, and to
insert instead the word "fifty." The
introduction of the new provision dealing with such large matters as premises,
equipment, and appliances belonging to
the Crown or of
Government
Department or local authority makes the
original maximum penalty of £5 somewhat out of date, and therefore it is
proposed to raise it to £50. But it is a
maximum penalty, and one can only
hope it will never be put in force at all,
and that if it is necessary for some comany
Air Raid Bill
Adjournment
15 NOVEMBER 1938
paratively trivial offence the court hearing the case will deal with the
matter as mercifully as possible. But it
is necessary, in my view, that in regard
to equipment intended for the high
purpose of national defence there should
be at the back a very strong penalty
for any misfeasance in connection with
those articles.
Mr. ROBB: The Government accept
this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

Question to Minister-
The Divorce Law
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 17th November, 1938.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Agriculture:
The Milk (Equalisation Payments)
Regulations (Northern Ireland), 1938,
No. 2.
Ministry of Finance:
Census of Population of Northern
Ireland, 1937. County of Tyrone.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Question to Minister.
THE DIVORCE LAW.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Captain
Perceval-Maxwell:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he can state when the Government intend introducing legislation
transferring the business of Divorce to
the Courts.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL:
The question which I have on the Paper
to-day has been put not to embarrass
or hurry the Government in any way,
but to allay certain public anxiety which
has been manifested to me in correspondence and verbally. I fully realise
the great difficulties which the Government have to meet in reforming the law
of divorce in this country. One of the
most difficult questions which they have
to deal with is the absence of a King's
Proctor. At present the Lord Chief
Justice has to ask petitioners whether
there is any collusion, and unfortunately, in the absence of a King's
Proctor, he is unable to take the steps
that would be taken in England to check
the answer. I agree that it will take
time and consideration before the Government are in a position to do anything
about this matter, but I hope that my
question will receive the answer that the
Government intend to take the necessary steps as soon as they can, and that
the matter has not been lost sight of.
a
One case has come to my notice where
eight years elapsed between the date on
which a judicial separation was granted
and the date on which the divorce was
granted, and that eight years lapse was
due to inability to find the necessary
finance. That sort of thing caused
great deal of disturbance in the minds of
the public. All I would like the Government to do at the moment, having
regard to the fact that the Session is
coming to an end and a new Session is
about to commence, is to give some
assurance that will have more weight
than any assurance I can give that the
matter has not been lost sight of, and
will be dealt with as soon as it can be.
at
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House):
The Government has the matter
present under consideration with a view
to the early introduction of legislation.
on the subject. The hon. and learned
Senator has pointed out that it is not
just a question of whether the same procedure shall be enacted here as is in
force in England. There are administrative difficulties, and the hon. and
learned Member has pointed out one,
namely, the absence in this country of
a King's Proctor. There are various
views about that. He suggests we
should appoint in this country an official
who will have the same duties. There
is an alternative, and it is necessary that
the advantages of each system should be
carefully weighed. The alternative is
that there should not be in our legislation a decree nisi at all, but that at
the trial a final decree should be pronounced. This is only one of a great
many difficulties that have arisen. As
I said in my formal answer, the matter
is at present under consideration with
a view to the early introduction of
legislation.

Royal Assent.
Mr. LONG: I have to report to the
House that His Grace the Governor of
Northern Ireland, in the name and on
behalf of His Majesty the King, has been
pleased to give his Assent to the following
Bills agreed upon by both Houses:-
Finance,
Expiring Laws,
University and Collegiate
Scientific Institutions,
:-
and
National Health Insurance (Amendment) (No. 2),
Education,
Queen's University of Belfast,
Appropriation (No. 2),
Factories,
PROROGATION OF PARLIAMENT.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have it in command
from his Grace the Governor to read
forthwith in this Chamber to the Senators
and Commons of Northern Ireland therein
assembled his Grace's Proclamation
proroguing this present Session of Parliament.
Resolved: "That the Gentleman
Usher of the Black Rod be commanded to inform the Commons that the
Proclamation proroguing the present
Session of Parliament is to be read
forthwith, and inviting the Commons to
attend in order to hear the same.”—(Mr.
Long.)
The Commons, being at the Bar, with
their Speaker, the Proclamation was read
to both Houses of Parliament by the
Speaker of the Senate (in pursuance of
his Grace's command), as followeth :-
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR OF
NORTHERN IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION
For Proroguing the present Parliament
of Northern Ireland.
WHEREAS it is provided by section
eleven of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, as modified by and in pursuance of
certain enactments amending the said Act,
that the Governor of Northern Ireland
shall, in his Majesty's name, summon,
prorogue and dissolve the Parliament of
Northern Ireland.
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that
this present Parliament of Northern
Ireland should be prorogued to the sixth
day of December, one thousand nine
hundred and thirty-eight:
NOW I, JAMES ALBERT EDWARD,
DUKE OF ABERCORN, KNIGHT OF
THE MOST NOBLE ORDER OF THE
GARTER, KNIGHT OF THE MOST
ILLUSTRIOUS ORDER OF SAINT
PATRICK, Governor of Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation do publish
Prorogation
End of Session
24 NOVEMBER 1938
and declare his Majesty's Pleasure that
the said Parliament be, as from the reading of this Proclamation in the Parliament
of Northern Ireland, prorogued to
Tuesday, the sixth day of December, one
thousand nine hundred and thirty-eight.
AND I do hereby further declare his
Majesty's Royal Will and Pleasure to be
that the said Parliament shall on the said
sixth day of December, one thousand
nine hundred and thirty-eight, assemble
and be holden at Stormont for the
despatch of divers urgent and important
affairs, and the Senators and Members of
the House of Commons of Northern
Ireland are hereby required to give their
attendance accordingly.
Given this Twenty-fourth day of
November, one thousand nine hundred
and thirty-eight.
(Signed by his own hand),
ABERCORN,
KING.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
End of the First Session (opened

Tuesday, 1st March, 1938) of the Fifth
Parliament of Northern Ireland, in the
second year of the Reign of His Majesty
King George the Sixth.

Tuesday, 6th December, 1938.
The Second Session of the Fifth Parliament of Northern Ireland, appointed to
meet on 6th December, 1938, in the
second year of the reign of King
George VI, was opened by the Governor
(His Grace the Duke of Abercorn, K.G.,
K.P.) in person.
The House met at Twelve noon of the
Clock, Mr. SPEAKER (The Right Hon.
Viscount Bangor, O.B.E., D.L.) in the
Chair.
KING'S SPEECH.
His Grace the Governor, being seated
on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black Road to let the
Commons know "It is his Grace's
pleasure that they attend him immediately in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with
their Speaker, his Grace delivered the
following Speech from the Throne:
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
It is a source of general satisfaction
that on the occasion of the inauguration of the Second Session of this
i
present Parliament the international
position has considerably improved.
The critical situation, which has
happily passed, called forth from our
people a manifestation of their constant readiness to respond to any call
which may be made on them to assist
in the defence of the Realm.
It is a matter of profound regret to
my Ministers that, owing to the unsettled conditions in many countries,
unemployment remains at a high level.
It is, however, gratifying that the
number of persons unemployed in
Northern Ireland has steadily declined
since July. My Ministers are hopeful
that the present downward trend in
the unemployment rate will be maintained, although, unfortunately, the
disturbing factors in world affairs may
prevent any substantial improvement
in the immediate future.
I trust, however, that the resumption of overseas purchases of our
manufactures on a normal scale, particularly in the United States of
America, will not be long postponed
and that, in view of the satisfactory
Trade Agreement concluded between
the Government of that country and
His Majesty's Government in the
United Kingdom, the position of our
linen industry will thereby be
improved.
A
King's Speech
King's Speech
THE SENATE
I am gratified that the stimulus
provided by the increase in the rate of
Unemployment Relief Grants has
resulted in the promotion of a large
number of schemes of public utility
by local authorities. Since the 1st
January last, fifty schemes, involving
a total expenditure of approximately
five hundred and fifty thousand
pounds have been sanctioned, as have
also a large number of agricultural
improvement schemes which have from
their inception provided spells of employment for more than seven thousand
unemployed farm labourers. I am
pleased to learn that the Government
is carrying out works of public utility
such as new and improved police
barracks, employment exchanges, and
new Post Offices, which are providing
substantial employment to the building and allied industries.
I note with satisfaction that the
facilities provided for the physical
training of unemployed persons have
met with a gratifying response on the
part of the unemployed, and have also
obtained a large measure of public
approval. I am gratified, too, that the
opportunities of training afforded by
the Government Training Centres in
Great Britain, and in the case of those
wishing to enter the British Merchant
Service by the Gravesend Sea School,
continue to be appreciated by young
men in Northern Ireland, and that
those who complete the courses of
training are highly successful in
obtaining employment.
The Measure sanctioned last Session
for the encouragement of physical
training is now in full operation, and
local authorities and voluntary organisations may therefore qualify for substantial financial assistance towards
the promotion of schemes designed to
provide new or increased facilities for
physical training and recreation. I
feel confident that full advantage will
be taken of the opportunity afforded
to the appropriate promoting bodies of
making so important a contribution to
the physical well-being and general
happiness of our people.
Noteworthy legislation of the past
Session in connection with our social
services included the passing of the
Factories Act and the Holidays with
Pay Act; two Measures which will
have far-reaching, and, I trust, beneficial effects on the lives of our industrial workers. The Factories Act will
favourably influence their health,
safety and general welfare conditions in
a marked degree, and will preserve the
uniformity of the Factory Code
throughout the United Kingdom.
The statutory means now provided for
enabling a week's paid holiday to be
given annually to employees in industries covered by Trade Boards will be
much appreciated, and no doubt will
encourage the extension of the movement to establish such schemes by
voluntary agreement in industry
generally which has become a prominent feature in recent times.
The Special Voluntary Pensions
Scheme enacted last year for the
benefit of persons of restricted means
who are outside compulsory insurance
has met with a considerable response.
About five thousand five hundred
persons have availed themselves of the
special opportunity to join the Scheme
at the minimum rates of contributions
during the first year of its operation.
This special opportunity expires on
the 2nd January, 1939, and it is the
earnest hope of my Government that
all who are qualified to participate in
the Scheme will take steps to do so
at once.
The situation which has arisen in
regard to the operation of the Road
and Railway Transport Act, 1935, has
been causing much concern to my
Government. This matter has been
the subject of two separate and exhaustive inquiries and the very important issues dealt with in the
valuable and informative reports will
be considered with a view to the formulation of proposals to deal with the
situation, and these will be embodied
in a Measure to be introduced at an
early date.
My Government proposes to set up
immediately a Committee to advise
when the next general revaluation
should take place and whether the
machinery of valuation could be
amended and the cost reduced. In
the meantime legislative proposals will
be laid before you with the object of
postponing the revaluation due to be
made in the year 1941.
King's Speech
Papers Presented
6 DECEMBER, 1938
You will be invited to consider proposals for the assimilation of the law
in Northern Ireland in regard to
Divorce to that
obtaining
now
in
England.
Among the Measures which will be
introduced will be Bills to deal with
the protection and welfare of young
people and the treatment of young
offenders; to amend the Unemployment Insurance Acts in order to follow
on the same lines as proposed by the
Imperial Government; and to amend
the law in connection with the superannuation of Local Government
Officers.
submitted
Proposals will also be
dealing with the pig and bacon industry and providing for the establishment of an Agricultural Wages
Board in Northern Ireland.
In order that full opportunity might
be afforded for consultation with the
local authorities, it was not considered
advisable to proceed with the Housing
Bill in the last Session, but now that
its terms have been widely circulated
this Measure will again be brought
forward for your consideration.
Members of the House of Commons.
The Estimates for the Public Services will be laid before you in due.
course.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I am gratified to learn that the steps
taken by the Government to attract
new, and to promote the extension of
existing industries are providing a
stimulus to industrial enterprise in
Northern Ireland.
I am pleased to observe the continued activity of the local education
authorities in the construction of new
school buildings, which has shown no
relaxation during the past year. The
new works are not confined to
elementary schools alone; much has
been done to improve the buildings
used for secondary and technical
education. The passing of the
Education Act in the last Session for
the raising of the school-leaving age
to fifteen is confidently expected to
lead to valuable results in the further
education of the young.
I earnestly trust that the appointment of an Agent in Great Britain will
serve to further Northern Ireland's
interests in the commercial and industrial fields and so ensure the continuance of that participation in the
reciprocal trade between the various
centres of the United Kingdom which
Northern Ireland has always enjoyed
through its happy position as a full
partner in one economic and fiscal
entity.
I pray that the blessing of Almighty
God may rest upon your deliberations.
Then His Grace was pleased to retire,
and the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
Papers Presented to Parliament.

1937-1938.
Ministry of Agriculture:
The Conditions of Employment and
Wages
in
of Agricultural Workers

Agriculture.

appointed under Rule 2 of the Schedule
appended to Council Order, Northern
Ireland, 20th January, 1938.
Census of Population of Northern Ireland, 1937. County and County Borough
of Londonderry.

Borough of Larne, Co. Antrim. Copy
of Letters Patent granting Charter of

1938.
