King's Speech-
Address in Reply to
THE SENATE
Address in Reply to His Majesty's
Most Gracious Speech.
Mr. SPEAKER having read the
King's Speech,
Major DOBBS (in the uniform of a
Deputy-Lieutenant): I beg to move,
That an humble Address be presented to
His Grace in the following words:
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's dutiful and loyal
subjects, the Senate of the Parliament
of Northern Ireland, beg leave to offer
our humble thanks to your Grace for
the Speech which your Grace has
addressed to both Houses of Parliament.
In rising to move this humble Address
of thanks for the Gracious Speech from
the Throne may I be allowed to say how
greatly I esteem this privilege, and that
I consider a great compliment has been
paid to me, and through me to the people
of County Antrim. It is a matter of the
deepest and most heartfelt satisfaction to
everyone of us here to-day that the very
critical situation in international affairs
has for the time being passed away, and
that a sense of stability and security is
being experienced by us through the
effective Measures now being put more
energetically into force to supply His
Majesty's forces with the very latest and
most up-to-date weapons-weapons of
defence and offence. I say this in no
grandiose spirit, but I would remind you
that we have great responsibilities, both
at home and abroad, and those responsibilities we must shoulder, and we must be
capable, too, of shouldering them
effectively.
The large amount of unemployment is
a matter which we cannot regard but
with the greatest concern. It is difficult
to see in what way the Government can
further ameliorate the position. The
employment of extra agricultural
labourers, made possible by purely agricultural improvement schemes which the
policy of financial help by the Government has made possible, is certainly
satisfactory. Again, no one will grudge
the money spent on works of public
utility mentioned in the Gracious Speech.
But these are but temporary Measures,
even though they may be spread over a
few years. However, we all, I am sure,
do feel that this problem has been, and
is being considered by our Government
of Northern Ireland with the closest
attention, and I myself do honestly feel
that the work they have already carried
out in fostering and helping the various
branches of agriculture and industry in
general, and the attention they have paid.
to the amelioration of the conditions of
the working classes augurs well for the
future, and that the cumulative effort
will in time gradually and in increasing
force make this Province one of the most
prosperous parts of our Empire.
I do not think that we should minimise
the value that the unemployed are obtaining from the facilities provided for educational and physical training. In helping
them we are helping ourselves. One
association has held classes in electric
welding, motor repairing, etc., and has
had camps in various parts of the
country. And here I want to make a
special appeal. The Government are
giving financial support, but it is limited
to new ventures. I appeal to them to
consider each most sympathetically. Do
not tie everything up with red tape. I
do not think they will, but a junior civil
servant might have to do so. Especially
do I ask them to be most sympathetic
and most broad-minded in regard to any
request from any well-founded organisation. I express also a hope that the
opportunities afforded by Government.
training centres in Great Britain and by
the Gravesend Sea School will be more
extensively advertised.
With all due humility, I would like to
amplify what is said in the Gracious
Speech upon the satisfactory trade agreement with the United States of America.
I was one of the many, who, when
negotiations were just begun, thought
that technical difficulties would prevent
anything in the nature of an important
and advantageous agreement being signed.
Consequently, I am agreeably surprised
and greatly pleased that a real and
valuable trade agreement has been
signed. This agreement, regarding it
from a narrow and selfish standpoint,
should improve the linen industry here.
But taking the large, the Imperial view,
this important agreement-and here I
would like to point out, that we Owe
much of its success to India and to the
Dominions, especially to Canada, in that
they have agreed to many modifications
of the Ottawa agreements-this im-
King's Speech
Address in Reply to
6 DECEMBER, 1938
portant agreement goes far beyond a
mere presumptive increase in trade. It
seems to me to show evidence of a
common outlook between the United
States of America and the British
Empire, and to be the drawing together
of the great English-speaking nations
into closer touch with one another, a boon
of the greatest magnitude.
And now a discordant note, the Transport Act. It is so easy to criticise comfortably after the event, but we must
realise what the situation was a few
years ago. My own view is that the
transport of the Six Counties would have
very soon completely broken down. The
whole situation was, at any rate, becoming deplorable. What was done after
the passing of the Act was a genuine
and a great effort to solve the transport
problem, and to put the Province's transport on an immediately satisfactory working basis. I think it was strange that the
Board did not learn to walk before they
tried to run. And why did they not
tackle first the passenger traffic problem,
and then in good time the goods traffic?
But there it is. We must hope that it
will be found possible to weld the present
issues into a homogeneous working
arrangement that will be of real benefit
to the business and travelling communities. It is a very difficult
problem, but, if tackled firmly and
resolutely, the result may yet be a credit
to Ulster and an example to other
countries.
With regard to legislation relating to
divorce, I am an old-fashioned person,
holding old-fashioned views, and I would
not like to see the divorce laws as now
constituted in England applying in their
entirety to this Province. But I do feel
that our divorce legislation does require
to be improved. This House did me the
honour of putting me on
the Joint
Standing Committee on Divorce, and
from what I have learnt on that Committee I do unhesitatingly say that legislation must be passed for the sake of
the innocent party. I have great hopes
that hon. Senators opposite will help to
make that legislation a real credit to the
Statute Book, because I am sure their
help, looking as they do at divorce from
a different angle to Members on this side
of the House, will be of real assistance
to the Government.
I am sure that everyone in this House
wishes every success to our agent in
England. I am sure, in these days, that
it is a very necessary post. Years ago
it was said in London that Messrs. Lipton
spent normally £100,000 each year on
advertising, but that when Sir Thomas
challenged for the America Cup that not
one penny that year was spent on advertising. I expect it was a complete
canard, but the idea was sound.
It pays
to advertise-advertise in the newspapers
or in other ways. We do not want our
agent to set the Thames on fire, but we
do want him to be a live wire, and to
keep Ulster before the business public's
Incidentally, I think we have the
eye.
right man.
Last session we discussed A.R.P.
under great difficulty, and I think
we must allow that views expressed
were generally chaotic. Since then the
Royal Society of Ulster Architects, under
the auspices of the Ministry of Home
Affairs, has held one public meeting and
two private technical meetings on structural air-raid precautions. Two experts
from London lectured at these meetings.
Those lectures, at which I had the good
fortune to be present, were characterised
by level-headed thinking and sound
commonsense. The lecturers spoke with
authority. They spoke from actual experience of what had been done in Spain,
and from experiments carried out by
ourselves, and they showed that for a
practically small expenditure the safety
from casualties of those living in the
poorer parts of Belfast could be increased
enormously. If these structural alterations, under our Ulster architects, were
carried out in Belfast, then our agent
in London, when talking to prospective
factory owners, could say: "Northern
Ireland is favourably situated with regard
to bombing attacks, and she is making
the working people of Belfast practically
safe from air-raid casualties. Would it
not be in your own interest to build your
factory over there, rather than
Slough or some other place near London."
And, remember, you are making the
people safe, not for to-day, or to-morrow,
but for many years. Make Belfast safe
for twenty years now.
near
May I be allowed respectfully to voice.
the opinion of this House and say how
very glad we are to learn that their Royal
Highnesses the Duke and Duchess of
King's Speech-
Address in Reply to
THE SENATE
[Major Dobbs]
Kent will visit Belfast in March. It is
not necessary for me to say how very
pleased everyone of us will be to have
their Royal Highnesses in our midst, and
may I express a hope that will be echoed
through the length and breadth of Ulster
that having arrived on our shoes they
will do us the honour of paying us a
lengthened visit, and, as far as possible
as the time at their disposal allows, travel
all over this loyal Province.
The Gracious Speech started with the
crisis. How nearly that crisis came to
becoming the most terrible and ghastly
war: a war that must have meant great
torment and misery to countless men,
Women and children, seems hardly
credible to us, even after these few weeks.
But when we do realise this awful contingency we determine that we must do
all in our power, everyone of us, to put
the possibility of such a crisis further and
further away from becoming reality.
Wherever we live in Northern Ireland,
wherever we go within her boundaries, on
our Sunday walks, on our daily occasions,
we cannot but feel what a wonderful,
what a lovely country this is of ours. And
so I say to this Government, and to the
Imperial Government, give us the hardest
tasks; command us as you will to enable
us to live in peace with honour. We are
prepared to fight for this country of ours;
if necessary die for her. Surely then we
are prepared to work for her.
Mr. TAYLOR (in morning dress): As
the most recently elected member of this
Chamber, I should feel quite unworthy
if I did not express my sincere thanks for
the signal honour bestowed on me in asking me to second the Motion for the
Address, so ably proposed by the hon.
and gallant Senator (Major A. F.
Dobbs). But I do not regard the compliment altogether in a personal sense. I
recognise that it is an honour done to the
working classes whom I with my two
colleagues have the privilege to represent. With the remarks made by Senator
Dobbs I entirely agree. We are loyal
subjects of His Majesty the King, and
have a passionate desire to do all we can
for the social uplift of the people.
It is very gratifying to know that the
number of unemployed persons has
steadily declined since July, and that the
Government is hopeful that the present
downward trend in the unemployment
rate will be continued. The satisfactory
trade agreement with the United States of
America should give a stimulus to the
linen industry, and, in consequence, many
textile workers who have been badly hit
by the depression will find welcome work.
We trust that an improvement will take
place in other industries.
We are pleased that the Measure to
encourage physical training among the
youth of the country is now in full operation. The time has long since gone by
when people, especially parents, thought
that no lessons were to be learnt other
than those of the classroom. To-day we
recognise that besides improving a boy's
physical development and health, the
playing-fields teach him many valuable.
things that cannot be learnt from books
alone. Games teach boys to be manly, to
keep a stiff upper lip when defeated, not
to be too much puffed up with success,
and, above all, to keep on trying, whether
at work or at play.
Then there is the case of old-age pensions, and I hope the Leader of the House
will tell us that the Government is doing
everything possible to have old-age pensions increased. It is sad to think of the
number of old-age pensioners, who are
compelled to apply to the poor-law
authorities for poor-law relief; while
many have actually to seek the hospitality of the workhouse, and become a
charge on the rates. That should not be
So. It should not be forgotten that those
old people did not share in the improved
conditions of the working classes which
exist to-day. They had not the benefits
of our modern social services, holidays
with pay, et cetera. Many of them commenced their industrial life at the early
age of 10 or 12 years, with a working week
of from 56 to 70 hours and upwards. The
result is that when they become eligible
for the contributory pension they are
physically wrought out. To expect those
old people to maintain themselves in
decency on the meagre allowance of 10s
per week is an utter impossibility. Something better should be done for them.
The Government is to be congratulated
on the introduction of the special voluntary Pensions Act. This brings within.
the scope of State insurance what has been
called the black-coated profession. We
all know that those belonging to that
class find it more difficult under their
circumstances to live than the ordinary
King's Speech-
Address in Reply to
6 DECEMBER, 1938
artisan, and I am confident that large
numbers of them will avail themselves of
the privileges and facilities offered by the
Act.
The Gracious Speech refers to the
Housing Bill which was introduced last
Session. This Measure will be again
brought forward this Session, and I hope
when it comes up the Government will
give sympathetic consideration to the
lower-paid working man, who, in many
cases, has to pay from 9s 6d to 12s 6d
weekly rent, approximately one-third of
his earnings. That leaves him with a
balance totally inadequate to maintain
his wife and family in any degree of
decency; indeed they are living or existing on the border line of starvation.
Other important subjects, such as rail
and road transport, will come before
Parliament for discussion, and I have no
doubt they will all receive careful and
serious consideration. We know that we
can rely upon the Government doing its
best in the interest of the whole community.
I am sure we are delighted that the
Prime Minister has been restored to
health and strength, and it is our earnest
hope that he may be long spared to the
Province for which he has done so much.
Stirring days may lie ahead, and we
need leaders with broad vision, stout
hearts, and resolute courage. We have,
however, faced many difficulties in the
past, difficulties which were regarded as
insurmountable, but with unity and
determination we overcame them, and we
can do the same again.
We have no reason to be despondent or
downhearted. If we are only true to
ourselves and to our trust, and co-operate
and work hard we can make Ulster, of
which we are all so proud, the most peaceable, prosperous and contented part of
the British Empire. I beg to second the
Motion.
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House): I
should like, first of all, to pay tribute to
the hon. Members who have moved and
seconded the Motion for the Address now
before the House. The hon. and gallant
Member, who moved the Motion, made a
lucid and interesting speech, such as we
would expect from him, and while the
seconder is a new-comer to this House I
think I can say that the speech he has
made gives promise that his contributions
to our deliberations in the future will be
both lucid and helpful.
The opening of the Gracious Speech
reminds us of the gravity of the international situation in September last, and
no responsible person can be other than
profoundly thankful that the anxiety of
those days has been relieved, and that we
have been spared, especially at Christmas.
time, the threat of war. The Speech
refers to the unanimity of the response of
the people of the British Isles generally,
and this area especially, to the calls that
were made upon them at that time, and I
think we can all agree with the last
speaker that whatever calls, great or
small, may be made in the future our
people will ever be ready to respond.
Next comes a reference to those knotty
problems of bad trade, and consequent
unemployment which are ever with us,
and possibly always will remain to be
grappled with. The Gracious Speech
makes mention of some of the ways in
which the Government have attacked
these problems. The direct assistance
implied by the sanction over a period of
less than a year of schemes assisted by
unemployment relief grants, upon which
expenditure to the magnitude of £550,000
will be incurred, is proof of the determination with which the Government
have acted. In addition, agricultural
improvement schemes and the provision
of new public buildings have also given
a large amount of employment.
I might mention that under the New
Industries (Development) Act, 1937,
there have been 68 formal applications for
assistance. Of these, 31 have already
been approved, and in 25 cases the applicants have accepted the form in which the
assistance was offered. It is estimated
that when the assisted firms are in operation they will provide employment for
2,200 persons. In addition, seven firms
have received assistance in the form of
site grants under the Act of 1932, and
these firms are at the present moment
giving employment to 5,270 persons.
Those are definite results, and, I think,
not too bad results of the New Industries
Development Acts in our area.
Reference has been made, both in the
Gracious Speech itself and in the speech
of the hon. and gallant Member, to the
establishment of the Ulster Office in
King's Speech-
Address in Reply to
THE SENATE
[Mr. Robb]
London. We all wish General Cooke-
Collis every success in the efforts that he
and his staff are making to advertise our
area and to attract new business to our
firms. May I say that it is the hope
of the Government that every Ulster
person going to London will go and see
this new Ulster Office. The last time I
was in London I took the opportunity
to visit the office. At that time there
was a little bit of confusion, as the Ulster
Agent had moved in only the day before,
but the office is admirably situated, and
is eminently calculated to fulfil the purposes the Government have in view.
In my
It is clear, however, that Northern
Ireland cannot solve its unemployment
problem in the face of trade restrictions
and the constant interruption of normal
trading by extensive warlike activity, and
by preparations for war amongst nations
who are her customers, and the customers
of Great Britain, her largest market. The
trade agreement between the United
Kingdom and United States of America
should be of marked assistance to the
linen industry, and will to some extent
offset the adverse influence of disturbed
conditions in other markets.
conversations with persons actually engaged in the linen trade I am glad to
have found that they were gratified at
the prospect that this agreement opened
up to them. Of course, one cannot definitely predict the future, but the effect
of the agreement on our linen trade may
be gauged from the fact that the reduction of duty on linens, based on the 1937
linen exports from Northern Ireland,
would be $1,619,000, or about 39 per cent.
of the estimated amount of duty paid in
that year.
This is equivalent to something in the neighbourhood of an all-round
reduction of 14 per cent. ad valorem.
The prospect of such a difference in the
cost of exported goods to the United
States must bring home to all those
engaged in the linen trade that the
United States has ever been the greatest
market for their produce. Of course, in
addition to the linen industry, textile
machinery, manufactures of tobacco, rye
grass seed, and woollen goods benefit, and
all these are exported in quantity from
this area to the United States.
We may be grateful that in spite of the
disturbed conditions we find that our unemployment condition has definitely
improved. In July of this year there
were 102,000 insured workers unemployed. In November the total had
fallen to 85,000. That is a great drop
in a comparatively short time, but it is
still a very heavy burden for this country
to carry.
After all, it is encouraging to
find that in four months there has been
a drop of something like 17,000 persons.
The plight of the unemployed has always
been weighing heavily upon the heart of
every well-intentioned person, and not
least on the hearts of the Government. A
small but interesting effort is mentioned.
in the Gracious Speech, the facilities for
physical training offered to unemployed
workers by the Ministry of Labour. The
opportunity was eagerly availed of. The
first courses were started on the 1st May
this year in Londonderry, where 606
young men have enjoyed them, and the
Belfast classes, which started in
September, have had an enrolment of
259. The Government realise that if
these classes were to have the hoped for
effect of brightening the lives of those
taking part in them and keeping them fit
to take work as it became available, more
than exercise was necessary, and milk and
biscuits were provided for those taking
part at these centres. I am glad to say
-as Senator Dobbs has mentioned-that
the scheme whereby persons from Northern Ireland receive training at the
Government training centres in Great
Britain has been markedly successful.
Since July, 1937, 321 unemployed persons
from this area have attended these centres
-of these 119 have been placed in
employment under the scheme; 10 have
completed the course, and have not yet
been placed in employment, and 100 are
at present in training. The position in
regard to the boys who have been trained
by the Gravesend Sea School to enter the
British Merchant Service is also very
satisfactory. 129 boys have gone from
Northern Ireland to the school since
June, 1937; 84 of these have been placed
in employment; two have completed
training and are awaiting employment;
and 15 are at present in training.
The voluntary pensions scheme was
provided to benefit a rather different and
much smaller social class. Nevertheless,
the scheme is of great interest, and should
be a boon to thousands of people with
small means. The Government desires
that the benefits to be derived from enter-
King's Speech-
Address in Reply to
6 DECEMBER, 1938
ing the scheme shall be widely understood and availed of, and I hope that the
publicity which the reference in the
Gracious Speech gives to the opportunity
of joining the scheme in its first years by
the payment of a minimum rate of contribution will bring many more persons
into it. The time in which this opportunity can be availed of expires in
January next, and it is hoped all those
who can will avail themselves of the low
rate.
In regard to the Physical Training and
Recreation Act I am glad to be able to
state that the working of the Act is proceeding smoothly and that the interest
evinced in this recent Measure is encouraging. I would, however, like again to
direct the attention of local authorities
to the financial assistance now available
from State resources towards the cost of
promotion by them of suitable schemes
for the provision of new or increased
facilities for physical training and recreation.
say,
May I
in answer to what Major
Dobbs suggested, that the schemes by
voluntary associations which come within
the Act in the different local authorities
will receive most sympathetic consideration from the Grants Committee. The
amount of money is not, of course, very
large, but it ought to be sufficient to
stimulate in this small area further provision, and that quickly, of these facilities
for developing the physical health and
incidentally, of course, the efficiency and
mental health of our younger people.
As to future legislation, the divorce
law of Northern Ireland has long been
anomalous, and, although it would seem
to be impossible to frame divorce legislation acceptable to everyone, I think
the great majority of persons in Northern
Ireland will agree that existing law in
Great Britain is more in accordance with
their desires than the law as it is in
Northern Ireland. The proposals of the
Government are not finally formulated,
and if the hon. and gallant Member or
anyone else has any suggestions to make
with regard to the form of our divorce
laws and brings them here the Government will give most earnest consideration
-I do not promise favourable consideration-but earnest consideration to any
suggestion made at any time from any
quarter.
Hon. Members will welcome the
announcement made in the Gracious
Speech that a Measure will be introduced
this Session dealing with the protection
and welfare of young people and the
treatment of young offenders. These are
matters of the greatest importance to the
State and to all of us. May I add a
personal view that the Education Act,
which was passed last Session, also ranks
as a Measure of first importance in the
protection of young people. The extra
year will do something to prevent delinquency among the older children. The
extra year at school, with the alternative
of careful selection of employment for
children entering the difficult years of
adolescence, should go far to provide the
steadying influence, and the greater
knowledge, which are essential bulwarks
against bad habits.
I need not refer to the Housing Bill
which it is proposed to reintroduce. The
terms have been before the House. They
have been the subject of a great deal of
discussion in various quarters. It is
hoped that when the Bill eventually leaves
Parliament as a Measure it will do what
it was intended to do, alleviate a great
deal of the evil of bad housing and overcrowding. Legislation is also to be introduced under which an agricultural
wages board is to be established.
Machinery for fixing agricultural wages is
in existence in other parts of the United
Kingdom. The question whether such a
board is desirable in Northern Ireland
has been the subject of very careful
inquiry, and on the basis of the report
from that inquiry the proposals will be
made. With regard to agriculture, proposals with regard to the pigs and bacon
industry on similar lines to those enacted
in England will be laid before the House
in the coming session.
Hon. Members will have noticed the
reference to revaluation. As the Gracious
Speech states, the Government propose to set up immediately a committee
to go into this question to consider
whether our revaluation machinery could
not be improved, and especially whether
it could not be cheapened. What the
expert advice of this committee will eventually be I have, of course, no means of
knowing, but the Government hope that
on some lines suggested by them it may
be found possible to get over some of the
Bill Presented
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Mr. Robb]
difficulties of revaluation, and especially
to cheapen the cost of it. In order that.
that might have time to fructify a
Measure will be laid before Parliament
proposing to postpone the revaluation due
to take place in 1941 to a later date.
What that date will be I am not quite
certain at the moment. At any rate,
there is no use in going on with revaluation on the old lines, if we are contemplating a change of system, large or small,
at any rate until the committee has reported. The Government propose
to
postpone the valuation due to be made
in the year 1941.
These are the matters dealt with in the
King's Speech, and I hope the House will
readily agree with me in congratulating
the mover and seconder, and in agreeing
to the Address.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move,
this debate be now adjourned."
On Question, Motion agreed to.

on Thursday next.
BILL PRESENTED.
Hairdressers Bill,
"to provide for the registration of premises in which the trade or business of
barber or hairdresser is carried on and
to enable local authorities to make bylaws
in respect of such premises and for purposes connected therewith," presented by
Mr. Leslie, and read a First time.
Ordered, That the Bill be read a
Second time on Thursday next.
Adjournment.
Resolved, That this House do now
adjourn until Thursday next.”—(Mr.
Long.)
Adjourned accordingly at a
Quarter past Four o'clock
until Thursday next.
Private Business
Question to Minister-
8 DECEMBER, 1938
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 8th December, 1938.
The Senate met a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Finance:
Accounts of Receipts and Payments for
the Financial Year ended 31st March,
1938, of the Ministry of Finance Pensions Account and the Pensions Account
(Northern Ireland), together with the
Reports of the Comptroller and Auditor-
General thereon.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
McGugan's Divorce Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons
and read a First time.
Plumb's Divorce Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons.
and read a First time.
Clark's Divorce Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons.
and read a First time.
Question to Minister.
THE TRANSPORT REPORT.
Mr. LESLIE: I desire to ask the
Leader of the House a question of which
I have given him private notice, namely,
whether he is in a position to make any
statement as regards what is known as the
Transport Report.
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House): The
Government have been considering the
recommendations on the transport system
in the report of the committee presided
over by Sir William McLintock. They
find themselves driven to the conclusion
that it must take a good deal of time before it will be possible to arrive at a
scheme they could recommend to Parliament to co-ordinate road and rail
transport in the Province. But in the
meantime they recognise the necessity of
endeavouring immediately to stop the
evasions of the existing law, and they
propose, as soon as a Bill can be prepared,
to lay before Parliament, possibly before
Christmas, a Measure designed to that
end.
Deputy Speakers.
Resolved, "That the Senators following be named Deputy-Speakers of the
Senate during the present Session of
Parliament:-
-
Captain Perceval-Maxwell.
Sir Roland Nugent."
-(Mr. Long.)
Joint Standing Committees.
Resolved, "That the Senators following be named of the Joint Standing
Committee on Divorce Bills:-
Major Dobbs.
Dr. Gibson.
Captain Perceval-Maxwell."
-(Mr. Long.)
Resolved, "That the Senators following be named of the Joint Standing
Orders Committee:-
Mr. Barclay!
Mr. Leslie.
Sir Roland Nugent."
-(Mr. Long.)
Resolved, "That the Senators following be named of the Joint Unopposed
Committee on Local Bills:-
Mr. J. Cunningham.
Mr. McLaughlin."
-(Mr. Long.)
Resolved, "That the Senators following be named of the Joint Kitchen
and Refreshment Room Committee:
Sir J. Davison.
Mr. Glendinning.
Mr. McAllister.
Mr. Maguire
Mr. Taylor."

Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
KING'S SPEECH.
Debate on the Address.
Order read for resuming debate on
Question (6th December), "That an
humble Address be presented to His
Grace in the following words :-
"Your Grace,
:-
"We, His Majesty's most dutiful
and loyal subjects, the Senate of the
Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg
leave to offer our humble thanks to
your Grace for the Speech which your
Grace has addressed to both Houses
of Parliament."-(Major Dobbs.)
Question again proposed.
on
Mr. LESLIE: I trust I may be permitted to begin the few remarks I am
about to address to the House by expressing my profound sense of the
obligation we are under to the hon. and
gallant Senator (Major Dobbs), who
moved the Address in this House
Tuesday, and also to the seconder of the
same Address (Mr. Taylor). The hon.
and gallant Senator is no stranger in
this House. We have heard him on
many occasions, and I hope we shall
hear him on many more.
He has always
contributed to our debates something of
real substance and value. The hon.
Member, who seconded the Address, is
a newcomer in our midst, and we have
on this occasion the almost unique opportunity of congratulating him on the fact
that his maiden speech was delivered on
the occasion when he was selected to
second the Address. The hon. Member
I have referred to, before he came to us.
had already rendered service both to the
State and to his Church, and we feel
sure that when he has been a little longer
with us he will be a very valuable
addition to our number.
Now I pass to the Gracious Speech itself.
It is a Speech, if I may say so, of considerable length; in fact, one is almost
driven to the conclusion that as the years
go on so do the speeches with which
Parliament is opened extend in length.
The Speech contains many matters which.
afford food for thought. The allusion
to the transport problem is not verv
diffusive, and I do not think that what is
said in the Gracious Speech as regards
the transport problem is sufficient for us
to base any lengthened argument on at
the present moment. I think, in short,
that the time is inopportune for endeavouring to discuss that complicated
subject at any length whatever.
But there are two remarks I desire
to take the earliest opportunity of
making. The first of them is that I
want from the very outset to register
my complete disagreement with one
passage which occurs in the valuable
report of the learned Recorder, and
again in the also valuable report of the
McLintock Committee. That is the
suggestion that when this Parliament
passed the Act of 1935 we expected and
intended that the acquisition of the road.
transport undertakings should be completed within a period of six months.
That was never in my own mind, and
indeed it may be remembered that it
was at my instigation at a later stage
that some slight procrastinating amendment was introduced in the Bill itself
by the Government. I rely now, as I
relied when the Bill was before us, on
the expression which will be found in
Section (5) of the Act of 1935, an expression which to my mind is emphatic.
That was that the acquisition was to be
accomplished within six months, or within such further time as the Ministry of
Home Affairs might allow. I think that
affords a very good indication of what
was the expectation of Parliament at
that time.
The other remark I propose to make
on the Report is that I hail with satisfaction the proposals as regards the
Clogher Valley Railway, because it may
be remembered that some eight years
ago I set my hand to a recommendation
as regards that railway which is reproduced in the report in question. I do
not propose to say any more as regards
the transport problem.
The next matter is this proposal to
postpone the date of revaluation. From
the point of view of the local authorities
who have to bear the expense, or a large
proportion of the expense, of that revaluation I imagine postponement will
be welcome. But the proposal will not
be so welcome at first sight to those
people who are looking forward to getting
their valuations reduced. But I think
the right hon. Gentleman, the Leader
of the House, will be able to assure us
that the postponement of the revaluation
will not in any way militate against the
right of any individual to bring forward
his claim for a reduction of his valuation
at the annual revision.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
8 DECEMBER, 1938
Mr. ROBB indicated assent.
Mr. LESLIE: The right hon. Gentleman assents to that. I should like to
commend that view to such of the public
who do me the honour to read what I
have said. The mere postponement of
general revaluation does not in any way
affect the annual right of applying for a
revision. The next matter is of a more
complicated character. Allusion is made
to the probabilities of the introduction
of a Bill dealing with the matter of
divorce. That matter has been before
us more than once within comparatively
recent times. I cannot, of course, depart
from the position I have always taken
up on this question, and that is that
those of us who hold that marriage is
one of the Sacraments of the Church can
have nothing directly to do with any
proposals for divorce. There is the other
standpoint that those of us who hold
those views do not and never have
claimed the right to endeavour to impose
our own personal opinion on other people.
I think we must leave it at that, but
we must recognise that any increased
facilities for divorce are, in the words
of our late Bishop, Dr. Grierson, merely
an increase of facilities for a breach of
the Seventh Commandment, and that is
of necessity an affront to the sentiments
of Christian people.
Passing to the more practical and more
mundane difficulties in the way, the right
hon. Gentleman said the other day that
the Government's Bill was not yet in
draft, and he would welcome suggestions.
on the subject. It seems to me that if
we look at the matter from the point of
view of Mr. Herbert's Bill-although the
Bill which finally received the Royal
Assent
was introduced, not by Mr.
Herbert, but by Mr. De La Bere-those
legislative proposals will be
be of the
greatest importance, because they will
for the first time lay down definitely the
grounds on which Parliament propose to
allow divorce. That has never been
done in this country before, and the
general opinion is that it is time it was
done.
There will be other difficulties, the
mechanical difficulties of how the courts
are to operate, because I imagine that one
of the principal objects of the new legislation will be to give this matter to the
courts, where it belongs, and to relieve
the joint committee of Parliament of the
very distasteful duty they have had to
perform. The question of whether you
are going to work through a decree nisi
or have a King's Proctor is a matter the
Government will have to consider.
The three points I want to direct
attention to are, first of all, whether the
circumstances in this Province require
anything in the shape of what is called
a Conscience Clause, a Clause which protects Ministers of the Established Church
in England from any proceedings,
ecclesiastical, civil, or otherwise, for
refusing to perform a marriage between
divorced people. In this Province we
have no established church, but the question may arise whether a particular
minister may not have entered into some
arrangement with a particular congregation for the performance of ministerial
functions and whether they may by any
chance incur any civil liability for refusing to perform a marriage of this
character. That is a matter for consideration with the representatives of those
religious bodies who can possibly be
effected.
The second proposal is the proposal in
the English Act for dealing with divorce.
on the grounds of incurable insanity. Now
I understand, and, in fact, one sees with
fair frequency in the reports in "The
Times" newspaper that the provision as
it exists in England to-day is not working.
to complete satisfaction. There are
difficulties, apparently more difficulties of
detail than of principle. That is a matter
I press upon the Government. They should
consider carefully what arrangement they
should make in that regard.
The third point is rather a more
difficult one.
If I remember the terms
of the English Act correctly there is a
provision that the innocent party having
obtained a decree nisi, which answers, of
course, to our decree a mensa et thoro,
and does not dissolve the bond of matrimony-there is a provision that if the
successful party in that suit does not within three years take steps to have what I
think I may call the decree made absolute
then the other party, that is, the guilty
party, may himself or herself take steps
to have the decree nisi converted into a
decree absolute. If that proposal is
adopted on this side of the water I would
have to oppose it because it would be a
Parliamentary suggestion to force the
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
conscience of the innocent party, and from
that I think the innocent party should be
protected. I have done with divorce.
There is a proposal now for a Bill to set
up an agricultural wages board. I presume that what we call our summer is
over for this year, but the deluge appears
to continue. Does anybody really think
the time is opportune for introducing any
legislation which would tend to render the
position of the farmer more difficult than
it is already? I am afraid it is common
knowledge that the agricultural insurance
scheme, passed a short time ago by this
Parliament, has had the effect of increasing unemployment amongst the people
who work on the land. The effect has
been that farmers and others who employ people, and regarded themselves as
under some obligation to keep them in
employment and pay them, take up the
position now, that having paid the contribution for insurance benefit for so
long they may as well get the benefit of
it, and put the men off their pay roll and
on to the pay roll of unemployment
scheme. That, I think, has rather
tended to increase unemployment. Again,
of course, the high rates paid in unemployment insurance benefit are out of
accord with the ordinary rate of wages
paid by the farmer. In fact, the position.
would seem to be that the State
in its desire to alleviate the position
of the agricultural worker has first
forced up the rate of benefit for unemployment insurance to a point above the
wages level, and is now going to set on
foot machinery for raising the wages to
the point of unemployment insurance
benefit. That seems a matter which is
not unlikely to be found difficult, and I
should like to record my definite opinion
that the present is not the time for adding to the burdens of anybody engaged
in the cultivation of the land.
ex-
There is a reference to the Education
Bill we passed last Session and an
pression of confident hope-I share the
hope, if not the confidence that the
additional
it has provaluable
very
year
vided for education will have good results. It has been said that what you
want in this matter of extending the
period of education is to educate the
parents first in order that they may see
the advantage that the additional year's
education is likely to be to their
children. It is fair to say as regards
that, I think, that the child of to-day is
the parent of to-morrow, and therefore
you cannot begin too soon. There will
be very great difficulties, such as we
discussed a few weeks ago, over these
matters of exemption, but we may hope
that sooner or later the good intentions
prompting that Act will meet with their
reward. After all the youth of the
nation, as has been remarked before-I
quoted it myself in the debate-are the
trustees of posterity, and we can only
hope that what we are doing for them
may have good results.
The only other matter to which I want
to allude for the moment is the appointment of our agent in London. I should
like to congratulate the Government on
the admirable selection they made. I
consider we were very fortunate to obtain
the services of Sir James Cooke-Collis,
and his efforts will be of great advantage
to the nation.
-
Captain PERCEVAL MAXWELL:
The Speech we have before us is, as the
hon. and learned Senator said, more
than usually full. And I am pleased to
see that one of the two pieces of legislation it is proposed to introduce is a
Bill to reform the law of divorce, and
the other to safeguard the young and to
deal with the question of young offenders.
As regards the amendment of the law
of divorce, I must say I was a little disappointed by the speech of the hon. and
learned Senator, who, I thought, had
long ago recognised the importance of
this change in the law, and who, as an
expert in that law, would naturally regard a non-legal court as being an unsuitable method of trying these cases.
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
Captain PERCEVAL MAXWELL :
I see that he agrees with me on that
point. I will not go deeper into the
question of the personal views which
Members may have on the question of
marriage. That, I think, is really beside the point, for we have in this
country many people who are not
members of one or other of the religious
bodies who believe that divorce is not
justified in any case, and we have to
legislate for these people as well as for
members of the Christian churches. Un-
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
8 DECEMBER, 1938
fortunately, it can be said that no State
is a Christian State in the sense that a
certain portion of the persons who have
to be looked after and protected by the
State are not Christians, and many of
our practising professing Christians have
not got any very strong or firm
principles on some of these questions
which vex the minds of theologians. I
do not wish at this stage to introduce
any controversial questions into this
matter of divorce.
I believe that what
the Government are doing will strengthen
the marriage tie rather than weaken it,
and I believe that it will be a benefit to
this country to have the law, which,
after all, has proved to be, on the whole,
satisfactory in
in England, and which
must be more satisfactory than a law
which is administered by a tribunal that
does not pretend to any qualifications
which other legal tribunals possess.
Now as regards the treatment of the
young offenders I regard this as perhaps
one of the most important Measures which
the Government have introduced for
many years, because, after all, it is no
good building up and maintaining a State.
if you do not provide for the children,
and see that they grow up into adequate
and useful citizens. No one can go
through the streets of any of our big
towns without great anxiety as to the
future of many of the children one sees.
The absence of playgrounds drives them
into the streets. Perhaps their parents
are overworked. They have to be overworked in order to make a living, and
they cannot afford to provide for the
necessary supervision of their children,
and in those cases the children are left
very much to their own devices.
go wrong it seems to me that that is not
the surprising thing. The surprising thing
is that so few of them go wrong.
If they
I would ask the Government when dealing with this matter to try to put away
from its mind-if it can be said to have a
corporate mind-the idea of crime in
connection with children. In some cases,
of course, you have a type of young person
who is definitely criminal in mentality,
but many of the so-called crimes which
children commit are due to effervescence
of spirits which, in other circumstances,
would lead, perhaps, to no delinquency
at all. It is a difficult problem, and one
which it would be very difficult for the
Government or anyone else to solve as to
where to draw the dividing line between
punishment as such and psychological
correction and direction. It is a difficult
problem, and one which has never been
solved satisfactorily in any country.
America has gone on certain very advanced
lines and England appears to be advancing
more rapidly, I think, than we would be
prepared to go in this country at the
present moment.
In
But there is one particular line in which
the Government could minimise the evils
of child delinquency, and that is providing an adequate probation system. At
present probation has got the idea of a
let-off, whereas the true idea of probation
is binding over under supervision.
former times it was thought that this
thing of probation could be managed
satisfactorily by well-meaning persons
capable in many ways, but without any
definite training. Such research as I have
been able to make during the time I was
on the committee dealing with the matter
has convinced me that this is an erroneous
idea, and that if probation is to be
a success it must be worked by trained
persons.
Since the war the science of psychology
has made great strides. In some respects
it is rather an empirical science, and I am
not sure that all the claims that have
been made for it will be justified in the
future. But there is no doubt that a
certain amount of knowledge of psychology
is essential for those who either teach or
train under modern conditions, and I
should very much like to see probation
officers appointed who could afford to give
the whole of their time to this very
important subject, and not only part of
their time, although I believe that many
of the people who devote part-time work
to this cause do it because of their affection
for children and their love of the cause,
and, therefore, do it very well. Nevertheless, they are handicapped, particularly
as it takes time to supervise children who
are placed on probation, and it is necessary for them to know not only the
children but the parents, as well as something about the homes from which the
children come if they are to advise the
magistrates properly when the question
comes to be decided whether further probation is required or whether institutional
treatment is necessary.
There will inevitably be considerable
expense attached to any efficient change
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Captain Perceval-Maxwell]
in the law relating to the treatment of
delinquent children, and I think we must
face that expense. It is not always
possible to provide the treatment in the
home. Sometimes it will be necessary
to send children for institutional treatment, and a question which may easily.
divide opinion in this House and opinion
elsewhere is as to how far centres for the
protection and training of children in
this country are justified by the
amount of crime which statistics
show has been committed. That is
a question I am not going to deal with
to-day. It is a big question, and one on
which there may very well be differences
of opinion, but it is a question the
Government will have to consider most
carefully before introducing this reform.
There is one more reform in connection
with child delinquency which I would
like to mention, and that is the introduction of the lay element in children's
courts. There were many many objections-some of them were shared by
Members on the opposite side of the
House, and some were not to the lay
form of judicial procedure in summary
cases. Most of those objections, I think,
were based on the argument that crime
was a matter to be dealt with largely
by experienced persons and persons with
technical knowledge. I am asking the
House for the moment to regard the bulk
of juvenile crime as more a psychological
or mental question than a criminal question, and if they do that they may think.
the question of the introduction of the
lay element, particularly the female lay
element, in children's courts is one which
is desirable, when you are dealing with
certain cases relating to young persons.
There is a great deal of knowledge and
experience of childhood in the layman.
or laywoman who may have children of
his or her own, and, therefore, may have
a specialised knowledge which ought to be
at the disposal of the court. Again, the
question of law is not so likely to arise
in these cases as questions of commonsense and knowledge of the subject dealt.
with. Therefore, I do urge
the
Government the suggestion that they
should accept the principle of lay element.
in children's courts.
on
The only other question I would like
to discuss is that of transport. I do feel
that the Government should not be
pressed unduly over this matter. They
have had an experience of the working
of the Act of 1935, and they have had
the experience which the report gives
them, the work of the best brains they
could obtain, and I do feel that it would
be fatal for the Government to introduce
a Measure too quickly. We have been
told by the Prime Minister and by
certain other Ministers that, after all,
the main responsibility for the Transport
Act must be accepted by Parliament as
a whole. With very great respect to
those who make that suggestion I am
very far from accepting it in its entirety.
I think it would lead one to the conclusion that if Parliament passed an Act
which proved to be disastrous and it was
necessary for the Government to resign,
then, if their theory of Parliamentary
responsibility was correct, it would be
necessary for the whole Parliament to
resign, and I do not think any person
would go to the length of suggesting
that. But it does draw our attention.
to one very important point, and that is
that it is possible for anyone to suggest
that Parliament has the corporate responsibility for legislation which is passed,
and, therefore, it is very necessary that
Parliament should examine legislation
very carefully, and not allow doubtful
legislation to reach the Statute Book.
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
Captain PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: If
that is what the Prime Minister meant I
endorse his views, and I hope that, when
this Bill comes before Parliament, Parliament will be given a free hand to suggest
alterations. The executive cannot take
the responsibility of refusing to allow
Parliament to make suggested alterations,
and, at the same time, claim that Parliament is responsible for a Bill which does
not contain the alterations which have
been suggested. That seems to me to be
a very important point. We have allowed
a Bill to pass through this House and
through another place which has caused
great dislocation, and
have not
improved the situation very much by the
passing of that Bill. I think we ought to
carefully consider every Measure the
Government introduce to improve the
position, and say whether, in fact, the
new Measure is likely to improve it or
not, or whether it may not create an even
we
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
8 DECEMBER, 1938
more serious position. I feel very strongly
that if the transport position is not
improved it must cause very serious
damage. Therefore, it is very important
that we should have plenty of time not
only to consider the report, but any
Measure which the Government may wish
to introduce to put matters right.
The only other thing I wish to say is
this. When reading the report one is
struck by the frequent remark of the
investigators that nothing which they do
or say will be of any use unless it is
adopted with good-will on the part of all
concerned. I am sorry to say that such
investigation as I have been able to make
into the transport question has shown that
there is a minimum of good-will on the
part of a great many of the persons
affected. I feel that anything that we do
in this House is bound to fail unless individual interests realise that the most
important thing in this country is that
the transport position should be saved,
and they must be prepared to give away
certain private and personal interests for
the benefit of the country, as a whole.
beg to support the Motion for the
Address.
I
I
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH:
would like to join with other hon. Members in congratulating the mover and
seconder of the Motion for the Address.
What has been said about the way they
discharged their duty was richly deserved
and I heartily support it. I think the
most important matter referred to in the
Speech is that of transport. That problem
must be solved, but he would be a bold
man here or anywhere else in Northern
Ireland who would say what is the right
and proper thing to do. I have no doubt
that as a result of the experience gained
from the working of the Act of 1935, and
the reports of the two committees which
investigated the question, the problem
will be solved, and that transport in
Northern Ireland will be placed on a
proper and a paying basis.
be
I desire, particularly, to refer to the
proposal of the Government to appoint a
committee to advise when the next general
revaluation shall take place, and whether
the machinery of revaluation can
amended and the cost reduced, and, in the
meantime, to postpone the revaluation.
due to take place in 1941. I entirely
agree with that proposal. In anything I
say on this subject I do not speak for the
country or the farming community. Farm
buildings were left entirely outside the
Revaluation Act because it did not affect
them at all. If their valuations were increased the Government paid any increase
in taxes because they are derated, and
with that I find no fault.
I may now turn to the effect of revaluation generally. I think it is one
of the worst things that ever happened
to the city. I am speaking with the
knowledge I have of the city, and not so
much of the rest of the Province. What
refers to the city will refer to the city of
Derry and the towns and villages. In
the city of Belfast nobody thought revaluation would have had such far-reaching effects. I was one who held the view
that in view of the terms of the Act the
matter would be serious. We were
placed practically on the same footing as
Great Britain, though not altogether.
My hon. and learned Friend opposite may
help me. I think I heard him say more
than once that the taxable capacity of
Northern Ireland was not comparable to
that of Great Britain. What was the
shock then that the ratepayers in the
city of Belfast received when it came out
that the increase in valuation was over
45 per cent.
I re-
I was one of those who thought that a
levelling up and the removal of anomalies.
were certainly needed, but general revaluation on those drastic lines was the
worst thing that could possibly happen
as far as the community was concerned.
It has proved so. I thought the time
was not opportune. We were getting out
of a cycle of good trade into a depressed.
time, and instead of getting encouragement all of a sudden we got this cold
douche of a 45 per cent. increase.
member expressing my views at a public
meeting. I left that meeting and went
into a rent agent's office in Belfast. The
head of the firm came up rubbing his
hands and said: "I heard your speech,
and you are going to get it!" I said,
"What about yourself?" "Oh," he
said, "it is not going to affect me. It
is only intended for some people who get
big rents and pay little taxation."
asked him what rent he paid. He told
me he did not pay rent; he owned his
own house. I asked him what the house
was worth-what was the valuation. He
said the valuation was £24 and that the
house next door was let at £80. I said,
I
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
""
[Sir Crawford McCullagh]
"My friend you will be very lucky if the
valuation is not doubled.' When the
valuations came out I was anxious to
have a look at them, and the results were
just as I expected. The valuations had
gone up 30, 50, 100, and even in some
cases 200 per cent. I went back to my
friend. He started to abuse the Government, and said it was the damnedest
Act that ever was passed. I asked him
what was the matter. He said, "My
valuation is up to £39. I am going to
appeal." I said he was lucky, and that
if I were he I should pay and not say a
word about it.
That was really the feeling in the city
of Belfast. As long as it did not touch
the individual pocket it did not matter.
As long as the other fellow had to pay
it was all right, but when it touched the
individual himself it was all wrong. As
to postponement, some Members take the
view that revaluation should not be postponed. They hoped that the valuation
would be reduced by 1941. What is the
theory of revaluation? I have never
known of it yet except with the one
object, that is, to raise the valuation
and get further taxation for the country.
Consequently, I rather welcome its being
postponed until 1941, because while we
have had a general levelling up and a
good many anomalies removed it is
open to anyone in the meantime to appeal
and, if possible, to get a reduction.
far as appeals are concerned, I am not
aware of any great reduction when
appeals are made. I do know that anyone who has taken the trouble to appeal
to the Recorder has got a reduction.
That goes to show that the highest
authority we have says in reality that
the valuation of the city is too high.
We all know that rates should not be
affected, but it does not work out that
way. We all know if the valuation goes
up the poundage ought to go down and
vice versa. But the harshest treatment
has been given to shopkeepers, and they
are the community who, above all, should
get some consideration. I took the
trouble when going down York Street
from Donegall Street to a little bit past
the railway to count, and I counted 21
vacant shops. If you ask the cause
everyone will tell you it is the high
valuation. We all know when an individual is buying a house the first
question is, what is the valuation? If
the valuation is high it frightens the
buyer off. I think there is nothing
which has done more harm to the city
than revaluation. It was just brought in
at a time when we needed a little bit
of help instead of what was done.
Struggling shopkeepers have had to give
up their shops. Then you find in the city
of Belfast the Government paying over
£112,000 a year to wealthy concerns
who are making huge profits and paying
huge dividends.
The whole thing is wrong. What I
suggest-I suggested it before, but the
Government turned it down--what I suggest in all sincerity for the benefit of the
city and of the Province is to bring in a
Bill immediately to give some relief not
only to Belfast, but to the entire Northern
area. I do not know what the figures
are in the rest of the Province. I know
Belfast had its valuation increased by over
45 per cent. I do not think it would be
so much in the rest of the Northern area.
I do suggest that the Government should
postpone revaluation until the committee
to be appointed is formed. Let them have
all the knowledge available and let them
go into the circumstances of the case as
far as valuation and the taxation of the
Province are concerned, and, in the meantime, let them make a reduction. It will
cause no trouble in the wide world. The
half would not be too much, that is 221
per cent. It might be too much in place
like Ballymena. I say again I am prepared to suggest a fair average for the
whole Province. Let the Government
bring in a Bill to reduce valuations by a
certain percentage whatever it may be.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: In the
first place I should like to congratulate.
the proposer and seconder of the Address.
They certainly did their part extremely
well. As to the speech of the Lord Mayor,
I feel that he is just expressing the
identical views of a considerable number
of business people of the city. He told a
good story of some experiences of his own.
I had a remarkable experience with reference to the Revaluation Act. Members
of the Corporation were considering the
Revaluation Bill, and they were almost
going mad condemning the Government.
Foolishly enough I was the only person
standing up in favour of the Government
and expressing the opinion that revalua-
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
8 DECEMBER, 1938
I thought there were
tion was necessary.
a great many anomalies that required
rectifying. There were some serious cases.
But you can judge of my feelings when
the result of revaluation came out. The
little home I have a small business in and
which was valued at £30 was only increased to £73 at one slap. It took the
patriotism and support I had for the
Government out of my head.
That is really what is taking place all
over the city. It was too much. I believed myself that there was a great deal
of improvement, and that increases were
necessary in a great many cases, but I
think the increases that where imposed
were outrageous, and they knocked the
heart out of business people. There is no
doubt about it business people were called
upon to pay taxes as well as heavy rates
because, unfortunately, the Corporation
has not reduced the poundage as it was
expected they would do. The rates are
fairly heavy owing to the high valuations
and it has been a very serious matter for
the business people of the city. If the
committee, which is to be appointed to
examine this question, can see its way to
recommend any reduction of that large
increase in valuations it would greatly
help a large number of the poorer business
people who are struggling at the present
time to make ends meet.
The first reference in the Gracious
Speech is to the crisis through which we
recently passed. We all felt very thankful when we found that our people were
as ready as ever to shoulder the responsibility of defending their country if the
call came to do so. Some years ago we
read of resolutions being passed at universities and colleges refusing to fight for
King and Country in any future war, but
the recent crisis proved that to-day our
people are just as prepared as ever they
were to defend their homes and their
country when they are threatened.
With regard to the problem of unemployment, we look forward year after
year to some amelioration of that question,
but on each occasion the position is much
the same. Our young people, especially,
are in a deplorable state at the present
time. It is all very well to talk about
physical training and training centres and
an extra year at school, but it does not
help the father and mother who have
three or four sons unemployed and unable
to earn the money necessary to keep them.
It is almost impossible for boys and girls
to obtain employment here. Many of
them come to me in the hope that I may
be able to direct them to some channel
where they will get work. It is a great
pity that something cannot be done for
these young people. In this respect we
are in a worse position than any other
part of the United Kingdom. The establishment of the aeroplane factory in
Belfast has been of great benefit to the
unemployed. A considerable number of
young people have found employment
there at good remuneration, but, on the
other hand, our shipbuilding industry is
very slack at the present time, so that
while there is a measure of prosperity in
one branch of industry the prospects in
another branch are not so good.
It used to be that the linen industry
was the bright spot when the shipbuilding
industry was depressed, but at the present
time the linen trade is not in a very
prosperous condition, although we are
living in expectation of an improvement
as a result of the agreement which has
been signed between Great Britain and
the United States. I sincerely hope
that pact will bring about the desired
improvement in the linen trade.
I
The mover of the Motion for the
Address made special reference to the
training centres in Great Britain, and to
the Gravesend Sea Training School.
think those training facilities should be
much more widely known, and people
should be made acquainted with the
opportunities there are in that direction.
I have come across one or two young men
who have been at the Gravesend Sea
School, and they were delighted with it.
It is a great hardship that the parents.
of boys undergoing training have to
maintain them while they are there, and
if a way could be found of assisting them
while they are receiving training it would
induce a considerable number of boys
from Belfast to undertake that training.
At the present time very few seem to
know anything about it, and, in my
opinion, it should be more effectively
advertised.
The most serious matter we have to
deal with is that of transport, and I am
sure we all regret that the experiment of
the 1935 Act did not prove to be more
satisfactory. I do not think it can be
claimed that its failure was altogether
the fault of the Act. I think what really
421788
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
happened was that instead of our having
the most shrewd men on the Transport
Board there were even more shrewd
people who sold their stock and goodwill
and then recovered it again. They sold
their business at a generous price and
then they turned round and created another business that deprived the Transport Board of the business they thought
they had acquired. There was a loophole in the Act, and these people took
full advantage of it. I think the
Government went out on the right lines
when they set up this Transport Board,
but they should make use of both road
and rail for the carriage of passengers
and freight.
There are some people who say the
railways should be scrapped, but to
my mind such people have not
given sufficient consideration to the
matter. At the present time the roads
are very dangerous for both motor traffic
and pedestrians. People are being killed.
at a terrible rate, and in view of that fact
it would surely be a calamity if the railways were closed down and the roads became even more congested. The railways
have a safe, smooth track for carrying
passengers and freight. They are giving
steady employment to a fine class of men,
and if you scrap them you will throw out
of employment a large number of men who
are receiving good remuneration and are
working under proper conditions. The
same thing could not be said of road
transport as it formerly existed. Lorry
drivers worked from early morning until
late at night, and the conditions under
which many of the employees worked
were deplorable. Therefore I hope the
railways will receive all the support possible, and that they will be utilised to the
fullest possible extent.
It is no use saying the railways are
antiquated, because on some of the lines
we have the internal combustion engine
that has done away with the steam engine.
It is a very handy locomotive that can
move carriages easily, and it must reduce
expenditure to a considerable extent. I
think further economies could be made if
a freight waggon could be constructed
which could be lifted by a crane off the
chassis on to a lorry at its destination.
That would save a great deal of handling
and thus reduce the cost. It is possible
to lift a waggon of coal into the hold of a
ship, and surely it should be possible to
put a light body on a railway waggon
which could be lifted by a crane on to a
lorry at the station. I hope the Government will not be influenced by those
persons who say the railways should be
scrapped, and that only road transport
should be used, because it would mean
that the roads would become even more
congested than they are at the present
time, and instead of hundreds of people
being killed annually thousands would be
slaughtered.
He
I am glad to see that the Government
intend to reintroduce the Housing Bill
which was not proceeded with last Session.
I have always been most anxious to see
everything possible done to have our
people properly housed. I always recall
the statement made by Lord Roberts when
he was warning the country of the
dangers of a war with Germany.
made a statement to the effect that if the
people were comfortably housed they
would have a greater incentive to fight in
defence of their homes. If our people
are provided with comfortable and
healthy homes they will have a greater
respect for and a stronger attachment to
their homeland than they can possibly
have while living in slums. In the York
Street area, one of the oldest parts of
Belfast, there are districts, where the
doors are not more than three feet from
each other, and houses in which all the
occupants cannot congregate at the same
time. I am not surprised that a good
many boys hang about street corners. The
sun does not penetrate into those places,
and there is no distance between the
backs and the fronts of the houses.
If the Government would encourage the
local authorities to wipe out squares of
such houses and make temporary provision
for the displaced tenants-which I understand the Housing Bill will do-it would
enable good tenement houses to be erected
in the area so that both the sun and good
fresh air could penetrate. Of course it
would be impossible to remove a square
of these dwellings and erect the same
number of tenement houses in their place.
And yet the people want to remain there.
They want to be near their employment.
They do not want to travel by tram or
train. I do not know of any other way
of solving this difficulty but by good
tenements. We have seen a good many
cases in London, Manchester and Glas-
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
8 DECEMBER, 1938
gow, and I believe they would take well
in a city like Belfast, especially in a congested district like York Street. I find
most of these poor people keep their
small houses spotlessly clean, and I do
think if they had good sanitary places
they would be just as particular to keep
them clean. If the housing of the people
was attended to and a better transport
system for the business community in
the city I believe things would be a great
deal better and the people would be
happy and content.
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to associate
myself with the remarks of previous
speakers in congratulating the mover and
seconder of the Address. I am not going
to occupy much time, as I feel I am not
in a position to improve on anything that
has been said. We have had expressions
of opinion from legal and trained minds,
and I am conscious that I have neither
a legal nor a trained mind. I am, however, very pleased to see in the Gracious
Speech that employment has been found
for a number of people and that unemployment has decreased. At the same
time we have a large surplus of unemployed men and women. Senator
Cunningham has made reference to the
young. Some time ago I made reference
to young boys, and said the time was
coming when the doors of the colonies
would be open for emigrants, and that
the class of men they would require
would be men who understood agricultural work. They did not want
men from the street corners or mechanics
or carpenters or joiners, but men to
cultivate the soil. The suggestion I
made was that the Ministry of Labour
should make arrangements with the
farmers of the country so that each
farmer might take one or two of these
boys to serve an apprenticeship to agricultural work, so that when the time
would come they could take their places
in the colonies.
There is also the question whether
all the unemployed we have with us are
ours or not. I read some time ago where
men in County Derry were prosecuted
for drawing unemployment benefit to
which they were not entitled, and the
resident magistrate
magistrate said there was
something wrong with the system.
He said there were over 300 men coming
out of another State and signing the
register in Northern Ireland for unemployment. If that is the case all round
the Border I say the Minister of Labour
should make inquiry. I believe it is a
fact, for the magistrate would not make
such a statement if it were not true. I
would also suggest that the Ministry of
Labour should change the staff at these
labour exchanges every 12 months, because there is a possibility of the staff
getting too well known and too friendly
with those on the employment register.
I
There is another thing I would like to
draw attention to and that is the cost of
living. It is far too high for those whom
I represent in this House, that is, the
poor working-class people, the unemployed,
the old-age pensioner, and so forth.
understand that there is £2 10s a ton of
a quota levy put upon all flour coming
into Northern Ireland and the unemployed man has got to pay this £2 10s a
ton on the flour he has to buy to feed his
children, I think something should be
done. Then I understand when a farmer
grows a rood, half an acre, or an acre of
wheat for his own use, and takes that
wheat to the mill to be ground the miller,
in addition to charging so much for grinding, is obliged to charge 6s a sack of 20
stone as quota levy. Where does that go?
The consumer is not benefited by it, and
the farming community of Northern Ireland benefit nothing by it. It goes to
enrich the wealthy farmers in the South
of England. You are taking this bread
out of the stomachs of the poor people. I
think something should be done in this
respect to make the cost of living cheaper
for these poor people.
I am
I was very glad to hear the Lord Mayor
make the suggestion that we should have
a reduction in the valuation, although I
myself have no room to complain, for I
benefited by the Revaluation Act.
paying this year £3 less than I did under
the old system, but if there is a reduction
I may be able to save £3 more. In water
rate I benefit by 1s a year. My valuation
was high and when the levelling came and
the rate was struck I stood to benefit.
again congratulate the mover and
seconder of the Address.
I
Mr. ROBB: By leave of the House.
Like the last speaker I am one of those
people who benefited by revaluation, so I
am not as touchy as some other people
might be on that question. I should
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Robb]
like to point out to the Lord Mayor of
Belfast that I understood that he had
had great applause in the city of Belfast
because he was enabled to get the rates
for the current year reduced from 10s 4d
to 10s. If the proposal he makes to
reduce the valuation by 22 per cent.
were carried out then to raise the same
amount of money the rates imposed
would go up to 12s 3d.
To begin with my hon. and learned
Friend opposite, may I say I shall
willingly bring the points he has
mentioned touching divorce before those
in charge of the drafting of the Bill. I
do not like to speak definitely, but I do
not think the conscience Clause would
affect any class of clergy in this country,
but, lest it might, it is a matter which
ought to be considered. With regard to
the difficulties arising from the insanity
Clause of the Divorce Act, these, I know,
have been considered, but whether any
satisfactory conclusion has been come to
I am not aware. His third point with
regard to allowing the respondent to
move for a decree absolute after three
years if petitioner does not move to make
the decree nisi absolute is a matter on
which opinions differ. My own view
would be that it would be no unjust
thing after a decree nisi was granted
if the petitioner does not move that ultimately the respondent should be entitled
to move to make it absolute. I can
assure him that these are matters that
will be bought before those in charge
of the Bill. I thank him for what he
said about the appointment of Sir James
Cooke-Collis. I hope any Senator visiting London will go and see Sir James
Cooke-Collis and encourage those in
charge of the work there in what is, after
all, a difficult task of advertising Northern Ireland in England, Scotland, Wales,
and elsewhere.
-
The hon. learned and gallant Senator
(Capt. Perceval Maxwell), who sits
behind me, made, if I may say so, an
illuminating speech. He dealt with a
familiarity which most of us have not
got with the problem of the young
offender. I will see that the points he
has made are brought to the attention
of those in charge of the drafting of the
Measure that may be brought in. The
part of his speech which dealt with the
constitutional problem of the corporate
responsibility of Parliament was interesting. Perhaps it was necessary that
that should be said in Northern Ireland.
It is one of those very difficult problems
how far the corporate responsibility
of Parliament is to be divided from
the corporate responsibility of the
Government and how far the Government should be made responsible
for everything that happens in Parliament, and, on the other hand, how far
Parliament may be charged with what
may be, after all, the fault of the Government. These are difficult things, but it
is a good thing our attention should be
drawn to this matter, and that we should
be forced to turn over in our own minds
what the position really is.
With regard to advertising the assistance given in the training unemployed
boys for the sea service I will bring that
to the notice of the Ministry of Labour.
I agree that far more should be known
about the possibilities of this. As to the
possibility of including in the scheme.
allowances or other assistance to these
boys I am not prepared to do more than
say that I will see that the matter is considered. There is a difficulty with regard
to it. If you do it for one class you must
do it for all, no matter how situated. The
suggestion Senator Cunningham made as
