to the employment of containers for
through traffic by road and rail is one I
know occupied the mind of those who have
the administration of railway matters in
their charge for some time. There is a
difficulty, of course, of immediate large.
capital expenditure, and whether our
traffic here would justify that expenditure
is a matter on which I understand
opinions differ very much. I sympathise
with all he has said with regard to housing. The Government's Bill will, I hope,
do something to alleviate what is admittedly a real difficulty of the present day,
not only here, but elsewhere.
Senator Barclay's suggestion that the
staffs of the employment exchanges should
be changed every 12 months is a very interesting one, but I should not like him
or the House or the public for a moment
to consider that I would agree in the
suggestion he has made that the administration may not be all that it might be if
the staff is kept too long in one place.
I am aware that it is the practice of the
English Revenue authorities to change
their income-tax inspectors from time to
time, but that it should be necessary at
Hairdressers Bill-
Second Reading
8 DECEMBER, 1938
our employment exchanges to change the
staff on the ground the hon. Senator has
suggested I would not for a moment like
to admit.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered, That the said Address be
presented to His Grace by such Members
of this House as are of His Majesty's
Privy Council of Northern Ireland.
Hairdressers Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LESLIE: This is a very short
Bill, and it is, as of course the House
will recognise, a Bill in the same form
as the Bill which left the Senate at the
conclusion of the last Session. The
House may remember that the Bill we
dealt with last Session came to us from
the House of Commons, and when I
introduced it in this House I said I could
not ask the House to pass it unless
certain mechanical alterations were made.
Accordingly I suggested at a later stage.
the minimum of alterations which seemed
to me to be necessary to give the Bill a
chance of being a working success. It
happened that one of the amendments in
particular evoked some criticism in another place, and the Bill was not proceeded with there. It was therefore
necessary to start de novo, and it was
thought better to begin in this House
rather than in another place.
In all private Bills there seems
to be an opportunity afforded of goodhumoured raillery, but it is a pity that
the opportunity last Session was unsuitable. It happened to be the last day
before the Prorogation, and there was
no time for communication between the
two Houses. Those who sit in another
place approached the consideration of the
matter in a holiday spirit, and certainly
some of the remarks made prompt
one to take that view. One hon.
and learned Senator-I must say
he ought to have known betterintimated to the House that in the
Factory Acts the right of a factory
inspector was limited to the day time. I
suppose he forgot there are Regulations.
in the Factory Acts that prevent overworking at night by women and young
persons. Another hon. Member laid
great stress on the horrible things that
might happen to the owner of premises,
but he forgot that an amendment made
in this House took the owner out of it
altogether.
More particularly criticism was passed
on sub-section (2) of Clause 3, the power
given to the court by which any person
convicted of an offence against the Act
or the by-laws can have the registration.
of his premises cancelled in addition to
being fined. That must have been the
cause of a great deal of misapprehension.
It was not appreciated, perhaps, that in
this case registration was equivalent to
licence, because it is really the case that
in most of the instances where local
authorities regulate this or that it is more
usual to do it by licence issued by the
local authority, usually on payment of
a fee, and it is even more usual when
that has been done for the local authority to retain the power of revoking that
licence. It is obvious, of course, that it
does not matter whether a man operates
by virtue of registration or licence. What
does matter is, I think, the necessity
which was shown in all Acts dealing with
matters of the kind of leaving in the
hands of the local authority the ultimate
power, if the need should unfortunately
arise, of getting that licence or registration cancelled. In some instances there is
a power given to suspend the licence for
varying periods before requiring it to be
forfeited, but the objection to that is
that you would require the unfortunate
local authority to keep tinkering with
their register, which seems unnecessary
in such a small matter as this.
The precedents are ample, I think.
The requirements of control are expressed
in the Act itself by requirements or conditions or common sense. You will always
find that power of revocation or cancellation existing. In England Lord Charnwood introduced a Bill in the House of
Lords dealing with the regulation of
performing animals, and the same regulation appears there. I am afraid I
must trouble the House with some of the
precedents. First of all, there is the Ice
Cream Act of 1937, in which these actual
words occur in conjunction with registration. Going further back to the Protection of Animals (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act of 1926 I am going to tell the
House a little of the provisions there.
There it is required that a man wanting to
exhibit or train performing animals-
Hairdressers Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
animals seem to come a great deal into
this matter has to register with the
police. It is provided that where it is
proved to the satisfaction of the court of
summary jurisdiction on complaint that
a registered man ought to be prohibited
or only allowed to go on subject to conditions, the court may make an order
against the person in respect of whom the
complaint is made prohibiting the training, and imposing conditions, and they
shall take various steps, which amount to
the cancellation of his registration.
Again, we have the Moneylenders Act
of 1933. It will be remembered that a
moneylender cannot get his licence until
he has got a certificate, and there is a
provision there for cancelling the certificate, and there is nothing said about
whether it is the first or second or fifteenth
offence. You have also the Marketing of
Eggs and the Marketing of Fruit Acts,
where the registration or licence can be
cancelled by the Minister without appeal.
There is the Midwives Act of 1929 and
the Street Trading Act of the same year,
in which a licence can be cancelled on the
ground of misconduct. You have alsoand to this I want to draw the particular
attention of the House-the Slaughtering
of Animals Act, 1932, which provides
that a licence to slaughter and so on shall
not be granted, and so on. The local
authority may suspend a licence at any
time for such period as they may determine, and where they are satisfied that
such person is not a fit and proper person
to hold a licence they may revoke his
licence. There is an appeal over from
the decision of the local authority to a
court of summary jurisdiction.
But the particular point about this Act
I want to draw attention to is that the
Act originated in this House where it was
moved by the hon. and learned Senator
who now represents the Central Division
of Belfast in another place, and who dealt
particularly severely with the sub-clause
in the Bill now before us. The Bill
before us gives the power of cancellation
not to the local authority, which to some
extent might be regarded as an interested
party, but to the court of summary
jurisdiction, and I must point out that
any suggestion that that court is not a fit
and proper tribunal to deal with such a
trivial matter is a very unjustifiable slur
sought to
be cast on the resident
magistrates who will adjudicate. Even
supposing your resident magistrate is deserving of the slur, I must point out that
the Bill provides for an appeal to a court
of quarter sessions. I do not think the
man who gets his registration without
paying any fee, and without the local
authority having any right to say
whether he shall have it or not, has any
right to complain if in certain circumstances a magistrate cancels his registration and he has an appeal over.
That being really the only point in
dispute, it seemed to me that the best
thing we could do was to pass the Bill
again exactly as it was and send it back
to another place in order that they may
give this particular matter reconsideration. If they still really think that the
sub-section ought not to stand, and if
they are satisfied that they can induce
the local authorities to work this permissive Measure without such a subclause, then they can send it back to us
and say so. In the alternative they can
perhaps suggest some constructive alteration which we might consider would be
sufficient for us to pass on to the local
authorities to induce them to work the
Bill. I beg to move, "That the Bill be
now read a Second time."
Mr.
JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
would like again to support the Second
Reading of this Bill. I find that the
respectable barbers in the city are
anxious to have this Measure. Probably
there are others of lesser repute who are
not so anxious about it, but I think it
will do a great deal of good. It seems
strange that the Bill passed muster in
another place as it left the hands of our
Parliamentary draughtsman, and that
when amendments were inserted by the
Senate they refused to accept the
Measure. I think a further amendment
might be made to the second Clause of
the Bill. In addition to imposing a
penalty for a first and second offence the
court might also cancel the registration,
and such an amendment might make it
possible for another place to accept the
Bill. I support the Second Reading and
hope the Bill will pass through both
Houses.
Mr. LESLIE: I need only say in
reply to my hon. Friend that I thank
him for supporting the Bill. The
Adjournment
Business of the House
8 DECEMBER, 1938
difficulty about that first and second
offence is that we cannot conjecture what
class of offence it may be. Putting anything of that sort in does, to my mind,
interfere with the jurisdiction of the
court. The court has full discretion. If
they think in another place that is a
workable solution I do not suppose this
House will object.
Question put and agreed to.
ADJOURNMENT.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That
this House do now adjourn until such
day as Mr. Speaker shall appoint."
Mr. LESLIE : I suppose the hon.
Gentleman is not in a position to give
any indication of when we are likely to
be called together again.
Mr. LONG: I am not.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Five o'clock until such
day as Mr. Speaker shall
appoint.
Private Business
Road Transport Bill
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Monday, 19th December, 1938.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Commerce:
Report of the Registrar of Friendly
Societies for Northern Ireland, Part B,
for the year ended 31st December, 1937.
Ministry of Finance:
Account of the Receipts and payments
of the Teachers' Superannuation Fund
for the year ended 31st March, 1938,
together with the Report of the Comptroller and Auditor-General thereon.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
McGugan's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a Second time."
Clark's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a Second time."
Plumb's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a Second time."
Business of the House.
Resolved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking the Second
Reading of the Road Transport (No. 2)
Bill." (Mr. Robb).
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Road
Transport (No. 2) Bill, to which they
desire the concurrence of this House.
Road Transport (No. 2) Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long).
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House): I
beg to move, "That this Bill be now read
a Second time." As the House is aware,
the Government have decided to set up
a Joint Committee of both Houses to
consider generally the two reports that
have been presented respectively by His
Honour the Recorder of Belfast and the
Committee presided over by Sir William
McLintock, and to make such recommendations as they may see fit on those
two reports. In the meantime, and
without in any way endeavouring to
entrench upon the province of this Joint
Committee, this short Bill has been put
forward, and has already passed through
another place.
The Bill provides for three things.
First, it provides that there shall be
made a special register of goods vehicles
containing the particulars set out in
Clause 1. The report of the McLintock
Committee recommended a system of
licensing. Whether or not the Joint
Committee that will examine these
matters will recommend the establishment of such a system or not the Government does not attempt to prejudge,
but in order that matters might be made
ripe for the enforcement of any such
provision it is deemed useful to have a
register of all road transport freight
vehicles for the use of the Ministry of
Home Affairs so that time may not be
lost if a system of licensing should be
recommended by the Committee.
The second Clause deals with this
position. The Road Transport Board
acquired a number of undertakings, and
in order that they may enjoy to the full
the benefit of the goodwill and business
connection of the undertakings for which
they have paid, and in many cases paid
very handsomely, it is proposed that there
should be introduced a statutory provision similar to what would be introduced in the case of the sale of any
business by one person to another, and
in the sense that the vendor should not
indo anything, either directly or
directly, to prevent or hinder, or in any
way interfere with, the enjoyment by
the Board of the full benefit of the goodwill for which they have paid.
Road Transport Bill-
Second Reading
19 DECEMBER 1938
The third Clause amends Section 5 of
the Act of 1935 with regard to the
acquisition of transport undertakings.
There are still a number of road transport undertakings that have not been
transferred to the Board, and this Clause
sets a limit of time within which the
Board may be required to take over any
such undertakings. That date is 1st
March next. The Clause provides that
the Board shall not be bound to take
over any road undertaking unless they
shall have received notice to take it over
before that date. That, however, is not
an absolute and final date, because paragraph (c) provides that notwithstanding
that notice shall not have been served
before that date, any owner may, at any
time after the fixed date, apply to the
High Court, and the High Court may,
if it is satisfied that the undertaking is
a
road transport undertaking which
should have been taken over, and that
the applicants had reasonable cause for
their failure to make application to the
Board to take it over, extend the time
and make an order directing the Board
to acquire the undertaking.
These are the three provisions of the
Bill. They are not very far-reaching,
nor are they, I think, in any degree
controversial.
Mr.
McLAUGHLIN: I would like
the Leader of the House to give me some
information as to the number of
accidents and the number of deaths due
to transport traffic in 1920 and 1937 or
1938, if the figures are available.
Mr. ROBB: I have not the information at my disposal now, but these
figures have, of course, already been
published.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: We all know
perfectly well that the roads were never
built for the transport they have to carry
at the present time, and that question
will have to be dealt with sooner or later.
It is a great reflection on the Transport
Board that the Government have now to
bring in a Bill containing a protective
Clause which, if the members of the
Board had been business men, they
would have had incorporated in their
own agreements to acquire undertakings.
When any professional or business man
of common sense is going to purchase a
profession or business of any kind from
a particular party he will make sure that
a clause is incorporated in the agreement
preventing the seller from entering into.
competition with him in that business
within a certain radius.
It is a very
serious reflection on the members of the
Transport Board that when they entered
into these agreements to take over road
transport undertakings, and paid very
handsomely for them, they should have
left a loophole for these people to trade
against them.
I am surprised that when the Ministry
of Home Affairs, which was responsible
for the Road and Rail Transport Act of
1935, were appealed to by the Transport
Board, they failed to give the Board any
assistance whatever in carrying out their
own Act. I have yet to learn of any
other Government which was as anxious
as was the Northern Government to get
a Bill passed, and then refused to implement its provisions by providing the
necessary machinery.
I would like to ask the Leader of the
House if this Bill is intended to cover
such private merchants as grocers who,
since the 1935 Act was passed, have
bought motor lorries. These lorries are
sent from country towns to the city of
Belfast laden with grass-seed or oats or
shrubs which they are not entitled to
carry and take back loads of coal and
other goods which they are legitimately
entitled under the Act to carry. That
practice has been going on, and the
Ministry of Home Affairs has winked at
it. If the Ministry is going to allow it
to continue there is very little use in the
Government setting up a Joint Committee to frame a new Act, or in Parliament passing any such Act.
Sir ROLAND NUGENT: I understand this Bill is an emergency Measure,
and as such it is intended to eliminate
some of the worst features of the present
transport position. It has also another
aspect. It should provide a certain
amount of extremely valuable data to
enable the Joint Committee of both
Houses eventually to reach a decision.
I think it is generally accepted that the
real crux of the transport crisis in this
country centres in the carriage of goods
by road, and always has centred in that.
The original Act was passed on the
assumption that it was necessarily and
inevitably the fact that a large centra-
Road Transport Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Sir Roland Nugent]
lised unit could carry goods by road more
economically and more efficiently than
a large number of private enterprises.
That seems to me a very bold assumption. Sir Felix Pole said it was so and
the McLintock Committee implies that
it is so but neither of them produced
any adequate evidence to prove it. And
yet this is the whole basis of the Act.
It is the whole basis of the McLintock
Committee's recommendations.
This present Bill when it is in operation will give us a certain amount of
information on that point. Clause 1
will give us the ownership type and a
broad outline of the use to which existing road vehicles are put. Clause 2, if
it is effective, will eliminate the competition of the so-called pirate, and we
shall be left with the real picture of
the amount of traffic which the Board
has lost by the legitimate owner-user
carrying his own goods in his
Own
can be
vehicle. If that information
supplemented with an examination of
the costs of the private owner as against
the costs of the Board, we should really
then and for the first time be in
position in which a decision can be taken
with knowledge of the facts.
a
Let us
assume for the moment that
this assumption which Sir Felix Pole
and the drafters of the 1935 Act made and
Sir William McLintock's committee again
makes is correct, that a big centralised
unit is necessarily the cheapest and best.
If it turns out to be wrong the whole basis
of the 1935 Act and the whole basis of
the McLintock Committee's recommendations-everything that we have done so
far in road transport-will have proved
to have been built on an entirely false
foundation. I have great reverence for
their Own
the views of experts in
capacity. I was once an expert myself,
but I would hesitate a very long time
before accepting the mere assertion of
an expert in a matter of this importance.
That is why I welcome very much the
aspect of this Bill which may enable
us to get some information on this very
difficult point.
I therefore wish to urge on the Government that if this Bill becomes an Act,
ample time should be given to us to see
the result of its operation, especially in
the checking of what are called pirate
enterprises. I do not think we shall see
the result in the first few months. The
result we want to see is how much
traffic comes back to the Transport
Board. I very much fear myself we
shall see relatively little traffic coming
back, because I believe the legitimate
competition of the private owner has
done far more to defeat the Board. If
this is so then the Joint Committee of
both Houses will have to take a very
serious
to
decision. They will have
decide whether they are going to adopt
the principle of the McLintock Report
knowing as they then would know that
the principles are based on an economic
fallacy, and in order to carry out those
principles whether they are going to
accept the creation of a vast centralised
enterprise owned and very largely run
by the State and in addition an artificial
inflation of the cost to private individuals who are legitimately running
their own business.
It may be that the assumptions of Sir
Felix Pole and the McLintock report
are correct. They are very great experts
and may be right. If they are correct
then we accept them. If they are not
correct then this Joint Committee will
have to think of some other solution of
the real difficulty-the transport by
road difficulty. The Committee will
have a very serious responsibility, and
I do most earnestly trust they will not
be in any way hurried in their deliberations or in any investigations they may
think fit to make. I have an
fortable feeling that there is a spirit of
in the air.
urgency
"We are losing
thousands a week. Get on with it."
That is not the way a problem of this
sort should be faced. We may easily by
few thousands now
trying to save a
lose millions. I do press that the
Government should give the Committee
if necessary a year or eighteen months
to investigate the whole matter.
uncom-
Mr. McALLISTER: I think it is
agreed on all sides of the House that
the issue at stake at the present time
is too big to make it a party question.
It affects the interests of every constituency, of every Member of Parliament, and every interest represented by
the Senate. Therefore I appeal to the
a party
Government, not to make it
question as they did in 1935. They
rammed that Bill down the throats of
Road Transport Bill-
Second Reading
19 DECEMBER 1938
the people and the Opposition by force.
of the party Whips at each and every
stage of the Bill. If the Government
are in earnest in the idea of not making
this a party question they will leave it
to the free vote of the public representatives in both Houses of Parliament.
If they had followed our advice in
1935 this calamity would not have
happened.
We opposed that mad
gamble at every stage and on every
occasion
we possibly could, and that
I think will show that we had the
interest of this part of the country as
much at heart as any other party in this
State.
I myself remember describing that
Bill as
a mad gamble and I am convinced by what I have seen now that
the Government are about to embark on
a still madder gamble at the present
time. I remember well those on this
side of the House argued that the
Government in their attempt to escape
responsibility for the Transport 1935
Act set up a committee, the Transport
Board, to run that Act. I remember I
said if the Act was successful then the
Government would say they in establishing a committee were responsible for the
success of the Act, and that if the Act
failed, as it has disastrously failed, they
had the Transport Board or committee
to stand between them and responsibility.
But the Government have found on this
occasion that the public are not so easily
gulled. They will not place the
responsibility on the Transport Board.
We are told there are something in
the nature of pirates. I say there is no
evidence good or bad or indifferent that
they were the cause of the failure of
the Act. With regard to the Transport
Board, some of whom I know personally,
instead of making an attack upon them
I sympathise with them. They are men
who have made good in all their
business avocations, men of the highest
probity, honour and integrity. They
have my sympathy because they were
told to carry out an absolutely impossible
task. I say with great deliberation,
and the House must know it is true,
that no Government in any country in
the world would face the public after
as has been brought about by this Act.
such colossal mismanagement and failure
Certainly no Minister responsible for
that Act would ever do anything else.
but resign office.
It is the duty of the Government, if
they have learned the lesson they should
have learned, before rushing into
legislation, as the last speaker very
wisely said, before rushing into further
catastrophe to find out by some plebiscite or some means the views of the
public, because the public as far as I
can ascertain are not behind them in
the lines they are pursuing now. There
are one or two things about this Committee I would like to say. I am not
going to reflect on a single one of them,
but it is a strange thing that Belfast is
excluded from the
operation of the
Transport Board, and yet the chairman
of this Committee, for whom we have
all great respect as far as ability and
integrity are concerned, is a Belfast man
representing
Mr. LESLIE: On a point of order.
Has the Committee appointed a chairman yet?
Mr. SPEAKER: I do not think the
hon. Member is making a speech on the
Second Reading of the Bill. The
Motion for the appointment of the Committee will come up to-morrow.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I will have something to say about it then. There is
one thing. I hope that the business
men of Northern Ireland will probe into
the Clauses in this Bill which confer
upon the Minister power to prescribe
rules and regulations. I remember the
first day when with the late Mr. Joseph
Devlin I entered the Northern Parliament. We made an agreement that we
would never make an attack upon a civil
servant or an official who could not
defend himself. We would attack the
Minister in charge of anything to which
we objected. As I said in this House
over and over again, and an abler and
more experienced man than I also said,
this is a most fatal method of legislation, to give a Minister behind locked
doors and in private power to make
rules and regulations far in excess of
what would be tolerated by either House
of Parliament. I would suggest to the
Leader of the House if he must insist
on reserving this right to make rules and
regulations that a Clause be added in
any future Bill dealing with this, com-
Road Transport Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
come before
pelling the Minister to
Parliament to get sanction by vote of
Parliament before he is allowed to put
any regulations made behind locked
doors into operation.
My own view is, be the loss as it may,
what we ought to do is to scrap the 1935
Act, which has not a redeeming feature
or anything in its favour. Scrap it
entirely. I think that is the view of
the great volume of opinion in Northern Ireland so far as the rural and
urban areas are concerned, to which this
Bill applies. There is another thing on
which I would like an assurance from
the Leader of the House. I hope the
issue of these licences to eight or nine
thousand lorries owned by private
business men in Northern Ireland is not
going to be made a semi-financial
Measure like so many other Measures
passed by this House. I would like to
know that in the issue of these licences
an additional tax is not placed on the
hard-pressed business men in Northern
Ireland at this time of keen depression.
I would also like an assurance from
the right hon. Gentleman that if a person
is refused a licence by the Minister of
Home Affairs he should have the right
of appeal say, to the County Court
Judge. I do not think it is fair to men
carrying on business to have the right
of transport placed entirely in the hands
of the Minister of Home Affairs. They
have the right as taxpayers and ratepayers to have a ruling independent of
the Minister by a County Court Judge or
some other legal tribunal. That is all
I want to say on the Second Reading.
There are other matters dealing with
the Committee I will refer to at a later
stage.
Sir JAMES WILTON: I do not propose to occupy more than a few seconds
of the time of the House, because I feel
the urgent nature of the Bill does not
require a great deal of time to be spent
on it. Really I am at a loss to know
why a Bill of this nature was not introduced sooner. I agree with a great deal
of the criticism that has been levelled at
the Transport Board, but I think at
most one can only say of them that they
have not been efficient, whereas the
practices which this Bill seeks to curtail
or do away with can be described
as nothing short of downright dishonesty.
I shall be very pleased to support the
Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me that
there is, perhaps, very little arising out
of the Bill itself which can be usefully
Isaid this afternoon. I will deal in a
minute or two very briefly with the
Clauses, but before I do that I want to
express my sense of welcome to the
observations which fell from the hon.
Senator opposite (Sir Roland Nugent) as
regards the fact that road freight transport is the crux of the whole position.
I think it is very desirable that that
statement should be made authoritatively
as has been done this afternoon.
should also like to support the hon.
Senator in his suggestion that the
deliberations of this Joint Committee,
which we understand is to be appointed,
should not be in any way hurried. I
presume that we may trust the Committee as a Joint Committee of both
Houses of Parliament to see that they
do not allow themselves to be fettered
or to be influenced by any outside body.
It is, of course, most important that all
Parliamentary Committees should bear
that proposition well in mind and act
upon it in the future as I think we may
say they have acted in the past.
I do not think myself that the Bill
before us affords any legitimate opportunity for attacking either the Transport.
Board or the Minister responsible for the
supervision of that Board. Nor do I
think that it is the occasion for anything
in the nature of praise of their efforts.
As a matter of fact we must remember
that in this House we neither make nor
unmake Governments, nor do we retain or
remove from office individual Ministers.
I think these matters are really better
left outside the discussion.
Now I want to refer briefly to the
Clauses, having regard to one or two
things said. The hon. Senator who
followed the right hon. Gentleman, the
Leader of the House, made some inquiry
as to whether the owner of a private
lorry would be affected. By a private
lorry I mean, of course, a privately
owned lorry which a man or company
owns for the purpose of carrying on
business. Such people, if they hold a
licence granted under Part III of the
Act of 1934, will certainly be affected.
Road Transport Bill-
Second Reading
19 DECEMBER 1938
a
Te

le
1.
$
1.
at

I
I
I
If they do not hold such a licence they
will not. Again, I think it was the hon.
Senator to whom I have referred who
pointed out the great necessity there will
be for the Committee to have before them
such information as will be collectable
under Clause I of the Bill before us.
They must have full information, and it
seems to me that the provision in the
Clause will enable them to get it more
quickly than they otherwise could. No
doubt they would have power to send for
persons, papers and records, and I think
the Clause before us does confer a reasonable possibility of getting the information accurately and with expedition.
Something has been said about the
power of the Ministry to make rules and
regulations under this Clause. If the
hon. Senator (Mr. McAllister) will look
again I think he will see a reference to
Section 53 of the Act of 1934. That
Section provides, as most of my hearers
no doubt will remember, that any rules
or regulations made under that
should be laid before both Houses of
Parliament for the statutory period, that
is, five sitting days covering a period not
less than ten days, and be liable to
annulment on an address from either
House of Parliament, SO that any
regulation made under the Act, if
Parliament sanctions the Clause, will not
be entirely removed from the supervision of Parliament. I think that is
all I can say about Clause I.
As regards Clause 2, which I think
the hon. Senator (Sir James Wilton)
discribed as one to secure honest dealing
-I think that was the purport of his
observation-we are all in sympathy
with that. Something was said about
the failure on the part of those responsible as regards the insertion in their
contract of a clause protecting the Board
from competition by individual operators.
The reference was to the omission of such
a clause from the arrangement. Before
that criticism is passed people ought to
reflect on the great body of rules of law
dealing with contracts in restraint of
trade and the validity of any such contract or arrangement. I do not mean
to say that the proposals in this Clause
really operate in restraint of the owner's
trade, but I am going to say that they
will afford a definite statutory powerthe power to secure honesty. We know
that such contracts have been differently
interpreted by the courts from time to
time.
The first decision I recall was an early
18th century decision, and that has been
the subject of consideration by the courts
over and over again. The particular
decision in my mind at the moment is
one about the middle 'nineties-a decision
of the House of Lords dealing with one
of those machine-gun manufacturers. I
forget whether it was Maxim's or Nordenfelt's. One of the learned law lords who
heard that case remarked that all such
contracts must be considered according
to the circumstances of the time. His
Lordship drew attention to the fact that
the introduction of the telephone, for
instance, had a great deal of influence
on that subject, and said that all these
matters must be interpreted in the light
of present-day conditions. I think in the
same case, as in all the older cases, great
stress was laid on the fact that the
necessary ingredient of a valid contract
of that sort was valuable consideration.
It appears from the Clause before us that
valuable consideration has been paid,
but in fact the Road Transport Board
have not always received value for their
money. I do not think there is anything
in the wording of this Clause to which
objection can be taken. I think it is a
necessary and convenient Clause as tending to prevent the necessity or the urge
for litigation.
Clause 3, of course, is a simple Clause,
because it simply provides a limitation
of time. Anybody who does not avail
himself of that Clause will, at the expiration of the period which will be laid
down in the Act, be simply operating his
vehicle without a licence, and the
ordinary law will, under the 1935 Act,
then operate. I do not see any reason or
justification that this House will have for
rejecting the Bill before us, but I want
once more to emphasise the great necessity
which there will be for this Joint Committee to
to have ample time. Their
sittings that is a matter for themselves
-must not be too consecutive, if I may
use the word. There must be time for
thought and consideration. I do not,
myself, believe that it is likely that the
necessary material for a Bill can be prepared in under twelve months at the very
earliest. When their decision is taken
it must be as I have said, a free decision,
Hairdressers Bill
Adjournment
19 DECEMBER 1938
[Mr. Leslie]
taken after considering the whole matter
on the broadest possible lines without
any undue regard for any private
interests, but with the sole view of providing for the public a satisfactory and
economic system of public transport.
Mr. ROBB: I want to say only two
things. In reply to Senator McAllister
may I point out that this is not
necessarily a finance Measure. The only
part of it that deals with vehicles is that
relating to a census of vehicles, and there
is nothing in it as to the imposition of
any fee for a licence. It is only for the
purpose of collecting particulars with regard to existing vehicles that are licensed.
May I assure hon. Members that no
pressure as to the deliberations of the
Committee will be put upon it by the
Government. The matter is entirely for
the Committee itself. As Sir Roland
Nugent said, one of the main objects of
the Bill is to try to collect as soon as
possible information that must be
necessary for the Committee if they are
to consider the whole problem as it exists
to-day.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill accordingly read a Second time,
and committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.
Hairdressers Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Leslie.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
Mr. McALLISTER: This Bill does
not appear on the Order Paper, and it
is not competent for us to consider it.
The CHAIRMAN: An order was given
for the Bill to be considered in Committee on this day, and by error it was
omitted from the Order Paper.
Mr. McALLISTER: I accept that
explanation.
CLAUSES 1 (Registration of premises
to be used for trade or business or barber
or hairdresser), 2 (Power of local
authority to make by-laws), 3 (Penalties).
4 (Prosecution of offences), 5 (Extent and
application of Act), 6 (Expenses of local
authority to make by-laws), 3 (Penalties),
(Short title) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered, That the Bill be reported
without amendment to the House.
Ordered, That the House do now
resume.
Bill reported without amendment.
Resolved, "That this Report be now
received." (Mr. Leslie.)
Ordered, That the Bill be read the
Third time to-morrow.
Adjournment.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
House do now adjourn." The intention
is to take the remaining stages of the
Transport Bill to-morrow, and then to
meet for the Royal Assent at Twelve
o'clock on Friday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Five Minutes after Four
o'clock.
Private Business
Road Transport Committee
20 DECEMBER 1938
THE SENATE

Tuesday, 20th December, 1938.
The Senate met at a quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Plumb's Divorce Bill.
:-
The following Report was made from
the Joint Committee on Divorce Bills:-
That they had considered Plumb's
Divorce Bill, and that the averments in
the Preamble had been proved to their
satisfaction, and that they had gone
through the Bill and made an Amendment thereto.
McGugan's Divorce Bill.
The following Report was made from
the Joint Committee on Divorce Bills:-
That they had considered McGugan's
Divorce Bill, and that the averments in
the Preamble had been proved to their
satisfaction, and that they had gone
through the Bill and directed the same
to be reported to the House without
amendment.
Clark's Divorce Bill.
The following Report was made from
the Joint Committee on Divorce Bills :-
That they had considered Clark's Divorce
Bill, and that the averments in the
Preamble had been proved to their satisfaction, and that they had gone through
the Bill and directed the same to be
reported to the House without amendment.
Message from the Governor.
Mr. ROBB (Leader of the House): I
have received the following Message from
His Grace the Governor :
I have received with great satisfaction the expression of your thanks for
the Speech with which I opened the
Session
present
of Parliament.-
-(Signed) Abercorn.
Business of the House.
Resolved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking the remaining
stages of the Road Transport (No. 2)
Bill."-(Mr. Robb.)
Road Transport Joint Select Committee.
Mr. ROBB: I beg to move,
"That the Senators following be
named of a Joint Select Committee of
both Houses of Parliament, consisting
of twenty-four Members, to consider
the Reports of His Honour Herbert
M. Thompson, K.C., and of the Committee presided over by Sir William
McLintock, Bart., C.B.E., C.V.O.,
C.A., in regard to public transport in
Northern Ireland, and to report
whether any, and if so what, modifications in the recommendations thereof
are deemed to be necessary, or to suggest an alternative scheme which will
place transport in Northern Ireland on
a sound and economic basis: -The
Mayor of Londonderry, Major Boyle,
Mr. J. Cunningham, Major Dobbs, Sir
E. Herdman, Mr. Maguire, Sir J.
Davison, Mr. Leslie, Captain Perceval-
Maxwell, Sir R. Nugent, Mr.
McLaughlin, Mr. Taylor.
That the Committee have power to
send for persons, papers and records.
That twelve be the quorum of the
Committee.
That the Committee have leave to sit
notwithstanding any adjournment of
the House.
Mr. McALLISTER: I rise to oppose
the appointment of this Committee, and
I do so as a Northern Ireland man and
with all the sincerity I can command.
This Committee is being appointed merely
for the express purpose of distracting
public attention from the tragic view
which it holds to-day of the transport
situation. It is mere camouflage on the
part of the Government to enable them
to say, when questioned or challenged on
this matter, "We have set up a Committee of both Houses, representative of
all parties, to deal with this question."
In connection with another matter I
myself had experience of one of these
committees. The committee dragged on
for a year and I was muzzled. I, like
B
67 Road Transport Committee
Road Transport Committee 68
THE DENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
my colleague, could not speak on the
question with which I was vitally concerned for a whole year. I certainly will
not fall into that trap again.
I do not know the constitution of the
Committee representing the other House,
but I find that out of the twelve Members
of this House who are to serve on the Committee five have no concern, good, bad
or indifferent, with the question at issue.
These five Members represent Belfast
and Londonderry-
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry to interrupt the hon. Member, but the Motion
Ideals with the twelve Members of this
House who are to serve on the Joint Committee. The hon. Senator cannot refer
to the Members of another House who
have been selected to serve on the Committee. That point does not arise.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I referred to five
of the twelve Members to be appointed by
this House. I did not deal with the
Members of the Committee appointed by
the other House. I said that five of the
twelve Members to be elected by this
House had no concern with the matter at
issue, good, bad, or indifferent. I also
object strongly to the terms of reference.
At the end of the first part of the Motion
there is a suggestion that an alternative
scheme might be adopted, but the whole
body of the Resolution deals with the
McLintock Committee's Report. Public
bodies and Chambers of Commerce in
Northern Ireland have almost universally
condemned the findings of that Committee, and I know that public opinion
is appalled at the suggestion that the
Government should attempt to follow that
Report. If that is the line the Government intend to take it will only aggravate the situation and make public
opinion far more uneasy than it is at the
present moment.
I could not find words too strong to
condemn the setting up of this Committee. It is merely a Committee which
the Government is appointing to hide
behind, and I for one would certainly not
touch it on any consideration. The Lord
Mayor of Belfast wisely advised the
Belfast Corporation to stay out of the
scheme established by the Act of 1935.
The Lord Mayor stood loyally by his
Corporation and the citizens of Belfast.
The Mayor of Derry also succeeded in excluding the transport of the city of Derry
from the 1935 Act, and I do suggest with
all sincerity that this Resolution should
not be accepted by the House.
Mr. ROBB: The hon. Member seems
to think that this Committee is being set
up solely for the Government to shelter
behind if they wish to adopt the
McLintock Committee's Report. The
Committee is being set up to investigate
the whole problem of transport in
Northern Ireland, and the terms of reference do not prevent the Committee from
recommending anything directly contrary
to the recommendations of the McLintock
Committee. The Joint Committee will
examine the recommendations that have
been made by the McLintock Committee,
and, as Members representative of the
whole of Northern Ireland, they will see
whether the system recommended by the
McLintock Committee is the most appropriate, or whether they can suggest an
alternative scheme that would be better.
The Committee is entirely free to examine
the whole problem applicable to the whole
of Northern Ireland. Belfast forms an
important part of Northern Ireland and
ought to be represented on the Committee.
There is no intention to muzzle anybody in regard to what they want to say
about the Government, Sir William
McLintock, or anybody else. The
Government hopes that as a result of the
investigations which will be made by the
Committee some scheme, whether the
McLintock scheme or some other, will
eventually be evolved that will be suitable
to the conditions in Northern Ireland.
It is for that purpose, and in order to get
a scheme that will have the backing of
public opinion in Northern Ireland, that
this Joint Committee is being set up. It
is quite true to say that the Transport
Board suffered greatly from the fact that
there was a prejudice in the minds of
many people against its operations, and
it is hoped that some scheme will now be
evolved that will have the sympathy of
the people of Northern Ireland and not
their antagonism, and that it will be a
scheme that will prove to be economical,
efficient and useful, and one that will
command the support of the public.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I would like to
ask the Leader of the House one question.
When he says the Joint Committee may

On Question, Motion agreed to.
Road Transport (No. 2) Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill.”—(Mr. Robb.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSES 1 (Special register of goods
vehicles) and 2 (Restriction on certain
road transport operations) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Amendment as to acquisition of transport undertakings).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: As this Bill is
now about to pass may I respectfully remind the Government that such people as
have been trying to evade the Act,
traders or otherwise, are now about to be
dealt with under this Bill, and I suggest
that those also who, in a certain way,
have not co-ordinated transport in the
way this Act intended should be severely
dealt with by the Government if they do
not toe the line. I am referring to the
Transport Board and the wasteful and uneconomic competition in which they
indulged. The railways have undertaken.
to do all they possibly can in connection
with this, and I do strongly urge the
Government that, whilst the Committee
is sitting, in the interests of the new Act
the Transport Board be urged to carry out
the intention of the Act, that is, the coordination of transport by road and rail.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I associate myself entirely with what my Friend has
suggested. I think steps should be taken
I
to try to prevent any man carrying on
his trade after he has had full compensation for goodwill. This is a serious
question. I understand that there are
about 8,000 or 9,000 motor vehicles in
Northern Ireland privately owned.
think it very wrong to leave them in the
hands of the Ministry of Home Affairs
without some appeal to an independent
tribunal. I always protested against
that, and I would like the right hon.
Gentleman, the Leader of the House, to
consider this question of licensing and to
give an assurance before this Bill passes
through the House that the public will
have the right of appeal from the Minister in his office to a judge.
Mr. ROBB: In answer to that question
I may say a licence is never refused except
upon the ground of a mechanical defect
in the lorry. There is no question of
what the lorry is used for. The Ministry's inspectors-I do not know exactly
their qualifications-examine the vehicles,
and it is only on the ground of mechanical
defects that licences are refused-no
other.
Mr. LESLIE: What Clause in the Bill
are we discussing?
The CHAIRMAN: That is what I was
wanting to know.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 4 (Interpretation) and 5
(Short title) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered, That the Bill be reported
without amendment to the House.
Ordered, That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without amendment.
Mr. ROBB: I beg to move, "That this
Report be now received."
Mr. MCALLISTER: I did not intend
to speak again but for the intervention
of the hon. and particularly learned
Senator who sits behind. He delivered a
lecture yesterday to the House and we
find him intervening to-day to educate
Road Transport Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
Members in the discharge of their duty.
I did not deal in detail with the question
at issue yesterday, because I thought
every Member of this House, even the
most newly appointed Member, knew the
system of laying Regulations before the
House on the Table. If I had dealt with
that I would have felt I was taking up
unnecessary time. Indeed I felt I would
have been insulting the Members. But
I do suggest that before these regulations,
tabled or otherwise, come into operation
they should be passed by this House.
That is the point I raised. Perhaps I have
made it sufficiently clear for the hon.
Member to follow the point to-day.
Mr. LESLIE: I fear I did not quite
catch the hon. Member's reference to myself. I think he was referring to the suggestion I made that the House should
refer to Section 53 of the Motor Transport Act, 1934, because that reference
seemed to me to be rather necessary to
enable us to understand the exact purport of the proposal in Clause 1 of the
Bill before us. Apparently the hon.
Member's point is that the Regulations
should not be made with a view to subsequent tabulation-if I may use the
word but the Regulations should
invariably be made by Parliament and
then incorporated in the particular Bill.
Mr. MCALLISTER: No, no.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I am afraid I am
still at a loss to understand what the hon.
Member did mean. I thought that his
remarks this afternoon were directed to
the point, which is sometimes made, that
Parliaments nowadays leave too much to
the discretion of the Minister in the way
of drawing up regulations and things of
that sort. That is a common complaint,
and the usual answer is that Parliament
has not got the time to go meticulously
into details and, therefore, certain details
are relegated to the schedules or left in
the power of the Minister to draft regulations subject to submission to Parliament.
In this third case the Regulations have
to be submitted to both Houses of
Parliament and they have to lie on the
Table for five sitting days, and during
that period they are liable to attack in
both Houses and the House may vote.
Hon. Members may remember that during last Session we did attack in this
House Regulations made by a Ministry,
and the House divided, and the Regulations were upheld by 9 votes against 8.
I think that shows that Parliament does
not neglect the opportunity provided for
it of considering these Regulations if they
think the necessity arises. I do not think
there is anything more I can say to make
matters clearer.
Mr. McALLISTER: On a point of
correction. No Member of this House
receives any communication, good, bad or
indifferent that these far-reaching rules
and regulations are what is called laid
upon the Table. It was to remedy that
abuse I raised the question.
Mr. LESLIE: The fact that a Paper
has been laid is, of course, recorded in
the Debates.
Mr. MCALLISTER: Afterwards.
Mr. LESLIE: During the statutory
period.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I should like to
know if a Member is notified that these
Regulations are laid on the Table. The
only thing I knew was when it was on
the Order Paper the day afterwards. I
do hold with Senator McAllister that we
are entitled, as Members of Parliament,
to know when Regulations are to lie on
the Table, and we should receive due
notice. Any public representative is
entitled to that.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved, "That the Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Mr. Robb.)
Resolved, "That the Bill do now pass."
-(Mr. Robb.)
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any amendment.
Hairdressers Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, "That
this Bill be now read the Third time.'
In making that Motion I should like, if
the House will permit me, to express the
earnest hope that if those who sit in
Hairdressers Bill-
Third Reading
20 DECEMBER 1938
another place, to which this Bill is about
to be dispatched, find on reconsideration
that Sub-clause (2) of Clause 3 is repugnant to them they will, in that event,
consider not only the myriad maze of
precedents, but what has been said in
this House, and submit to us a reasonable, working alternative which we can
accept. This House will readily accept
anything which will provide what I regard as an essential protection for the
local authority in the event of grave
misfeasance. That matter need not arise.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved, "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Mr. Leslie.)
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to carry down the said Bill
and desire their concurrence.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn until Friday.-(Mr. Long.)
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty minutes before
Four o'clock until Five
minutes after Twelve
o'clock noon on Friday.
